haystack: how much does garden clearance cost in the uk? honest uk garden clearance pricing for 2026. see real cost ranges for small, medium and large clearance jobs, with factors that push the price up or down. how much does garden clearance cost in the uk? garden prices & costs garden clearance prices in the uk vary more than almost any other garden service. a small back garden tidy can come in at under 200 pounds while a full overgrown half-acre clearance can run to several thousand. this guide breaks down what drives the cost, what you should expect to pay, and how to keep your final bill predictable. we work across buckinghamshire, oxfordshire and berkshire and the prices here reflect what we see locally. quick answer for a typical uk residential garden clearance in 2026, expect to pay somewhere between 250 and 750 pounds for a standard back garden that has got out of hand. smaller jobs in good condition can come in at 150 to 250. larger overgrown plots, especially those needing waste removal, can run from 800 to 2,500 or more. the single biggest factor is the volume of waste. a garden full of brambles, ivy and broken sheds produces a surprising amount of green waste. removing that waste to a licensed tip costs money, often 100 to 200 per tonne including transport. ballpark price ranges small back garden tidy: 150 to 350 standard clearance with waste removal: 350 to 750 heavy overgrowth, brambles, large shrubs: 750 to 1,500 full plot clearance including shed and tree work: 1,500 to 3,500 what affects the cost of garden clearance garden size this is the most obvious factor. a 50 square metre courtyard garden takes a few hours to clear. a 500 square metre plot can take a small team three full days. we price by the area plus the labour and waste, but as a rough guide a small team can clear about 100 square metres of moderately overgrown garden per day. level of overgrowth a light tidy where we mow, edge and prune is much quicker than clearing a jungle of brambles and self-seeded sycamores. heavy bramble coverage can take four times as long as light overgrowth for the same area. ivy climbing into trees and along fences is particularly time consuming because every strand has to be cut and pulled. waste volume and disposal green waste disposal in the uk is regulated. we have to use a licensed commercial tip, and we pay per tonne. a typical clearance of an overgrown back garden produces 1 to 3 tonnes of waste. larger jobs can produce 6 tonnes or more. disposal alone can add 150 to 600 to the bill. if you have a council green waste bin and the clearance is light, we can sometimes leave waste in the bin for weekly collection. this saves the disposal cost but takes weeks to clear all the waste. access to the garden if we can get a wheelbarrow from the front of the house to the back garden in a straight line, clearance is quick. if we have to carry waste through the house, up steps, or through a narrow side passage, the work takes much longer. houses with poor access often cost 20 to 40 percent more. what needs removing different elements have different labour profiles: brambles and ivy: slow to cut, slow to bag, painful to handle old sheds and outbuildings: demolition plus wood disposal small trees and large shrubs: cutting plus stump treatment patio and paving removal: heavy work, hard waste to dispose of soil and rubble: classified as inert waste, cheaper to dispose of than green waste hidden costs to watch for asbestos in old sheds and garages old concrete sheds, particularly those built between 1940 and 1980, often have asbestos cement roofs. this has to be removed and disposed of separately by a specialist contractor. if we spot asbestos during a clearance we stop work and bring in a licensed asbestos contractor. budget 300 to 800 for a small asbestos garage roof. tree preservation orders and conservation areas if your property is in a conservation area or has trees with a tree preservation order, you need council permission before removing trees or even doing major pruning. permission takes 6 weeks and is free, but doing the work without permission can lead to fines up to 20,000 pounds. check with your local council first. how to get the best price send photos first any reputable clearance company will want to see photos before quoting. we ask for photos from every angle and a quick measurement or estimate of garden size. this lets us give you a realistic price range before we visit. without photos, we have to add a contingency and you pay more. send us photos on whatsapp for a same-day estimate. book in the right season january and february are the quiet months for garden clearance companies. we have more availability and our prices can be slightly keener than in peak spring. if your clearance is not urgent, booking in late winter can save 10 to 15 percent. be clear about what you want do you want a tidy up that keeps the existing layout, or a full clearance ready for landscaping? the difference in cost can be enormous. the more specific you can be, the more accurate your quote will be. ask about waste if you have space for a skip or a hippobag, you can save on labour time. we can fill a skip in a few hours instead of making multiple trips to the tip. a 6 yard skip costs about 250 including delivery and collection, and saves us several hours of driving. what is included in our garden clearance our standard clearance includes: cutting back all overgrowth to ground level removal of brambles, ivy and self-seeded weeds pruning of shrubs to a manageable size digging out invasive roots where requested removal of all green waste sweeping and basic tidy of hard surfaces we can also include shed demolition, small tree removal, patio lift and stump grinding as add-ons. each is priced separately so you only pay for what you need. what garden clearance does not include it is just as important to know what is not in the standard clearance. we do not include: replanting or new landscaping (quoted separately) hard landscaping like new patios or paths tree surgery on trees over 5 metres (specialist required) removal of (specialist contractor required by law) removal of asbestos (specialist contractor required) is a particular issue in our area. it is classified as controlled waste and can only be disposed of at licensed facilities. if you suspect you have , contact a specialist. doing the work yourself is illegal and can spread the plant. try our clearance calculator we have built a quick clearance calculator that gives you an estimate range based on garden size, overgrowth level and waste volume. it takes about 2 minutes to fill in. the answer is a wide range, deliberately, because we cannot give an exact price without seeing the garden. use the clearance calculator final thoughts garden clearance is one of those jobs that is almost always cheaper and faster than people expect when done by a professional team with the right equipment. doing it yourself is possible for small jobs but the waste disposal, the physical effort and the time often make it more expensive in real terms than hiring someone. if you have a garden that needs clearing, the best first step is a few photos and a quick whatsapp message. we can tell you within a day whether your budget is realistic and what we can do for it. get a written quote frequently asked questions can i get a fixed price for garden clearance? yes, after a site visit. our initial estimate is a range, but once we have seen the garden we give a fixed price that we stick to. if we find something unexpected like asbestos or hidden rubbish, we stop and discuss before adding to the bill. how long does a garden clearance take? a small back garden takes half a day. a typical family garden takes 1 to 2 days with a 2-person crew. a large overgrown plot can take 3 to 5 days. we always work in teams of 2 or 3 to keep the job moving. do you take away everything? yes, including green waste, soil, rubble, old fencing, sheds and small trees. we leave the garden clean and tidy. the only things we cannot take are asbestos and hazardous chemicals. what areas do you cover? we cover the whole of oxfordshire, buckinghamshire and berkshire. see our location pages for town-specific information. sage weeds weed bramble brambles ivy ant rat cat january jan february feb how much does regular garden maintenance cost in 2026? real uk garden maintenance pricing for 2026. see what a typical fortnightly visit costs, what affects the price, and how to choose the right gardener. how much does regular garden maintenance cost in 2026? garden prices & costs regular garden maintenance is the most cost effective way to keep your garden looking good without spending a fortune. a fortnightly visit from a professional gardener keeps on top of weeds, lawns and hedges, and stops small problems becoming big ones. this guide explains what maintenance costs in the uk in 2026, what is included, and how to get the best value. quick answer for a typical uk family garden of around 100 to 200 square metres, expect to pay 30 to 60 pounds per visit for a gardener spending 1 to 2 hours. most clients book fortnightly visits, which works out at 60 to 120 pounds per month. larger gardens with lawns, hedges and multiple borders can run from 150 to 400 per month. the hourly rate for a professional gardener in southern england in 2026 ranges from 30 to 50 pounds per hour, with a typical visit lasting 1.5 to 3 hours. cheaper rates are available but usually mean unqualified or uninsured gardeners, which is a false economy. typical monthly costs small courtyard garden, monthly visit: 40 to 80 per month family garden, fortnightly: 80 to 180 per month large garden with lawn, fortnightly: 180 to 350 per month premium estate garden, weekly: 400 to 800 per month what affects the cost of garden maintenance garden size this is the biggest factor. a small courtyard garden can be kept tidy in an hour. a large family garden with a lawn, several beds and a hedge takes 2 to 3 hours per visit. most maintenance gardeners can cover about 80 to 100 square metres of mixed garden per hour. frequency of visits fortnightly visits are the sweet spot for most uk gardens. weekly visits are needed only for very formal gardens or large estates. monthly visits are cheaper but the garden can look shaggy between visits, especially in the growing season. most clients find that fortnightly from march to october and monthly through winter works well. standard of finish there are three broad levels of maintenance: basic tidy: mowing, edging, weeding, light pruning. the garden looks presentable but not manicured. well-kept: all of the above plus deadheading, more careful pruning, hedge trimming in season, leaf clearance. the garden looks like it is being looked after. premium estate standard: everything plus specialised care, season-specific work, formal lawn treatment, specimen plant care. the garden looks like a national trust property. premium standard can cost 2 to 3 times what a basic tidy costs. most family gardens sit comfortably in the well-kept bracket. lawn size the lawn is usually the most time consuming part of a maintenance visit. a small lawn takes 20 minutes to mow and edge. a large lawn of 200 square metres takes an hour. if you want the lawn striped or cut to a specific height with a cylinder mower, expect to pay more. hedges hedges need cutting once or twice a year depending on species. beech and yew need one cut. privet needs three or four. a 20 metre hedge takes about 3 hours to cut both sides and top. hedge cutting is usually billed as a separate job rather than included in the regular maintenance price. waste removal if you have a council green waste bin, your gardener can put cuttings in it for free. if not, you need to either compost at home, take waste to the tip yourself, or pay for waste removal. we charge a small fee per visit for waste removal, typically 15 to 25 pounds. what should be included in a maintenance visit a proper maintenance visit should include: lawn mowing and edging weeding of beds and borders deadheading of flowering plants light pruning of shrubs and perennials sweeping of paths and patios check of any irrigation or watering removal of all waste it should not include major landscaping, tree surgery, hedge cutting of large hedges, or specialist work like lawn renovation. these are quoted separately. how to choose a gardener check insurance public liability insurance is essential. if a gardener damages your property or injures someone, the insurance covers the cost. ask for proof of insurance. we carry public liability insurance. check qualifications there is no legal requirement for gardeners to be qualified, but a professional gardener should have some horticultural training. look for rhs qualifications, city and guilds or nvq in horticulture. these show a commitment to the trade. ask for references any established gardener should be able to give you contact details for two or three existing clients. ask them about reliability, quality of work and how the garden looks. see our portfolio page for examples of our work. get it in writing a proper maintenance agreement should specify the frequency of visits, what is included, the price per visit, and the terms of cancellation. verbal agreements lead to misunderstandings. we always confirm in writing before starting regular work. watch out for red flags quotes that are significantly cheaper than others (often means no insurance or cash in hand only) no business address or landline unwilling to put anything in writing pressure to pay cash no seasonal considerations spring (march to may) this is the busiest time for gardeners. borders need clearing, lawns need their first cut, hedges need attention. expect slightly higher rates as demand peaks. book early to secure a regular slot. summer (june to august) the growing season. lawns need weekly cutting if you want them looking good. weeds grow fastest. most maintenance clients have fortnightly visits through summer. autumn (september to november) leaf clearance is the big job. lawns slow down. good time for planting perennials and shrubs. most clients drop to monthly visits as growth slows. winter (december to february) quiet season. lawns do not need cutting unless very mild. borders need clearing of dead growth. hedges can be pruned (avoiding conifers and evergreens in freezing weather). many clients pause maintenance through january, restarting in february. how to reduce maintenance costs plant low-maintenance varieties some plants need regular deadheading, pruning and dividing. others look after themselves. see our guide to low-maintenance plants for chiltern gardens for recommendations. reduce lawn area lawns are the most labour-intensive part of a garden. replacing some lawn with beds of shrubs and ground cover can reduce maintenance significantly. a 50 square metre shrub bed takes 30 minutes to maintain per year. the same area of lawn takes 30 minutes per week in summer. use ground cover plants ground cover plants like hardy geraniums, bergenia and epimedium suppress weeds and reduce the amount of hand weeding needed. a well-planted border needs much less attention than an empty one. install irrigation a simple drip irrigation system on a timer can save hours of watering each week, especially for containers and newly planted areas. we install basic systems from 250 pounds. mulch annually a 5cm layer of bark or compost mulch in spring suppresses weeds for the whole season and reduces watering. a one-off mulch can save 30 percent of maintenance time through the year. try our maintenance calculator we have built a quick calculator that gives you a monthly estimate range based on your garden size, frequency and standard of finish. it takes 2 minutes and gives you a wide range rather than a specific price, because every garden is different. use the garden care level assessment final thoughts the cheapest gardener is rarely the best value. a gardener charging 25 pounds an hour may take twice as long as one charging 40 pounds an hour, and the quality of work may be lower. a good professional gardener pays for themselves by keeping your garden healthy, spotting problems early, and freeing up your weekends. if you have a garden that needs regular care, the best first step is a free site visit. we can assess the garden, suggest a realistic maintenance schedule and give you a written quote. there is no obligation. book a free site visit frequently asked questions do i need to be at home for maintenance visits? no. most of our regular clients are at work or away. we agree access arrangements in advance. most clients give us a key or leave a side gate unlocked. we always lock up after ourselves. can i pay monthly? yes. we offer monthly direct debit for regular maintenance clients. the annual cost is split into 12 equal payments, which makes budgeting easier. most clients prefer this to paying per visit. what if i want to skip a visit? no problem. just give us 48 hours notice. we can also add extra visits if you are preparing for a party or selling the house. do you work in winter? yes. winter visits are mostly about keeping the garden tidy, clearing leaves and preparing for spring. we avoid cutting hedges in freezing weather and do not mow lawns when the ground is waterlogged. epimedium bergenia yew privet beech geranium weeds weed rust ant ants when should hedges be cut in the uk? uk hedge cutting calendar with species-specific timings, legal nesting bird rules, and aftercare advice from chiltern garden maintenance. when should hedges be cut in the uk? hedges, pruning & cutting times hedge cutting timing matters more than most people realise. cut at the wrong time and you can lose a year of growth, damage the hedge, or fall foul of wildlife protection laws. this guide explains when to cut each type of hedge in the uk, what the law says about nesting birds, and how to keep your hedge healthy for decades. quick answer the best time to cut most uk hedges is late winter (february) for deciduous hedges like beech and hornbeam, and late summer (august) for evergreen hedges like leylandii and laurel. formal hedges that need two cuts should be done in june and again in september. the main rule is to avoid cutting between 1 march and 31 august when birds are nesting. bird nesting season under the wildlife and countryside act 1981, it is an offence to damage or destroy the nest of any wild bird while it is being built or used. the main nesting season in the uk is 1 march to 31 august. you can technically cut hedges during this period but you must check carefully for nests first. if in doubt, wait until september. hedge cutting calendar by species beech (fagus sylvatica) the classic chiltern hedge. cut once a year in late august or early september. this keeps the brown leaves on through winter for privacy. a harder renovation prune can be done in february when the hedge is dormant. beech responds well to hard pruning and will regrow from old wood. hornbeam (carpinus betulus) similar to beech in habit and pruning. cut once in late august. hornbeam holds its brown leaves through winter less reliably than beech, but the pruning is the same. yew (taxus baccata) the king of formal hedges. cut once or twice a year. a light trim in late june keeps the shape, and a harder cut in late august gives a clean finish for winter. yew regenerates from old wood so overgrown hedges can be cut back hard in february. leylandii (x hesperotropsis leylandii) fast growing conifer that needs cutting once or twice a year. trim in late may or june and again in september. never cut into old brown wood because leylandii does not regrow from bare stems. keep the top narrower than the base to let light reach the bottom. privet (ligustrum ovalifolium) fast growing. cut 2 to 3 times through the growing season from may to september. privet regrows quickly and tolerates hard pruning. laurel (prunus laurocerasus) cut once in late summer after the new growth has finished. laurels can be cut with secateurs rather than hedge trimmer to avoid cutting leaves in half, which looks unsightly. a harder renovation prune is fine in february. hawthorn (crataegus monogyna) native hedge. cut once a year in late winter (february). hawthorn flowers on old wood so if you want the spring blossom, prune after flowering in june. avoid cutting during nesting season unless you have checked for nests. box (buxus sempervirens) formal low hedges. cut once or twice a year, traditionally on derby day in late may and again in september. with box blight now widespread, many people are replacing box with box alternatives like japanese holly. escallonia and pittosporum mild climate hedges. light trim after flowering, in late summer. avoid cutting in cold weather as frost can damage cut stems. the legal position on hedge cutting nesting birds all wild birds, their nests and their eggs are protected by law in the uk. it is an offence to: intentionally kill, injure or take any wild bird take, damage or destroy the nest of any wild bird while it is in use or being built take or destroy the egg of any wild bird the maximum penalty is 5,000 pounds and up to 6 months in prison. the main nesting season is 1 march to 31 august, but birds can nest outside this window, especially in mild years. some species like collared doves and pigeons nest all year round. if you absolutely must cut a hedge during nesting season, check thoroughly first. watch the hedge for 30 minutes from a distance. if birds are flying in and out, there is an active nest and you should wait. see the rspb guidance on hedge cutting . high hedges under the anti-social behaviour act 2003, local councils can deal with complaints about evergreen hedges over 2 metres tall that are affecting reasonable enjoyment of a property. if you have a neighbour whose leylandii is blocking your light, you can complain to the council. they can order the hedge to be reduced. this law applies only to evergreen or semi-evergreen hedges, not to deciduous ones like beech. how to cut a hedge properly tools for small hedges, hand shears are still the best tool. they give a cleaner cut and let you see what you are doing. for larger hedges, a powered hedge trimmer is essential. petrol trimmers are more powerful and cable-free. electric trimmers are quieter and lighter but need a power source. battery trimmers are improving rapidly and are now a viable option for most gardens. shape the golden rule of hedge cutting is to keep the top narrower than the bottom. this is called the a-frame or batter. it lets light reach the lower branches, which keeps the hedge green all the way down. a hedge that is wider at the top than the bottom will go bare at the base within a few years. cut depth for formal hedges, cut back to the same point each time. this creates a dense surface of leaves and twigs. for informal hedges, cut back by about a third of the new growth. this keeps the hedge natural while still tidy. cleanup remove all cuttings from the top of the hedge. leaves left on top can rot and cause dieback. rake up cuttings from the ground. if you have a lawn under the hedge, lay a tarpaulin down before cutting to make cleanup easier. aftercare feeding hedges are hungry plants. a spring feed with a balanced fertiliser such as growmore or blood, fish and bone will keep growth strong. apply about 50g per square metre and water in if the soil is dry. mulching a 5cm mulch of compost or bark around the base of the hedge suppresses weeds and retains moisture. keep the mulch away from the stems to prevent rotting. watering newly planted hedges need watering through the first two growing seasons. once established, most hedges are drought tolerant. yew, beech and hornbeam all develop deep roots and can cope with dry periods. renovation of overgrown hedges overgrown hedges can usually be renovated. the technique depends on the species: yew, beech, hornbeam, hawthorn: can be cut back hard to within 30cm of the ground in february. they will regrow over 2 to 3 years. privet, laurel: can be cut back hard in late winter. leylandii: cannot be cut back into old brown wood. the only option is to remove and replant. box: can be cut back hard in february but is susceptible to blight on the new growth. when renovating, cut one side one year and the other side the next year. this is less of a shock to the hedge and means you still have some screening while it regrows. common mistakes cutting leylandii into old wood this is the most common mistake we see. leylandii does not regrow from brown wood. once you have cut into the brown, that part of the hedge is bare forever. the only solution is to replant. cutting in freezing weather conifers and evergreens should not be cut in freezing weather. the cut ends can be damaged by frost and turn brown. wait for a mild spell. letting hedges get too tall it is much easier to keep a hedge at the right height than to reduce an overgrown one. once a hedge reaches the height you want, cut the new growth back to that height each year. not checking for nesting birds this is both a legal and an ethical issue. always check before cutting in spring and summer. final thoughts hedge cutting is one of those garden jobs that rewards getting the timing right. a well-maintained hedge cut at the right time will look good for 50 years or more. a poorly maintained hedge can become a liability within a decade. if you have a hedge that needs cutting, especially a tall one or one that needs renovation, give us a call. we have the right equipment and the right training to do the job safely and properly. our hedge cutting service frequently asked questions can i cut my neighbour's hedge if it overhangs my garden? you can cut back any branches that overhang your property to the boundary line. you should offer the cuttings back to your neighbour. you cannot enter their property to cut the hedge without permission. how tall can a hedge be? there is no general limit. but if an evergreen hedge is over 2 metres and is affecting reasonable enjoyment of your property, you can complain to the council under the high hedges legislation. why is my leylandii going brown inside? leylandii naturally sheds older leaves from inside the hedge as new growth shades them. some browning is normal. if the browning is extensive, it may be due to drought, aphids or being cut back too hard into old wood. how much does hedge cutting cost? for a typical 20 metre hedge, expect to pay 150 to 350 per cut. taller hedges needing ladders or platforms cost more. see our estimate tools for a quick guide. pittosporum prunus box buxus yew privet laurel leylandii beech how to restore an overgrown garden: a step by step guide practical uk guide to clearing and restoring an overgrown garden. real timeframes, real costs, real decisions, no fluff. how to restore an overgrown garden garden clearance & tidy-up guides an overgrown garden can feel overwhelming. whether you have just moved into a property with a jungle out the back, or your own garden has got away from you over a few seasons, the process of restoration is the same. this step by step guide walks you through how to tackle an overgrown garden, what order to do things in, and how to keep costs down. quick answer restoring an overgrown garden happens in five stages: assess, clear, repair, replant, maintain. for a typical 100 square metre family garden, the full process takes 5 to 10 working days spread over 2 to 4 weeks, and costs between 1,000 and 3,500 pounds depending on what is involved. the most important thing is to do the work in the right order. clearing before you know what you want, or replanting before the soil is sorted, wastes time and money. stage 1: assess the garden take photos before you start before you cut anything, photograph the garden from every angle. you will be amazed how useful these photos are later, both for showing the transformation and for understanding what was there before. wait a full season if you can if you have just moved in and the garden is overgrown, wait one full growing season before clearing if possible. many plants that look like weeds in spring turn out to be treasured perennials by mid-summer. we have seen clients pay to dig up plants that turned out to be expensive specimens like peonies or hellebores. if waiting is not practical, mark anything you are unsure about with a coloured ribbon and leave it standing through the clearance. identify the keepers walk the garden and tag anything you want to keep: mature trees and large shrubs (these are expensive to replace) established hedges that just need a cut specimen plants like mature roses, magnolias, wisteria any plant you recognise and want to keep tag these with bright ribbon or paint. make sure anyone working in the garden knows what the tags mean. check for invasive species look out for giant hogweed and himalayan balsam. these are controlled weeds that require specialist treatment. if you find either, do not cut it or dig it up. fragments as small as 1cm can regrow into new plants. you need a specialist contractor who is a member of the property care association. also check for schedule 9 invasive plants listed under the wildlife and countryside act, including rhododendron ponticum in woodland settings. see the rhs invasive plants guidance . check for nesting birds if you are doing the work between march and august, check all shrubs and hedges for nesting birds before cutting. see our article on when to cut hedges for the legal position. stage 2: clear the garden start with the big stuff take out large unwanted trees and shrubs first. this opens up the space and makes the rest of the work easier. small trees (under 5 metres) can be felled by a competent gardener. larger trees need a qualified tree surgeon with insurance. stumps can be ground out with a stump grinder, which we hire in for around 150 per day. alternatively, treat the cut stump with a glyphosate-based stump killer to prevent regrowth. cut back brambles and ivy brambles are the most common overgrowth problem. cut the stems at ground level with loppers or a brush cutter. then dig out the root crowns with a mattock or spade. leaving the roots means regrowth within months. ivy climbing into trees should be cut at the base. the upper growth will die and fall off over a year or two. pulling live ivy out of trees damages the bark. strip the weeds for ground covered in weeds, the approach depends on what is there: annual weeds: hoe them off and leave on the surface to dry perennial weeds like bindweed, couch grass, ground elder: dig out every root. any fragment left will regrow nettles and docks: dig out the long tap roots moss on paths and patios: pressure wash or treat with a moss killer remove the waste this is often the slowest and most expensive part. options: hire a skip: 200 to 400 for a 6 yard skip. good for mixed waste including soil and rubble. hippobag: 150 to 250 for a megabag, collected when full. good for green waste only. green waste bin: cheap but slow. only works for small amounts. professional waste collection: we charge 140 to 200 per tonne including transport and disposal. deal with the soil once the garden is clear, you will have bare soil. this is the right time to improve it. add a 5cm layer of compost or well rotted manure and dig it in to the top 20cm. on heavy clay, add horticultural grit as well. on sandy soil, add extra organic matter to hold moisture. stage 3: repair the hard landscaping check fences and boundaries now the garden is clear, you can see the fences. replace any rotten posts and panels. treat timber with preservative. concrete posts last forever but cost more. repair paths and patios lift and relay any uneven paving. pressure wash slippery surfaces. re-point joints that have crumbled. see our pressure washing service for what this involves. check drainage look for areas of standing water after rain. these indicate drainage problems. solutions include installing land drains, raising the soil level, or planting moisture-loving plants like willows or dogwoods in the wet patch. service or replace sheds and outbuildings old sheds often have rotting floors and roofs. decide whether to repair or replace. asbestos cement roofs on old sheds need specialist removal. stage 4: replant have a plan before buying any plants, sketch out what you want. mark the position of paths, beds, lawn, hedges and specimen trees. note the sun and shade areas. note the soil type. a simple plan saves money and prevents impulse buys that do not fit. choose the right plants for the soil on the chalky soils of the chilterns, choose plants that thrive in alkaline conditions: lavender, rosemary, clematis, alliums, beech and yew. on heavy clay, choose moisture tolerant plants: hydrangeas, hostas, ferns, dogwoods. see our plant library for detailed recommendations. plant in the right season the best planting time is autumn (october to december) when the soil is still warm and roots can establish before summer. spring planting is fine but requires more watering. avoid planting in summer if possible. plant in drifts plant in groups of 3, 5 or 7 of the same variety. a drift of 5 salvias looks much better than 5 single different plants. plan for the mature size of each plant and leave enough space. use ground cover plant ground cover plants like hardy geraniums, bergenia or epimedium between larger specimens. these suppress weeds and reduce maintenance. mulch after planting a 5cm mulch of bark or compost around new plants suppresses weeds and retains moisture. keep the mulch away from plant stems. stage 5: maintain watering new plants need regular watering for the first growing season. a good soak once a week is better than a light sprinkle every day. water in the morning or evening to reduce evaporation. feeding feed in spring with a balanced fertiliser. avoid over-feeding which produces soft growth that is vulnerable to pests and frost. pruning each plant has its own pruning requirements. see our plant library for the pruning times and methods for each species we recommend. weeding keep on top of weeds for the first two years. once plants establish and fill out, weeds are much less of a problem. regular maintenance visits most restored gardens benefit from a regular maintenance visit. see our guide to maintenance costs for what this should cost. time and cost estimates garden size clearance repairs replanting total small courtyard 30 sqm 300 to 600 200 to 500 200 to 500 700 to 1,600 family garden 100 sqm 600 to 1,500 500 to 1,500 500 to 1,500 1,600 to 4,500 large garden 300 sqm 1,500 to 4,000 1,000 to 3,000 1,000 to 3,000 3,500 to 10,000 these are rough guides. a proper quote requires a site visit. get a quote common mistakes to avoid skimming on clearance trying to save money by not digging out weed roots is a false economy. the weeds come back twice as strong within months, and you have to do the work again. planting before improving soil plants grow badly in poor soil. spend a day improving the soil before any planting and the plants will reward you for years. planting too densely it is tempting to fill the garden immediately. plants need space to grow. a properly planted garden looks sparse for the first year and fills out over 3 to 5 years. ignoring the seasons some work has to be done at the right time. hedge cutting during nesting season is illegal if birds are nesting. planting in summer droughts kills new plants. plan the work around the seasons. final thoughts restoring an overgrown garden is one of the most rewarding things you can do. the transformation from jungle to garden over a few weeks is dramatic, and a well restored garden will give pleasure for decades. the key is doing the work in the right order, using the right plants for the soil, and not skimping on the groundwork. a few days of careful preparation saves years of frustration. if you have an overgrown garden you best low-maintenance plants for chiltern gardens ten plants that thrive in chiltern chalky soil with minimal care. real recommendations from a real chiltern gardener. best low-maintenance plants for chiltern gardens plants, borders & planting ideas chiltern gardens sit on chalk, with free-draining alkaline soil that suits some plants perfectly and defeats others. the trick to a low-maintenance garden here is choosing plants that thrive in these conditions rather than fighting them. this guide lists ten plants we install again and again for clients who want a beautiful garden without constant work. why chiltern soil is different the chiltern hills run from oxfordshire across buckinghamshire to hertfordshire. the underlying rock is chalk, which gives a shallow, free-draining, alkaline soil. in dry summers the soil bakes hard. in wet winters it can waterlog briefly but drains quickly. this is bad news for acid-loving plants like rhododendrons and camellias, which need to be grown in pots of ericaceous compost. but it is excellent news for mediterranean plants, silver foliage, herbs and many classic english cottage garden plants. the plants in this list all thrive in chiltern conditions with very little attention once established. 1. lavender the classic chiltern plant. lavender loves the free-draining chalky soil and full sun. plant in spring, water through the first summer, then leave alone. cut back by one third in late august after flowering. a single plant will flower for 6 to 8 weeks and attract dozens of bees. full lavender profile 2. rosemary another mediterranean native that loves chiltern conditions. rosemary is evergreen, drought tolerant, and flowers in late winter when bees need it most. plant in full sun. prune lightly after flowering. choose an upright variety like miss jessopp's upright for a formal hedge, or a prostrate form for trailing over walls. full rosemary profile 3. salvia (perennial sage) hardy salvias flower from may to october if sheared back after the first flush. they love sun and well-drained soil. the varieties mainacht, caradonna and amethyst are reliable and long flowering. salvias are also aromatic, which deters rabbits and deer. full salvia profile 4. hardy geranium (cranesbill) the most useful ground cover plant in any garden. hardy geraniums flower for months, suppress weeds, and tolerate almost any condition. the variety rozanne flowers from may to october with no deadheading. plant at the front of borders in drifts of 3 or 5. full geranium profile 5. nepeta (catmint) catmint is the perfect partner for roses. it flowers in late spring and again in late summer, with soft violet-blue flowers and grey-green foliage. the variety walker's low is the most reliable. bees love it. cut back hard after the first flush for a tidy second show. full nepeta profile 6. verbena bonariensis the see-through plant for the back of a border. tall wiry stems carry clusters of tiny purple flowers from july to october. butterflies adore it. self-seeds gently in warm gardens. plant in full sun in well-drained soil. full verbena profile 7. alliums ornamental onions flower in late may and june, bridging the gap between spring bulbs and summer perennials. the big round flower heads of purple sensation and globemaster are dramatic and the seed heads stay decorative for months. plant bulbs in october in clusters of 7 or 9. full allium profile 8. stipa tenuissima a low tousled grass that catches the light beautifully. stipa needs full sun and free-draining soil. once established it needs nothing except a comb through with gloved hands in spring to remove dead leaves. pairs beautifully with lavender and salvias. full stipa profile 9. yew for structure and hedging, yew is hard to beat. native to the uk, it grows in any reasonable soil including chalk. yew clips beautifully into formal hedges and topiary, and regenerates from old wood. plant a yew hedge once and it will still be there in 100 years. full yew profile 10. hellebores for shadier parts of the garden, hellebores are the answer. they flower from january to april when little else is happening. the new hybrid varieties have upward-facing flowers in pink, white, purple and almost black. cut the old leaves off in january to show off the flowers. full hellebore profile plants to avoid on chiltern soil some popular plants struggle on chalky soil and are best avoided unless you are willing to grow them in pots: rhododendrons: need acid soil, will go yellow and die on chalk camellias: same as rhododendrons blueberries: need acid soil pieris: needs acid soil heathers (calluna and erica): most need acid soil. some erica varieties tolerate chalk. magnolias: many but not all need acid soil. magnolia kobus and stellata tolerate chalk. if you really want these plants, grow them in large pots of ericaceous compost and feed with an ericaceous plant food twice a year. designing a low-maintenance chiltern garden reduce the lawn lawns are the highest maintenance part of any garden. reducing lawn area and replacing with beds of low-maintenance shrubs and perennials cuts maintenance dramatically. a bed of hardy geraniums and salvia takes 30 minutes a year to maintain. the same area of lawn takes 30 minutes a week in summer. use ground cover plant ground cover plants like hardy geraniums, bergenia, epimedium and ajuga between larger plants. these suppress weeds and reduce the need for mulching. plant in drifts planting 5 of the same variety together gives more impact than 5 different plants scattered. it also means when one plant finishes flowering, the whole drift has the same impact. choose long-flowering plants some plants flower for 2 weeks, others for 4 months. choose plants that give long lasting colour: salvias, hardy geraniums, nepeta, verbena, agapanthus, japanese anemones. a border of long-flowering plants needs less deadheading and looks good for longer. mulch annually a 5cm mulch of bark or compost in spring suppresses weeds for the whole growing season and reduces watering. it is the single most effective low-maintenance thing you can do. install irrigation for containers if you have lots of pots, install a drip irrigation system on a timer. this takes 2 hours to set up and saves hours of watering each week through summer. common mistakes planting too close every plant has a mature size. read the label and plant accordingly. planting too close means constant pruning and crowding. not improving the soil even drought-tolerant plants need good soil to establish. add compost at planting time and mulch annually. buying on impulse garden centres are designed to make you buy what is in flower now. plan your plant choices for year round interest, not just what looks good on the day you visit. ignoring mature size a pittosporum in a 2 litre pot looks small. in 5 years it will be 2 metres tall and wide. plan for the mature size. final thoughts a low-maintenance garden is not a no-maintenance garden. even the easiest plants need watering through the first season, an annual mulch, and occasional pruning. but choosing the right plants for the chiltern chalk makes a huge difference. plants that fight the conditions need constant attention. plants that suit the conditions thrive with very little help. the ten plants in this list are the ones we plant most often for clients who want a beautiful garden without becoming a slave to it. if you would like help designing or planting a low-maintenance chiltern garden, get in touch for a free site visit. get a why is my lawn full of moss? causes and solutions real reasons your uk lawn is full of moss and the practical steps to fix it long term, without wasting money on the wrong treatments. why is my lawn full of moss? lawn care & lawn repair moss in lawns is one of the most common complaints we hear from uk garden owners. the good news is that moss is a symptom, not a disease. if you just kill the moss without fixing the underlying cause, it will come back within months. this guide explains what causes moss in lawns, how to get rid of it for good, and how to keep it from coming back. quick answer moss grows in lawns when the grass is weak. the four main causes are: shade, poor drainage, compaction, and acidic soil. killing the moss with iron sulphate is the easy bit. the hard bit is fixing whatever caused it in the first place. if your lawn is more than 50 percent moss, the cheapest long-term solution may be to start again. strip the lot, improve the soil, and reseed with a grass mix suited to the conditions. the four causes of moss 1. shade grass needs at least 4 hours of direct sun a day. less than that and the grass thins, leaving bare patches that moss happily colonises. shade can come from buildings, fences, hedges and trees. the most common cause in uk gardens is the north side of a house or fence. solutions for shade: prune or thin trees: particularly evergreens and dense shrubs. even removing lower branches can let in a lot of light. reduce fence height: if practical, replace solid panels with trellis to let light through. plant a shade-tolerant grass mix: fescues and bentgrass tolerate shade better than ryegrass. see rhs lawn seed guidance . accept the moss: in heavy shade, moss is often the best ground cover. it is green, soft and needs no mowing. 2. poor drainage waterlogged soil kills grass roots but moss loves it. if your lawn has standing water after rain, or stays squelchy for days, drainage is the problem. this is particularly common on heavy clay soils like those in aylesbury and parts of reading. solutions for poor drainage: aerate the lawn: use a fork or hollow-tine aerator to create holes 10cm deep. fill with sand to improve drainage. top-dress with sand: apply a mix of 70 percent sand and 30 percent soil to the lawn after aeration. this improves surface drainage over time. install land drains: for severe cases, a french drain around the lawn can carry water away. this is more expensive but solves the problem permanently. re-level low spots: any dip in the lawn will collect water. level with topsoil and reseed. 3. compaction heavy foot traffic, children playing, and regular mowing with a heavy mower all compact the soil. compacted soil has no air pockets, which kills grass roots. moss tolerates compaction better than grass. solutions for compaction: aerate annually: the single most effective thing you can do. hollow-tine aeration removes plugs of soil, letting air and water into the root zone. avoid walking on wet lawns: this is when most compaction happens. install a path: if there is a regular route across the lawn, install stepping stones or a proper path. use a lighter mower: heavy cylinder mowers compact the soil. rotary mowers are lighter. 4. acidic soil grass prefers a soil ph of 6.0 to 7.0. below ph 5.5, grass struggles and moss thrives. this is less common on the chalky soils of the chilterns but very common in other parts of the uk, especially where there are conifers or where lawn sand has been overused. solutions for acidic soil: test the ph: simple kits cost 5 pounds and tell you the ph in minutes. apply garden lime: if ph is below 6.0, apply ground limestone at 50g per square metre in autumn. re-test the following year. avoid over-use of ammonium sulphate: this common lawn fertiliser acidifies the soil. other contributing factors low mowing height cutting the lawn too short weakens the grass. moss tolerates short cutting better than grass. never cut more than one third of the grass height in one mowing. set the mower to 30 to 40mm for family lawns, higher in dry weather. poor feeding under-fed grass is weak grass. apply a balanced lawn fertiliser in spring and again in mid-summer. avoid high-nitrogen feeds in autumn as they produce soft growth that is damaged by frost. thatch build-up thatch is the layer of dead grass and moss that builds up at the base of the lawn. a thin layer (under 1cm) is fine. more than that and water cannot reach the soil, which weakens grass. scarify annually in autumn to remove thatch. tree roots trees near lawns compete for water and nutrients. large trees like oak and beech can dry out a lawn completely in summer. the only solution is to water the lawn in dry spells or to extend the border around the tree so the lawn does not reach the trunk. how to get rid of moss step 1: kill the moss apply iron sulphate (also called ferrous sulphate or sulphate of iron) at 5g per square metre in spring or autumn. this kills the moss within 2 weeks and turns it black. the grass is unharmed. iron sulphate also greens up the grass and slightly acidifies the surface, which most grasses tolerate. avoid moss killers that contain dichlorophen as these can damage grass in warm weather. iron sulphate is safer, cheaper and more effective. step 2: scarify two weeks after applying iron sulphate, scarify the lawn to remove the dead moss. for small lawns, use a spring-tine rake. for larger lawns, hire a powered scarifier. scarifying removes a surprising amount of material. a 50 square metre lawn can produce 4 or 5 bin bags of dead moss and thatch. step 3: aerate after scarifying, aerate the lawn to improve drainage and reduce compaction. use a garden fork or hire a hollow-tine aerator. push the fork in 10cm deep and rock it back and forth to widen the holes. fill the holes with a 70/30 sand and soil mix. step 4: top-dress apply a top dressing of 70 percent sand and 30 percent loam to fill the aeration holes and level the surface. work it in with the back of a rake. the top dressing should be no more than 1cm thick or it will smother the grass. step 5: overseed where the moss has killed the grass, you will have bare patches. overseed with a grass mix suited to the conditions. for shaded lawns use a shade-tolerant mix. for family lawns use a hard-wearing ryegrass mix. sow at 25g per square metre and rake lightly into the top dressing. step 6: feed apply a balanced lawn fertiliser 4 to 6 weeks after overseeding. this helps the new grass establish and strengthens the existing grass. water well after feeding. step 7: fix the underlying cause if you do not fix the underlying cause, the moss will be back within a year. prune trees for shade. install drains for waterlogging. aerate annually for compaction. apply lime for acidity. when to replace the lawn if your lawn is more than 50 percent moss, or if the underlying cause cannot be fixed (such as permanent heavy shade from a building), it may be better to start again. the process is: strip the existing lawn with a turf cutter (hire for 80 per day) dig or rotavate the soil to 20cm deep add compost and improve the soil level and consolidate the soil sow new seed or lay new turf for a 50 square metre lawn, the whole job takes 2 to 3 days and costs 400 to 800 in materials and equipment hire. see our lawn recovery service for what we offer. try our lawn calculator we have built a calculator that gives you an estimate range for lawn renovation based on lawn size, the problem you have and the finish you want. it takes 2 minutes and gives a realistic price range. use the lawn calculator moss prevention calendar month job march first cut of the year, light feed with spring lawn fertiliser april scarify light thatch, apply iron sulphate if moss is present may aerate compacted areas, top-dress with sand june to august regular mowing, watering in dry spells september scarify heavier thatch, overseed bare patches october aerate, apply autumn lawn feed (low nitrogen, high potash) november last cut of the year, clear leaves december to february keep off the lawn in frost and wet weather common myths about moss myth: moss kills grass moss does not kill grass. moss grows where grass has already died. the grass dies for one of the four reasons above, and moss colonises the bare ground. myth: you can dig moss out you can dig moss out, but it will come back unless you fix the cause. moss produces thousands of spores and grows back from any fragment of stem left in the soil. myth: moss killer is enough moss killer kills the moss that is there now. it does nothing to stop new moss growing. without fixing the underlying cause, you will be applying moss killer every year. myth: lawn sand is the best moss killer lawn sand is a mix of ammonium sulphate, iron sulphate and sand. it does kill moss but the ammonium sulphate acidifies the soil, which can encourage moss in the long run. plain iron sulphate is better. final thoughts moss in lawns is one of those problems that is easy to fix temporarily and hard to fix permanently. the temporary fix is iron sulphate and scarifying. the permanent fix is addressing whatever is weakening the grass. if you have a mossy lawn and would like help, get in touch. we can assess the causes and give you a plan to fix the problem for good. book a lawn assessment what to do with a neglected garden before selling a house practical advice on preparing a neglected garden for a house sale. what to spend money on, what to skip, and what adds the most value. what to do with a neglected garden before selling a house gardens for selling or renting when you are selling a house, the garden is the first thing buyers see from the outside and the last thing they remember. a neglected garden can knock thousands off the sale price or even put buyers off entirely. the good news is that a few days of focused work can transform an overgrown garden into an asset. this guide explains what to do, what to skip, and what adds the most value. quick answer for most neglected gardens, 500 to 1,500 pounds of focused work will add 5,000 to 15,000 pounds to the sale price. the key is to focus on kerb appeal, decluttering, and making the garden look easy to maintain. do not invest in expensive landscaping or specimen plants. buyers want a clean canvas, not a finished garden they did not choose. why the garden matters when selling according to studies by estate agents, a well-presented garden can add 5 to 20 percent to the value of a property. even more importantly, a tidy garden makes the property sell faster. buyers form an emotional first impression within 8 seconds of arriving, and the garden is part of that. the reverse is also true. an overgrown garden suggests the property has been neglected in other ways. buyers worry about damp, structural issues, and pests. a tidy garden reassures them that the property has been looked after. what buyers actually look at kerb appeal from the front the front garden is the first impression. buyers will drive past, park outside, and walk up to the front door. they will judge the property before they even step inside. sense of space from the back buyers want to feel the back garden is bigger than they expected. cluttered, overgrown gardens feel smaller than they are. clean, open gardens feel larger. maintenance burden buyers want a garden they can imagine looking after easily. a complicated garden with high hedges, lots of beds and awkward levels suggests work. a simple lawn with neat edges suggests easy weekends. privacy buyers want to feel the garden is private from neighbours and passers by. overgrown hedges that need reducing, or gaps in screening, both raise concerns. the high-impact, low-cost jobs 1. cut the lawn and edges cut the lawn the day before a viewing if possible. freshly cut grass looks and smells good. edge the lawn with a half-moon edger for a crisp line. this single job transforms the look of any garden. cost: 0 if you do it yourself, 30 to 60 if we do it. 2. cut back overgrowth anything overhanging paths, patios or the lawn needs cutting back. buyers walking around the garden should not have to push past branches. cut shrubs back to a tidy shape, not necessarily to the ground. cost: 0 if you do it yourself, 100 to 300 if we do it. 3. clear all rubbish old pots, broken furniture, piles of bricks, dead plants in containers. get rid of all of it. a skip or a trip to the tip costs 50 to 200 and makes the garden look twice as big. 4. weed paths and patios weeds in paving look terrible. either pull them out by hand, treat with weedkiller, or pressure wash the whole surface. pressure washing is more work but the result is dramatically better. cost: 0 if you do it yourself, 150 to 350 if we pressure wash for you. 5. clean the patio and decking pressure wash patios and clean decking. the transformation is dramatic. a green slimy patio looks neglected, a clean one looks inviting. 6. tidy the sheds and outbuildings clear out and tidy any sheds. if the shed is rotten or beyond repair, consider removing it. an empty shed is better than a full one. a clean empty shed suggests storage space. a full dirty shed suggests there is no storage. 7. trim hedges cut all hedges to a neat shape. reduce hedges that are too tall. a neat hedge frames the garden. a scruffy hedge dominates it. cost: 100 to 400 depending on hedge size. see our hedge cutting service . 8. paint fences and structures a coat of fence paint transforms tired timber. dark colours like dark brown or black make the garden look bigger by receding into the background. bright colours make the garden look smaller. 9. plant a few cheap pots of colour buy 4 or 5 pots of ready-grown bedding plants from a garden centre. place them by the back door and on the patio. this adds instant colour and life. cost 50 to 100. 10. clean windows and gutters not strictly garden work, but buyers look at the outside of the house from the garden. clean windows and clear gutters make a difference. what not to do before selling do not install expensive landscaping buyers want to put their own stamp on a garden. a 10,000 pound new patio or designer planting scheme is unlikely to add 10,000 pounds to the sale price. worse, buyers may rip it out and start again. save the money. do not plant specimen trees and shrubs mature specimen plants are expensive and the buyer may not like them. better to leave the garden as a clean canvas. do not over-personalise quirky garden art, brightly coloured fences and themed planting divide opinion. keep the garden neutral so the widest range of buyers can imagine themselves there. do not do major tree work without permission if you are in a conservation area or have trees with a tree preservation order, you need council permission for any work. doing it without permission can lead to fines and may delay the sale. do not leave garden ornaments gnomes, wind chimes, garden signs. pack them away. they distract buyers and make the garden feel cluttered. cost versus value job cost added value roi cut lawn and edges 30 to 60 500 to 1,500 excellent clear rubbish 50 to 200 1,000 to 2,500 excellent cut back overgrowth 100 to 300 1,500 to 3,000 excellent pressure wash patio 150 to 350 1,000 to 2,500 very good trim hedges 100 to 400 500 to 1,500 very good paint fences 100 to 300 500 to 1,000 good pots of colour 50 to 100 500 to 1,000 excellent new patio or decking 2,000 to 8,000 0 to 3,000 poor mature specimen plants 500 to 2,000 0 to 500 poor timing the best time to do the garden work is 2 to 3 weeks before going on the market. this gives the lawn time to recover from cutting, the hedges time to look settled, and any new plants time to settle in. doing the work too early means it needs doing again before the first viewing. if you are selling in winter, focus on structure rather than colour. tidy hedges, clean hard surfaces, and add a few evergreen pots by the door. if you are selling in spring or summer, the lawn and any flowering plants will be at their best. cut the lawn the day before each viewing. the photography question estate agent photographs are taken once, when the property goes on the market. they are what buyers see first on rightmove and zoopla. the garden needs to look its best for the photography session, not just for viewings. ask the photographer to take wide shots from the corners of the garden, and detail shots of any attractive features. make sure the sun is in the right direction. most photographers prefer to shoot gardens in the morning or late afternoon. specific issues that put buyers off subsidence from trees large trees close to the house can cause subsidence, particularly on clay soils. if there are signs of cracking in the walls, get a structural survey before going on the market. buyers will ask. asbestos in old garages and sheds asbestos cement roofs are common on pre-1980 outbuildings. buyers will spot them. either remove the asbestos before going on the market (300 to 800) or be prepared to negotiate on price. boundaries and access issues make sure you know where the boundaries are and that fences are in good repair. disputed boundaries can delay or kill a sale. if access to the back garden is only through the house, mention this in the particulars so buyers are not surprised. final thoughts selling a house with a neglected garden is not a disaster, but it does require focused work. the key is to spend money on the things that buyers see first: the front garden, the lawn, the patio, the hedges. skip the expensive landscaping and specimen plants. for most properties, 500 to 1,500 pounds of focused garden work will add several thousand to the sale price and speed up the sale. it is one of the best returns on investment in property preparation. if you are selling and need help getting the garden ready, give us a call. we do one-off pre-sale clearances and tidy-ups across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire and berkshire. get a quote further reading how to restore an overgrown garden how much does garden tidy-up checklist before summer practical spring checklist to get your uk garden ready for summer. real jobs, real timeframes, no fluff. garden tidy-up checklist before summer seasonal garden jobs spring is the time to get the garden ready for summer. a few focused weekends of work in march, april and may mean you can actually enjoy the garden in june, july and august rather than spending them catching up. this checklist walks through every job that needs doing, in the right order, with realistic timescales. quick answer the spring tidy-up covers six areas: lawn, borders, hedges, hard surfaces, structures, and tools. most family gardens need 2 to 3 full days of work to get properly ready for summer. tackle the lawn first because everything else happens around it. 1. the lawn first cut the first cut of the year should be light. set the mower to its highest setting and just take the top off. cutting too short too early stresses the grass and encourages moss. wait until the grass is actively growing, usually mid-march in southern england. edge the lawn use a half-moon edger to cut a clean edge along all borders. this single job transforms the look of the garden. aim for a 5cm wide trench between the lawn and the border. scarify if needed if the lawn has a thick layer of thatch (dead grass and moss at the base), scarify with a spring-tine rake or powered scarifier. this is hard work but makes a huge difference. best done in april when the grass is growing strongly. aerate compacted areas use a garden fork to spike the lawn 10cm deep, every 10cm. rock the fork back and forth to widen the holes. this improves drainage and root growth. for larger lawns, hire a hollow-tine aerator. apply spring feed a balanced spring lawn fertiliser greens up the grass and encourages strong root growth. apply at 30g per square metre in late march or early april. water in if no rain within 2 days. treat moss if present if moss is a problem, apply iron sulphate at 5g per square metre two weeks before scarifying. this kills the moss and turns it black, making it easier to remove. see our full guide on moss in lawns . overseed bare patches after scarifying, overseed any bare patches with a grass mix suited to your conditions. sow at 25g per square metre, rake in lightly, and water. cover with netting to keep birds off. 2. borders and beds clear last year's growth cut back all dead stems from perennials. most can be cut to within 5cm of the ground. new growth will come from the base. leave any seed heads that look attractive or are feeding birds. weed thoroughly spring is the best time to weed. the soil is soft, the weeds are small, and the ground is too cold for seeds to germinate quickly. get every perennial weed root out now and you save hours of work in summer. see our overgrown garden guide for dealing with established weeds. dig in compost add a 5cm layer of compost or well rotted manure around plants. work it into the top 5cm of soil. this improves soil structure, retains moisture, and feeds plants through the season. apply mulch after weeding and composting, apply a 5cm mulch of bark or gravel. this suppresses weeds for the whole growing season and reduces watering by 50 percent. keep mulch away from plant stems. divide perennials perennials that have formed large clumps can be lifted and divided. lift the whole clump with a fork, divide into 3 or 4 sections with a spade, and replant the outer sections. water well. best done in march or april. plant new perennials and shrubs spring is a good time to plant, though autumn is better. water new plants regularly through their first summer. plant in drifts of 3 or 5 for impact. support tall plants put plant supports in place now for tall perennials like delphiniums, peonies and verbena. it is much easier to put a support in before the plant needs it than to wrestle a mature plant into a support. prune spring shrubs after flowering shrubs that flower in spring like forsythia, flowering currant and japonica should be pruned immediately after flowering. cut back the flowered stems to a strong new shoot. 3. hedges check for nesting birds before any hedge cutting between march and august, check carefully for nesting birds. watch the hedge for 30 minutes from a distance. if birds are flying in and out, leave it until september. see our hedge cutting guide for the legal position. first cut of the year most formal hedges need their first cut in late may or june once the spring growth has finished. cut to shape with the top narrower than the base. see our hedge cutting service . feed and mulch hedges are hungry. apply a balanced fertiliser at 50g per square metre around the base. mulch with compost to retain moisture. 4. hard surfaces pressure wash paths and patios winter leaves paths and patios green with algae and slippery. pressure wash in march or april when the weather is warming up but before the dry summer. see our pressure washing service . re-point paving joints if pointing has crumbled over winter, re-point with a 4 to 1 sand and cement mix. use a pointing trowel and brush off excess once it has started to set. treat timber decks clean the deck with a deck cleaner, then apply a deck oil or stain. best done in late spring when the timber is dry. clean out drains and gullies clear leaves and debris from drains and gullies. summer thunderstorms can cause flooding if drains are blocked. 5. structures inspect fences check all fence posts for rot at the base. replace any rotten posts before they fall over. treat timber with preservative. repair sheds and outbuildings check shed roofs for leaks. replace felt if needed. treat timber with preservative. oil door hinges and locks. clean and treat garden furniture clean furniture with warm soapy water. treat timber furniture with teak oil or a preservative. check metal furniture for rust and treat with rust killer. service the mower if you have a petrol mower, service it now. change the oil, clean the air filter, replace the spark plug, and sharpen the blade. a sharp blade cuts cleanly and is better for the grass. clean and sharpen tools clean all hand tools with a wire brush. sharpen secateurs, shears and loppers with a sharpening stone. oil moving parts. hang tools rather than leaving them on the floor. 6. containers and pots refresh pot compost remove the top 5cm of compost from each pot and replace with fresh. add a slow-release fertiliser. this feeds the plant for the whole season without daily feeding. repot root-bound plants if roots are coming out of the drainage holes, the plant needs repotting. move to a pot 5cm wider. use fresh compost. plant summer bedding wait until mid-may after the last frost before planting summer bedding. pelargoniums, begonias, lobelia and petunias are reliable choices. install irrigation for containers if you have lots of pots, install a drip irrigation system on a tap timer. this takes 2 hours to set up and saves hours of watering each week through summer. 7. wildlife clean out bird boxes clean bird boxes in late winter with boiling water to kill parasites. old nests can harbour mites that affect new chicks. put up new bird boxes early spring is the time to put up new bird boxes. face them north or east to avoid strong sun. clean bird feeders clean bird feeders with a mild bleach solution. birds can catch diseases from dirty feeders. create a wildlife corner leave a corner of the garden undisturbed with a pile of logs and leaves. this provides habitat for hedgehogs, frogs and beneficial insects. 8. pond remove dead leaves and debris use a net to remove leaves and debris from the pond. decaying matter uses up oxygen and encourages algae. check the pump and filter clean the pump and filter. replace any worn parts. restart the pump once the water temperature is above 10c. divide overgrown plants water lilies and marginal plants that have outgrown their baskets should be divided and repotted in fresh aquatic compost. schedule week of job time early march first lawn cut, edge lawn, clear borders 1 day mid march weed borders, apply compost, prune spring shrubs 1 day late march apply mulch, divide perennials, plant new perennials 1 day early april scarify lawn, apply spring feed, treat moss half day mid april aerate lawn, overseed bare patches, pressure wash paths 1 day late april inspect fences, repair sheds, service mower half day early may first hedge cut, install plant supports half day mid may refresh pot compost, clean furniture half day late may plant summer bedding, install irrigation half day cost if you pay someone else if you would rather not do the work yourself, here is roughly what the spring tidy-up costs if we do it: small garden: 300 to 500 for full spring tidy family garden: 500 to 900 for full spring tidy large garden: 900 to 1,800 for full spring tidy lawn scarification and aeration are usually charged separately. pressure washing is charged separately. see our estimate tools for quick quotes. final thoughts the spring tidy-up is one of those jobs that pays off all summer. a weekend or two of focused work in march and april means you can sit in the garden in june rather than catch up. and the jobs done in spring keep the garden healthier through the year. if you would like help with the spring tidy-up, give us a call. we do one-off spring cleans and set up regular maintenance visits for the season ahead. book a spring tidy-up further reading how much does regular garden maintenance cost? how often should a gardener visit? best low-maintenance how often should a gardener visit? real advice on gardener visit frequency for uk gardens. weekly, fortnightly or monthly? find the right rhythm for your garden. how often should a gardener visit? regular garden maintenance one of the most common questions we get asked is how often a gardener should visit. the answer depends on the garden, the season, the budget and the standard you want. this guide explains the trade-offs of each frequency and helps you choose the right rhythm for your garden. quick answer for most uk family gardens, fortnightly visits from march to october and monthly visits from november to february works well. this keeps the garden looking good without breaking the bank. smaller courtyard gardens can be monthly year round. large or formal gardens may need weekly visits in the growing season. the three main options weekly visits weekly visits are for formal gardens, large estates, and serious gardeners who want the garden to look immaculate every day. the lawn is cut every week without fail, borders are weeded before weeds are visible, and hedges are kept perfectly shaped. pros: garden always looks pristine smaller jobs each visit, easier to keep on top problems spotted quickly cons: most expensive option, typically 200 to 600 per month not necessary for most family gardens some jobs only need doing monthly anyway fortnightly visits the sweet spot for most uk gardens. the lawn is cut every two weeks, which is the right interval for most of the growing season. weeds are caught before they get big. hedges are checked regularly. pros: good balance of cost and quality lawn cut at the right interval for most of the year most family gardens thrive on this schedule cons: lawn can look slightly long by week 2 in peak growing season some fast-growing weeds may need pulling between visits monthly visits monthly visits work for small courtyard gardens, low-maintenance gardens, and through the winter. a monthly visit can keep on top of things but the lawn will need cutting between visits in summer. pros: cheapest option, typically 40 to 150 per month fine for low-maintenance gardens perfect for winter when growth is slow cons: lawn grows too long between visits in summer weeds can get established before they are caught borders can look untidy by week 3 how to choose garden size larger gardens need more frequent visits because more goes wrong between visits. a 50 square metre courtyard garden can be kept tidy monthly. a 500 square metre garden with extensive borders needs fortnightly as a minimum. lawn area the lawn is the main driver of visit frequency. lawns need cutting every 7 to 10 days in peak growing season (may to july). if you have a lawn, fortnightly is the minimum through summer. standard of finish if you want the garden to look like a national trust property, you need weekly visits. if you want it to look presentable and well cared for, fortnightly is enough. if you just want it not to be a jungle, monthly will do. budget be honest about what you can afford. a fortnightly visit at 50 pounds per visit is 100 pounds per month, or 1,200 per year. weekly at the same rate is 2,400 per year. monthly is 600 per year. see our maintenance cost guide for more detail. your own time and interest if you enjoy gardening and want to do some yourself, monthly visits from a professional to handle the heavy work can be enough. if you would rather never touch the garden, fortnightly or weekly is better. seasonal variations march to may (spring) fortnightly is ideal. the garden is growing fast and needs regular attention. lawn needs its first cuts, borders need clearing and weeding, hedges need their first cut. june to august (summer) fortnightly for most gardens. weekly for formal lawns. the lawn grows fastest in may and june, then slows in dry july and august. september to november (autumn) monthly is usually enough. growth is slowing. leaf clearance is the main job. lawn still needs cutting but less frequently. december to february (winter) monthly or even less. most gardens need very little through winter. a tidy in january to clear dead growth and check structures is enough. special cases lawns if you have a lawn, you need fortnightly visits at minimum through the growing season, even if the rest of the garden is low maintenance. lawns grow too fast for monthly visits. either cut the lawn yourself between professional visits, or arrange weekly cuts through may, june and july. hedges hedge cutting is a separate job, not part of regular maintenance. most hedges need cutting once or twice a year. book this as a one-off job. see our hedge cutting guide . containers and pots pots need daily watering in summer. if you are away or forget, the plants die. either install drip irrigation on a timer, or arrange for someone to water daily through the hottest months. newly planted gardens new plants need regular watering through their first growing season. weekly visits to check and water are sensible for the first year. vegetable gardens vegetable gardens need attention every few days in summer. pests, watering, harvesting and sowing successional crops all need doing. most maintenance gardeners do not specialise in vegetable growing. how visit frequency affects cost frequency hours per visit cost per visit monthly cost annual cost weekly 1 to 2 40 to 80 160 to 320 1,920 to 3,840 fortnightly 2 to 3 60 to 120 120 to 240 1,440 to 2,880 monthly 3 to 4 90 to 160 90 to 160 1,080 to 1,920 these are typical ranges for southern england. smaller gardens cost less. larger or more formal gardens cost more. see our garden care level assessment for a cgm care level assessment. signs your schedule is wrong signs you need more frequent visits lawn looks long between visits weeds are flowering before they are pulled hedges are losing their shape paths and patios are growing green algae you are doing significant work between visits signs you need less frequent visits the gardener is finishing in 30 minutes there is nothing for them to do in winter your garden is mostly hard landscaping you are doing the work yourself between visits flexibility a good gardener should be flexible. you should be able to add an extra visit before a party or a house sale, and skip a visit when you are away or the garden does not need it. we allow clients to pause in winter and add visits in summer without penalty. final thoughts choosing the right visit frequency is about balance. too infrequent and the garden looks scruffy between visits. too frequent and you are paying for work that does not need doing. most uk family gardens thrive on fortnightly visits from march to october and monthly through winter. if you are not sure what your garden needs, ask for a free assessment. we can look at the garden, talk through your priorities and budget, and suggest the right schedule. book a free site visit further reading how much does regular garden maintenance cost?
