is it cheaper to clear a garden yourself or hire someone? honest cost comparison of diy garden clearance versus hiring a professional. what you save in money you spend in time and pain. is it cheaper to clear a garden yourself or hire someone? garden prices & costs it is tempting to think that doing garden clearance yourself will save money. sometimes it does. often it does not. this guide compares the real costs, both financial and in time, of diy clearance versus hiring a professional. the answer may surprise you. quick answer for small, light clearances (under 50 square metres of moderate overgrowth), diy is usually cheaper if you have the time and tools. for larger or heavily overgrown gardens, hiring a professional is often cheaper in real terms when you factor in tool hire, waste disposal, and the value of your own time. the hidden costs of diy tool hire to clear a moderately overgrown garden you need: brush cutter or strimmer: 50 to 80 per day from a hire shop hedge trimmer: 30 to 50 per day wheelbarrow: 20 to 40 per day mattock and loppers: 30 to 60 to buy if you do not have them protective gear: gloves, goggles, ear defenders: 30 to 60 to buy waste bags or hippobag: 10 to 150 for a 2-day clearance, tool hire alone can come to 200 to 350. waste disposal this is the big hidden cost. green waste disposal is regulated. you cannot just put it in your black bin. options: council tip: free for household waste but most tips limit you to 6 bags per visit. a typical clearance produces 20 to 40 bags. that means 4 to 7 trips. council green waste bin: 60 to 100 per year subscription. takes months to clear a clearance worth of waste. hippobag: 150 to 250 per megabag including collection. each bag holds about 1 tonne. skip hire: 200 to 400 for a 6 yard skip. need a permit if on the road, 30 to 80. for a typical clearance, waste disposal alone costs 150 to 400 if you do it yourself. your time this is the cost most people ignore. a professional 2-person crew can clear a 100 square metre moderately overgrown garden in 1.5 days. that is 24 person-hours of work. if you do it alone, it will take you 4 to 6 days of hard physical work. what is your time worth? if you are paid 20 pounds an hour, 6 days of work is nearly 1,000 pounds of your time. if you would rather spend that 6 days with family, on hobbies, or just relaxing, the cost is even higher. physical cost garden clearance is hard physical work. brambles cut you. ivy is heavy. soil and rubble break your back. most people who do a full clearance themselves end up with cuts, blisters, aching muscles, and sometimes worse. back injuries from heavy lifting are common. if you are not used to physical work, budget 2 to 3 times longer than you think. and accept that you will be useless for anything else for a few days after. pest and disease risks overgrown gardens harbour rats, ticks, and wasps. weil's disease from rat urine in stagnant water can be fatal. tick bites can cause lyme disease. wasp stings can be dangerous if you are allergic. professionals know how to spot and avoid these risks. real cost comparison garden diy cost diy time professional cost professional time (your time) small courtyard 30 sqm light overgrowth 50 to 150 1 to 2 days 250 to 400 1 hour family garden 100 sqm moderate overgrowth 250 to 500 4 to 6 days 500 to 900 1 hour large garden 300 sqm heavy overgrowth 800 to 1,500 10 to 14 days 1,500 to 3,000 1 hour full plot with shed, trees, brambles 1,500 to 3,000 3 to 4 weeks 2,500 to 5,000 1 hour the professional costs include labour, waste disposal and all equipment. the diy costs include tool hire, waste disposal and protective gear but not your own time. when diy makes sense small gardens if your garden is small and the overgrowth is light, diy is genuinely cheaper. a few hours with a pair of secateurs and a trip to the tip is all you need. you have the tools already if you already own a strimmer, hedge trimmer, wheelbarrow and the basic tools, the cost of diy drops significantly. most of the cost of a one-off clearance is tool hire. you have the time and fitness if you are retired, between jobs, or just enjoy hard physical work, the time cost is less of an issue. diy becomes a reasonable choice. you have a council green waste bin if you have a council green waste bin and can clear the garden over a few months, the waste disposal cost drops to nearly nothing. you trade time for money. you want the exercise garden clearance is genuinely good exercise if you are fit. some people enjoy the satisfaction of seeing a transformation they did themselves. when hiring a professional makes sense large or heavily overgrown gardens the bigger the job, the more sense it makes to hire someone. professionals have the equipment, the experience, and the crew to do in 2 days what would take you 2 weeks. you need waste removal if the garden has lots of green waste, hiring a professional with a tipper truck is much easier than multiple trips to the tip. they will also take the waste away for you. time pressure if you are selling the house, moving in, or just want the garden usable for a specific date, hiring someone is the only realistic option. most people cannot take 2 weeks off work to clear a garden. physical limitations if you have any physical limitation, bad back, or are simply not used to physical work, do not attempt a full garden clearance. the risk of injury is high. large shrubs and small trees anything with a trunk over 10cm diameter needs proper equipment to remove. hand saws work but take forever. chain saws are dangerous in untrained hands. asbestos or hazardous waste if the garden has an old shed with an asbestos roof, or chemicals, or anything you are not sure about, hire a professional. asbestos removal is a specialist job. the halfway option there is a middle ground. you can do the easy bits yourself and hire a professional for the hard bits. for example: you cut back the light overgrowth and the professional handles the brambles and trees you do the clearance but hire a waste removal company to take the waste away you clear the garden but hire someone to do the pressure washing and patio work this can save money while still getting the hard work done professionally. we are happy to quote for part of a job. common diy mistakes underestimating the waste most diy clearances produce 3 to 5 times more waste than people expect. a "small pile of brambles" turns into 15 bin bags. always have more waste bags than you think you need. digging out weed roots too shallow perennial weeds like bindweed and ground elder regrow from any root fragment left in the soil. digging out the top growth is pointless. you need to dig out every piece of root, often 30cm deep. cutting into old wood on leylandii leylandii hedges do not regrow from old brown wood. if you cut too hard, the hedge is permanently bare on that side. see our hedge cutting guide . pulling ivy off walls ivy holds on with tiny roots that damage mortar. pulling live ivy off a wall can pull off the render or damage the mortar. cut the stems at the base and let the top growth die before removing. ignoring nesting birds if you cut hedges or shrubs during nesting season (march to august) you can be prosecuted. always check first. try our clearance calculator before you decide whether to diy or hire, use our clearance calculator. it gives you a realistic estimate range for professional clearance based on your garden size and overgrowth level. compare that to your diy cost (including tool hire and waste disposal) and the answer becomes clear. use the clearance calculator final thoughts diy garden clearance is not always cheaper than hiring a professional. for small light clearances, yes. for anything bigger, the cost of tool hire, waste disposal, and your own time often exceeds what a professional would charge. the question is rarely "can i afford to hire someone?" it is more often "can i afford to do it myself?" factor in your time, your fitness, the equipment you would need, and the cost of waste disposal. if you decide to hire someone, get in touch. we can quote for the whole job or just the bits you would rather not do yourself. get a quote further reading how much does garden clearance cost? how to restore an overgrown garden how much does regular garden maintenance cost? rose leylandii weeds weed bramble brambles ivy bindweed ground elder ant wasp wasps rat rats cat pest disease march mar may august aug dec garden maintenance best time to plant hedges in the uk practical uk guide to the best time to plant hedges, covering bare root, root ball and pot grown options with species specific timing and aftercare tips. best time to plant hedges in the uk hedges, pruning & cutting times planting a hedge is one of the most rewarding things you can do in a uk garden, but getting the timing right makes the difference between a hedge that romps away and one that sulks for years. the best planting window depends on whether you buy bare root, root ball or pot grown plants, and on the species you have chosen. this guide walks through when to plant each type of hedge, how to prepare the ground, and what aftercare gives the highest success rate. we have planted miles of hedges across the chilterns and the rules here are the ones we actually follow. quick answer the best time to plant a hedge in the uk is between mid october and late march, when plants are dormant. bare root and root ball plants must be planted in this window. pot grown plants can go in at any time of year, but autumn and early spring give the best results because the soil is moist and the plants can establish roots before the dry summer months. for most uk gardens, the sweet spot is november or march. november planting gives the hedge the whole winter to settle in before growth starts. march planting works if the ground is not frozen or waterlogged, and is ideal for bare root beech, hornbeam and hawthorn. planting windows at a glance bare root (deciduous): november to march bare root (evergreen yew): september to april root ball (evergreen): october to march pot grown (any species): all year, but best in autumn or spring avoid: july and august unless you can water weekly the three ways hedges are sold before you can decide when to plant, you need to know what you are buying. hedges are sold in three forms, each with different planting windows and different prices. bare root bare root plants are dug from the field in winter when dormant, with the soil shaken off the roots. they are the cheapest option, typically half the price of pot grown plants, and they establish quickly because the roots spread into the surrounding soil rather than staying in a pot-shaped ball. the downside is the narrow planting window: november to march only. once they start into growth in spring they cannot be moved. bare root is the best choice for deciduous hedges like beech, hornbeam, hawthorn, blackthorn and dogwood. yew is also sold bare root and establishes well. most native mixed hedges are planted bare root. root ball root balled plants are field grown but dug up with a ball of soil around the roots, wrapped in hessian. they are used for larger evergreens like cherry laurel, portugal laurel, holm oak and large yew. the soil ball protects the roots, so they transplant more reliably than bare root evergreens. planting window is october to march. root ball plants are more expensive than bare root but cheaper than equivalent pot grown stock. they are a good halfway house when you want a mature evergreen hedge quickly. pot grown pot grown plants spend their whole life in containers. they can be planted at any time of year because the root system is intact. this makes them the most flexible option but also the most expensive. a pot grown yew can cost twice as much as a bare root yew of the same size. pot grown is the right choice when you miss the winter window, when you want to plant in summer (with regular watering), or when you want instant impact from larger specimen plants. for long hedges, pot grown gets expensive fast. planting windows by season autumn (october and november) autumn is the ideal planting season for almost all hedges. the soil is still warm from summer, which encourages root growth, but the air is cool so the tops do not lose water fast. rainfall is increasing so you do not need to water as much. bare root plants become available from mid november. the only downside of autumn planting is that the ground can be dry in some years, especially on light chalky soils. if the soil is dry at planting time, water thoroughly once after planting and then leave the hedge to winter rains. winter (december to february) winter planting works well as long as the ground is not frozen or waterlogged. frozen soil is impossible to dig properly and roots cannot grow into it. waterlogged soil compacts when dug, destroying the structure and suffocating roots. if you buy bare root plants and the ground is frozen when they arrive, heel them in. dig a shallow trench in a sheltered spot, lay the plants in at an angle, and cover the roots with soil. they will keep for weeks like this. spring (march and april) spring is the second best planting window. the soil is warming up, roots are starting into growth, and the plants have the whole growing season ahead to establish. march is the last month for bare root planting. the risk with spring planting is that the hedge has not had time to root out before the dry summer months. you will need to water through the first summer more carefully than for autumn planted stock. summer (may to september) only pot grown plants can be planted in summer, and only if you commit to watering. a newly planted hedge in june will need a thorough soak once a week for the whole summer, more in hot dry spells. planting in summer is more about necessity than choice: if you have just moved house and want a hedge before the children start school, summer planting is possible but not ideal. choosing the right species the right hedge species depends on your soil, your site and what you want the hedge to do. see our guide to cutting hedges for maintenance once they are established, and our yew plant profile for details on the king of formal hedges. on chalky chiltern soil beech, yew, hornbeam, hawthorn, box, privet and lavender all thrive on chalk. avoid rhododendron and camellia hedges, which need acid soil. see our article on low-maintenance plants for chiltern gardens for more suggestions. on heavy clay hornbeam is the best deciduous hedge for clay. yew copes if the drainage is reasonable. hawthorn and blackthorn are native and tolerant. avoid beech on wet clay because it rots at the roots. see our guide to improving clay soil before planting. for exposed sites hawthorn, blackthorn, dogwood and field maple are the toughest native hedge plants and will cope with wind and exposure. they are the choice for exposed gardens on the tops of the chilterns. for shade yew is the only conifer that copes with shade. holly, hornbeam and beech all tolerate partial shade. see our guide to plants for shade gardens for more options. preparing the ground hedge preparation is the unglamorous part that determines whether your hedge thrives or struggles. a hedge is a long row of hungry, thirsty plants competing with each other. get the soil right and they will romp away. clear the strip mark out a strip about 60cm wide where the hedge will go. spray off any existing grass and weeds with glyphosate two weeks before planting, or dig the turf off and stack it upside down to compost. do not leave grass in place because it will compete with the young hedge for water and nutrients. dig over and improve the soil dig the strip to a spade depth, breaking up any compaction. incorporate plenty of organic matter: garden compost, well rotted manure or soil improver. on heavy clay, add horticultural grit as well. on sandy or chalky soil, the organic matter is even more important because it holds water. this is also the time to remove large stones, perennial weed roots and any old building rubble. a hedge planted into rubble will never thrive. spacing spacing depends on the species and the size of plants. as a rule of thumb for a single row hedge using 60 to 80cm tall bare root plants: beech and hornbeam: 3 to 4 plants per metre hawthorn and native mix: 5 to 6 plants per metre yew: 2 to 3 plants per metre privet: 4 to 5 plants per metre leylandii: 2 plants per metre laurel: 2 to 3 plants per metre for a thicker stock proof hedge, plant a double staggered row 40cm apart, with 30cm between rows. this is traditional for native hawthorn hedges and gives a denser result. planting technique step by step the planting cut dig a slot or hole wide enough to take the roots spread out. for bare root plants, a slot dug with a spade to one spit depth and a trowel width is enough. for pot grown and root ball plants, dig a hole twice the width of the root ball and the same depth. planting depth plant at the same depth the plant was growing before. you can see the soil mark on the stem of bare root plants. planting too deep suffocates the roots. planting too shallow leaves them exposed. backfilling and firming hold the plant at the right depth and trickle soil around the roots, shaking the plant gently to settle the soil between the roots. firm with the heel of your boot, but do not stamp so hard that you compact the soil. water thoroughly after planting even if rain is forecast. support bare root plants in exposed sites may need a stake for the first year. most hedge plants do not need staking because they are small and flexible. if rabbits or deer are present, install a spiral guard or plastic tube around each plant immediately. one night of rabbit attention can destroy a newly planted hedge. aftercare in the first two years watering the single most important aftercare job is watering. a newly planted hedge needs about 10 litres per metre per week from april to september for the first two years. in hot dry spells, water twice a week. a length of porous pipe laid along the hedge row makes this much easier. do not rely on a quick spray with the hose. a proper soak that wets the root zone is what the plants need. water in the evening or early morning to reduce evaporation. mulching a 5cm layer of bark or compost mulch along the hedge row suppresses weeds and retains moisture. mulch right after planting and top up each spring. keep the mulch a few centimetres away from the stems to prevent rotting. mulch is more effective than weed fabric for hedges because it improves the soil as it breaks down. see our guide to preventing weeds in garden beds for more on mulching. feeding in the second spring after planting, give the hedge a feed with a balanced fertiliser like growmore or blood, fish and bone. apply about 50g per square metre and water in. do not feed in the first year because the roots are not established enough to use it. pruning cut newly planted bare root deciduous hedges back by a third at planting time. this seems brutal but it forces the plants to bush out from the base, giving a denser hedge. evergreens like yew should not be cut back at planting; instead, give them a light trim in the first summer to shape them. in the second winter, cut the hedge back by another third. this will delay the hedge reaching its final height by a year, but it will be much denser as a result. see our hedge cutting service for what is involved in keeping a mature hedge in shape. what does it cost to plant a hedge? the cost of planting a hedge is mostly the cost of the plants plus some ground preparation. for a 10 metre hedge using bare root plants, expect to pay: beech or hornbeam, 60cm bare root: 60 to 120 pounds for plants native mix, 60cm bare root: 40 to 80 pounds for plants yew, 60cm pot grown: 200 to 400 pounds for plants laurel, 60cm root ball: 150 to 250 pounds for plants add 150 to 400 pounds for ground preparation and planting labour if you are paying a gardener. the rhs has useful guidance on planting hedges if you want to do it yourself. common mistakes planting too close together more plants per metre is not always better. overcrowded hedges compete for water and nutrients and never reach their full size. stick to the spacing guidance for your species. not watering in the first summer this is the number one reason newly planted hedges fail. even a few days of dry soil in june can kill a young plant. water weekly from april to september for two years. letting weeds grow at the base grass and weeds at the base of a young hedge steal water and nutrients. keep a 60cm wide strip clear of weeds for the first two years, either by mulching or by careful hoeing. not cutting back bare root plants if you do not cut back bare root deciduous plants at planting time, they grow into single stems with bare bottoms. cutting back forces bushy growth from the base. planting in summer without watering pot grown plants can be planted in summer but only if you commit to watering. planting in june and going on holiday for two weeks in july is a recipe for a dead hedge. final thoughts planting a hedge is an investment that pays back for decades. a well planted beech or yew hedge will outlive the person who planted it. getting the timing right, the soil preparation right, and the aftercare right in the first two years is what makes the difference between a hedge that romps away and one that limps along for years. if you are planning a hedge, the best first step is to talk through your site and your needs with someone who has planted a few. we are happy to advise on species choice, spacing and timing, and we can plant the hedge for you if you prefer. hedge planting service frequently asked questions can i plant a hedge in january? yes, as long as the ground is not frozen or waterlogged. january is a good month for bare root planting. if the ground is frozen, heel the plants in somewhere sheltered and wait for a mild spell. how far apart should i plant a beech hedge? for a single row, plant 3 to 4 bare root plants per metre. for a double staggered row, plant 5 to 6 per metre. the closer spacing gives a denser hedge sooner but costs more in plants. do i need to water a hedge planted in november? only if the winter is unusually dry. november planted hedges usually establish on winter rainfall. start regular watering in april if the spring is dry. how long until a newly planted hedge gives privacy? a bare root beech hedge planted at 60cm tall takes 4 to 6 years to reach 1.5 metres. yew takes 5 to 8 years. for instant privacy, plant larger pot grown stock, but expect to pay 5 to 10 times more per metre. further reading when should hedges be cut in the uk? how to improve clay soil in your garden learn how to improve clay soil in your uk garden with practical methods for drainage, structure and fertility, plus the best plants for heavy clay ground. how to improve clay soil in your garden soil, compost & mulch clay soil gets a bad press, but it is actually one of the most fertile soil types in the uk. the challenge is its structure: it holds water in winter and turns concrete hard in summer. improve the structure and clay soil will grow exceptional plants, from roses to fruit trees. this guide covers practical methods for improving clay soil in uk gardens, what plants thrive in it, and what to avoid. we work on heavy clay across the thames valley and the methods here are battle tested. quick answer clay soil is improved by adding organic matter every year, avoiding compaction, and where drainage is poor, installing land drains or building raised beds. it takes 2 to 3 years of consistent work to transform heavy clay into workable garden soil, but even one season of improvement makes a noticeable difference. the golden rules of clay soil never dig it when wet. never walk on it when wet. add organic matter every spring and autumn. use raised beds for vegetables. mulch heavily to prevent cracking in summer. understanding clay soil what makes clay soil clay clay soils are made up of tiny mineral particles less than 0.002mm across. these particles pack together tightly, leaving little space for air and water to move through. that is why clay holds water in winter (drainage is slow) and goes hard in summer (particles stick together as they dry). the good news is that clay particles are chemically active. they hold nutrients like calcium, potassium and magnesium that would wash out of sandy soils. this is why clay soils are inherently fertile. testing your soil the simplest test is the sausage test. take a handful of soil from 10cm down, moisten it, and roll it between your palms. if you can roll it into a long thin sausage that bends without breaking, you have heavy clay. if it forms a sausage but breaks when bent, you have clay loam. if it will not form a sausage at all, you have sandy or silty soil. you can also test drainage by digging a hole 30cm deep and 30cm wide, filling it with water, and seeing how long it takes to drain. if water is still there after 24 hours, you have a drainage problem. pros and cons of clay pros: high fertility, holds moisture in summer, good for many shrubs and perennials, holds nutrients well. cons: heavy to dig, waterlogged in winter, hard when dry, slow to warm in spring, prone to compaction. how to improve clay soil structure add organic matter this is the single most important thing you can do. organic matter opens up the clay structure, creating spaces for air and water. it also feeds the soil life that helps to break clay into crumb-like aggregates. add a 5cm layer of garden compost, well rotted manure or spent mushroom compost each spring and autumn. either dig it in to the top 20cm or leave it on the surface for worms to incorporate. over 2 to 3 years this transforms the topsoil. for new beds, dig in a 10cm layer of organic matter to one spade depth before planting. this is the equivalent of adding a wheelbarrow full of compost per square metre. see our uk composting guide for how to make your own. add horticultural grit for heavy clay that needs to drain more freely, add horticultural grit as well as organic matter. use about 5kg per square metre, dug in to one spade depth. sharp sand (not builder's sand) also works but is less effective than grit. do not add sand alone to clay. mixing sand into clay without enough organic matter can create a concrete-like substance. always combine grit with generous amounts of organic matter. use gypsum gypsum (calcium sulphate) is a clay breaker that works by causing clay particles to clump together into larger aggregates. it does not change the ph the way lime does, so it is safe for acid loving plants. apply about 1kg per square metre in autumn and water in. gypsum is not a substitute for organic matter. it helps, but the long term improvement still comes from regular additions of compost and manure. never dig wet clay if you dig clay when it is wet you destroy its structure. the spade smears the clay particles together, creating a solid layer that water cannot drain through. wait until the soil has dried enough that it crumbles when squeezed rather than forming a sticky ball. in a wet spring this might mean waiting until may to do spring digging. use raised beds for early crops so you are not waiting on the soil to dry. improving drainage if your clay soil is waterlogged for days after rain, organic matter alone will not solve the problem. you need drainage. french drains a french drain is a trench filled with gravel that carries water away. dig a trench 30cm deep and 30cm wide, line it with permeable membrane, fill with 20mm gravel, and fold the membrane over the top. the drain carries water to a soakaway or a lower part of the garden. for a small garden, one drain along the lowest side is often enough. for larger waterlogged areas, a herringbone pattern of drains works better. expect to pay 30 to 60 pounds per metre for a french drain installed professionally. raised beds where drainage cannot be improved, raised beds are the answer. build beds 30cm high from timber, brick or sleepers, fill with a mix of topsoil and compost, and plant into that. the raised soil drains freely and warms up earlier in spring. raised beds are the standard solution for growing vegetables on heavy clay. see our soft landscaping service for what is involved in building them. land drains for serious waterlogging, perforated land drain pipes buried in gravel trenches are the most effective solution. these are typically 60cm deep and connect to a soakaway or surface drain. installation is a major job, usually 60 to 120 pounds per metre, but it transforms a boggy garden. plants that thrive in clay soil many plants love clay soil. once you have improved the structure, you can grow a wide range successfully. shrubs for clay roses: all types, see our rose plant profile hydrangeas: especially the paniculata and arborescens types, see our hydrangea profile weigela, philadelphus, deutzia: old fashioned flowering shrubs viburnum: most varieties spirea: tough and reliable perennials for clay astilbe: loves moisture hellebores: see our hellebore profile hardy geraniums: most varieties cope well monarda, lysimachia, filipendula: moisture loving hostas: if you can fend off the slugs, see our hosta profile trees for clay apple and pear: fruit trees do well on clay hawthorn, rowan, birch: native trees oak: long term, for big gardens only hornbeam: for hedges or specimen trees what to avoid lavender, rosemary, most mediterranean herbs and many silver leaved plants struggle in clay because they need sharp drainage. grow them in raised beds or containers of free draining compost. see our lavender profile for what lavender needs. rhododendrons and camellias need acid soil. most clay in our area is neutral or alkaline, so they will not thrive unless you grow them in pots of ericaceous compost. maintaining improved clay soil mulch every year improving clay soil is not a one off job. you need to add organic matter every year to maintain the structure. a 5cm mulch of compost in spring keeps the soil workable and suppresses weeds. see our article on preventing weeds in garden beds for mulching in practice. avoid walking on the soil compaction is the enemy of clay. walk on paths or boards, not on the soil. in vegetable beds, use raised beds with narrow paths between so you can reach the centre without standing on the soil. use a no dig approach no dig gardening is especially good for clay. instead of digging, you add layers of organic matter to the surface each year and let the worms incorporate it. this preserves the soil structure and the fungal networks that help plants grow. charles dowding has pioneered no dig in the uk and the rhs has a useful guide to no dig gardening on their site. use green manures on vegetable beds green manures are crops grown specifically to be dug back into the soil, or left on the surface in a no dig system. on clay they are invaluable because they keep the soil covered over winter, preventing compaction from rain, and their roots open up the structure. good green manures for clay include field beans, winter rye, phacelia and mustard. sow in september or october and dig in or cut down in march before they flower. mustard is particularly useful because it grows fast in autumn and its roots break up compacted clay. avoid mustard if you grow brassicas because it can carry clubroot. the organic matter from a green manure crop is equivalent to a good dressing of compost, and the roots do the work of opening up the clay that you would otherwise have to do with a fork. for a new vegetable plot on heavy clay, two years of green manures before proper cropping makes a remarkable difference. working with the seasons spring wait until the soil has dried before digging. apply mulch to the surface. sow seeds in modules indoors if the soil is too wet to sow direct. summer clay soil can crack in dry summers. water deeply once a week rather than little and often. keep mulching to retain moisture. autumn the best season for digging and incorporating organic matter. the soil is workable and the winter frost will break down the clods. plant new trees and shrubs in autumn to give them a head start. winter stay off the soil. if you must work in the garden, lay planks to walk on. use the time to plan next year's improvements. cost of improving clay soil for a typical 50 square metre bed on heavy clay, expect to spend: compost or manure: 100 to 200 pounds per year for the first 3 years horticultural grit: 50 to 100 pounds for a one off incorporation gypsum: 20 to 40 pounds for a one off application raised bed kit and topsoil: 200 to 500 pounds for a 5 by 2 metre bed french drain: 300 to 600 pounds for a 10 metre drain the investment is significant but pays back over decades. improved clay soil grows better plants, drains better, and is much easier to work. common mistakes adding sand without organic matter this creates a cement-like mix that is worse than the original clay. always pair grit with compost. digging when wet this smears the clay and destroys structure. wait for it to dry. walking on wet clay compaction from a single footstep can take a year to undo. use planks or stay off. expecting a quick fix clay improvement takes 2 to 3 years of consistent work. anyone telling you it can be done in a weekend is selling something. final thoughts clay soil is not a curse, it is an asset. once you have improved the structure with regular organic matter, clay grows better plants than almost any other soil type. the trick is patience and consistency: a mulch every year, careful timing of any digging, and a willingness to work with the seasons rather than against them. if you have a clay garden that needs improving, the best first step is a soil test and an honest look at the drainage. we can advise on what your soil needs and do the heavy work of double digging, drain installation or raised bed construction. soft landscaping service frequently asked questions can i grow vegetables in clay soil? yes, but raised beds are easier. clay grows excellent brassicas, beans and peas. root vegetables like carrots struggle unless the soil has been improved with grit and sand for a few years. how long does it take to improve clay soil? you will see a noticeable difference after one season of regular organic matter additions. a real transformation takes 2 to 3 years. after 5 years of consistent mulching, even heavy clay becomes workable. should i dig or no dig on clay? no dig works well on clay once the initial structure is improved. for a new bed on heavy clay, dig once to incorporate compost and grit, then switch to no dig with annual surface mulching. will lime improve my clay soil? lime raises ph and can help clay particles clump, but it makes the soil alkaline. only use lime if your soil is acid and you want to grow vegetables. test the ph first. further reading composting in the uk: a complete beginner guide how to prevent weeds in garden beds long term low-maintenance plants for chiltern gardens soft landscaping service lavender rosemary hydrangea camellia rhododendron hosta composting in the uk: a complete beginner guide complete uk composting guide for beginners. learn what to compost, which bin to choose, how to layer materials, and how to fix common compost problems. composting in the uk: a complete beginner guide soil, compost & mulch composting is the single most useful thing you can do in a uk garden. it turns kitchen and garden waste into free, high quality soil improver, cuts your bin charges, and reduces your environmental footprint. this beginner guide covers what to compost, what to avoid, which bin to choose, how to layer materials, and how to fix common problems. we have been composting for decades across gardens in buckinghamshire and oxfordshire, and these are the methods we actually use day to day. quick answer composting is the natural breakdown of organic matter into a dark, crumbly soil improver. to compost successfully you need a roughly equal mix of green material (grass, kitchen scraps, soft prunings) and brown material (paper, cardboard, twigs, autumn leaves). a well managed compost bin produces usable compost in 6 to 12 months. the golden ratio aim for roughly 50 percent green and 50 percent brown materials by volume. too much green makes a smelly slimy mess. too much brown makes a dry pile that does not decompose. mix as you add, and your compost will work. what is composting? composting is the controlled decomposition of organic matter by bacteria, fungi, worms and other soil life. the end product is humus, a dark brown crumbly material that looks and smells like woodland soil. humus improves soil structure, holds moisture, and feeds plants slowly and naturally. in nature, leaves fall to the woodland floor and rot down over years. in a compost bin, we create ideal conditions for the same process to happen in months rather than years. what to compost green materials (high in nitrogen) fruit and vegetable peelings tea bags (check they do not contain plastic) coffee grounds grass cuttings soft green prunings annual weeds that have not gone to seed cut flowers poultry and horse manure (in moderation) brown materials (high in carbon) shredded paper and cardboard (no glossy print) egg boxes toilet roll inners autumn leaves twigs and prunings (shredded) straw and hay sawdust and wood shavings (from untreated wood) cotton and wool (in small amounts) what not to compost cooked food (attracts rats) meat, fish and bones dairy products cat and dog faeces (risk of parasites) diseased plant material perennial weed roots like bindweed and couch grass plastic, glass and metal coal ash (contains toxins) glossy magazine paper treated wood and sawdust diseased plant material and perennial weed roots should be burned or put in the council bin, not composted. the average home compost heap does not get hot enough to kill them. see our guide to preventing weeds in garden beds for dealing with perennials. choosing a compost bin plastic dalek bins the classic uk compost bin is a black plastic cone about a metre tall. they cost 30 to 60 pounds, or are sometimes available free or cheap from local council recycling schemes. they are small, neat, and keep heat in well. the downside is the small opening makes turning the compost difficult. wooden bin boxes wooden bins made from slatted timber look better and are easier to manage because you can remove the front slats to get to the compost. they cost 80 to 200 pounds for a single bay, or 200 to 500 pounds for a 3 bay system. timber bins blend into the garden better than plastic. hot composters insulated hot composters like the hotbin reach temperatures of 40 to 60 celsius, hot enough to break down cooked food, meat and weeds. they produce compost in 30 to 90 days rather than 12 months. they cost 200 to 350 pounds but they are a serious piece of kit for serious composters. tumblers compost tumblers are rotating drums on a stand. you turn the drum every few days to mix the compost. they are good for small gardens and produce compost quickly, but they are expensive (100 to 300 pounds) and have limited capacity. open heaps the cheapest option is just a heap in the corner of the garden. it works, but slowly, and looks untidy. rats and other wildlife can get to it easily. best for large gardens where appearance does not matter. how to build a compost heap layering the classic method is to add materials in layers. start with a 10cm layer of twigs or coarse material at the bottom for drainage. then alternate 10cm layers of green and brown material. a handful of garden soil or finished compost between layers introduces the microbes that do the work. in practice, most people add materials as they come. the trick is to keep an eye on the balance. if the heap is wet and slimy, add brown material. if it is dry and not breaking down, add green material and water. size matters a compost heap needs to be at least a metre cubed to generate enough heat to decompose quickly. smaller heaps still work but more slowly. a small dalek bin works because it is insulated; an open heap that small would just sit there. position site the bin on bare soil, not on paving or concrete. this lets worms and other soil life move up into the heap. choose a sheltered spot in sun or partial shade. avoid full shade because the heap needs some warmth to work. hot versus cold composting cold composting most home compost heaps are cold heaps. you add materials as they come, the heap slowly decomposes at ambient temperature, and you get compost in 9 to 18 months. cold composting is easy and forgiving but slow. hot composting hot composting requires a balanced mix of green and brown, enough moisture, and regular turning. a hot heap reaches 50 to 70 celsius and produces compost in 6 to 8 weeks. the heat kills weed seeds and plant diseases. to hot compost, build the heap all at once with the right mix of materials. turn it every week for the first month. the heap should be steaming in the morning. if it is not, add more green material and water. the rhs has detailed guidance on composting for those who want to dig deeper. common compost problems smelly, slimy heap cause: too much green material, usually too much grass. fix: add brown material like shredded paper, cardboard or dry leaves. mix it in with a fork. the smell should go within a week. dry heap that is not breaking down cause: too much brown material, or the heap has dried out. fix: add green material and water the heap until it is damp like a wrung out sponge. cover with a piece of old carpet or cardboard to retain moisture. rats in the heap cause: cooked food or meat in the heap, or the heap is too dry (rats like dry nesting sites). fix: never add cooked food. if rats are present, stop adding material for a few weeks, soak the heap to make it less attractive as a nest, and consider a sealed bin like a hotbin. flies around the heap cause: fruit waste exposed on the surface. fix: bury fruit waste under a layer of brown material or soil. the flies will disappear. slow decomposition cause: heap too small, too dry, or wrong mix. fix: make sure the heap is at least a metre cubed. add water if dry. add green material if there is too much brown. turn the heap to introduce air. using your compost as a mulch a 5cm layer of compost around plants in spring suppresses weeds, holds moisture, and feeds the soil. this is the simplest and most effective use. see our article on preventing weeds in garden beds for how mulching fits into weed control. as a soil improver dig compost into the top 20cm of soil when preparing new beds. this improves clay soil structure and adds organic matter to sandy soil. see our guide to improving clay soil . as a potting mix sieved home compost can make up to a third of a potting mix. mix 2 parts compost with 1 part loam and 1 part sharp sand for a general purpose mix. do not use pure compost for seedlings because it can be too rich and introduce disease. as a top dressing for lawns sieved compost spread thinly over a lawn in autumn improves the soil under the grass. see our article on why lawns get mossy for how soil compaction affects lawns. making leaf mould autumn leaves are too slow to compost with other materials. instead, make leaf mould. collect leaves into black bin bags, punch a few holes for drainage, tie the top, and leave for 2 years. the result is a dark crumbly material that makes an excellent mulch and soil improver. alternatively, build a simple leaf pen from chicken wire and posts. fill it with leaves in autumn and forget about it for 2 years. see our autumn garden checklist for when to collect leaves. composting through the year spring the heap comes back to life as the weather warms. turn it to introduce air. start adding grass cuttings as the lawn starts growing. balance with cardboard or paper. summer peak composting season. the heap should be warm and active. keep it damp in dry spells. add weeds before they go to seed. autumn lots of material available: leaves, spent plants, grass. separate leaves for leaf mould. this is a good time to start a new heap with the autumn clear up. winter decomposition slows right down. keep adding kitchen scraps. cover the heap to keep the worst of the wet off. use the cold months to turn last year's heap. costs of composting plastic dalek bin: 30 to 60 pounds single wooden bay: 80 to 200 pounds 3 bay timber system: 200 to 500 pounds hotbin: 200 to 350 pounds compost tumbler: 100 to 300 pounds home made from pallets: free if you can find pallets most councils in the uk offer subsidised compost bins through schemes like get composting. check your local council website for offers before buying at retail. common mistakes adding too much grass grass cuttings are 80 percent water and very high in nitrogen. a heap of pure grass turns into a smelly slimy mess. always mix with brown material. not adding brown material most households produce far more green waste than brown. shredded paper and cardboard are the easiest way to balance this. keep a bin of shredded paper next to the compost bin and add a handful every time you add kitchen waste. letting the heap dry out in dry summers a compost heap can dry out completely and stop working. water it occasionally, or cover with carpet to retain moisture. expecting compost too quickly cold composting takes 9 to 18 months. be patient. if you want fast compost, you need a hot heap with regular turning. final thoughts composting is one of those habits that becomes second nature. once you have a bin going and you see what comes out, you will wonder why you ever threw kitchen scraps in the bin. good compost is the foundation of a good garden: it improves soil, feeds plants, suppresses weeds, and costs nothing but a bit of attention. start with a single bin and a balanced mix of green and brown. after a year you will have your first batch of compost. after three years you will wonder how you ever gardened without it. garden maintenance service frequently asked questions can i compost weeds? annual weeds are fine. perennial weeds like bindweed, couch grass and ground elder should not be composted because the roots survive and regrow. either burn them, drown them in a bucket of water for 6 weeks, or put them in the council bin. can i put citrus and onions in compost? yes, in moderation. the old advice to avoid citrus and onions was about wormeries, where they can be too acidic. in a normal compost heap they break down fine. how do i know when compost is ready? finished compost is dark brown, crumbly, and smells like woodland soil. you should not be able to recognise the original materials. if you can still see whole leaves or sticks, it needs more time. do compost bins attract rats? only if you add cooked food or meat, or if the heap is very dry and provides a nesting site. a well managed heap with no cooked food is no more attractive to rats than the rest of the garden. further reading how to improve clay soil in your garden how to prevent weeds in garden beds long term autumn garden checklist garden maintenance service box pear bay moss weeds weed bindweed ground elder ant ants rat rats cat dog dry compaction drainage shade exposed pest disease diseases sep dec spring summer autumn fall winter garden maintenance maintenance pruning mulching winter garden tasks checklist for uk gardens essential winter garden tasks checklist for uk gardens. covers december to february jobs including pruning, protecting plants, lawn care and wildlife help. winter garden tasks checklist for uk gardens seasonal garden jobs winter is not a time to forget the garden. from december to february there is plenty to do that sets up the rest of the year. pruning dormant trees and shrubs, protecting tender plants, clearing fallen leaves, and planning next year's planting are all winter jobs that pay off later. this checklist covers the essential winter garden tasks for uk gardens, what to do each month, and how to protect your garden from cold and wet. we work through every winter and these are the jobs we actually prioritise. quick answer the main winter garden tasks are: pruning dormant deciduous trees and shrubs, clearing leaves, protecting tender plants from frost, lifting and dividing perennials, planning next year's planting, and maintaining tools and hard surfaces. most winter jobs are about preparation rather than active growth, so timing is flexible. winter golden rules stay off frozen or waterlogged lawns. do not prune in freezing weather. protect tender plants before the first hard frost. keep feeding the birds. use the quiet months to plan and maintain. december jobs protect tender plants by december, tender plants like dahlias, cannas, bananas and tree ferns need protection. in mild areas you may get away with wrapping the crown in fleece and straw. in colder areas lift dahlias and store the tubers in a frost free shed. see the rhs winter protection guide for detailed methods. prune deciduous trees and shrubs december to february is the dormant season, the right time to prune many deciduous trees and shrubs. without leaves you can see the structure clearly. remove dead, diseased and crossing wood. thin crowded branches. aim for an open goblet shape on fruit trees. do not prune stone fruits like cherries and plums in winter because they are prone to silver leaf disease. prune them in summer instead. plant bare root trees and hedges december is a good month for planting bare root trees, hedges and shrubs, as long as the ground is not frozen or waterlogged. see our guide to the best time to plant hedges for the full planting calendar. clean and sharpen tools winter is the time to give your tools some attention. clean off soil and sap, sharpen secateurs and shears, oil moving parts, and treat wooden handles with linseed oil. replace any tools that are beyond repair. service the mower book the mower in for a winter service. the blades need sharpening, the oil needs changing, and any worn parts need replacing. a mower serviced in december is ready for the first cut in march. january jobs clear fallen leaves by january most leaves are down but there are still plenty around. keep clearing them from lawns (where they smother the grass) and paths (where they become slippery). collect into bags or a leaf pen to make leaf mould. see our autumn checklist for more on leaf management. prune apple and pear trees january is the ideal month for winter pruning of apple and pear trees. the trees are fully dormant and the structure is visible. remove dead and diseased wood, thin crowded spur systems, and shorten the previous year's growth by about a third. prune wisteria wisteria is pruned twice a year. the winter prune in january or february involves shortening the side shoots cut back in summer to 2 or 3 buds. this encourages flowering spurs rather than leafy growth. plant snowdrops in the green snowdrops establish much better when planted in the green, that is, in leaf just after flowering, than as dry bulbs in autumn. january to march is the time to buy and plant snowdrops in the green. force rhubarb cover a rhubarb crown with a forcing jar or upturned bucket in january to exclude light. the stems will grow long, tender and pink and be ready to harvest 6 to 8 weeks earlier than uncovered plants. check ties and stakes check tree ties and stakes on young trees. loosen ties that are too tight, replace broken stakes, and remove stakes from trees that have been in for more than 2 years and no longer need support. february jobs prune late summer flowering shrubs shrubs that flower on the current year's growth, like buddleia, caryopteris, hardy fuchsia and hydrangea paniculata, are pruned hard in february. cut back to a low framework of stems, about 30 to 60cm tall. this encourages strong new growth that will flower in late summer. see our hydrangea profile for pruning details by species. cut back ornamental grasses deciduous grasses like miscanthus and calamagrostis should be cut back to about 10cm in february before new growth starts. evergreen grasses like stipa and carex just need a comb through to remove dead leaves. prepare seed beds cover vegetable beds with polythene or fleece to warm the soil for early sowing. the soil under cover can be 5 celsius warmer than uncovered soil, which means you can sow seeds 2 to 3 weeks earlier. sow early seeds in a greenhouse or on a windowsill, start sowing seeds of tomatoes, peppers, aubergines, onions and leeks. these need a long growing season and benefit from an early start under cover. top dress containers remove the top 5cm of compost from permanent containers and replace with fresh compost mixed with a slow release fertiliser. this refreshes the nutrients without repotting the whole plant. plant bare root roses february is the last good month for planting bare root roses. see our rose profile for spacing and aftercare. protecting plants in winter fleece and bubble wrap wrap tender shrubs like ceanothus, hebe and bay in horticultural fleece when hard frost is forecast. for potted plants that cannot be moved, bubble wrap the pot to protect the roots from freezing. mulch tender plants a 10cm mulch of bark or compost around the base of tender plants like dahlias (left in the ground), agapanthus and cordylines protects the roots from frost. apply after the first frost so rodents do not nest in the loose mulch. move tender pots under cover pelargoniums, citrus, olives and other tender potted plants should be moved to a frost free greenhouse, conservatory or shed by december. a cool but frost free spot with maximum light is ideal. brush snow off branches heavy snow on evergreen shrubs and conifers can break branches. use a broom to gently brush snow off after each fall. do not try to remove ice because the branches will be brittle. winter lawn care stay off the grass the single most important winter lawn job is to stay off it when it is frozen or waterlogged. walking on frozen grass breaks the blades. walking on waterlogged grass compacts the soil and destroys drainage. see our article on why lawns get mossy for what compaction does. clear leaves and debris keep the lawn clear of leaves and debris through winter. a layer of leaves left for a few weeks will smother the grass and leave dead patches. avoid walking on frosty lawns walking on a frosty lawn breaks the grass blades and leaves black footprints that take weeks to grow out. wait until the frost has lifted before going on the grass. plan spring lawn work winter is the time to plan spring lawn renovation. book scarifying and aerating for march or april. see our lawn recovery service for what this involves. winter wildlife care feed the birds winter is when garden birds need the most help. put out a mix of seeds, fat balls and suet. keep feeders clean to prevent disease. provide fresh water every day, breaking the ice in freezing weather. create habitat piles a pile of logs, branches and leaves in a quiet corner provides shelter for hedgehogs, frogs, toads and beneficial insects. leave it undisturbed through winter. see our guide to creating a wildlife garden for more. plant for winter berries if you have space, plant shrubs that provide winter berries for birds: holly, pyracantha, cotoneaster, viburnum, and hawthorn. these give birds natural food through the coldest months. leave seed heads resist the urge to cut everything back in autumn. seed heads of plants like teasel, echinacea, rudbeckia and sunflowers provide food for finches and other birds through winter. winter garden maintenance clean paths and patios winter is a good time to clean slippery paths and patios. algae and moss build up over winter and become dangerously slippery. pressure wash on a dry day, or treat with a moss killer. see our pressure washing service . plan next year's borders winter is when you can see the bare bones of the garden. use the dormant season to assess which beds are working and which need a rethink. take photos and notes, sketch new planting schemes, and order plants for spring delivery. a bit of planning in january saves expensive impulse buys at the garden centre in april. look at the evergreen structure first. if the garden looks flat and empty in winter, add some evergreen shrubs like viburnum, sarcococca, or pittosporum in the spring. then look at the deciduous shrubs and consider whether any need moving, replacing, or pruning harder next winter. a garden that has been assessed in winter always looks better by the following winter. check fences and structures inspect fences, pergolas, trellis and arches for damage from winter winds. repair or replace before the growing season puts weight on climbing plants. clean greenhouse glass wash greenhouse glass inside and out to maximise light transmission. even a thin layer of algae can reduce light by 20 percent, which matters when days are short. order seeds and plan winter evenings are for browsing seed catalogues and planning the year ahead. order seeds in january because popular varieties sell out by february. plan crop rotation in the vegetable garden and plan new planting schemes in ornamental beds. costs of winter garden work winter pruning visit: 60 to 150 pounds depending on size lawn leaf clearance: 40 to 100 pounds per visit greenhouse clean: 80 to 200 pounds pressure washing patio: 150 to 400 pounds depending on size mower service: 60 to 120 pounds fleece and mulch for tender plants: 30 to 80 pounds for materials many clients reduce maintenance visits to monthly through winter but keep the garden ticking over. see our guide to how often a gardener should visit for scheduling. common winter mistakes walking on frozen lawns this breaks the grass blades and leaves black footprints that take weeks to disappear. stay off when it is frosty. pruning in freezing weather cuts made in freezing weather do not heal properly and can let in disease. wait for a mild spell. leaving tender plants unprotected a single hard frost can kill a dahlia or a banana that has been thriving for years. protect before the first frost, not after. cutting everything back in autumn seed heads and standing stems provide winter food and shelter for wildlife. they also look beautiful in frost. leave them until february. forgetting the birds birds that have relied on your garden all summer need you most in winter. keep feeding through the cold months. final thoughts winter is a quieter time in the garden but it is not a time to switch off. the jobs done in december, january and february set up the rest of the year. pruning done well in winter means healthy plants in summer. protection done in time means tender plants survive. planning done in the dark evenings means a garden that delivers when spring arrives. use the winter months well and the rest of the year becomes much easier. ignore the garden in winter and you will spend spring catching up. winter maintenance service frequently asked questions can i mow the lawn in winter? only if the grass is still growing and the ground is firm and dry. in a mild winter you might mow once in january or february with the blades set high. in a cold wet winter do not mow at all. when should i prune my roses? most roses are pruned in late february or early march, when the worst of the winter is over but before new growth starts. see our rose profile for details. should i clear snow off shrubs? yes, gently. heavy snow can break branches, especially on evergreens. use a broom to brush it off. do not try to remove ice. what can i plant in winter? bare root trees, hedges and shrubs can be planted from november to march as long as the ground is not frozen. winter is also a good time to plant deciduous container grown trees and shrubs. further reading autumn garden checklist spring garden checklist best time to plant hedges in the uk how to create a wildlife garden stipa wisteria hydrangea agapanthus pittosporum ferns rose roses viburnum hawthorn holly pyracantha cotoneaster sarcococca miscanthus carex calamagrostis echinacea rudbeckia apple pear pear tree plum bay snowdrop snowdrops dahlia dahlias moss ant ants rat cat waterlogged dry compaction drainage frost snow autumn garden checklist: what to do in october and november autumn garden checklist for uk gardens. practical october and november jobs including leaf clearing, bulb planting, lawn care and tender plant protection. autumn garden checklist: what to do in october and november seasonal garden jobs autumn is the busiest season in a uk garden. the transition from summer growth to winter dormancy creates a long list of jobs that need doing in a narrow window. leaf clearing, bulb planting, lawn care, lifting tender plants, and preparing beds for winter all need to happen in october and november. this checklist covers the essential autumn jobs, in priority order, with timing and practical tips from our own garden maintenance work. we work through every autumn across the chilterns and these are the tasks we prioritise first. quick answer the main autumn garden jobs are: clearing fallen leaves, planting spring bulbs, giving the lawn an autumn feed and aerating, lifting and protecting tender plants, planting bare root trees and hedges, clearing spent annuals, and mulching beds for winter. october is the busier month; november is for finishing and protecting. autumn priorities 1. clear leaves from lawns and paths weekly. 2. plant spring bulbs before the first frost. 3. lift tender plants before they get hit. 4. give the lawn its autumn feed and aerate. 5. mulch beds while the soil is still warm. october jobs plant spring bulbs october is the prime month for planting spring flowering bulbs. daffodils, tulips, crocuses, alliums, hyacinths and fritillaries all go in now. the general rule is to plant bulbs at 2 to 3 times their own depth, with the pointed end up. daffodils need to be in by mid october; tulips can wait until november. for naturalising in grass, scatter bulbs and plant them where they land, using a bulb planter or a narrow spade. for formal beds, plant in clusters of 5 to 7 bulbs for impact rather than single rows. see our spring garden checklist for what to expect from bulbs planted now. clear leaves once leaves start falling, clear them weekly. leaves left on lawns smother the grass and create dead patches. leaves on paths become slippery and dangerous. leaves in borders are fine in moderation because they act as a mulch, but heavy layers should be cleared. collect leaves into a leaf pen or black bin bags with holes punched in. leave for 2 years to make leaf mould, an excellent soil improver and mulch. see our composting guide for more on leaf mould. lift tender plants by mid october, tender plants like dahlias, cannas, gladioli and begonias need attention. in mild areas you may get away with leaving them in the ground with a thick mulch. in colder areas, lift the tubers or corms after the first frost has blackened the tops, dry them off, and store in a frost free shed in dry compost or vermiculite. tender shrubs in pots like pelargoniums, fuchsias and citrus should be moved under cover before the first frost. a frost free greenhouse or conservatory is ideal. give the lawn an autumn feed an autumn lawn feed is different from a spring feed. it is higher in potassium and lower in nitrogen, which strengthens the roots and hardens the grass for winter rather than encouraging soft growth. apply in early october when the soil is still warm and the grass is still growing. after feeding, scarify to remove thatch and moss, and aerate with a fork or hollow tine aerator. top dress with a mix of sand and loam if the lawn is on heavy soil. see our lawn recovery service for what is involved. plant bare root trees and hedges bare root plants become available from mid october. october and november are ideal planting months because the soil is still warm and the plants can root out before winter. see our guide to planting hedges for the full planting calendar and species recommendations. sow autumn vegetables sow broad beans, peas, winter lettuce, spinach and onions (from sets) in october for early crops next year. cover with fleece or cloches in colder areas. garlic is best planted in october or november because it needs a cold period to form bulbs. divide perennials october is a good month for lifting and dividing overcrowded perennials. dig up the clump, split it with two forks back to back or a sharp spade, and replant the healthiest divisions. discard the woody centre. water in well and mulch. see our guide to gardener visit frequency for fitting this into a maintenance schedule. november jobs finish planting tulips november is the ideal month for planting tulips. planting later reduces the risk of tulip fire, a fungal disease that thrives in warm wet soil. plant tulips at 3 times their depth, 10 to 15cm apart in groups. plant bare root roses bare root roses are available from november. plant at the same depth as they were in the field, with the graft union just below soil level. water in well and prune lightly. see our rose profile for aftercare. protect tender plants by november, all tender plants should be protected. wrap outdoor bananas, tree ferns and palms in fleece and straw. mulch around the base of dahlias left in the ground. move potted tender plants under cover. see the rhs winter protection guide for detailed methods. cut back perennials by november most perennials have died back and can be cut down. cut to about 5cm above ground level. leave some seed heads for the birds and for winter interest: teasels, echinacea, rudbeckia, sedum and ornamental grasses all look beautiful in frost. do not cut back evergreen perennials like heuchera, hellebores or epimedium. just tidy any dead leaves. see our hellebore profile for winter care. mulch beds and borders november is the ideal time to mulch. the soil is still warm from summer, so mulching now keeps the warmth in. a 5cm layer of compost, bark or well rotted manure suppresses winter weeds, protects roots from frost, and improves soil structure as it breaks down. see our article on preventing weeds in garden beds for the practical details of mulching. clean and store tools clean, sharpen and oil tools before storing for winter. service the mower, sharpen secateurs and shears, and treat wooden handles with linseed oil. see our winter garden checklist for more on winter tool care. clean greenhouse wash greenhouse glass inside and out. a layer of algae over winter reduces light by 20 percent, which matters when days are short. tidy up, remove spent tomato and cucumber plants, and disinfect benches and pots. leaf management why leaves matter autumn leaves are not a nuisance, they are a resource. leaf mould is one of the best soil improvers you can make, and it is free. every garden should have a system for collecting and storing leaves. making leaf mould the simplest method is to fill black bin bags with wet leaves, punch a few holes for drainage, tie the top, and leave for 2 years. the result is a dark, crumbly, sweet smelling material that is excellent as a mulch, a soil improver, or a component of potting mixes. for larger gardens, build a leaf pen from chicken wire and posts. fill it in autumn and forget about it. after 2 years you have a ready supply of leaf mould. leaves on lawns leaves left on lawns for more than a few days smother the grass and create dead patches. clear weekly with a rake or a leaf blower. a leaf blower is worth the investment for large gardens; for small gardens a spring tine rake is fine. leaves in ponds keep leaves out of ponds because they rot and deplete oxygen, harming fish and wildlife. net the pond in autumn to catch falling leaves, and remove the net once the trees are bare. lawn care in autumn autumn feed an autumn lawn feed strengthens roots and hardens the grass for winter. apply in october when the soil is warm and the grass is still growing. do not use spring feed in autumn because the nitrogen encourages soft growth that is damaged by frost. scarifying scarifying removes thatch and moss from the lawn. use a spring tine rake or a powered scarifier. the lawn will look terrible afterwards but will recover over a few weeks. see our guide to mossy lawns for what to do with the moss you remove. aerating aerating relieves compaction and improves drainage. use a garden fork to spike the lawn every 10cm, or hire a hollow tine aerator that removes cores of soil. brush sharp sand into the holes on heavy clay soils to keep them open. top dressing after scarifying and aerating, top dress with a mix of loam and sand. this improves the soil surface and fills in hollows. spread about 1 to 2kg per square metre and work in with the back of a rake. repair bare patches autumn is a good time to repair bare patches in the lawn. loosen the soil, sow grass seed at about 30g per square metre, and cover with a thin layer of compost. keep moist until germination. see our lawn recovery service for full renovation. tender plant care dahlias in mild areas, dahlias can be left in the ground with a 10cm mulch of bark or compost. in colder areas, lift the tubers after the first frost has blackened the tops. cut back to 10cm, lift carefully, wash off the soil, dry upside down for a week, then store in trays of dry compost in a frost free shed. cannas and bananas in mild areas, cut back the foliage and cover the crown with a thick layer of straw or bark, then fleece. in colder areas, lift the rhizomes and store like dahlias. pelargoniums and fuchsias potted plants should be moved to a frost free greenhouse or conservatory. keep barely moist through winter. hardy fuchsias in the ground can be left, with a mulch around the base. olive, citrus, and bay move potted specimens to a sheltered spot or unheated greenhouse. wrap the pot in bubble wrap to protect the roots. in mild areas, outdoor plants can be protected with fleece in cold snaps. planting in autumn bare root trees and hedges autumn is the best season for planting bare root trees, hedges and shrubs. the soil is still warm, the plants are dormant, and they have the whole winter to root out before spring. see our hedge planting guide for the full details. spring bulbs october and november are the months for planting spring bulbs. daffodils in october, tulips in november. plant in clusters for impact and naturalise in grass for a wild look. bare root roses bare root roses are available from november. they are cheaper than pot grown and establish well in autumn soil. see our rose profile for planting and aftercare. winter bedding plant pansies, violas, wallflowers, primulas and cyclamen in containers and beds for winter colour. these plants are tough enough to flower through cold weather and will give colour from november to april. costs of autumn garden work autumn clearance visit: 80 to 200 pounds depending on garden size lawn autumn feed and scarify: 100 to 250 pounds leaf clearance per visit: 40 to 100 pounds spring bulbs planted: 50 to 150 pounds for labour plus bulbs mulching a 50 square metre bed: 100 to 250 pounds including materials tender plant lifting and storing: 60 to 120 pounds most clients book an autumn clearance in october and a follow up in november to clear leaves and finish mulching. see our garden maintenance service for what we cover. common autumn mistakes leaving leaves on the lawn a few weeks of leaf cover will kill the grass underneath. clear weekly through autumn. planting tulips too early tulips planted in early october are more prone to tulip fire. wait until november. not feeding the lawn in autumn a spring feed is no substitute for an autumn feed. autumn feed strengthens roots, spring feed encourages top growth. cutting everything back too early leave seed heads and standing stems for the birds and for winter interest. cut back in february, not october. forgetting tender plants one hard frost can kill a dahlia that has been thriving for years. lift and protect before the first frost, not after. final thoughts autumn is the season that defines the next year in the garden. bulbs planted now give spring colour. lawn work done now means a healthy lawn next summer. mulch applied now protects soil and plants through winter. tender plants lifted now survive to grow again next year. the temptation in autumn is to wind down and let the garden go. resist it. a few weeks of focused work in october and november pays back all year. see our summer tidy checklist for what to do before autumn arrives. autumn garden service frequently asked questions when should i plant tulips? november is the ideal month. planting later reduces the risk of tulip fire, a fungal disease. plant at 3 times the bulb depth, 10 to 15cm apart. can i leave dahlias in the ground over winter? in mild areas with well drained soil, yes, with a thick mulch. in cold or wet areas, lift them and store in a frost free shed. the risk of leaving them is rot in wet soil or death in a hard frost. how often should i clear leaves? weekly through the main leaf fall in october and november. less often in december as the leaves thin out. is it too late to plant in november? no, november is a good month for planting bare root trees, hedges, roses and tulips. the soil is still workable and the plants will root out through winter. further reading winter garden tasks checklist spring garden checklist composting in the uk best time to plant hedges
