spring garden checklist: february to may jobs spring garden checklist for uk gardens covering february to may. practical jobs including seed sowing, lawn care, pruning, mulching and dividing perennials. spring garden checklist: february to may jobs seasonal garden jobs spring is the most exciting and demanding season in a uk garden. from the first signs of life in february to the full flush of growth in may, the jobs come thick and fast. pruning, mulching, sowing, planting, mowing, weeding and feeding all need doing in a few busy months. this checklist walks through the key spring jobs by month, with practical timing and priorities based on years of garden maintenance work. we work through every spring across the chilterns and these are the tasks we focus on first. quick answer the main spring garden jobs are: pruning late summer flowering shrubs in february, mulching and feeding in march, sowing seeds and starting the first lawn cuts in march, dividing perennials in march and april, planting out tender plants after the last frost in may, and keeping on top of weeds through all four months. spring rewards early action; jobs done in february and march pay off all summer. spring priorities 1. prune shrubs that flower on new wood in february. 2. mulch and feed beds in march before weeds germinate. 3. start mowing when the grass is dry enough. 4. sow hardy seeds outdoors from late march. 5. plant out tender plants after the last frost in may. february jobs prune late summer flowering shrubs shrubs that flower on the current year's growth are pruned hard in february. this includes buddleia, caryopteris, hardy fuchsia, hydrangea paniculata, perovskia and leycesteria. cut back to a low framework of stems, about 30 to 60cm tall. this encourages strong new growth that will flower in late summer. see our hydrangea profile for the differences between pruning mophead, lacecap and paniculata types. cut back ornamental grasses deciduous grasses like miscanthus, calamagrostis and pennisetum should be cut back to about 10cm before new growth starts. evergreen grasses like stipa and carex just need a comb through with gloved hands to remove dead leaves. cut back perennials left for winter any perennials you left standing for winter interest should be cut back now. leave a few centimetres of stem so you can see where the plant is, and to protect new shoots from slugs. prune wisteria the winter prune of wisteria happens in february. shorten the side shoots cut back in summer to 2 or 3 buds. this encourages flowering spurs rather than leafy growth. prepare seed beds cover vegetable beds with polythene or fleece to warm the soil. the covered soil can be 5 celsius warmer than uncovered, allowing earlier sowing. sow early seeds indoors in a greenhouse or on a windowsill, start sowing tomatoes, peppers, aubergines, onions and leeks. these need a long growing season and benefit from an early start under cover. plant bare root roses and hedges february is the last good month for planting bare root roses, hedges and trees. see our guides to planting hedges and the rose profile . march jobs first lawn cut march is when the first cut happens, weather permitting. set the blades high for the first cut, removing no more than a third of the grass height. do not cut if the ground is wet or frosty. see our article on mossy lawns if your lawn is more moss than grass. mulch beds and borders march is the ideal month for mulching. the soil is warming up, weeds have not germinated yet, and a 5cm layer of compost or bark will suppress weeds for the whole growing season. see our article on preventing weeds in garden beds for the practical details. feed beds and borders apply a balanced fertiliser like growmore or blood, fish and bone around the base of shrubs and perennials. about 50g per square metre, watered in if the soil is dry. this gives plants a boost as they start into growth. divide perennials march is a good month for lifting and dividing overcrowded perennials. dig up the clump, split with two forks back to back or a sharp spade, and replant the divisions. water in well. discard the woody centre of old clumps. plant summer flowering bulbs dahlias, gladioli, lilies, crocosmia and begonias can be planted in march. in colder areas, wait until april. start dahlia tubers in pots in a greenhouse for earlier flowers. sow hardy seeds outdoors from mid march, sow hardy annuals like calendula, cornflower, nigella and larkspur directly where they are to flower. sow vegetable seeds like carrots, beetroot, parsnips, spinach and peas. protect with fleece if frost is forecast. prune bush roses march is the main pruning month for bush roses. cut back to an outward facing bud, removing dead and crossing wood. reduce height by about a third to a half. see our rose profile for details. top dress containers remove the top 5cm of compost from permanent containers and replace with fresh compost mixed with slow release fertiliser. see our guide to container gardening for more. april jobs lawn renovation april is the month for serious lawn work. scarify to remove thatch and moss, aerate to relieve compaction, top dress with sand and loam, and over-seed bare patches. see our lawn recovery service for what is involved. plant potatoes first early potatoes go in late march, second earlies in early april, maincrops in mid april. plant 10 to 15cm deep, 30cm apart in rows 60cm apart. earth up the shoots as they appear to protect from frost and encourage tuber formation. harden off tender seedlings seedlings raised indoors need hardening off before planting out. put them outside during the day for a week, then day and night (with protection) for another week, then plant out. this prevents shock and checks to growth. sow tender vegetables under cover sow courgettes, squash, pumpkins, sweetcorn, french and runner beans in pots in a greenhouse. these are frost tender and cannot go outside until late may. plant out hardy annuals hardy annuals sown in march can be thinned and transplanted in april. plant in drifts for natural effect. watch for pests april is when pests start to appear. check for aphids on new growth, slug damage on hostas and delphiniums, and lily beetles on lilies. see our hosta profile for slug control methods. start hedge cutting for formal hedges formal hedges like box, yew and privet may need their first cut in late april or may. check for nesting birds first. see our guide to when to cut hedges for the legal position on nesting birds. may jobs plant out tender plants after the last frost (mid to late may in southern england), plant out tender vegetables like tomatoes, courgettes, squash, sweetcorn and beans. also plant out tender bedding plants like pelargoniums, begonias and impatiens. harden off first. plant out dahlias dahlia tubers started in pots, or planted directly in the ground, can go out in late may after the last frost. stake tall varieties at planting time to avoid damaging roots later. summer bedding plant summer bedding in containers and hanging baskets from mid to late may. use a mix of trailing and upright plants for impact. see our container gardening guide for compost and watering. keep on top of weeds may is peak weed growth. hoe weekly, hand weed between plants, and mulch any bare soil. weeds that go to seed now will be a problem for years. see our weed prevention guide for long term control. water newly planted areas anything planted in the last 12 months needs watering through dry spells. a thorough soak once a week is better than a daily sprinkle. see our guide to gardener visit frequency for fitting watering into maintenance. mow weekly by may the lawn should be mown weekly. keep the blades at a medium height. do not scalp the lawn because this weakens the grass and encourages moss. stake tall perennials put stakes around tall perennials like delphiniums, hollyhocks and peonies before they need them. trying to stake a plant that has already flopped damages the stems. spring lawn care first cut the first cut happens in march, weather permitting. set blades high and remove no more than a third of the height. subsequent cuts can be lower as the season progresses. feed a spring lawn feed in late march or april gives the grass a boost for the growing season. use a feed high in nitrogen to encourage green growth. do not feed too early because soft growth is damaged by late frosts. scarify and aerate if you did not do this in autumn, april is the time. scarify to remove thatch and moss, aerate to relieve compaction. see our article on moss in lawns for what to do with the moss you remove. over-seed bare patches loosen the soil, sow grass seed at 30g per square metre, cover with compost, and keep moist. germination takes 10 to 14 days. keep off the area until the new grass is 5cm tall. mowing frequency march: maybe once. april: every 10 days. may onwards: weekly. in a hot dry summer, raise the blades and mow less often to help the grass cope with drought. spring weed control hoeing a dutch hoe is the best tool for annual weeds in beds and vegetable plots. hoe on a dry day and leave the weeds on the surface to dry out. hoeing is most effective on small weeds; large weeds need hand pulling. hand weeding for perennial weeds like dandelions, dock and bindweed, hand weeding is essential. dig out the whole root with a fork or a long handled weed tool. any root left behind will regrow. the rhs has useful guidance on identifying and controlling garden weeds if you are unsure what you are dealing with. the key with hand weeding is to do it after rain when the soil is soft. pulling dandelions out of dry soil in may is a disheartening business; the same job after a wet april weekend takes seconds per plant. keep a bucket by the door and pull a few weeds every time you walk past the bed. little and often beats a heroic once a year assault. mulching a 5cm layer of compost or bark mulch in march suppresses weeds for the whole growing season. mulch after weeding, not before. see our weed prevention guide for the full mulching method. weed fabric for new beds, weed fabric under mulch is very effective. plant through slits cut in the fabric. the fabric suppresses weeds for 5 to 10 years before needing replacement. spring planting bare root february and march are the last months for bare root planting. trees, hedges and roses planted now will establish before summer. see our hedge planting guide . pot grown trees and shrubs pot grown plants can be planted all spring, but earlier is better. plants put in march have time to root out before the dry summer months. anything planted in may needs careful watering through summer. summer bulbs dahlias, gladioli, lilies and crocosmia go in from march to may. plant at 2 to 3 times the bulb depth. stake tall varieties at planting. summer bedding plant summer bedding in late may after the last frost. use fresh compost, water in well, and feed weekly through the growing season. costs of spring garden work spring tidy and mulch: 150 to 400 pounds depending on garden size lawn spring feed and scarify: 100 to 250 pounds hedge first cut: 100 to 300 pounds depending on length planting service: 30 to 50 pounds per hour plus materials container planting summer display: 100 to 300 pounds including plants most clients book a major spring tidy in march and then move to fortnightly maintenance. see our garden maintenance service for details. common spring mistakes planting tender plants too early a late frost in may can wipe out tomatoes, courgettes and bedding plants planted too early. wait until the last frost date for your area. not hardening off seedlings seedlings raised indoors need gradual hardening off. planting them straight outside checks their growth and can kill them. scalping the lawn cutting the lawn too short weakens the grass and encourages moss. never remove more than a third of the height in one cut. forgetting to water new plants anything planted in the last 12 months needs watering through dry spells. a weekly soak is better than a daily sprinkle. letting weeds go to seed one dandelion plant can produce 2,000 seeds. pull weeds before they flower and seed, not after. final thoughts spring is the most demanding season in the garden but also the most rewarding. everything you do now pays off through the rest of the year. mulch applied in march means fewer weeds in july. lawn work in april means a healthy lawn in august. seedlings sown in february mean tomatoes in july. the trick is to start early and keep on top of the work. a few hours a week through spring keeps the garden looking good all year. let it slide and you will be playing catch up all summer. see our autumn checklist for what to do when spring work pays off. spring garden service frequently asked questions when is the last frost in southern england? typically mid to late may in the home counties. in sheltered gardens in london and the south coast, mid may. in exposed gardens, late may or early june. wait an extra week after the forecast last frost before planting out tender plants. when should i cut my grass for the first time? usually march, when the grass is dry and growing. set blades high and remove no more than a third of the height. do not cut if the ground is wet or frosty. can i plant in march? yes, march is a good month for planting bare root trees, hedges and roses, pot grown shrubs, summer bulbs, and hardy perennials. wait until may for tender plants. how often should i water newly planted shrubs? once a week through the first growing season, more in hot dry spells. a thorough soak at the root zone is better than a daily sprinkle that only wets the surface. further reading autumn garden checklist winter garden tasks checklist how to prevent weeds in garden beds best time to plant hedges in the uk stipa wisteria hydrangea hosta hostas rose roses box yew privet holly miscanthus pennisetum carex calamagrostis delphinium crocosmia pear lily lilies dahlia dahlias begonia begonias moss weeds weed bindweed dandelion dandelions aphid aphids slug container gardening tips for uk gardens container gardening tips for uk gardens. choose the right pots and compost, water and feed correctly, and keep container displays looking good all year. container gardening tips for uk gardens garden clearance & tidy-up guides container gardening is the answer for small gardens, rented properties, paved areas and anyone who wants flexible planting. pots, troughs, window boxes and hanging baskets can transform a patio or a front door, but they need different care from plants in the ground. this guide covers choosing containers, the right compost, planting, watering, feeding, winter care, and the best plants for uk containers. we plant hundreds of containers every year for clients across the chilterns, and these are the methods that consistently work well for us. quick answer container gardening succeeds when you choose the right pot, the right compost, and the right plants, and then water and feed consistently. the main mistakes are pots that are too small, compost that is too poor, watering that is too inconsistent, and forgetting to feed. get these right and containers will outperform beds for colour and impact. container golden rules use the biggest pots you can afford. use peat free multipurpose compost for short term plants and loam based compost for permanent ones. water thoroughly when the top 2cm is dry. feed weekly in the growing season. raise pots off the ground on feet for drainage. choosing containers material terracotta looks classic but is porous, meaning compost dries out fast. glazed ceramic holds moisture better and looks smart but is heavy and fragile. plastic is light, cheap and retains moisture well, but can look cheap. wood looks natural but rots over time. metal is modern but can overheat roots in sun. composite stone is heavy, durable, and looks like real stone. size bigger is better. small pots dry out fast, heat up in sun, freeze in winter, and restrict root growth. a 40cm diameter pot is the minimum for most shrubs and perennials. a 50cm pot is better. for trees and large shrubs, go 60cm or larger. the cost of plants to fill a big pot is not much more than to fill a small one, but the effect is dramatically better and the maintenance is much lower. drainage every container must have drainage holes. without them, water collects at the bottom and roots rot. if a pot has no holes, drill them. cover the holes with crocks (broken terracotta) or mesh to stop compost washing out. raising pots off the ground stand pots on pot feet, bricks or tiles to lift them a few centimetres off the ground. this stops the drainage holes blocking and prevents the pot sitting in standing water in winter. it also protects wooden decks from staining. choosing compost multipurpose compost for annual displays and short term planting, peat free multipurpose compost is the standard choice. it is light, holds moisture, and has enough nutrients for the first 4 to 6 weeks. after that, plants need regular feeding. john innes compost for permanent planting like shrubs, trees and perennials, loam based john innes no 3 is the better choice. it is heavier, holds its structure for longer, and drains better than pure multipurpose. it is more expensive but worth it for plants that will be in the same pot for years. ericaceous compost for acid loving plants like rhododendrons, camellias, blueberries and pieris, use ericaceous compost. these plants struggle in the alkaline soil of the chilterns but thrive in pots of ericaceous compost, fed with an ericaceous plant food. mixing compost for mixed summer displays, a mix of 2 parts multipurpose compost and 1 part john innes no 3 gives a good balance of moisture retention and drainage. add a handful of slow release fertiliser and some water retaining gel for extra reliability. peat free peat extraction destroys rare habitats, and the uk garden industry is phasing out peat. peat free composts based on coir, wood fibre, green compost and bark are now widely available and perform well, though they can dry out more quickly than peat. see our guide to composting for making your own soil improver. planting a container preparation cover the drainage holes with crocks. add a layer of grit or coarse compost at the bottom for extra drainage. fill with compost to about 5cm below the rim, leaving space for watering. planting water the plants in their original pots before planting. remove from the pot, tease out the roots if they are congested, and plant at the same depth as they were in the original pot. firm in gently and water thoroughly. spacing for a summer display, plant closer than you would in the ground. the plants will fill out and the effect is fuller. for permanent planting, give plants room to grow. top dressing after planting, add a layer of gravel or bark on top of the compost. this retains moisture, suppresses weeds, and looks tidy. top up annually with fresh compost and slow release fertiliser. watering containers how often in summer, containers need watering every day, sometimes twice a day in hot weather. in spring and autumn, every 2 to 3 days. in winter, only when the compost is dry. the simple test is to push a finger into the top 2cm of compost. if it feels dry, water. if it feels moist, leave it. overwatering kills as many plants as underwatering. how much water until it runs out of the drainage holes. this ensures the whole root ball is wetted, not just the surface. a quick sprinkle does more harm than good because it encourages shallow roots. when water in the early morning or evening, not in the heat of the day. watering in full sun wastes water through evaporation and can scorch leaves if droplets act as lenses. self watering containers self watering pots have a reservoir in the base that the roots draw from. they need refilling every few days rather than daily, and are good for busy gardeners or for holiday periods. the rhs has a useful guide to watering containers for those who want detail. irrigation systems for gardens with lots of containers, a drip irrigation system on a timer is worth the investment. each pot gets a dripper that delivers water directly to the root zone. a basic system costs 100 to 200 pounds and saves hours of watering each week. feeding containers liquid feed container plants need regular feeding because nutrients wash out of the compost with each watering. from april to september, feed once a week with a liquid feed like tomato feed, seaweed feed or a general purpose fertiliser. tomato feed is high in potassium and is excellent for flowering plants. slow release fertiliser slow release granules mixed into the compost at planting time release nutrients over 6 months. they are convenient but only feed for one season, so annual top ups are needed. top dressing each spring, scrape off the top 5cm of compost from permanent containers and replace with fresh compost mixed with slow release fertiliser. this refreshes nutrients without repotting. winter care for containers pot protection frost can crack terracotta and ceramic pots and freeze the roots of plants. wrap pots in bubble wrap or hessian in cold areas. move tender plants in pots to a sheltered spot or unheated greenhouse. raising pots pots standing directly on the ground can freeze to the surface and block drainage. stand on pot feet or bricks to keep drainage clear. watering in winter containers still need water in winter, but much less. check weekly and water only if the compost is dry. overwatering in cold weather rots roots. protecting tender plants tender plants in pots can be moved under cover for winter. move pelargoniums, citrus, olives and fuchsias to a frost free greenhouse or conservatory. see our winter checklist for more on protecting tender plants. best plants for containers summer bedding trailing: surfinia, lobelia, bacopa, calibrachoa upright: pelargoniums, begonias, antirrhinum, salvia foliage: coleus, ipomoea, helichrysum permanent shrubs lavender: see our lavender profile rosemary: see our rosemary profile hydrangea: see our hydrangea profile bay, box, dwarf conifers: classic evergreens shade containers hostas: see our hosta profile ferns: see our fern profile hellebores: see our hellebore profile begonias, fuchsias: for summer colour in shade winter containers pansies and violas: the classic winter bedding plant cyclamen: hardy cyclamen flower from autumn to spring primulas and polyanthus: colour from late winter winter flowering heathers: erica carnea and darleyensis evergreen shrubs: skimmia, sarcococca, viburnum tinus vegetables in containers tomatoes: tumbling varieties for hanging baskets salad leaves, herbs, cut and come again: easy in troughs potatoes: in special potato bags or large pots strawberries: in strawberry planters or hanging baskets container design tips the thriller, filler, spiller formula for mixed containers, use one tall plant as the thriller (a phormium, a cordyline, or a tall dahlia), three or four bushy plants as fillers (pelargoniums, begonias, salvia), and two or three trailing plants as spillers (surfinia, lobelia, ivy). this gives height, body, and overflow. colour schemes stick to 2 or 3 colours for impact. single colour schemes (all white, all blue) are sophisticated. hot colour schemes (red, orange, yellow) are dramatic. cool schemes (blue, purple, white) are calming. repetition repeat the same container and the same planting in several places for a unified look. three identical pots of lavender along a path looks deliberate; three mismatched pots with random plants looks accidental. height variation vary the height of containers in a group. three pots of different heights, with the tallest at the back, looks more interesting than three identical pots in a row. costs of container gardening 40cm terracotta pot: 30 to 80 pounds 50cm glazed pot: 60 to 150 pounds multi purpose compost (60 litres): 8 to 15 pounds john innes no 3 (25 litres): 8 to 12 pounds summer bedding plants: 30 to 80 pounds to fill a 40cm pot drip irrigation kit: 100 to 200 pounds for a small system a fully planted 50cm container with quality plants and compost will cost 120 to 250 pounds to put together. see our soft landscaping service if you would like us to plant your containers. common container mistakes pots too small small pots dry out fast and restrict roots. go bigger than you think you need. wrong compost multipurpose in a permanent planting degrades after a year and the plants suffer. use john innes no 3 for shrubs and trees. inconsistent watering letting the compost dry out completely between waterings stresses the plants and reduces flowering. keep the compost consistently moist. not feeding container plants cannot send roots out for nutrients. they depend on you. feed weekly from april to september. no drainage pots without drainage holes drown the roots. if your pot has no holes, drill some. crowding too many plants more is not better. overcrowded plants compete for water and nutrients and never reach their full size. a 40cm pot needs 3 to 5 plants for a summer display, not 10. trust the plant labels and give each plant room to fill its space. if you want a fuller look quickly, choose plants that grow fast and trail, like surfinia, lobelia and bacopa. these will fill a pot in a few weeks without needing to be planted too densely at the start. final thoughts container gardening is one of the most rewarding ways to garden. a few well chosen pots can transform a patio, a front door, or a balcony. the key is to invest in decent pots, the right compost, and consistent watering and feeding. done well, containers outperform beds for colour and impact. the secret is to start small, get a few pots working well, and expand as your confidence grows. a garden full of beautiful containers is built up over years, not in a single trip to the garden centre. container planting service frequently asked questions how often should i water my containers? daily in summer, sometimes twice in hot weather. every 2 to 3 days in spring and autumn. weekly or less in winter. push a finger into the compost; if it feels dry below the surface, water. what is the best compost for containers? for short term summer displays, peat free multipurpose compost. for permanent shrubs and trees, john innes no 3. for acid loving plants, ericaceous compost. mixing the two gives the best of both for mixed displays. can i reuse container compost? yes, but refresh it. remove the top 10cm, add fresh compost and slow release fertiliser, and mix in some of the old compost. do not reuse the same compost for more than 2 years because it becomes exhausted and can harbour disease. how do i protect pots from frost? wrap pots in bubble wrap or hessian. raise them on pot feet to keep drainage clear. move tender plants under cover. in very cold areas, choose frost proof pots (fibreclay, composite, or frost resistant terracotta). further reading composting in the uk winter garden tasks checklist spring garden checklist best plants for shade gardens lavender rosemary salvia best plants for shade gardens in the uk best plants for shade gardens in the uk. practical recommendations for dry shade, damp shade and partial shade, with soil preparation and design tips. best plants for shade gardens in the uk plants, borders & planting ideas shade is one of the most common garden challenges in the uk, whether from buildings, fences, trees or neighbouring hedges. the good news is that some of the most beautiful garden plants actually thrive in shade and look better there than in full sun. this guide covers the best plants for dry shade, damp shade and partial shade in uk gardens, with soil preparation tips, design ideas, and what to avoid. we work with shaded gardens across the chilterns and these are the plants we genuinely rely on. quick answer the best plants for shade depend on whether the shade is dry or damp. for dry shade under trees, choose ferns, epimedium, bergenias, hardy geraniums and hellebores. for damp shade, choose hostas, astilbe, primulas, ferns and rodgersia. for partial shade, the choice is wider, including hydrangeas, foxgloves, dicentra and pulmonaria. shade gardening golden rules improve the soil before planting. mulch annually. water new plants through the first season. plant in drifts for impact. do not try to grow sun loving plants in shade; choose plants that thrive in the conditions you have. understanding shade types of shade not all shade is the same. the main types are: light shade: under open trees or on the north side of a fence, with some reflected light. partial shade: 3 to 6 hours of direct sun, either morning or afternoon. dappled shade: under deciduous trees, with moving patches of sun and shade. deep shade: under evergreen trees or dense structures, with no direct sun. dry shade: shade combined with dry soil, usually under trees whose roots take all the water. damp shade: shade combined with moisture, often on heavy soil near water or in low lying spots. why shade matters shade affects plants in two ways: reduced light for photosynthesis, and reduced soil moisture (because trees and hedges take the water). the combination of dry and shade is the hardest to plant for. damp shade is easier because the moisture supports a wider range of plants. assessing your shade watch your garden through a day and note where the sun reaches. a spot that gets morning sun until 11am and then shade is very different from a spot that gets sun from 4pm onwards. both are partial shade but suit different plants. also dig a small hole and check the soil. if it is dry and full of tree roots, you have dry shade. if it is damp and rich, you have damp shade. the right plant choice depends on knowing what you have. plants for dry shade dry shade is the toughest gardening challenge. under large trees, the soil is dry, full of roots, and often poor. but some plants will thrive. top plants for dry shade bergenia: evergreen, leathery leaves, pink flowers in spring epimedium: delicate looking but tough, with spring flowers and bronze new leaves hardy geraniums (geranium macrorrhizum): spreading, drought tolerant, pink or white flowers ferns (dryopteris, polystichum): see our fern profile hellebores: see our hellebore profile ajuga: low growing ground cover with blue flowers lamium: silver leaved ground cover vinca (periwinkle): evergreen with blue flowers ivy: the ultimate dry shade ground cover, if you can contain it japanese anemone: late summer flowers, spreads in shade bulbs for dry shade spring bulbs do well under deciduous trees because they flower before the leaves come out and shade the ground. try: snowdrops winter aconites daffodils (especially smaller species types) bluebells (english, not spanish) wood anemones crocuses shrubs for dry shade aucuba (spotted laurel) mahonia skimmia sarcococca (sweet box) osmanthus daphne laureola plants for damp shade damp shade is much easier. the moisture supports a wide range of lush foliage plants. top plants for damp shade hostas: the classic shade plant, see our hosta profile astilbe: feathery plumes of pink, white or red flowers in summer primulas: candelabra and vulgaris types love damp shade rodgersia: dramatic foliage and pink or white flowers darmera: umbrella like leaves and pink flowers on bare stems in spring ligularia: large leaves and tall yellow flower spikes ferns (all types): especially shuttlecock fern (matteuccia) and royal fern (osmunda) solomon's seal: arching stems with white bell flowers lungwort (pulmonaria): early flowers and spotted leaves brunnera: forget me not like flowers and heart shaped leaves shrubs for damp shade hydrangeas: see our hydrangea profile dogwoods (cornus): grown for winter stem colour willow (salix): many smaller varieties suit damp shade camellia: needs acid soil but loves shade pieris: needs acid soil fothergilla: needs acid soil, beautiful autumn colour plants for partial shade partial shade opens up a much wider range of plants. most gardens have some partial shade, and many of the best garden plants prefer it. top perennials for partial shade foxgloves: see our foxglove profile delphiniums: tall spires of blue, purple, pink or white dicentra (bleeding heart): arching stems with heart shaped flowers aquilegia: delicate flowers in spring, self seed freely heuchera: evergreen foliage in many colours hellebores: see our hellebore profile pulmonaria: early flowers and spotted leaves japanese anemone: pink or white flowers from august to october hardy geraniums: most varieties tolerate partial shade brunnera: forget me not flowers and variegated leaves shrubs for partial shade hydrangeas: all types, see our hydrangea profile pieris: acid soil only camellia: acid soil only daphne: scented flowers in winter and spring viburnum: many varieties, winter and spring flowers skimmia: evergreen with spring berries fatsia: large evergreen leaves aucuba: evergreen, tough, berries on female plants climbers for shade ivy: self clings, evergreen hydrangea anomala petiolaris: climbing hydrangea, self clinging clematis (group 3): late flowering types tolerate shade honeysuckle: native and garden varieties, scented schizophragma: similar to climbing hydrangea, pink or white soil preparation for shade improve before planting shade soil is often dry and impoverished, especially under trees. before planting, dig in plenty of organic matter: garden compost, well rotted manure, or leaf mould. this improves moisture retention and feeds the soil life that helps plants establish. see our guide to composting for making your own organic matter, and our guide to improving clay soil for heavy soils. mulch annually shade gardens benefit more from mulching than sunny ones. a 5cm layer of compost or leaf mould each spring retains moisture, suppresses weeds, and feeds the soil. under trees, mulch also protects the tree roots from compaction and damage. water new plants new plants in shade need watering through the first growing season, even if the spot looks damp. tree roots compete fiercely for water, and a new plant cannot yet reach the deeper moisture. water weekly from april to september for the first year. choose the right size plants in dry shade, larger plants establish better than tiny plug plants. a plant in a 2 litre pot has a bigger root system and can compete better with tree roots. buy larger plants for difficult shade positions. designing a shade garden plant in drifts plant in groups of 3, 5 or 7 of the same variety for impact. a single plant in shade gets lost; a drift of the same plant looks deliberate and lush. use foliage for colour in shade, foliage is more reliable than flowers. use plants with variegated leaves (brunnera, pulmonaria, euonymus), coloured leaves (heuchera, ajuga), or dramatic shapes (ferns, hostas, rodgersia) to create interest. add white flowers for light white flowers light up shady corners. japanese anemones, astilbe, dicentra, brunnera and foxgloves all have white forms that glow in shade. create layers mimic a woodland by planting in layers: tall shrubs and small trees at the back, medium perennials in the middle, and low ground cover at the front. this gives a natural look and uses the space efficiently. add structure with ferns ferns are the backbone of a shade garden. they provide structure when flowering plants are dormant, and their fronds unfurl beautifully in spring. mix evergreen ferns like polystichum and dryopteris with deciduous types like the shuttlecock fern and the royal fern. see our fern profile for the best varieties. a shade border with at least 30 percent ferns always looks good. they fill the gaps between flowering perennials, hide the dying foliage of spring bulbs, and provide winter interest when the herbaceous plants have died down. plant them in groups of three for impact rather than dotting single plants around. include spring bulbs spring bulbs do well under deciduous trees because they flower before the leaves come out. snowdrops, winter aconites, daffodils and bluebells give early colour in shade gardens. see our spring checklist for planting times. plant bulbs in drifts rather than single rows. a sweep of 50 snowdrops through a shady border looks deliberate and natural; 5 snowdrops dotted around look lost. plant in autumn, at 2 to 3 times the bulb depth, and mark the spot so you do not accidentally dig them up later in the year. plants to avoid in shade sun loving mediterranean plants lavender, rosemary, thyme and other mediterranean herbs need full sun and sharp drainage. they will sulk and die in shade. see our lavender profile for what lavender needs. most roses roses need at least 6 hours of direct sun. in shade they become leggy, diseased and flower poorly. a few varieties like rambling roses tolerate partial shade, but most do not. drought loving perennials plants like gaura, gypsophila, perovskia and nepeta need sun and well drained soil. they flop and die in shade. lawn grass turf struggles in shade. moss takes over. if you have shade, consider replacing the lawn with shade tolerant ground cover or a gravel garden. see our article on why lawns get mossy for what to do. costs of planting a shade garden shade perennial in 2 litre pot: 8 to 15 pounds ferns in 2 litre pot: 10 to 20 pounds hosta in 3 litre pot: 12 to 25 pounds hydrangea in 5 litre pot: 20 to 40 pounds compost or soil improver (75 litres): 8 to 15 pounds planting service: 30 to 50 pounds per hour for a 10 square metre shade border, expect to spend 200 to 500 pounds on plants and compost if you do the work yourself, or 500 to 1,000 pounds with professional planting. see our soft landscaping service for what we cover. common shade garden mistakes planting sun lovers in shade this is the most common mistake. choose plants that thrive in shade, not plants that tolerate it. the rhs has a useful guide to shade plants for further reading. not improving the soil shade soil is often dry and poor. add organic matter before planting and mulch annually. not watering new plants new plants in shade need regular watering through the first season. tree roots take all the water. planting too close to tree trunks the soil directly under a tree is the driest and most root filled. plant at the edge of the tree canopy, not against the trunk. expecting flowers all year shade gardens are about foliage, not flowers. plan for foliage interest and let flowers be a bonus. final thoughts shade is not a problem, it is an opportunity. some of the most beautiful gardens in the uk are shade gardens, with lush foliage, early flowers, and a cool calm atmosphere that sunny gardens cannot match. the trick is to choose the right plants for the type of shade you have, improve the soil, and mulch annually. if you have a shaded garden that needs planting, the best first step is to assess the shade type and soil moisture, then choose plants that thrive in those conditions. we can advise on plant choices and do the planting for you. shade garden planting service frequently asked questions will grass grow under trees? rarely successfully. grass needs sun and moisture, both of which trees take. replace the grass with shade tolerant ground cover like ivy, vinca, or epimedium, or use a mulch of bark under the tree. can i grow vegetables in shade? leafy vegetables like lettuce, spinach, chard and rocket tolerate partial shade. root vegetables and fruiting vegetables like tomatoes and courgettes need full sun. see our spring checklist for vegetable sowing times. what grows in deep shade under conifers? very little. conifers make the soil extremely dry and acidic. try ivy, periwinkle, or a thick mulch of bark. often the best answer is to remove lower branches to let in light, or accept the bare ground and use containers instead. can hydrangeas grow in full shade? hydrangeas prefer partial shade but will tolerate deeper shade, with fewer flowers. they need moisture, so make sure the soil does not dry out. see our hydrangea profile for details. further reading composting in the uk how to improve clay soil spring garden checklist low-maintenance plants for chiltern gardens how to create a wildlife garden in the uk how to create a wildlife garden in the uk. practical steps for ponds, native plants, habitat piles and chemical free gardening that supports native wildlife. how to create a wildlife garden in the uk wildlife & wildflower gardens a wildlife garden is one of the most rewarding things you can create. by providing food, shelter and water for native species, you turn your garden into a haven for birds, bees, butterflies, hedgehogs, frogs and countless insects. the good news is that wildlife gardening is also lower maintenance than traditional gardening, because you are working with nature rather than against it. this guide covers practical steps to create a wildlife garden in the uk, from ponds to plant choices. we have created wildlife gardens across buckinghamshire and these are the methods that work. quick answer to create a wildlife garden, provide water (a pond is best), plant native species and nectar rich flowers, leave areas of long grass and fallen leaves, build habitat piles, stop using chemicals, and feed the birds. a typical wildlife garden takes 2 to 3 years to mature, but you will see more wildlife within months of making changes. wildlife garden golden rules provide water. plant for year round nectar. leave some mess. stop using pesticides. feed the birds. compost your waste. choose native plants where possible. build habitats, not just beds. why create a wildlife garden? the decline in uk wildlife uk wildlife has declined dramatically in the last 50 years. farmland bird populations have fallen by 50 percent since 1970. hedgehog numbers have dropped by 30 percent since 2000. insect populations are collapsing, with consequences for everything that feeds on them. gardens cover about 400,000 hectares of the uk, more than all the national nature reserves combined. what we do in our gardens matters. the benefits a wildlife garden is more interesting, more beautiful, and more resilient than a sterile one. birds eat garden pests. frogs and toads eat slugs. bees pollinate fruit and vegetables. a garden teeming with life is a constant source of interest and a small but real contribution to nature conservation. provide water: build a pond why a pond is the best thing you can do a pond is the single most valuable wildlife feature in any garden. even a small pond attracts frogs, toads, newts, dragonflies, water boatmen and a host of other creatures. birds and hedgehogs come to drink and bathe. a pond transforms a garden's wildlife value overnight. pond design a wildlife pond should have sloping sides so creatures can get in and out, and a shallow beach area at one end. aim for at least 1 metre deep in the middle so the pond does not freeze solid in winter. use a butyl rubber liner, and cover the edges with turf or stones so the liner does not show. do not stock the pond with fish. fish eat tadpoles and other wildlife. a wildlife pond is a fish free pond. pond plants include a mix of submerged oxygenators, floating plants, and marginal plants around the edge: oxygenators: hornwort, water starwort, water milfoil floating: water lily, frogbit (do not use invasive species) marginals: water forget me not, marsh marigold, purple loosestrife, iris pseudacorus native plants are best for wildlife. the rhs has a useful section on wildlife gardening with plant lists and pond advice. pond size even a small pond 1 metre by 1 metre is valuable. if you have room, a 3 metre by 2 metre pond will support a much wider range of wildlife. see our soft landscaping service for pond construction. plant for nectar and pollen year round nectar the aim is to provide nectar and pollen from late winter to late autumn. this supports bees, butterflies and other pollinators through their active season. winter and early spring hellebores: see our hellebore profile winter flowering heathers (erica carnea and darleyensis) crocuses snowdrops mahonia viburnum tinus sarcococca (sweet box) spring and early summer forget me not aquilegia wallflowers lupins foxgloves: see our foxglove profile alliums geraniums summer lavender: see our lavender profile rosemary: see our rosemary profile marjoram and thyme echinacea rudbeckia scabious verbena bonariensis buddleia (the butterfly bush) marigolds late summer and autumn sedum (ice plant) japanese anemone asters michaelmas daisies dahlias (single flowered types) ivy (flowers in autumn, vital for late bees) choose single flowers single flowered varieties are better for pollinators than double flowers. double flowers have been bred for showiness at the expense of nectar and pollen. a single rose is a feast for bees; a double rose is a dead end. plant native species why native plants matter native plants support a wider range of insects than exotic plants because they have evolved together. a native oak supports over 300 species of insect. an ornamental cherry supports a fraction of that. the more insects, the more birds, bats and other wildlife. native trees and shrubs oak (if you have space) birch rowan hawthorn blackthorn holly beech hornbeam field maple spindle hazel dogwood a native hedge of hawthorn, blackthorn, hazel and dogwood is one of the best wildlife features you can plant. see our hedge planting guide for how and when. native wildflowers primrose cowslip red campion white campion foxglove teasel knapweed field scabious oxeye daisy yarrow you can grow wildflowers from seed in a wildflower meadow area, or plant plug plants into existing borders. avoid wildflower seed mixes that contain non native species. create habitat features habitat piles a pile of logs, branches and bark in a quiet corner provides shelter for hedgehogs, frogs, toads, slow worms and countless insects. pile logs of different sizes, with gaps between them, and leave the pile undisturbed. leaf piles a pile of autumn leaves in a corner provides habitat for overwintering insects and amphibians. hedgehogs hibernate in leaf piles. see our autumn checklist for what to do with autumn leaves. bug hotels a bug hotel is a structure of hollow stems, drilled wood, pine cones and bark that provides nesting sites for solitary bees and other insects. buy one or build your own from scrap materials. site it in sun, facing south or southeast. stone piles a pile of stones or a rockery provides habitat for slow worms, lizards and ground beetles. site it in sun so the stones warm up. compost heaps a compost heap is a habitat in itself. slow worms, grass snakes, toads and many insects live in compost heaps. see our composting guide for how to make one. hedgehog highways hedgehogs need to roam 1 to 2 kilometres each night to find food and mates. a solid fence stops them. cut a 13cm by 13cm hole in the bottom of your fence, or dig a tunnel underneath, to let hedgehogs pass through. talk to your neighbours and create a hedgehog street. hedgehog streets, coordinated by the hedgehog street campaign, have shown that connecting even a handful of gardens makes a measurable difference to local hedgehog populations. one hedgehog with access to 10 connected gardens has a far better chance of surviving the winter than one trapped in a single small garden. lawns for wildlife leave some long grass a close mown lawn is a desert for wildlife. leaving even a small patch of long grass provides habitat for caterpillars, beetles and the insects that feed birds. mow paths through the long grass so it looks deliberate. no mow may the plantlife no mow may campaign encourages gardeners to leave the whole lawn uncut in may. the result is a flush of daisies, dandelions, clover and self heal, all valuable for bees. try it for one month and see what appears. reduce or stop fertiliser fertilised grass grows fast and crowds out wildflowers. stop feeding the lawn and you will get more diversity over time. plant bulbs in grass crocuses, snowdrops and daffodils planted in the lawn give early nectar for bees. mow around them until the leaves die back, 6 weeks after flowering. stop using chemicals no pesticides pesticides kill the insects that birds and other wildlife depend on. even organic pesticides like pyrethrum are broad spectrum and kill beneficial insects along with pests. the wildlife gardening approach is to tolerate some pest damage and let natural predators (birds, ladybirds, lacewings, frogs) do the work. no herbicides glyphosate and other herbicides kill plants but also affect soil life. hand weed or hoe instead. see our guide to preventing weeds in garden beds for non chemical methods. no slug pellets metaldehyde slug pellets are now banned in the uk because they poison wildlife. even iron phosphate pellets, sold as wildlife friendly, can affect soil organisms. use physical barriers (copper tape, wool pellets), encourage predators (frogs, toads, hedgehogs, ground beetles), and use nematodes for serious infestations. peat free compost peat extraction destroys peat bogs, which are vital wildlife habitats and carbon stores. use peat free compost in your garden. see our composting guide for making your own. feed the birds year round feeding birds benefit from feeding all year, but especially in winter and early spring when natural food is scarce. put out a mix of seeds, sunflower hearts, fat balls and suet. avoid bread, which has little nutritional value. feeders and tables use a mix of hanging feeders (for tits and finches), a bird table (for robins, blackbirds and thrushes), and ground feeding (for dunnocks, wrens and sparrows). clean feeders regularly to prevent disease. water for birds birds need water for drinking and bathing. a shallow bird bath, or the edge of a pond with shallow sides, is essential. keep the water fresh and break the ice in winter. nest boxes nest boxes encourage birds to breed in your garden. different birds need different box sizes and entrance holes. site boxes facing north or east, out of direct sun, at 2 to 4 metres high. costs of creating a wildlife garden small pond (2m by 1m): 150 to 400 pounds including liner and plants larger pond (4m by 2m): 400 to 1,000 pounds bug hotel: 20 to 60 pounds, or free if diy bird feeder and food: 30 to 80 pounds initial, 100 to 200 pounds per year ongoing nest box: 15 to 40 pounds wildflower plug plants: 1 to 3 pounds each native hedge (10 metres): 80 to 200 pounds for plants a modest wildlife garden transformation can be done for 200 to 500 pounds. a full transformation with pond, hedge and extensive planting will cost 1,000 to 3,000 pounds. see our soft landscaping service for what we cover. common mistakes tidiness wildlife needs mess. a perfectly tidy garden is a desert for wildlife. leave some long grass, some leaf piles, some standing seed heads. putting fish in the pond fish eat tadpoles and other pond life. a wildlife pond has no fish. using pesticides pesticides break the food chain. stop using them and let natural predators take over. it takes a year or two for the balance to establish. planting non native invasive species avoid invasive plants like rhododendron ponticum, , himalayan balsam and giant hogweed. they escape into the wild and damage native ecosystems. cutting hedges in nesting season birds nest in hedges from march to august. do not cut hedges in this period. see our guide to when to cut hedges for the rules. final thoughts creating a wildlife garden is one of the most rewarding projects you can undertake. within months of making changes, you will see more birds, more bees, more butterflies. within a few years, your garden will be a thriving ecosystem that needs less maintenance, fewer chemicals, and gives more pleasure than a sterile lawn and bedding scheme ever could. the key is to work with nature rather than against it. provide water, plant for nectar and pollen, leave some mess, and stop using chemicals. the wildlife will come. wildlife garden service frequently asked questions do i need a big garden for wildlife? no. even a small garden can support a lot of wildlife. a small pond, a few nectar rich plants, a bird feeder and a pile of logs will transform a courtyard garden. will a wildlife garden look messy? it does not have to. you can have formal areas and wildlife areas in the same garden. a neatly mown lawn with a wildflower border, a tidy patio with a wildlife pond nearby, both work. the key is to plan the mess into specific areas. are native plants essential? not strictly, but they support more wildlife. many non native plants like buddleia, lavender and sedum are excellent for pollinators. aim for a mix of native and well chosen non native plants. how do i stop slugs without killing wildlife? encourage predators (frogs, toads, hedgehogs, ground beetles), use physical barriers (copper tape, wool pellets), and use nematodes for serious infestations. see our hosta profile for slug control methods. further reading composting in the uk autumn garden checklist best time to plant hedges how to prevent weeds without chemicals lavender rosemary verbena rhododendron anemone japanese anemone hosta hellebore hellebores foxglove foxgloves rose dogwood rowan cherry field maple viburnum mahonia box beech hornbeam hawthorn blackthorn hazel holly sarcococca spindle geranium allium alliums lupin lupins iris aquilegia echinacea rudbeckia pear thyme marjoram snowdrop snowdrops daffodil daffodils how to prevent weeds in garden beds long term practical uk guide to preventing weeds in garden beds long term. covers mulching, ground cover plants, weed fabric, edging and no dig methods that work. how to prevent weeds in garden beds long term regular garden maintenance weeds are the number one frustration for uk gardeners. they seem to appear from nowhere, grow faster than the plants you want, and come back no matter how many times you pull them. but weed prevention is possible, and the methods are not complicated. this guide covers practical, long term weed prevention for garden beds, from mulching to ground cover to no dig methods. we have kept beds weed free across hundreds of gardens and these are the methods that actually work. quick answer to prevent weeds in garden beds long term, mulch heavily every spring, plant densely with ground cover, install weed fabric under new beds, use permanent edging to stop grass creeping in, and adopt a no dig approach that minimises soil disturbance. combining these methods can reduce weeding time by 80 percent or more. weed prevention golden rules mulch annually. plant densely. never leave bare soil. use weed fabric for new beds. edge beds properly. hoe weekly in the growing season. never let weeds go to seed. understanding weeds types of weeds weeds fall into three categories, each needing a different approach: annual weeds: grow, flower, set seed and die in one year. examples: chickweed, groundsel, hairy bittercress, annual meadow grass. controlled by hoeing and preventing seed set. biennial weeds: grow leaves in year one, flower and set seed in year two. examples: foxglove (wild), hogweed, spear thistle. controlled by digging out before flowering. perennial weeds: live for years and regrow from roots each spring. examples: bindweed, couch grass, ground elder, dandelion, dock, nettle. controlled by digging out every root, or by smothering. how weeds spread weeds spread by seed and by root. annual weeds are prolific seeders: a single chickweed plant can produce 2,000 seeds. perennial weeds spread by roots: a 1cm fragment of couch grass root can grow into a new plant. the trick is to stop both: prevent seed set, and remove every root. the soil seed bank every square metre of soil contains thousands of weed seeds, some viable for decades. every time you dig, you bring new seeds to the surface where they germinate. this is why no dig gardening reduces weeds: undisturbed soil keeps its seed bank dormant. mulching: the number one method why mulch works a 5 to 10cm layer of organic mulch on the soil surface blocks light, preventing weed seeds from germinating. it also retains moisture, improves soil structure, and feeds the soil life. mulching is the single most effective thing you can do to prevent weeds. what to use garden compost: the best all round mulch, free if you make your own. see our composting guide . well rotted manure: rich in nutrients, but can be too strong for some plants. bark: looks neat, lasts 2 to 3 years, but uses nitrogen from the soil as it breaks down. leaf mould: excellent for woodland plants, low in nutrients. wood chip: good for paths, coarse for beds. grass clippings: fine in thin layers but can slime if too thick. straw: good for vegetables, adds organic matter. how to mulch clear all existing weeds first. mulch on top of weeds does not kill them; perennial weeds just grow through. water the soil if dry before mulching. apply a 5 to 10cm layer, keeping the mulch away from the stems of plants to prevent rotting. mulch in early spring before weed seeds germinate. an annual mulch keeps beds weed free all season. see our spring checklist for timing. how much mulch for a 10 square metre bed with a 5cm layer, you need about 0.5 cubic metres of mulch. that is roughly 10 bags of 50 litre compost. expect to pay 50 to 100 pounds for enough mulch for a 10 square metre bed. planting dense ground cover why ground cover works bare soil is an invitation to weeds. planted soil is not. ground cover plants shade the soil, preventing weed seeds from germinating, and compete with any weeds that do appear. a well planted bed needs a fraction of the weeding of a bare one. best ground cover plants for uk gardens hardy geraniums: see our article on low maintenance plants bergenia: evergreen, tough, pink flowers in spring epimedium: evergreen, delicate flowers, thrives in shade ajuga: low growing, blue flowers, spreads steadily lamium: silver leaves, spreads in shade vinca (periwinkle): evergreen, blue or white flowers pachysandra: evergreen, spreads in shade heucherella: hybrid of heuchera and tiarella, colourful leaves stachys byzantina (lamb's ear): silver leaves, spreads thyme: for sunny dry spots, scented planting density plant ground cover closer than the label suggests, especially in the first year. the aim is to cover the soil as quickly as possible. as the plants fill in, you can lift and divide them to extend the planting. underplanting shrubs and trees shrubs and trees with bare soil underneath look unfinished and become weedy. underplant with ground cover to fill the space and suppress weeds. see our shade plant guide for plants that thrive under trees. weed fabric for new beds when to use weed fabric weed fabric is a porous membrane laid on the soil surface under mulch. it blocks light (so weeds cannot germinate) but lets water and air through. it is most useful for new beds where you want to suppress existing weeds long term. how to install clear the worst of the existing weeds. lay the fabric over the bed, overlapping joints by 10cm. pin down with metal pegs. cut crosses in the fabric where you want to plant, and fold back the flaps. plant through the holes. cover with a 5cm layer of mulch to hide the fabric. limitations weed fabric is not a permanent solution. it breaks down over 5 to 10 years. perennial weeds like bindweed can grow through it. and it makes future planting more difficult because you have to cut through the fabric each time. best used in shrub beds where planting is permanent, not in vegetable beds. what not to use do not use plastic sheeting or old carpet. plastic stops water and air getting to the soil, killing the soil life. old carpet breaks down into fibres that contaminate the soil and is now banned at most tips. no dig gardening why no dig reduces weeds every time you turn the soil, you bring weed seeds to the surface where they germinate. no dig gardening avoids this by leaving the soil undisturbed. you add organic matter to the surface each year and let the worms incorporate it. no dig is particularly effective against annual weeds, which rely on soil disturbance to germinate. perennial weeds need to be dug out first, but once the bed is clean, no dig keeps it clean. how to start a no dig bed for a new bed on grass or weeds, lay thick cardboard (overlapping joints) directly on the grass. cover with a 10cm layer of compost. plant into the compost. the cardboard suppresses the grass, which dies and rots down. within 6 months you have a clean, weed free bed. each year, add another 5cm of compost to the surface. do not dig. the soil structure improves, the worm population grows, and the weed seed bank stays dormant. the charles dowding method charles dowding has pioneered no dig in the uk and his trials show that no dig beds produce as much or more than dug beds, with a fraction of the weeding. the rhs has a useful guide to no dig gardening . permanent edging why edging matters grass creeps into beds from lawns, and weeds creep in from paths. without a solid edge, the boundary becomes a constant source of weeds. permanent edging stops this. types of edging metal edging: steel or aluminium, sharp clean line, lasts decades, expensive at 20 to 40 pounds per metre brick edging: laid on edge or flat, classic, lasts decades, 30 to 60 pounds per metre stone edging: natural stone, expensive but beautiful, 50 to 100 pounds per metre timber edging: treated timber, lasts 10 to 15 years, 15 to 30 pounds per metre spade edge: a cut edge with a half moon spade, free but needs redoing twice a year for long term weed prevention, a permanent physical edge is best. see our soft landscaping service for installation. edge maintenance even with permanent edging, check the boundary twice a year and trim back any creeping grass or weeds. a few minutes of edging saves hours of weeding. hoeing and hand weeding the weekly hoe despite all the prevention methods, some weeds will appear. a weekly hoe in the growing season keeps them under control. use a dutch hoe on dry days, slicing just below the surface. the weeds die on the surface. hoeing is most effective on small weeds. once a weed is more than 5cm tall, hoeing is less effective and hand pulling is better. hand weeding perennials perennial weeds like dandelion, dock and bindweed need hand digging. use a long handled weed tool or a fork to lift the whole root. any fragment left behind will regrow. for serious infestations of bindweed, couch grass or ground elder, the best approach is to dig out the whole bed, sieve the soil, and replant. this is hard work but it is the only reliable way to clear a badly infested bed. never let weeds go to seed the single most important rule of weeding: never let weeds go to seed. one dandelion head produces 200 seeds. one chickweed plant produces 2,000. pull weeds before they flower, not after. dealing with specific problem weeds bindweed bindweed has deep white roots that break easily. any fragment regrows. dig out as much as possible, then keep pulling the new shoots as they appear. eventually the root system exhausts. this takes 2 to 3 years of persistent pulling. couch grass couch grass spreads by underground stems. dig out the whole plant, sieving the soil to remove every fragment. mulching helps suppress it but does not kill it. ground elder ground elder spreads by underground stems. dig out thoroughly, removing every fragment. persistent pulling of new shoots will eventually exhaust it. see our guide to restoring overgrown gardens for serious infestations. dandelions and docks tap rooted weeds need the whole root lifted. use a long handled weed tool to lever the root out. any fragment left behind regrows. mare's tail mare's tail (horsetail) has deep roots and a waxy coating that resists weedkiller. the only reliable control is repeated cutting and improving drainage. it thrives in poorly drained soil. weed prevention in different bed types vegetable beds use no dig methods. mulch with compost annually. plant in close spacing to shade the soil. hoe weekly. use weed fabric on paths between beds. shrub and perennial beds mulch annually with compost or bark. plant densely with ground cover. use weed fabric under mulch for new beds. install permanent edging. rose beds mulch with compost or bark in spring. underplant with ground cover like hardy geraniums or catmint. avoid hoeing around roses because their roots are shallow; hand weed instead. see our rose profile . paths and patios use weed fabric under gravel. brush in polymeric sand between paving joints. treat weeds with a flame weeder or a contact weedkiller. see our pressure washing service for cleaning paving. costs of weed prevention mulch for 10 square metre bed: 50 to 100 pounds weed fabric (10 square metres): 20 to 40 pounds metal edging (10 metres): 200 to 400 pounds ground cover plants (10 plants): 50 to 100 pounds cardboard for no dig: free compost bin for making mulch: 30 to 60 pounds the investment in weed prevention pays back within a year in reduced weeding time. see our garden maintenance service if you would rather have us handle the weeding. common mistakes leaving bare soil bare soil is an invitation to weeds. mulch it or plant it. letting weeds go to seed one seeding weed creates years of work. pull before they flower. using weedkiller as a first resort glyphosate has its place for serious infestations, but it should be a last resort, not a habit. mulching, ground cover and good technique reduce the need for chemicals dramatically. not digging out perennial roots pulling the top off a dandelion does nothing. you have to lift the root. half measures with perennial weeds are a waste of time. digging when you do not need to every dig brings weed seeds to the surface. use no dig methods wherever possible. final thoughts weed prevention is not about one dramatic intervention, it is about a set of consistent habits. mulch every spring. plant densely. hoe weekly. never let weeds seed. use no dig. edge your beds. over a few years these habits transform a weedy garden into a manageable one. the aim is not a weed free garden, which is impossible, but a garden where weeds are a minor occasional task rather than a constant battle. get the prevention right and weeding becomes a five minute job rather than a weekend project. see our summer tidy checklist for fitting weed control into a maintenance routine. weed control service frequently asked questions what is the best mulch for weed prevention? garden compost is the best all round mulch because it suppresses weeds, improves soil, and is free if you make your own. bark is a good alternative if you want a longer lasting mulch. see our composting guide for making your own. does weed fabric work? yes, for new beds with permanent planting. it blocks light and prevents annual weeds. it is less effective against perennial weeds, which can grow through. it lasts 5 to 10 years before breaking down. how do i get rid of bindweed? dig out as much root as possible, then keep pulling new shoots as they appear. eventually the root system exhausts, but it takes 2 to 3 years of persistent pulling. do not use weedkiller as a first resort. is no dig really effective for weeds? yes. no dig leaves weed seeds dormant in the soil. once the bed is clear of perennial weeds, no dig keeps annual weeds to a minimum. see the charles dowding trials for evidence. further reading composting in the uk spring garden checklist best plants for shade gardens how to restore an overgrown garden catmint heuchera foxglove epimedium bergenia tiarella heucherella rose roses geranium pear mint thyme ajuga vinca periwinkle pachysandra weeds weed bindweed nettle dandelion dandelions thistle how to prepare your lawn for summer want a green lawn this summer? our uk guide covers spring preparation, mowing heights, feeding, watering and weed control so your grass copes with drought. how to prepare your lawn for summer lawn care & lawn repair a british summer can mean six weeks of drought followed by a thunderstorm, and your lawn has to cope with both. the work you do in march, april and may is what gets the grass through june, july and august without stress. this guide covers the practical steps to prepare a lawn for summer, including feeding, mowing, watering, weed control, moss management and drought planning. it is written for family lawns in the chilterns and similar areas on chalk or heavy clay soils. quick answer summer lawn preparation is mostly about building deep roots before the dry weather arrives. feed in late march or early april, scarify lightly to remove dead material, aerate compacted areas, mow regularly but not too short, and water deeply once a week if there has been no rain. skip the spring moss treatment if you can, because scarifying weakens the grass just as it is trying to grow. if you only do three things, do these: feed with a spring lawn fertiliser in early april, raise the mower to 40mm, and water deeply in dry spells. everything else is fine tuning. why spring preparation matters grass in the uk grows most actively from late march to late may. during this window the plant is building roots, side shoots and food reserves. if the lawn is fed, watered and mown correctly in this period, it will enter summer with a deep root system and dense sward that can shrug off a fortnight of dry weather. if you miss this window, the lawn goes into summer weak. the first hot spell browns it off, you panic and water it daily, the roots stay shallow, and the lawn depends on you for the rest of the summer. doing the preparation properly in spring buys you a low maintenance summer. the 30 second rule if you walk across the lawn and the grass springs back within 30 seconds, it does not need watering. if the footprints stay visible, the lawn is under stress and needs a deep soak. this is more reliable than a calendar or a schedule. the spring preparation timeline march: first cut and clear up once the soil has warmed and the grass is visibly growing, give the lawn its first cut. set the mower high, around 50mm, and only take the top third off. the aim is to tidy the lawn, not scalp it. pick up leaves, twigs and any debris that has accumulated over winter. check for compaction in areas of heavy foot traffic. if water sits on the surface after rain, or the ground feels hard underfoot, plan to aerate in april when the soil is softer. april: feed, scarify and aerate april is the most important month for summer preparation. apply a spring lawn fertiliser when the soil is moist and rain is forecast within 48 hours. spring feeds are high in nitrogen to encourage leaf growth. apply evenly at the rate on the packet, typically 30 to 50g per square metre. two weeks after feeding, lightly scarify the lawn with a spring tine rake or powered scarifier. the aim is to remove dead grass and thatch, not to tear up the lawn. if you scarify too hard in spring you weaken the grass just as it needs to grow. save heavy scarifying for autumn. after scarifying, aerate compacted areas with a garden fork. push the fork in 10cm deep every 15cm, and rock it back and forth to open the holes. brush a 70/30 sand and soil mix into the holes to keep them open. may: mow, edge and watch for weeds by may the grass is growing fast. mow weekly with the mower set to 35 to 40mm. never remove more than one third of the grass height in a single cut. lower the mower gradually over a few cuts rather than all at once. cut a clean edge along borders and paths with a half moon edging iron. a crisp edge makes even an average lawn look well kept. weed out dandelions and daisies with a long handled weed knife, or spot treat with a selective weedkiller if the infestation is heavy. mowing for summer resilience the biggest single mistake uk garden owners make is mowing too short. a lawn cut at 25mm looks neat for a day, but the short grass has no leaf area to photosynthesise, no shade for the soil, and shallow roots. in the first dry spell it goes brown. raise the mower to 40mm for family lawns in late spring, and to 50mm in june, july and august. longer grass shades the soil, keeps the roots cool, and survives drought far better. you will mow slightly less often because the grass grows more slowly when it is not stressed. keep the mower blade sharp. a blunt blade tears the grass rather than cutting it, leaving a ragged tip that goes white and is more prone to disease. sharpen the blade once a year, ideally in late winter before the cutting season starts. mowing heights quick reference lawn type spring (mm) summer (mm) autumn (mm) family lawn (ryegrass mix) 35 to 40 40 to 50 40 ornamental (fescue and bent) 15 to 20 20 to 25 25 shaded lawn 45 to 50 50 to 60 50 newly seeded lawn 50 50 40 feeding for summer a spring feed in early april gives the grass the nutrients it needs to build a dense sward before summer. use a balanced spring fertiliser with an npk ratio of around 10:3:5. apply at the rate on the packet when the soil is moist and rain is forecast. avoid feeding after mid may. late feeds push soft, lush growth that is exactly what you do not want going into a hot dry summer. the grass becomes top heavy, the roots do not keep up, and the lawn is more prone to disease. if you want a second feed, use a slow release summer fertiliser in mid june at half the spring rate. slow release feeds drip feed nitrogen over 8 to 12 weeks and produce steady growth rather than a flush. watering in summer most established uk lawns do not need watering to survive summer. the grass goes brown and dormant in dry weather, but the crown and roots stay alive. when the rain returns, the lawn greens up within a week or two. if you decide to water, do it properly. a light daily sprinkle encourages shallow roots and makes the lawn dependent on you. a deep soak once a week, the equivalent of 25mm of rain, encourages roots to grow down to find moisture. water in the early morning or late evening to reduce evaporation. to measure how much you are watering, place an empty tuna tin or similar shallow container on the lawn. when it has 25mm of water in it, you have watered enough. this typically takes 30 to 60 minutes with a sprinkler depending on water pressure. weed and moss control before summer hand weed dandelions, plantains and daisies with a long handled weed knife in april. get the whole taproot or they will return. for heavy infestations, use a selective lawn weedkiller in mid may when the weeds are growing actively. do not use a total weedkiller such as glyphosate on a lawn. for moss, the best approach in spring is to address the cause rather than kill the moss. aerate compacted areas, raise the mowing height, and accept some moss in shaded areas. heavy scarification and iron sulphate treatment are best done in autumn when the grass has time to recover. see our guide to moss in lawns for the full process. drought warning sign if the grass takes on a blue grey tint and footprints stay visible for several minutes, the lawn is heading into drought stress. a single deep soak of 25mm will protect it for a week. do not wait until the grass is fully brown, as recovery is slower from that stage. drought planning for new lawns if you are sowing or turfing a new lawn in spring, choose a grass seed mix suited to your conditions. for family lawns, a ryegrass and fescue mix is hard wearing and reasonably drought tolerant. for shaded lawns, look for a shade tolerant mix with more fescue and bentgrass. avoid pure ryegrass mixes for lawns that may experience drought. ryegrass has shallow roots compared to fescue and browns off faster. see the rhs guidance on choosing lawn seed for more detail. if you are laying turf, lay it in mid march to mid april so it has time to root before summer. turf laid in may or june needs daily watering for the first month and is at high risk of failure in a dry spell. common summer lawn problems brown patches brown patches in summer are usually drought, dog urine, or a buried object such as a stone or rubble just below the surface. check the patch with a fork. if the soil is rock hard, it is compaction or rubble. if the patch is uniformly yellow with a green ring around it, it is dog urine. if the whole lawn is brown, it is drought. fairy rings fairy rings are dark green circles of grass, sometimes with mushrooms, sometimes with a ring of dead grass inside. they are caused by fungi breaking down organic matter in the soil. they are hard to eliminate. aerate the ring thoroughly, water it well, and mask the dark green with a nitrogen feed. red thread red thread appears as small pink or red patches on the leaf blades in humid summer weather. it is a sign of nitrogen deficiency. a light feed with a nitrogen rich fertiliser clears it within two weeks. summer maintenance calendar month job march first cut at 50mm, clear debris, check for compaction april spring feed, light scarify, aerate compacted areas may mow weekly at 40mm, edge borders, spot weed june raise mower to 45mm, water deeply in dry spells july mow as needed at 50mm, water deeply if no rain for 10 days august keep mower high, watch for chafer grub damage when to get professional help if your lawn has been failing for several summers in a row despite good maintenance, the problem is almost certainly in the soil. compaction, poor drainage, a thin layer of topsoil over rubble, or a ph problem will defeat any amount of feeding and watering. a professional can do a soil test, check for thatch and compaction, and give you a renovation plan. for lawns that need a full renovation, see our lawn recovery service page. most renovations are best done in early autumn, not spring, so book an assessment in august or september if you think your lawn needs it. frequently asked questions should i water my lawn every day in summer? no. daily light watering encourages shallow roots and makes the lawn weaker. water deeply once a week, applying the equivalent of 25mm of rain, and the roots will grow down to find more moisture. the lawn will be more drought tolerant as a result. why does my lawn go brown in summer even when i water it? usually because the watering is too light and too frequent. it can also be because the soil is compacted, so the water runs off rather than soaking in. aerate the lawn in spring and water deeply once a week. if the lawn is on very shallow topsoil over rubble, no amount of watering will keep it green in a hot summer. is it worth feeding the lawn in summer? a light feed with a slow release summer fertiliser in mid june can help, but avoid high nitrogen feeds after mid may as they produce soft growth that is damaged by drought. if you only feed once a year, do it in early april with a spring fertiliser. when should i stop mowing in summer? you do not need to stop mowing, but you should mow less often and at a higher setting in hot, dry weather. if the grass has stopped growing, do not mow. mowing a stressed lawn weakens it further. final thoughts preparing a lawn for summer is mostly about getting the basics right in spring. feed once in early april, mow at 40mm or higher, and water deeply when the grass shows stress. the lawns that survive summer best are the ones with deep roots, and deep roots come from good spring preparation and correct mowing, not from constant watering. if you would like help with spring lawn preparation, or if your lawn needs a more substantial renovation, get in touch with our team. we work across the chilterns and can give you a realistic assessment of what your lawn needs. further reading why is my lawn full of moss? garden tidy-up checklist before summer how often should a gardener visit? lawn recovery service pear moss weeds weed dandelion dandelions chafer grub ant rat cat dog drought dry compaction compacted poor drainage drainage shade
