autumn lawn care guide for uk gardens autumn is the most important season for uk lawns. our guide covers scarifying, aerating, top dressing, overseeding and autumn feeding for healthy grass. autumn lawn care guide for uk gardens lawn care & lawn repair autumn is when you do the serious work on a uk lawn. the grass is still growing, the soil is warm, and there is time for the lawn to recover before winter sets in. what you do in september and october will largely determine how the lawn looks the following spring and how well it survives the cold months. this guide covers scarifying, aerating, top dressing, overseeding, autumn feeding and leaf management, with a step by step process for a typical family lawn in southern england. quick answer autumn lawn care is about repairing the damage of summer and preparing the grass for winter. the four jobs that matter most are scarifying to remove thatch, aerating to relieve compaction, top dressing to improve the surface, and overseeding to thicken the sward. apply an autumn feed that is low in nitrogen and high in potash. keep the lawn clear of leaves through to the first frost. if you only have time for one job, scarify. removing the dead material that has built up over summer does more for the long term health of the lawn than anything else. why autumn is the best time most uk garden owners think of spring as the main lawn season, but autumn is when the real work happens. in autumn the soil is still warm from summer, but the air is cooler and there is more rain. grass seed germinates reliably, the new roots have months to develop before the stress of summer, and there is less weed competition than in spring. the other reason autumn matters is that the lawn has just come through a summer of mowing, foot traffic, and possibly drought. the thatch layer has built up, the soil is compacted, and there are bare patches. if you leave all this until spring, the lawn goes into winter weak and emerges in march thin and patchy. the autumn window the ideal window for autumn lawn work is mid september to mid october, while the soil is still warm enough for grass seed to germinate. leave it until november and the seed will sit dormant until spring, by which time birds and heavy rain will have removed half of it. the full autumn renovation process step 1: mow the lawn short a few days before you start, mow the lawn slightly shorter than usual, around 30mm. this makes scarifying easier and lets you see what you are doing. do not scalp the lawn, just take it down a notch from the summer height. step 2: scarify to remove thatch thatch is the layer of dead grass, moss and debris that sits at the base of the lawn, just above the soil. a thin layer of thatch, up to 1cm, is fine and helps retain moisture. more than that and it blocks water, air and fertiliser from reaching the soil. for small lawns, use a spring tine rake. for lawns over 50 square metres, hire a powered scarifier for around 60 to 80 pounds per day. set the scarifier to a moderate depth to start. you can always go over again. the aim is to pull out dead material, not to tear up the living grass. a properly scarified lawn looks terrible for a few days. do not panic. a 50 square metre lawn can produce 4 to 6 bin bags of thatch and dead moss. the grass will recover within a fortnight if the weather is mild. step 3: aerate to relieve compaction compaction is the silent killer of lawns. the soil gets compressed by foot traffic, mowing, and summer use, squeezing out the air pockets that roots need. compacted lawns drain badly, root shallowly, and are prone to moss. for small lawns, push a garden fork into the soil 10cm deep every 10cm and rock it back and forth. for larger lawns, hire a hollow tine aerator. hollow tining removes plugs of soil, which is more effective than solid tining. brush a 70/30 sand and soil mix into the holes to keep them open. step 4: top dress to level and improve the surface top dressing is a mix of sandy loam that you spread thinly over the lawn after aerating. it fills the aeration holes, levels minor dips, and improves the soil structure over time. the standard mix is 70 percent sharp sand and 30 percent sandy loam, or 60/30/10 with compost added. apply around 3 to 4kg per square metre, no more than 1cm thick. work it in with the back of a rake or a stiff brush. the grass should still be visible through the dressing. if you bury the grass, it will not recover. step 5: overseed bare and thin patches after scarifying and top dressing, the lawn will look thin. overseed at 20 to 25g per square metre across the whole lawn, or double that rate on bare patches. use a seed mix suited to your conditions. a hard wearing ryegrass mix is good for family lawns, a fescue and bent mix is better for ornamental lawns, and a shade tolerant mix for shaded areas. lightly rake the seed into the top dressing, firm with a roller or by walking on it, and water if no rain is forecast. keep the surface moist until the seed germinates, usually 10 to 14 days in september. step 6: apply an autumn feed autumn feeds are different from spring feeds. they are low in nitrogen, which would produce soft growth that winter frost would damage, and high in potash, which strengthens cell walls and helps the grass resist cold and disease. apply at the rate on the packet when the soil is moist and rain is forecast. do not use a spring and summer feed in autumn. the high nitrogen content will push the grass into soft growth that is exactly what you do not want going into winter. light renovation versus full renovation not every lawn needs the full process above. for a lawn that is basically healthy but a bit tired, a light renovation is enough. this involves a light scarify with a rake, a fork aeration of the worst areas, and an autumn feed. skip the top dressing and overseed only the obviously bare patches. a full renovation is needed if the lawn has more than 20 percent bare patches, more than 1cm of thatch, visible compaction, or heavy moss. for lawns that are more than half moss or have failed over several seasons, see our moss in lawns guide and consider a full returf or reseed. leaf management falling leaves are the single biggest threat to an autumn lawn. a layer of leaves left on the grass for more than a week blocks light, encourages worm activity, and creates the damp, dark conditions that moss loves. in a bad year, a layer of leaves left for a month can kill the grass underneath. clear leaves weekly from late september through to early december. use a leaf blower or a lawn mower with the collector on. the mower chops the leaves and collects them in one pass, which is much faster than raking. add the chopped leaves to the compost heap or use them as leaf mould. beech and oak leaves take longer to break down than most. if you have a lot of these, make a separate leaf mould pile in a corner of the garden. put the leaves in a simple wire mesh cage and leave them for two years. the resulting leaf mould is an excellent soil improver. autumn mowing keep mowing through autumn as long as the grass is growing, but raise the mower gradually. mow at 40mm in september, 45mm in october, and give a final cut at 50mm in november if the weather is mild. the last cut should be when growth has slowed to the point where you are only topping the grass. do not mow when the grass is wet or frosty. wet mowing tears the grass and compacts the soil. frost mowing can break the frozen grass blades and damage the crown of the plant. if the lawn needs tidying in winter, use a brush or leaf blower rather than the mower. last cut of the year the last cut of the year is usually in late october or early november depending on where you are in the uk. in the chilterns, which tend to be a bit colder than the thames valley, the last cut is typically early november. after this, keep off the lawn as much as possible through winter. wet soil warning if your soil is waterlogged after autumn rain, stay off the lawn entirely. walking on a saturated lawn compacts the soil and damages the grass crowns. wait until the surface has drained, even if it means falling behind on leaf clearance. dealing with worm casts worm casts are the small piles of soil that worms leave on the surface of the lawn in autumn and winter. they are a sign of healthy soil, but they are unsightly and can smear when mowed. the simplest solution is to wait for the casts to dry and then sweep them with a stiff brush. do this on a dry morning before they have time to harden. there are no worm killers legally available to home gardeners in the uk, and rightly so. worms are good for your soil. if the casts are a serious problem, reduce the ph slightly by avoiding lime and using acidifying fertilisers such as ammonium sulphate in autumn. this will not eliminate worms but may reduce activity on the surface. autumn lawn problems to watch for leatherjackets leatherjackets are the larvae of crane flies, or daddy longlegs. they feed on grass roots in autumn and can leave the lawn yellow and loose. if you can peel back the turf easily, you have leatherjackets. the biological control with nematodes is effective if applied in autumn while the soil is still warm. see the rhs guidance on leatherjackets . chafer grubs chafer grubs are the larvae of chafer beetles. they are larger than leatherjackets and feed on grass roots in late summer and autumn. birds, particularly crows and magpies, will tear up the lawn looking for them. the damage can be extensive. nematode treatment is effective but timing is critical. fusarium patch fusarium patch, also known as snow mould, appears as small circular patches of pinkish or white dead grass in late autumn and winter. it is encouraged by poor drainage, thatch, and excessive autumn nitrogen. scarletify, aerate, and avoid high nitrogen feeds in autumn. if fusarium appears, lightly brush the dew off the lawn in the morning. autumn lawn care calendar month job september scarify, aerate, top dress, overseed bare patches october apply autumn feed, keep clearing leaves weekly, raise mowing height november last cut of the year, treat for leatherjackets if needed, keep off in wet weather december stay off the lawn, brush worm casts on dry days tools and materials you will need for a full autumn renovation of a 50 square metre lawn you will need: a spring tine rake or powered scarifier, a garden fork or hollow tine aerator, 4 to 5 bags of top dressing (around 150 to 200kg), 1 to 1.5kg of grass seed, a 2kg box of autumn fertiliser, and a stiff brush for working in the top dressing. total material cost for a 50 square metre lawn is around 80 to 120 pounds if you do it yourself. hiring a powered scarifier adds another 60 to 80 pounds per day. professional autumn renovation typically costs 200 to 400 pounds for a similar sized lawn, depending on the work involved. when to call a professional if your lawn has been failing for several years, has heavy moss, deep compaction, or extensive bare patches, a professional renovation is worth it. we have the equipment to do the work quickly and the experience to know how hard to scarify, how much top dressing to apply, and which seed mix to use. see our lawn recovery service page for what we offer. autumn renovation is also worth booking if you are time poor. the work needs to be done in a tight window in september and october, and it is physical work. a professional team can do in a morning what would take a homeowner a full weekend. frequently asked questions can i scarify in spring instead of autumn? you can, but it is not ideal. spring scarifying weakens the grass just as it is trying to grow, and the lawn takes longer to recover. light raking in spring is fine, but heavy scarifying is best done in autumn when the grass has the whole winter to recover. do i need to top dress every year? no. top dressing once every two or three years is enough for most lawns. if your lawn is uneven or has heavy compaction, you may want to top dress annually for the first couple of years, then drop to every other year. will grass seed germinate in october? in a mild autumn, yes. grass seed germinates reliably at soil temperatures above 7 celsius. by mid october the soil is usually still warm enough in southern england, but seed sown in november will probably sit dormant until spring. if in doubt, sow by mid october. what happens if i skip autumn lawn care? the thatch builds up, the soil stays compacted, and the moss and weeds move in. the lawn goes into winter weak and emerges in spring thin and patchy. you will spend the following spring trying to fix problems that would have been easier to fix in autumn. final thoughts autumn is the season when uk lawns are made or broken. the grass is still growing, the soil is warm, and the conditions are right for repair work. a weekend of scarifying, aerating, top dressing and overseeding in september will give you a far better lawn the following spring than any amount of spring work. if you would like help with autumn lawn renovation, get in touch with our team. we work across the chilterns and can advise on what your lawn needs and when to do it. further reading why is my lawn full of moss? garden tidy-up checklist before summer lawn recovery service when to plant trees and shrubs in the uk wondering when to plant trees and shrubs in the uk? our guide covers bare root vs container plants, best seasons for each species, soil prep and aftercare. when to plant trees and shrubs in the uk plants, borders & planting ideas plant a tree at the right time and it will establish in a single season. plant at the wrong time and you will be watering it through its first summer and watching it struggle for years. this guide explains when to plant trees and shrubs in the uk, the difference between bare root and container plants, and how to give newly planted specimens the best start. it is written for gardens on the chalk and clay soils typical of the chilterns. quick answer the best time to plant trees and shrubs in the uk is between november and march, when the plants are dormant. bare root plants must be planted in this window. container grown plants can be planted at any time of year, but planting in autumn or early spring gives the best results. avoid planting in summer unless you can water reliably for the first season. if you only remember one rule, plant deciduous trees and shrubs in late autumn or early winter, when the soil is still warm from summer but the plant is dormant. bare root versus container grown the timing of planting depends mostly on whether the plant is bare root or container grown. these are two different ways of buying and planting, with different windows. bare root plants bare root plants are dug from the field in autumn when they lose their leaves, and sold with no soil around the roots. they are cheaper, often half the price of container grown plants, and they establish quickly because the roots adapt to the new soil from day one. bare root plants are only available from november to march, when the plant is dormant. the downside is that the roots must not dry out. bare root plants should be planted within a few days of delivery, or heeled into a corner of the garden until you are ready. if the roots dry out, the plant will fail. container grown plants container grown plants are sold in pots with their rootball intact. they can be planted at any time of year, but establishment is best in autumn and spring when the soil is moist and the temperatures are mild. container plants are more expensive than bare root, but they are the only option for evergreen specimens and for planting outside the dormant season. container plants have a higher failure rate if planted in summer because the leaves are transpiring moisture faster than the roots can take it up. if you must plant in summer, water every few days for the first season. rootball versus bare root rootballed plants are field grown, dug up with a ball of soil around the roots, and wrapped in hessian. they combine the value of bare root with the flexibility of container grown for larger specimens. rootballs are mainly used for large evergreens such as yew and laurel, and are planted from october to april. the best planting windows october to november: ideal for most plants early to mid autumn is the best planting window for most trees and shrubs in the uk. the soil is still warm from summer, which encourages root growth, but the air has cooled enough that the plant is not losing moisture through its leaves. bare root deciduous plants are just becoming available. planting in october gives the roots two or three months to establish before the really cold weather arrives. by spring, the plant is already settled in and can put its energy into top growth rather than root growth. december to february: dormant season planting mid winter is the heart of the bare root season. plants are fully dormant and can be lifted, transported and planted with minimal stress. the main constraint is the weather. avoid planting when the soil is frozen or waterlogged. frozen soil is impossible to dig properly, and waterlogged soil compacts when you walk on it. if you cannot plant immediately, heel bare root plants into a spare corner of the garden. dig a shallow trench, lay the plants in at a 45 degree angle, cover the roots with soil, and firm in. they will keep for several weeks. march to april: late window for bare root early spring is the last chance to plant bare root specimens before they come into leaf. once the buds start to break, the plant is no longer dormant and bare root planting is risky. container grown plants can still be planted, but you will need to water through the first summer. march is also a good month for planting evergreens, which prefer slightly warmer soil than deciduous plants. wait until the worst of the winter is past before planting yew, holly, box, or laurel. may to september: container only, with watering summer planting is possible with container grown plants but not recommended for anything you cannot water reliably. the plant is in full leaf and losing moisture fast, while the roots are confined to the potting compost in the pot. until the roots grow out into the surrounding soil, the plant is dependent on you for water. for summer planting, water every 3 to 4 days for the first month, then weekly for the rest of the first summer. a thick mulch around the plant helps retain moisture. species specific planting times deciduous trees (oak, beech, birch, rowan, cherry) plant bare root from november to march, container grown at any time but ideally october to april. these are the easiest and most forgiving trees to plant. evergreen trees (yew, holly, pine, fir) plant container grown or rootballed from september to april. avoid mid summer planting as evergreens continue to transpire through their leaves all year. early autumn is ideal. fruit trees (apple, pear, plum, cherry) plant bare root from november to march, on a mild day when the soil is not frozen. container grown fruit trees can be planted at any time but autumn is best. avoid planting in frost pockets or in waterlogged soil. hedges (hawthorn, beech, yew, laurel, privet) plant bare root deciduous hedges from november to march. plant container or rootballed evergreen hedges from september to april. for a long hedge, bare root is significantly cheaper. see our hedge cutting service for aftercare. roses plant bare root roses from november to march, container grown at any time but ideally autumn or early spring. avoid planting in frosty weather. our rose profile has more detail. soil preparation before planting the single biggest factor in whether a tree or shrub establishes is the soil preparation. a plant put into a small hole in compacted soil will struggle for years, no matter when you plant it. dig a planting hole twice the width of the rootball or the spread of the bare roots, and no deeper than the rootball. the top of the rootball should sit at the same level as the surrounding soil, or slightly proud in heavy clay. planting too deep is a common cause of failure, especially with roses and fruit trees. break up the bottom and sides of the hole with a fork to allow roots to penetrate. mix a couple of handfuls of well rotted compost or farmyard manure into the backfill soil. do not add fresh manure or strong fertiliser to the planting hole as it can burn the roots. on heavy clay soils, do not improve the backfill too much. if the planting hole is full of rich compost while the surrounding soil is solid clay, the roots will stay in the planting hole and the plant will never properly establish. improve the wider soil over a few seasons with mulch. planting technique bare root planting soak bare root plants in a bucket of water for an hour before planting. prune any broken or damaged roots with sharp secateurs. place the plant in the hole and spread the roots out. hold the plant at the correct depth, with the nursery soil mark level with the surrounding soil, and backfill with the improved soil. firm gently with your foot as you go, and water in well to settle the soil around the roots. container grown planting water the plant thoroughly in its pot before planting. remove the pot and tease out the roots if they are circling the pot. plant at the same depth as in the pot, backfill, firm gently, and water in. add a stake for trees taller than 1.2 metres. staking trees trees over 1.2 metres need a stake for the first year or two. use a single stake driven in at a 45 degree angle, tied to the tree with a flexible tree tie. the stake should be low, around 60cm above ground, so the trunk can sway in the wind. this swaying encourages the trunk to thicken and the tree to develop a strong root system. remove the stake after 18 months to 2 years. the mulch circle after planting, mulch around the base of the tree or shrub with a 5cm layer of bark or compost, in a circle 60cm wide. keep the mulch clear of the trunk. this suppresses weeds, retains moisture, and protects the soil. refresh annually for the first 3 years. aftercare for the first season the first season after planting is critical. the plant is establishing its root system and is vulnerable to drought. water deeply once a week from spring to autumn for the first year, more often in dry spells. a deep weekly soak is better than a light daily sprinkle. keep a 60cm circle around the plant free of weeds and grass for the first 3 years. grass is a fierce competitor for water and nutrients, and young trees and shrubs lose out. mulch helps suppress weeds and reduces competition. prune only dead, damaged or diseased wood in the first year. resist the urge to shape the plant until it is well established. for shrubs that need pruning to shape, see our guide to when to cut hedges for general timing principles. planting on chilterns chalk the chilterns are predominantly chalk, which is alkaline and shallow in places. this affects what you can plant and how. acid loving plants such as rhododendrons, camellias and pieris will not thrive on chalk without major soil amendment. choose instead from plants that tolerate alkaline conditions: yew, box, holly, beech, hornbeam, lavender, rosemary and most roses. on shallow chalk soils, dig a wider rather than deeper planting hole. the roots will spread sideways rather than down. add organic matter to the backfill and mulch annually. see the rhs guidance on soil types for more on chalk soils. common planting mistakes planting too deep the most common mistake. the top of the rootball should be level with the surrounding soil, never below it. planting too deep causes the bark to rot at soil level and suffocates the roots. not teasing out container roots if the roots have been circling the pot, they will keep circling in the ground. the plant never properly establishes and can blow over in a wind years later. always tease out circling roots before planting, and cut any that are too thick to tease. skimping on the planting hole a hole the same size as the pot is too small. dig twice the width of the rootball, and break up the sides and bottom with a fork. the roots need loose soil to grow into. forgetting to water in the first summer even container grown plants planted in autumn need watering in their first summer. the roots have not yet spread out into the surrounding soil and the plant is dependent on you. water weekly from may to september in the first year. planting calendar quick reference plant type best planting time acceptable time bare root deciduous november to february october, march container deciduous october to november december to april container evergreen september to october november to april rootballed evergreen october to november september, march to april bare root rose november to february october, march fruit tree (bare root) november to march october, april frequently asked questions can i plant a tree in summer? you can, if it is container grown, but it is not ideal. summer planted trees need watering every few days for the first season, and the failure rate is higher. if you must plant in summer, choose a cool overcast day, water thoroughly, and mulch heavily. how late in spring can i plant bare root? up to about mid march in southern england, depending on the weather. once the buds are breaking and the plant is coming into leaf, it is too late. bare root plants in leaf rarely establish well. should i add fertiliser to the planting hole? no, not directly. a couple of handfuls of well rotted compost mixed into the backfill is fine. strong fertiliser in the planting hole can burn the new roots. apply a general fertiliser as a top dressing in the second spring after planting. how big should the planting hole be? twice the width of the rootball or the spread of the bare roots, and no deeper than the rootball. the plant should sit at the same depth as it was in the nursery, with the top of the rootball level with the surrounding soil. final thoughts planting trees and shrubs at the right time, in a properly prepared hole, gives them the best chance of establishing quickly and thriving for decades. the work of digging the hole is more important than the work of planting the plant. a weekend spent on soil preparation in autumn will save years of struggling specimens. if you are planning a larger planting project, such as a new hedge or a small orchard, get in touch with our team. we can advise on species choice for the chilterns, supply plants at trade prices, and handle the planting. further reading when to cut hedges in the uk low maintenance plants for the chilterns yew plant profile soft landscaping service lavender rosemary camellia rhododendron rose roses rowan mulching guide: benefits, types and how to apply complete uk mulching guide covering benefits, types (bark, compost, gravel, manure, leaf mould), when and how to apply, costs and common mistakes to avoid. mulching guide: benefits, types and how to apply soil, compost & mulch mulch is the single most useful thing you can do for your garden soil. a layer of organic material on the surface suppresses weeds, retains moisture, regulates soil temperature, and feeds the soil life that in turn feeds your plants. this guide covers the benefits of mulching, the main types available in the uk, when and how to apply them, the costs involved, and the common mistakes to avoid. it is written for gardeners on the chalk and clay soils typical of the chilterns. quick answer mulching means covering the soil surface with a layer of material, usually organic. the main benefits are weed suppression, moisture retention, soil temperature regulation, and slow feeding of the soil. apply a 5cm layer of organic mulch in late winter or early spring, after weeding but before the spring growth spurt. top up annually. the best mulch depends on what you are mulching and what you want to achieve. if you only do one thing to improve your garden soil, mulch it. every year. for a decade. the transformation is gradual but the result is dramatic. why mulch matters in nature, the soil is rarely bare. leaves fall, plants die back, and a layer of organic material sits on the surface, slowly breaking down and feeding the soil. in a garden, we rake up leaves, pull out dead growth, and leave the soil exposed. mulching simply restores the natural layer that the soil is missing. the benefits of mulching are well documented. a 5cm layer of organic mulch reduces water loss from the soil by up to 70 percent, suppresses annual weeds by 90 percent, and can increase earthworm activity threefold within two years. earthworms are the engine of healthy soil, mixing organic matter down into the root zone and creating drainage channels. the five year test if you mulch a bed every spring for five years, you will not need to dig it. the soil will be dark, crumbly, and full of worms. plant roots will run freely through it. weeds will pull out easily. this is the goal of mulching: a self maintaining soil that does not need to be dug or fed with chemical fertilisers. the main benefits of mulching weed suppression a 5cm layer of mulch blocks the light that annual weed seeds need to germinate. seeds landing on the surface either fail to germinate or grow weakly and can be pulled out with two fingers. perennial weeds such as bindweed and couch grass will come through mulch, so dig these out before mulching. moisture retention mulch acts as a blanket over the soil, reducing evaporation. in a dry summer, mulched soil stays moist for a week longer than bare soil. this means less watering, less drought stress, and healthier plants. the moisture retention benefit alone is worth the cost of the mulch in most uk gardens. soil temperature regulation mulch keeps the soil cooler in summer and warmer in winter. the roots of most plants prefer stable temperatures. a 5cm mulch can keep the soil 2 to 3 celsius cooler in summer, which makes a real difference to shallow rooted plants such as rhododendrons and azaleas. soil improvement organic mulches break down over a year or two, adding organic matter to the top layer of the soil. this improves soil structure, feeds earthworms and other soil life, and slowly releases nutrients to the plants. the effect is cumulative: mulch for a few years and the soil becomes darker, richer and easier to work. erosion control on slopes or in areas of heavy rain, mulch prevents soil erosion. rain hits the mulch rather than the bare soil, and the mulch slows the water as it runs off. this is particularly useful on newly planted banks and in exposed gardens. types of mulch bark mulch bark is the most popular mulch in the uk. it is clean, attractive, slow to break down, and relatively cheap. landscaping bark, sold in 50 or 100 litre bags, costs 6 to 10 pounds per bag and covers about 2 square metres at 5cm depth. composted bark is darker and breaks down faster, feeding the soil more quickly. bark is best for ornamental beds and around trees and shrubs. avoid using it on vegetable beds because it ties up nitrogen as it breaks down, and do not use it around low growing plants such as strawberries where it can harbour slugs. garden compost home made compost is the best all round mulch if you have enough of it. it is free, full of soil life, and breaks down to feed the soil directly. the downside is that most gardens do not produce enough compost to mulch everything. use it on the beds that need it most: vegetable beds, hungry feeders such as roses, and newly planted specimens. a rough guide is that a 1 cubic metre compost bin produces enough compost to mulch about 5 square metres at 5cm depth. most gardens need 4 to 6 bins to be self sufficient in compost. farmyard manure well rotted farmyard manure, usually horse or cow, is a rich mulch for hungry feeders. it must be at least two years old, dark and crumbly, with no smell. fresh manure will burn plant roots and encourage soft growth that is damaged by frost. manure is ideal for roses, fruit trees, vegetable beds, and heavy feeders such as dahlias and delphiniums. apply in late winter or early spring. a 50 litre bag costs 5 to 8 pounds and covers about 1.5 square metres. leaf mould leaf mould is made by composting autumn leaves on their own for two years. the result is a dark, crumbly material that is excellent as a mulch and soil improver. it is not rich in nutrients but it improves soil structure dramatically and is free if you have deciduous trees. make leaf mould by putting leaves in a wire mesh cage or black plastic bags with holes punched in. leave for two years. oak and beech leaves make the best leaf mould. pine needles make an acidic leaf mould that is perfect for mulching acid loving plants. gravel and stone gravel and stone mulches are non organic, so they do not feed the soil, but they suppress weeds and retain moisture. they are useful for mediterranean style plantings, alpine beds, and around plants that hate winter wet such as lavender and rosemary. lay a weed suppressing membrane under the gravel to prevent weeds coming through. a 5cm layer of 10mm gravel over membrane costs about 8 to 12 pounds per square metre including the membrane. mushroom compost spent mushroom compost is a cheap, slightly alkaline mulch that is excellent for clay soils. it contains chalk and breaks down heavy clay over a few seasons. avoid using it on acid loving plants such as rhododendrons and camellias. a 50 litre bag costs 4 to 6 pounds. cocoa shells cocoa shells are a byproduct of chocolate manufacturing. they make an attractive dark mulch that smells of chocolate and breaks down slowly. they are expensive but look good in ornamental beds. avoid if you have dogs, as cocoa can be toxic to them. mulch comparison table mulch type cost per sq m lifespan best for landscaping bark 3 to 5 pounds 2 to 3 years ornamental beds, trees, shrubs garden compost free 1 year veg beds, roses, hungry feeders farmyard manure 3 to 5 pounds 1 year roses, fruit, veg beds leaf mould free 1 to 2 years soil improvement, woodland plants gravel over membrane 8 to 12 pounds 10+ years mediterranean plants, alpines mushroom compost 2 to 4 pounds 1 year clay soil, veg beds when to mulch the best time to mulch is late winter or early spring, after the worst of the winter but before the spring growth spurt. late february to mid march is ideal in southern england. the soil is warming up, weeds have not yet germinated, and the mulch will lock in the winter moisture that the soil is holding. a second mulching window is in autumn, after leaf fall. autumn mulching protects the soil from winter rain, insulates plant roots from frost, and gives the worms time to work the mulch into the soil over winter. avoid mulching in summer when the soil is dry. mulch on dry soil acts as a barrier that prevents rain reaching the roots. water the soil thoroughly before mulching in summer, or wait until autumn. how to apply mulch step 1: weed thoroughly mulch suppresses annual weeds but does not kill perennial weeds. dig out bindweed, couch grass, dandelions and ground elder before mulching. if you mulch over perennial weeds, they will come through and you will have wasted your money. step 2: water if dry if the soil is dry, water it thoroughly before mulching. the mulch will lock in the moisture. if you mulch dry soil, it will stay dry under the mulch until you water it enough to wet the mulch and the soil beneath. step 3: apply the mulch apply a 5cm layer of mulch across the bed. for trees and shrubs, mulch a circle at least 60cm wide around the base. do not heap the mulch against the trunk or stem, as this can cause rotting. keep a 5cm gap clear around the base of each plant. step 4: top up annually organic mulches break down over a year or two. top up annually with a 2cm layer to maintain the 5cm depth. after 3 or 4 years, you may need to dig the residual mulch into the soil before applying a fresh thick layer. the mulch volcano mistake never pile mulch up against the trunk of a tree in a volcano shape. this is a common mistake that causes bark rot, encourages fungal disease, and provides a home for rodents that may gnaw the bark. keep the mulch flat and clear of the trunk by at least 5cm. mulching specific situations roses and shrubs mulch roses and shrubs with well rotted manure or compost in early spring, after pruning. apply a 5cm layer in a 60cm circle around each plant. this feeds the plant, retains moisture, and reduces blackspot by preventing rain from splashing soil onto the leaves. see our rose profile for more on rose care. vegetable beds mulch vegetable beds with compost or manure in late winter, before sowing. this can be left on the surface or dug in lightly. for growing crops, mulch between rows with grass clippings or compost to suppress weeds and retain moisture. avoid bark on vegetable beds. trees and hedges mulch newly planted trees and hedges with bark or compost in a 60cm circle for the first 3 years. this keeps grass away from the young roots and dramatically improves establishment. see our guide to hedge cutting for related care. borders mulch ornamental borders in late winter with bark or compost. the mulch suppresses spring weeds and locks in winter moisture. pull the mulch back from emerging bulbs and perennials so they are not smothered. how much mulch do you need mulch is sold by volume, usually in litres or cubic metres. to cover a bed to 5cm depth, you need 50 litres per square metre, or 0.05 cubic metres. a 10 square metre bed needs 500 litres, or half a cubic metre. bulk bags of mulch are 1 cubic metre and cost 80 to 150 pounds depending on the type. they cover 20 square metres at 5cm depth. smaller bags of 50 to 100 litres are available at garden centres but work out 2 to 3 times more expensive per litre. common mulching mistakes mulching too thin a 2cm layer of mulch looks nice but does almost nothing. weeds come through, moisture still evaporates, and the soil does not improve. apply 5cm minimum, 7cm if you can afford it. mulching over weeds perennial weeds will come through any depth of mulch. dig them out first. if you have a serious infestation, lay cardboard under the mulch to smother them. cardboard breaks down in a year and is an effective weed barrier. using the wrong mulch mushroom compost on rhododendrons will slowly kill them. bark on vegetable beds ties up nitrogen. cocoa shells where dogs can reach them can poison them. match the mulch to the situation. not watering first mulch on dry soil keeps the soil dry. always water thoroughly before mulching, or wait until after rain. see the rhs guidance on mulches for more on choosing and applying mulches. mulching on a budget if you cannot afford to mulch the whole garden, prioritise. newly planted trees and shrubs benefit most, because they are establishing their roots. roses and other heavy feeders come second. established shrubs and perennials in good soil can wait. the cheapest mulch is what you can make yourself. a compost bin or two, a leaf mould pile, and a small area of comfrey or nettles for liquid feed will give you a steady supply of free mulch material. see our low maintenance plants guide for ideas on plants that need less mulching. frequently asked questions can i use grass clippings as mulch? yes, but with care. a thin layer, 1 to 2cm, is fine around established plants. a thick layer of grass clippings forms a slimy mat that excludes air and smells. add grass clippings in thin layers, mixing with dry material if possible. never use clippings from a lawn treated with weedkiller. how often should i mulch? top up organic mulches annually to maintain a 5cm depth. after 3 to 4 years, the residual mulch may have built up enough to need digging in. gravel and stone mulches do not need topping up. will mulch attract slugs? some mulches, particularly grass clippings and bark, can harbour slugs. on the plus side, they also harbour ground beetles and other predators that eat slugs. if slugs are a serious problem, use a coarser mulch such as gravel around vulnerable plants. see our hosta profile for slug control on susceptible plants. is it ok to mulch in summer? only if you water the soil thoroughly first. mulch on dry soil prevents rain reaching the roots. if the soil is already moist, summer mulch will help retain that moisture. if the soil is dry, water it deeply before mulching. final thoughts mulching is the single most useful thing you can do for your garden soil. it suppresses weeds, retains moisture, regulates temperature, and feeds the soil life that feeds your plants. the cost is modest, the work is light, and the benefits accumulate over years. if you do one thing in your garden each spring, mulch. if you would like help sourcing mulch in bulk, or with applying mulch across a larger garden, get in touch with our team. we can supply and apply mulch across the chilterns area. further reading low maintenance plants for the chilterns why is my lawn full of moss? common uk garden pests and how to control them practical uk guide to the most common garden pests: aphids, slugs, vine weevils, lily beetle, box moth and more. covers identification, prevention and control. common uk garden pests and how to control them garden problems & weed control every uk garden has pests. the aim is not to eliminate them, which is impossible, but to keep them at a level where the plants can cope. this guide covers the most common garden pests in the uk, how to identify them, the damage they do, and the practical control methods that work. it is written for gardeners who would rather not reach for chemicals first, and who want to encourage the predators that do much of the work for them. quick answer the most common uk garden pests are aphids, slugs and snails, vine weevils, lily beetle, box tree moth caterpillars, and winter moth caterpillars. for most of these, a combination of hand picking, encouraging predators, and physical barriers gives good control. chemical controls are a last resort, used when the infestation is serious and other methods have failed. the single most effective thing you can do is encourage birds, frogs, hedgehogs and beneficial insects into your garden. if you only do one thing, stop using broad spectrum insecticides. they kill the predators as well as the pests, and the pests bounce back faster. an integrated approach to pest control pest control in a garden is not about eliminating pests. it is about keeping them below the level where they cause serious damage. a garden with no pests is a garden with no pest predators, and the first pest that arrives will run riot. the aim is a balance, where predators and pests keep each other in check. the most effective pest control in any uk garden is a healthy population of birds, frogs, toads, hedgehogs, ground beetles, ladybirds, lacewings and hoverflies. these predators do most of the work for free, if you give them the habitat they need. a garden with a pond, a hedge, a wild corner, and a bird feeder will have far fewer pest problems than a sterile lawn and paving garden. the 10 percent rule if less than 10 percent of your plants are affected by a pest, do nothing. the predators will find the pest and deal with it. hand pick if you must, but do not spray. most pest outbreaks in a balanced garden never reach the level where they cause real damage. aphids aphids, also known as greenfly and blackfly, are small sap sucking insects that cluster on the soft growing tips of plants. they are the most common pest in uk gardens, affecting roses, broad beans, runner beans, dahlias, nasturtiums, fruit trees and many ornamental shrubs. identification aphids are 1 to 3mm long, soft bodied, and usually green or black but sometimes pink, brown or grey. they cluster on the undersides of leaves and on growing tips. the leaves may be curled, distorted, or sticky with honeydew, which is the sugary waste that aphids excrete. black sooty mould often grows on the honeydew. damage aphids weaken the plant by sucking sap, distort new growth, and can transmit virus diseases between plants. on roses they cause curled leaves and poor flowering. on beans they can reduce the crop. on tomatoes and cucumbers in the greenhouse they can transmit viruses. control hand pick or squash: for small infestations, simply rubbing the aphids off with your fingers or gloved hand is effective. blast with water: a strong jet from the hose dislodges aphids and they rarely climb back up. encourage ladybirds and hoverflies: both are voracious aphid predators. plant marigolds, poached egg plant, and other flat flowered annuals to attract them. soapy water spray: a teaspoon of washing up liquid in a litre of water, sprayed onto the aphids, kills them by suffocation. biological control: in greenhouses, parasitic wasps such as aphidius can be bought and released. slugs and snails slugs and snails are the number one pest in most uk gardens, particularly in wet years and on heavy soils. they eat the leaves and shoots of a wide range of plants, with hostas, dahlias, delphiniums, salad crops and seedlings being particular favourites. identification slugs and snails are easy to identify by the slimy trails they leave behind. the damage is irregular holes in leaves, often starting from the edge, and entire seedlings eaten off at the base. check under pots, boards, and at the base of plants in the early morning. control hand picking: go out at dusk or dawn with a torch and pick them off by hand. drop them into soapy water. repeat nightly for a week and you will dramatically reduce the population. beer traps: sink a small container of beer into the soil. slugs fall in and drown. empty and refill every few days. copper barriers: copper tape around pots gives slugs a mild electric shock and deters them. encourage predators: frogs, toads, hedgehogs, and ground beetles all eat slugs. a pond is the best thing you can do for natural slug control. nematodes: the biological control nemaslug contains nematodes that parasitise slugs. apply to moist soil in spring and autumn. pellets: ferric phosphate pellets are the only slug pellets safe for wildlife and pets. avoid metaldehyde pellets, which are now banned in the uk. see our hosta profile for specific advice on protecting hostas from slug damage. vine weevils vine weevils are among the most destructive pests in uk gardens, particularly in containers. the adult weevils eat notches out of the edges of leaves, but the real damage is done by the grubs, which eat the roots of plants and can kill a mature specimen in months. identification adult vine weevils are black, 8 to 10mm long, with a distinctive snout. they cannot fly but walk at night. the notches in leaves are a clear sign. the grubs are white, c shaped, with brown heads, found in the soil around the roots of affected plants. plants affected vine weevils particularly affect plants in containers: rhododendrons, azaleas, camellias, heucheras, primulas, fuchsias, and strawberries. they also attack plants in the open ground but are less obvious there. control adult trapping: go out at night with a torch and pick adult weevils off the plants. drop into soapy water. nematodes: the biological control nemasys contains nematodes that kill vine weevil grubs. apply to moist soil in august and september when the grubs are small. repotting: if a container plant is affected, knock it out of the pot, wash the roots, and repot in fresh compost. the grubs are in the old compost. chemical control: provado vine weevil killer (acetamiprid) is the main chemical option for containers. use sparingly as it affects bees. lily beetle the lily beetle is a bright red beetle that attacks lilies and fritillaries. both the adults and the larvae eat the leaves, and a serious infestation can defoliate a plant completely. identification adult lily beetles are 6 to 8mm long, bright red above and black below. they are slow moving and drop off the leaf when disturbed, often landing on their backs to expose the black underside. the larvae are orange, soft bodied, and cover themselves in their own excrement, which makes them look like small brown blobs. control hand picking: the most effective control. check lilies and fritillaries weekly from april. hold a container of soapy water under the leaf before disturbing the beetle, so it drops in. crushing larvae: the larvae are unpleasant to handle because of their excrement covering, but squashing them is effective. use a stick or wear gloves. chemical control: for serious infestations, a pyrethrum or neem oil spray is effective and less harmful to bees than synthetic insecticides. box tree moth the box tree moth is a relatively new pest in the uk, first recorded in 2008 and now widespread across southern england. the caterpillars defoliate box plants and can kill a mature hedge in a single season. see the rhs guidance on box tree caterpillar for more detail. identification the adult moth is white with a brown border, but you are more likely to see the caterpillars. these are green and yellow, up to 4cm long, with black stripes and black spots. they hide in webbing among the box leaves. the first sign is usually patches of pale leaves and webbing, then defoliation. control pheromone traps: hang a pheromone trap in late april to catch male moths and reduce mating. this also tells you when to look for caterpillars. hand picking: remove the caterpillars and webbing by hand. best done in late afternoon when the caterpillars are active. bacillus thuringiensis: a biological spray that kills caterpillars but is harmless to other wildlife. available as a bacterial spray. replacement: if your box is repeatedly devastated, consider replacing with alternative evergreens such as yew or japanese holly. winter moth caterpillars winter moth caterpillars are the small green caterpillars that appear in spring on apple, pear, cherry and plum trees. they eat the leaves and the blossom, and can seriously reduce the fruit crop. identification the caterpillars are pale green with yellow stripes, up to 2cm long. they hang from the tree on silk threads when disturbed. the damage is holes in the leaves and damaged blossom in april and may. control grease bands: wrap a grease band around the trunk of the tree in october to catch the wingless female moths as they climb up to lay eggs. this is highly effective. hand picking: for small trees, pick off the caterpillars by hand in april. bacillus thuringiensis: a biological spray for serious infestations on larger trees. other common pests cabbage white butterfly caterpillars the caterpillars of the large and small cabbage white butterflies eat the leaves of brassicas. control by hand picking, by covering plants with fine netting to prevent egg laying, or by spraying with bacillus thuringiensis. rose slug sawfly the larvae of the rose slug sawfly eat the surface of rose leaves, leaving a skeletonised appearance. control by hand picking or by spraying with soapy water. red spider mite tiny mites that suck sap from the underside of leaves, particularly in greenhouses. the leaves develop a mottled appearance and fine webbing. increase humidity to discourage them, and use a biological control with the predatory mite phytoseiulus in greenhouses. capsid bugs small green bugs that suck sap from the growing tips of many plants, leaving distorted leaves with small holes. control by weeding around the plants to remove overwintering sites, and by tolerating the damage on established plants. ants ants do not directly damage plants but they farm aphids for their honeydew, protecting them from predators. control ants by pouring boiling water on nests in paving, or by using ant bait stations. see our summer tidy checklist for general pest management. pest control calendar month pest active action january winter moth apply grease bands to fruit trees march aphids, slugs encourage predators, set beer traps april vine weevil adults, lily beetle hand pick at night, check lilies weekly may aphids, cabbage white, box moth hand pick, net brassicas, hang pheromone traps june lily beetle, rose slug sawfly hand pick, spray with soapy water july vine weevil grubs, red spider mite apply nematodes to containers august vine weevil grubs, box moth second generation continue nematode treatment, hand pick september slugs active again hand pick, set beer traps encouraging pest predators the single most effective pest control in any garden is a healthy population of predators. here are the most useful and how to encourage them. birds blue tits and great tits are voracious caterpillar eaters, feeding their chicks on thousands of them through the spring. put up nest boxes, provide food in winter, and provide water. avoid using any insecticides during the breeding season. frogs and toads frogs and toads eat slugs, snails and other pests. a wildlife pond, even a small one, will attract them. give them somewhere to hide during the day, such as a log pile or a stone pile in a shady corner. hedgehogs hedgehogs eat slugs, beetles and caterpillars. provide access to your garden via a small gap in the fence, a hedgehog house, and avoid using slug pellets and insecticides. hedgehog numbers have collapsed in the uk, so every garden that welcomes them helps. ground beetles ground beetles are nocturnal predators that eat slugs, vine weevil grubs, and other soil pests. encourage them with a log pile, a wild corner with long grass, and minimal use of insecticides. hoverflies and lacewings both have aphid eating larvae. the adults feed on nectar from flat flowered plants such as marigolds, poached egg plant, fennel, dill, and yarrow. plant a row of these among your vegetables. the predator garden a garden designed for predators has: a pond of any size, a hedge rather than a fence, a wild corner with long grass and nettles, a log pile, bird boxes and bird feeders, and a wide range of flowering plants including many native species. with these in place, most pest problems solve themselves. when to use chemical controls chemical controls are a last resort, used when the infestation is serious, other methods have failed, and the plant is at risk. when you do use chemicals, choose the most targeted option and apply it carefully. avoid broad spectrum insecticides such as those containing lambda cyhalothrin, which kill bees and other beneficial insects as well as the target pest. choose targeted products and apply in the evening when bees are not flying. never spray open flowers. the range of chemicals available to home gardeners has shrunk dramatically in recent years, as the eu and uk regulators have withdrawn products that harm bees and other wildlife. this is a good thing. the remaining products are largely biological or botanical, and they work well when used correctly. frequently asked questions are slug pellets safe for wildlife? ferric phosphate pellets are safe for wildlife and pets when used as directed. metaldehyde pellets, the older type, are now banned in the uk because they poisoned wildlife and pets. if you use slug pellets, choose ferric phosphate and use them sparingly. how do i get rid of aphids on my roses? start by squashing them by hand or blasting them off with the hose. encourage ladybirds and hoverflies by planting flat flowered annuals nearby. if the infestation is serious, spray with soapy water or a pyrethrum based spray in the evening. what is eating my hostas? almost certainly slugs or snails. check the underside of the leaves and the soil around the plant at dusk. hand pick, set beer traps, and apply nematodes in spring. see our hosta profile for full details. why are there so many caterpillars on my box hedge? this is box tree moth caterpillar, a serious pest of box. pick them off by hand, remove the webbing, and spray with bacillus thuringiensis. hang a pheromone trap in spring to monitor for the moth. if the box is repeatedly defoliated, consider replacing with yew or japanese holly. should i use biological controls? for specific pests in specific situations, biological controls are very effective. nematodes for slugs and vine weevils, parasitic wasps for greenhouse aphids, and bacillus thuringiensis for caterpillars are all worth trying. they are more expensive than chemicals but are targeted and wildlife friendly. final thoughts pest control in a garden is about balance, not elimination. a garden with no pests is a garden with no predators, and the first pest to arrive will run riot. the aim is to build a garden where predators do most of the work, where pests are kept at a manageable level, and where chemicals are a rare last resort. it takes a few years to build this balance. start with a pond, a wild corner, and a ban on broad spectrum insecticides. add bird boxes, a log pile, and a wide range of flowers. within three or four years you will find that pest problems have become far less frequent and far less serious. further reading hostas plant profile roses plant profile summer tidy how to water your garden during uk droughts practical uk guide to watering your garden during droughts. covers when and how to water, water butts, mulching, drought tolerant plants and hosepipe bans. how to water your garden during uk droughts regular garden maintenance uk summers are getting drier, and hosepipe bans are now a regular feature of garden life in southern england. the good news is that most gardens need far less watering than their owners think, and a few simple changes can dramatically reduce the amount of water you use. this guide covers when and how to water, how to store water, how to keep moisture in the soil, and which plants to choose for a garden that copes with dry spells. it is written for gardens on the chalk and clay soils of the chilterns. quick answer most uk gardens are over watered. established trees and shrubs rarely need watering, lawns do not need watering to survive summer, and even vegetable beds need less than you think. water deeply once a week rather than lightly every day, water in the early morning or evening, mulch heavily to retain moisture, and choose drought tolerant plants for dry spots. install as many water butts as you can fit, and you will rarely need a hose. if you only change one thing, water less often but more deeply. deep weekly watering encourages deep roots and drought tolerant plants. light daily watering does the opposite. why uk gardens need less water than you think the uk climate, even in southern england, provides enough rainfall for most gardens in most years. the problem is that the rain falls in winter, when plants are not actively growing, and the summers are getting drier. plants that are suited to the climate, planted correctly, and mulched properly can cope with most dry spells without supplementary watering. the reason most gardens need so much watering is that they are not set up to cope. the soil is compacted, the mulch is absent, the plants are in small pots or are shallow rooted, and the watering is done badly. fix these things and your water use will drop dramatically. the survival rule an established garden in southern england needs about 25mm of water per week in summer to thrive, and half that to survive. that is the equivalent of 25 litres per square metre to thrive, 12 litres to survive. a 100 square metre garden needs 1,000 to 2,500 litres per week in a dry summer. a single water butt holds 200 litres. when to water the best time to water is early morning, before the sun is high. the soil is cool, the water soaks in, and the leaves dry quickly, reducing the risk of fungal disease. the second best time is evening, after the sun has gone down. watering in the heat of the day wastes up to 50 percent of the water to evaporation. water when the soil needs it, not on a fixed schedule. push your finger into the soil. if it is dry 5cm down, water. if it is still damp, wait. most plants can cope with the top 5cm of soil being dry, because their roots are deeper. signs that a plant needs water: leaves are wilting in the morning, not just in the heat of the day leaves are darker than usual or take on a blue grey tint the soil is hard and cracked at the surface plants in pots are lighter than usual when lifted how to water the way you water matters more than how much you water. a light daily sprinkle encourages shallow roots and makes the plant dependent on you. a deep weekly soak encourages deep roots and drought tolerance. water the soil, not the leaves direct the water at the base of the plant, not over the foliage. wet leaves encourage fungal disease and waste water to evaporation. use a watering can with a rose removed, or a hose with the water turned down to a trickle. water deeply a deep watering wets the soil to 15 to 20cm depth, where the roots are. this encourages the roots to grow down to find moisture. a shallow watering wets only the top 2 to 3cm, where the roots stay, and the soil dries out again within a day. water slowly if you apply water faster than the soil can absorb it, the water runs off and is wasted. use a watering can, a soaker hose, or a hose with a trigger gun on a low setting. ponds of water on the surface mean you are watering too fast. use grey water where possible bath water, shower water, and vegetable washing water can all be used on the garden. use it on ornamental plants rather than vegetables, and avoid water with strong detergents or bleach. a simple diverter on your bath or shower downpipe can direct water to a water butt. how much to water as a rough guide, apply 25 litres per square metre per week to vegetables and containers in dry weather, and 12 litres per square metre per week to established borders and lawns. this is the equivalent of 25mm of rain per week. to measure how much you are applying, place an empty tuna tin or similar shallow container under the sprinkler. when it has 25mm of water in it, you have watered enough. this typically takes 30 to 60 minutes with a sprinkler, depending on water pressure. containers need more water than beds, because they have a small soil volume and dry out fast. a 30cm pot of geraniums in full sun may need watering every day in a hot spell. a 60cm pot of the same plant may need watering every 3 days. move containers to shadier spots during heatwaves. storing water water butts are the simplest and most cost effective way to store water for the garden. a standard 200 litre water butt costs 30 to 50 pounds and fills from a single downpipe during one decent rainfall. a house with a 50 square metre roof can fill 10 water butts in a typical winter. choosing a water butt for most gardens, a 200 to 250 litre butt is a good size. larger butts of 500 to 1,000 litres are available but are bulky. if you have space, fit several smaller butts rather than one large one, as this gives you more flexibility and a backup if one fails. installing a water butt install a water butt on a stand at every downpipe. the stand raises the butt high enough to get a watering can under the tap. cut the downpipe and fit a diverter, which directs water to the butt until it is full, then sends the rest down the drain. always fit a lid to keep out leaves, debris and children. connecting multiple butts if you want more storage, connect two or more butts in series with a short length of hose near the top. when the first butt is full, water flows into the second. this is a cheap way to add storage without needing a larger single butt. calculate your roof catchment for every square metre of roof, you can collect about 600 litres of water per year in southern england. a typical semi detached house has 60 to 80 square metres of roof, so the potential is 36,000 to 48,000 litres per year. even in a dry year, that is plenty for the garden if you store it. keeping moisture in the soil the best way to reduce watering is to keep the moisture that is already in the soil. mulch is the single most effective tool for this. a 5cm layer of organic mulch reduces water loss from the soil by up to 70 percent, which means you water 70 percent less. apply mulch in late winter or early spring, when the soil is wet from winter rain. the mulch locks in that moisture for the growing season. top up annually. see our summer tidy checklist for seasonal mulching reminders. other ways to keep moisture in the soil: improve the soil with organic matter: compost and manure act like a sponge, holding water in the soil. avoid hoeing in dry weather: hoeing opens the soil surface and lets moisture escape. weed by hand in dry spells. plant in groups rather than singly: the leaves shade the soil and reduce evaporation. provide shade: a temporary shade net over salad crops reduces their water need by half. drought tolerant plants the cheapest way to reduce watering is to choose plants that do not need it. drought tolerant plants have deep roots, silver or grey leaves, small leaves, or waxy leaves, all of which reduce water loss. silver and grey leaved plants the silver colour comes from tiny hairs on the leaf surface, which reflect sunlight and trap a layer of moist air next to the leaf. lavender, rosemary, sage, artemisia, stachys, and caryopteris are all drought tolerant once established. mediterranean herbs thyme, oregano, marjoram, sage, rosemary, and lavender all thrive in dry, sunny conditions on well drained soil. they are ideal for the chalk soils of the chilterns. plant them in the driest, sunniest spots in the garden. ornamental grasses most ornamental grasses are deep rooted and drought tolerant. stipa, miscanthus, pennisetum, and festuca all do well in dry conditions. they look good in late summer when other plants are struggling. established trees and shrubs most established trees and shrubs do not need watering. their roots go deep enough to find moisture. newly planted specimens need watering for the first summer only, then they are on their own. see our lavender profile and rosemary profile for two of the best drought tolerant shrubs. the rhs guidance on drought resistant gardening has a useful plant list. plants that need regular water avoid these in dry spots: hostas, astilbes, primulas, rodgersias, gunnera, hydrangeas (except in shade), and most moisture loving plants. if you must grow them, plant in shade and mulch heavily. see our hydrangea profile for more on these. watering different parts of the garden lawns most uk lawns do not need watering to survive summer. the grass goes brown and dormant in dry weather, but the crown and roots stay alive. when the rain returns, the lawn greens up within a week or two. if you must water, do it deeply once a week, the equivalent of 25mm of rain. vegetable beds vegetables need more water than ornamental plants, particularly when fruiting. tomatoes, runner beans, courgettes, and pumpkins all need consistent water to crop well. water deeply once or twice a week. water at the base of the plants, not over the leaves. containers containers are the highest maintenance part of the garden in a drought. they dry out fast and need daily watering in hot weather. add water retaining gel to the compost, group pots together to create a humid microclimate, and move pots to shade during heatwaves. greenhouses greenhouses need careful watering because the temperature is high and the plants cannot get water from the surrounding soil. drip irrigation on a timer is the most reliable approach. open the vents and door on hot days to reduce the temperature and the water demand. new plantings newly planted trees, shrubs, and perennials need watering through their first summer. water deeply once a week from may to september, more often in hot spells. after the first year, most established plants can fend for themselves. hosepipe bans hosepipe bans are imposed by water companies during serious droughts, typically in southern and eastern england. during a ban, you cannot use a hosepipe to water a garden, wash a car, or fill a paddling pool. the fine for breaking a ban is up to 1,000 pounds. you can still water your garden during a hosepipe ban, but you must use a watering can filled from a tap or a water butt. this is much slower than a hose, so prioritise. newly planted specimens, containers, and vegetables come first. lawns and established shrubs can wait. plan ahead for hosepipe bans. install water butts in winter, mulch in spring, and choose drought tolerant plants for the dry spots. a garden set up this way will sail through a hosepipe ban with minimal damage. drought watering priorities priority what to water frequency 1 containers and hanging baskets daily in hot weather 2 newly planted trees and shrubs (first summer) weekly, more in heat 3 fruiting vegetables (tomatoes, beans, courgettes) twice weekly 4 leafy vegetables (salads, spinach) twice weekly 5 greenhouse crops daily monitoring 6 established ornamental beds only in extended drought 7 lawns do not water unless essential signs your plants are stressed by drought plants show drought stress in stages. catching it early means a single watering is enough. leaving it late means the plant takes weeks to recover. early signs leaves take on a slightly darker or blue grey tint. growth slows. the plant looks slightly dull compared to a well watered plant of the same type. middle signs leaves wilt in the heat of the day but recover overnight. flower buds drop without opening. fruit develops slowly or drops. late signs leaves wilt and do not recover overnight. leaves turn brown at the edges. the plant drops leaves to reduce water loss. recovery from this stage takes weeks. watering myths myth: watering in the sun scorches the leaves this is mostly a myth. water droplets on leaves do not act as magnifying glasses and do not scorch the leaf. the real reason not to water in the sun is that you waste up to half the water to evaporation. myth: you should water every day in summer no. daily light watering encourages shallow roots and makes plants more dependent on you. water deeply once a week and the roots will grow down. myth: lawns need watering to survive summer no. most uk lawns survive summer drought perfectly well by going brown and dormant. they green up when the rain returns. watering a lawn in a drought is a waste of water. myth: rainwater is better than tap water mostly true. rainwater is slightly acidic, free of chlorine and chloramine, and at ambient temperature. tap water is alkaline and chlorinated. most plants do not notice the difference, but ericaceous plants such as rhododendrons and blueberries prefer rainwater. frequently asked questions how much water does a garden need in summer? about 25mm per week to thrive, half that to survive. this is 25 litres per square metre to thrive, 12 litres to survive. a 100 square metre garden needs 1,000 to 2,500 litres per week in a dry summer. can i use bath water on the garden? yes, on ornamental plants. avoid using it on vegetables, and avoid water with strong detergents or bleach. a diverter on your bath or shower downpipe can direct water to a water butt. will my lawn die if i do not water it in summer? no. most uk lawns survive summer drought by going brown and dormant. the crown and roots stay alive. when the rain returns, the lawn greens up within a week or two. watering a lawn in a drought is a choice, not a necessity. how many water butts do i need? as many as you can fit. a single 200 litre butt is the minimum. two or three butts on different downpipes gives you useful storage. a larger garden can use 1,000 litres or more in a dry week, so more storage is always better. what is the best way to water containers? water at the base of the plant, slowly, until water runs out of the drainage holes. add water retaining gel to the compost. group pots together to create a humid microclimate. move pots to shade during heatwaves. final thoughts uk gardens need far less watering than most owners think. the keys are deep infrequent watering, heavy mulching, choosing the right plants, and storing as much rainwater as you can. a garden set up this way will cope with dry spells and hosepipe bans without drama, and will be healthier for it. if you would like help setting up an irrigation system, installing water butts, or replanting a dry border with drought tolerant species, get in touch with our team. we work across the chilterns and can give you practical advice on reducing your garden's water use. further reading low maintenance plants for the
