choosing the right compost bin for your garden complete uk guide to choosing a compost bin. covers plastic, wooden, tumbler, hot bin and heap options, plus siting, what to compost, maintenance and costs. choosing the right compost bin for your garden soil, compost & mulch a compost bin is one of the best investments you can make in your garden. it turns kitchen and garden waste into free, high quality soil improver, and it reduces the amount you send to landfill. the question is which bin to choose, with options ranging from a free heap in a corner to a 500 pound hot bin that composts cooked food. this guide covers the main types of compost bin available in the uk, how to choose the right one for your garden, how to use it, and what to put in it. quick answer for most uk gardens, a simple plastic compost bin costing 30 to 60 pounds is the best choice. it is cheap, easy to use, and effective for kitchen and garden waste. for larger gardens or keener composters, a wooden bin system or a tumbler may be better. for cooked food and meat scraps, you need a hot bin or a green cone. site the bin on bare soil in a partly shaded spot, fill it with a mix of greens and browns, and turn it occasionally. if you only do one thing, get a basic plastic bin and start. composting is more about practice than equipment, and you can upgrade later. why compost about 30 percent of the average uk household bin is food waste, and a similar proportion of garden waste is compostable. sending this to landfill is a waste of a resource and contributes to methane emissions, as organic waste decomposes anaerobically in landfill. composting at home turns this waste into a valuable soil improver for free. home made compost is the best mulch and soil improver you can get. it is full of soil life, slowly releases nutrients to the plants, improves soil structure, and retains moisture. a garden that composts for a few years will have dramatically better soil than one that relies on chemical fertilisers. the two thirds rule a typical household can divert about two thirds of its kitchen and garden waste from the bin by composting. the remaining third is woody prunings (better burned or taken to green waste), cooked food and meat (need a hot bin), and non compostable packaging. types of compost bin plastic compost bin the standard plastic compost bin is the most popular type in the uk. it is a black or dark green plastic cone or square, usually 200 to 400 litres, with a lid on top and a hatch at the base for removing finished compost. the plastic is recycled and uv stabilised. advantages: cheap, easy to assemble, rodent resistant if sited correctly, available in many sizes. disadvantages: not the most attractive, can be hard to turn the compost, slower than insulated bins in winter. cost: 30 to 80 pounds for a 200 to 400 litre bin. many local councils in the uk offer subsidised bins at 15 to 30 pounds, so check with your council before buying. wooden compost bin wooden bins are made from slatted timber, usually pressure treated, in a square or rectangular shape. they look better than plastic bins, blend into the garden, and allow better air circulation through the slats. they can be bought as kits or made from reclaimed timber. advantages: attractive, good air circulation, modular so you can add bays for turning. disadvantages: more expensive, the wood rots eventually, more exposed to rodents unless lined. cost: 50 to 200 pounds for a single bay kit. a three bay system for turning compost costs 150 to 400 pounds. compost tumbler a compost tumbler is a drum mounted on a frame that you turn with a handle. the idea is that turning the drum mixes and aerates the compost, speeding up decomposition. tumblers come in various sizes from 100 to 600 litres. advantages: easy to turn, fast compost in ideal conditions, contained so no rodent issues, no heavy lifting. disadvantages: expensive, small capacity for the price, can be hard to turn when full, can become waterlogged if not managed. cost: 100 to 400 pounds. best for smaller gardens or keen composters who want fast results. hot bin a hot bin is an insulated compost bin that maintains high internal temperatures, allowing you to compost cooked food, meat, dairy, and bones as well as garden waste. the insulation means the compost gets to 40 to 60 celsius, killing weed seeds and pathogens. advantages: composts all food waste including cooked food and meat, fast compost in 30 to 90 days, kills weed seeds, no rats or flies if managed correctly. disadvantages: expensive, needs regular feeding to maintain temperature, needs a power source for some models, requires more attention than a basic bin. cost: 200 to 600 pounds. best for keen composters who want to compost all food waste, or for households that produce a lot of cooked food waste. green cone a green cone is a solar powered food waste digester rather than a composter. it is a sealed underground basket with a black cone above ground that absorbs heat. you put all food waste, including meat and dairy, into the cone, where it decomposes and the liquid seeps into the surrounding soil. advantages: composts all food waste, no finished compost to remove, very low maintenance, no rodent issues if installed correctly. disadvantages: needs a sunny spot with good drainage, does not produce compost you can use, slow in winter. cost: 80 to 150 pounds. best for households that want to dispose of food waste but do not need the compost. compost heap the traditional compost heap is simply a pile of organic material in a corner of the garden, optionally contained by wooden boards or wire mesh. it is free, can be any size, and is the best option if you have a lot of garden waste and plenty of space. advantages: free, any size, can handle large amounts of material. disadvantages: slow, exposed to weather and rodents, looks untidy, hard to turn, weed seeds survive. cost: free if you have the space. best for large gardens with a hidden corner. wormery a wormery uses special composting worms to break down kitchen waste, producing a small amount of high quality compost and a liquid fertiliser. wormeries are small and can be kept on a balcony or in a garage. advantages: small, can compost kitchen waste only, produces concentrated liquid fertiliser, can be kept indoors or in a shed. disadvantages: small capacity, worms are sensitive to temperature, can fail if not managed, needs regular feeding. cost: 60 to 150 pounds. best for small households with no garden waste, or as a supplement to a main bin. compost bin comparison type cost capacity best for plastic bin 30 to 80 pounds 200 to 400 litres most gardens, kitchen and garden waste wooden bin 50 to 200 pounds 200 to 1,000 litres larger gardens, attractive appearance tumbler 100 to 400 pounds 100 to 600 litres small gardens, fast compost hot bin 200 to 600 pounds 200 litres all food waste including cooked food green cone 80 to 150 pounds variable food waste disposal, no compost needed heap free any large gardens, lots of waste wormery 60 to 150 pounds 20 to 100 litres small households, no garden waste siting your compost bin where you put your compost bin matters more than you might think. the right site speeds up decomposition, reduces smells and pests, and makes the bin easier to use. on bare soil site the bin on bare soil rather than paving or concrete. this allows worms and other soil organisms to move up into the compost, and allows excess liquid to drain away. if you must site on paving, put a layer of soil at the bottom of the bin to introduce the worms. partly shaded a partly shaded spot is ideal. full sun dries the compost out, while full shade keeps it too cold in winter. a spot that gets morning sun but is shaded in the heat of the day is perfect. convenient put the bin close enough to the kitchen that you will use it. a bin at the far end of the garden means a long walk in the rain, and you will give up. a bin 10 paces from the kitchen door is ideal. out of sight if possible a compost bin is functional rather than beautiful. site it behind a shed, in a corner, or screened by a hedge if you can. do not put it right next to the patio where you sit. away from rat runs if you have rats in the area, site the bin away from their runs, which are usually along boundaries and under hedges. a bin in the open is less attractive to rats than one tucked against a fence. the two bin trick if you have space, fit two bins side by side. fill one while the other is maturing. when the first is full, start filling the second while the first finishes composting. this avoids the problem of fresh waste being mixed with finished compost in a single bin. what to compost good compost needs a mix of greens, which are high in nitrogen, and browns, which are high in carbon. the ideal ratio is roughly half and half by volume, though you do not need to be precise. greens (high nitrogen) fruit and vegetable peelings tea bags and coffee grounds grass clippings raw green garden waste (cut flowers, deadheaded flowers) young weeds that have not gone to seed poultry manure and pigeon manure browns (high carbon) autumn leaves shredded paper and cardboard (no glossy paper) twiggy prunings, shredded or chopped straw and hay sawdust and wood shavings (in small amounts) egg shells (technically mineral but useful for structure) what not to compost in a basic bin cooked food of any kind meat, fish, and bones dairy products grease and oil diseased plant material perennial weeds such as bindweed and couch grass roots weeds that have gone to seed cat and dog faeces glossy paper and cardboard coal ash (wood ash is fine in small amounts) cooked food, meat, and dairy can be composted in a hot bin or green cone, but not in a basic bin. they attract rats and flies and create smells. composting in practice chop material up the smaller the material, the faster it composts. chop fruit and vegetable waste, tear up cardboard, and shred twiggy prunings. a few minutes with a knife or shredder can halve the composting time. mix greens and browns add a mix of greens and browns each time you fill the bin. a layer of grass clippings followed by a layer of cardboard is ideal. too much green makes a slimy, smelly mess. too much brown makes a dry, slow heap. keep it moist the compost should be as damp as a wrung out sponge. if it is too dry, add water or more greens. if it is too wet, add cardboard, paper, or autumn leaves to absorb the moisture. keep it aerated compost needs air to decompose properly. turn the heap every 6 to 8 weeks with a fork, mixing the outside into the middle. if you cannot turn it, push a garden fork into the heap and wiggle it to create air channels. be patient compost takes 6 to 18 months in a basic bin, depending on the weather and how well you manage it. you cannot rush it. the compost at the bottom of the bin will be ready first, while the top is still fresh. common compost problems smelly slime cause: too much green material, particularly grass clippings, and not enough air. fix: turn the heap and mix in cardboard, paper, or autumn leaves. avoid adding thick layers of grass clippings. dry heap that is not composting cause: too much brown material, not enough moisture. fix: add water and green material. cover the heap to retain moisture. rats cause: cooked food, meat, or excessive grain in the bin. fix: stop adding cooked food. site the bin on bare soil away from rat runs. consider a closed bin such as a tumbler or hot bin. flies cause: fruit waste exposed on the surface. fix: cover fresh fruit waste with a layer of cardboard, paper, or soil. the flies are harmless but annoying. slow compost cause: cold weather, large pieces, dry heap, or insufficient green material. fix: chop material smaller, add greens, cover the heap to retain heat, and be patient. using your compost finished compost is dark, crumbly, and smells of woodland soil. it should have no recognisable pieces of the original material except the occasional twig or eggshell. use compost as a mulch around plants, dug into vegetable beds before sowing, mixed with soil in potting compost, or as a top dressing for lawns. a 5cm layer as a mulch suppresses weeds, retains moisture, and feeds the soil. see our guide to mulching for more detail. compost that is not quite finished, with some recognisable pieces, can be used as a mulch around established shrubs where the appearance does not matter. it will finish composting in place. council subsidised bins most local councils in the uk offer subsidised compost bins to residents, often at half the retail price or less. check your council website for details. the bins on offer are usually the standard 220 or 330 litre plastic bins, suitable for most gardens. some councils also offer subsidised hot bins, green cones, and water butts. the discounts can be substantial, so always check before buying at retail. see the rhs guidance on compost bins for more detail on choosing and using a bin. making your own bin if you are handy with a saw, you can make a wooden compost bin from reclaimed timber. pallets are ideal and often free from local businesses. stand four pallets on their edges, screw them together at the corners, and line the inside with cardboard to keep the compost in. total cost: a few pounds for screws and a morning's work. a two or three bay pallet bin system is the cheapest way to compost large amounts of material. fill one bay while the other is maturing, then turn the compost from one to the other. see our guide to restoring an overgrown garden for related clearance tips. frequently asked questions how big a compost bin do i need? for a typical household with a small to medium garden, a 200 to 400 litre bin is sufficient. for a larger garden producing a lot of waste, two or three bins or a single 600 litre bin is better. as a rough guide, allow 100 litres of bin capacity per person in the household. can i put cooked food in a compost bin? not in a basic compost bin. cooked food, meat, and dairy attract rats and flies and create smells. use a hot bin or green cone for these. a basic bin is only for raw fruit and vegetable waste, garden waste, and paper or cardboard. how long does compost take? six to 18 months in a basic plastic bin, three to six months in a well managed tumbler, and 30 to 90 days in a hot bin. the speed depends on the weather, the mix of materials, and how often you turn it. be patient: finished compost will arrive. why does my compost bin smell? usually because it has too much green material, particularly grass clippings, and not enough air. turn the heap and mix in cardboard, paper, or autumn leaves. avoid adding thick layers of grass clippings. a well managed bin smells of woodland soil, not rotting waste. can i compost weeds? annual weeds that have not gone to seed are fine. perennial weeds such as bindweed, couch grass and ground elder should not be composted, as they will regrow from fragments. drown them in water for 6 weeks first, or burn them. final thoughts a compost bin is one of the best investments a uk gardener can make. it turns waste into a valuable soil improver, reduces landfill, and saves money. the right bin depends on your garden size, your waste type, and your budget, but for most gardens a simple plastic bin is the best starting point. start with that, learn the practice, and upgrade later if you need to. if you would like help setting up a compost area, building a wooden bin system, or improving your soil with compost, get in touch with our team. we work across the chilterns and can advise on the best setup for your garden. further reading low maintenance plants for the chilterns garden clearance cost guide garden maintenance service how to restore an overgrown garden pear bay weeds weed bindweed ground elder ant pruning guide: when and how to prune uk shrubs complete uk pruning guide covering why and when to prune shrubs, the three pruning groups, tools, techniques, species specific advice and common mistakes. pruning guide: when and how to prune uk shrubs hedges, pruning & cutting times pruning is the garden job that worries people most, but the basics are simple. prune at the right time, with the right tool, and follow a few principles, and your shrubs will thrive. prune at the wrong time and you can lose a year of flowers. this guide covers why we prune, when to prune different shrubs, the three pruning groups used by the rhs, the tools you need, and the techniques that work. it is written for shrubs commonly grown in the uk. quick answer prune spring flowering shrubs such as forsythia and flowering currant immediately after flowering. prune summer flowering shrubs such as buddleia and fuchsia in late spring, after the last frost. prune evergreens in late spring. use sharp, clean tools, cut just above an outward facing bud, and never remove more than a third of the plant in a single year. when in doubt, do not prune: most shrubs tolerate neglect better than over pruning. if you only learn one rule, prune immediately after flowering for shrubs that flower before midsummer, and prune in late spring for shrubs that flower after midsummer. why prune pruning has four main purposes: to control size and shape, to encourage flowering and fruiting, to remove dead or diseased wood, and to rejuvenate old or overgrown shrubs. a shrub that is never pruned will eventually become congested, flower less, and accumulate dead wood in the centre. not all shrubs need regular pruning. many can be left to grow naturally and will do fine. the shrubs that benefit most from regular pruning are those that flower on new wood, where pruning encourages the growth that produces flowers, and those that are grown for coloured stems or foliage, where the best colour is on the youngest growth. the neglect test if you are not sure whether a shrub needs pruning, leave it alone for a year and see what happens. if it flowers well and stays a reasonable size, it does not need pruning. if it becomes congested, flowers poorly, or outgrows its space, prune it the following year. most shrubs tolerate a year of neglect better than a year of incorrect pruning. the three pruning groups the rhs classifies shrubs into three pruning groups based on when they flower and how they grow. knowing which group your shrub belongs to tells you when and how to prune it. group 1: spring flowering shrubs these shrubs flower on wood grown the previous summer and autumn. they include forsythia, flowering currant (ribes), winter jasmine, philadelphus, weigela, deutzia, and chaenomeles (flowering quince). prune them immediately after flowering, in late spring, by removing the flowered stems back to a strong new shoot lower down. do not leave it until later in the year or you will remove next year's flower buds. the technique is to remove one in three of the oldest stems right down to the ground each year. this keeps the shrub young and flowering well without reducing its size too drastically. group 2: summer flowering shrubs these shrubs flower on wood grown in the same year, so pruning in late spring encourages the new growth that will flower later. they include buddleia, fuchsia, hydrangea (some types), caryopteris, perovskia, hardy fuchsia, and lavatera. prune in late march or early april, after the worst of the winter but before growth starts. the technique varies by shrub. buddleia and fuchsia are cut back hard, to within 10cm of the ground, each spring. caryopteris and perovskia get the same treatment. hydrangeas are lighter pruned, removing the dead flower heads and a few of the oldest stems. group 3: late summer and autumn flowering shrubs and evergreens this is a varied group including ceanothus, pyracantha, cotoneaster, camellia, rhododendron, and most evergreen shrubs. most of these need only light pruning to shape, and many should not be pruned at all except to remove dead wood. prune ceanothus and pyracantha immediately after flowering. prune camellia and rhododendron immediately after flowering if needed, but they often need no pruning. pruning calendar month what to prune january winter jasmine after flowering, wisteria (summer prune side shoots to 2 buds) february fuchsia, buddleia, caryopteris (hard prune), late summer clematis march roses (in southern england), dogwood and willow for coloured stems, hydrangea april evergreen shrubs, lavender, rosemary after winter may forsythia, ribes, philadelphus, weigela after flowering june ceanothus, pyracantha, deutzia after flowering july wisteria (side shoots to 15cm), early summer shrubs after flowering august summer flowering shrubs as they finish, lavender after flowering september hedges, yew, box (light trim only) october stop pruning to allow plants to harden for winter tools for pruning good tools make pruning easier and produce cleaner cuts that heal faster. the four essential tools are secateurs, loppers, a pruning saw, and hedge shears. secateurs secateurs are for cuts up to about 2cm thick. there are two types: bypass secateurs, which have a sharp blade cutting against a flat anvil, and anvil secateurs, which have a sharp blade cutting onto a soft anvil. bypass are cleaner and better for live wood. anvil are better for dead wood. most gardeners only need bypass secateurs. buy the best you can afford. a pair of felco secateurs costs 40 to 60 pounds and lasts a lifetime with replacement blades. cheap secateurs cost 10 pounds and last a season. loppers loppers are for cuts 2 to 5cm thick, where secateurs are not strong enough. they have long handles for leverage. bypass loppers are cleaner than anvil loppers. a good pair costs 30 to 60 pounds. pruning saw a pruning saw is for cuts over 5cm thick. the blade folds or fixes to a handle, and cuts on the pull stroke. a good pruning saw costs 20 to 40 pounds and will cut through a 10cm branch in seconds. hedge shears hedge shears are for shaping hedges and topiary, not for shrub pruning. manual shears are cheap and quiet. electric hedge trimmers save time on long hedges. see our hedge cutting service for hedge specific advice. tool hygiene clean your tools between plants, particularly when pruning diseased material. wipe the blades with methylated spirits or a diluted bleach solution. a dirty pair of secateurs can spread disease from one plant to another, including the rose disease coral spot and the box disease box blight. pruning techniques the basic cut cut just above a bud, at a slight angle, with the angle sloping away from the bud. the cut should be 5mm above the bud, no closer (which damages the bud) and no further (which leaves a stub that dies back). choose a bud that faces outward, so the new shoot grows away from the centre of the shrub rather than crossing back into it. removing a whole branch to remove a branch back to the trunk, make the cut just outside the branch collar, which is the slight swelling at the base of the branch. do not cut flush with the trunk, as this damages the trunk. do not leave a stub, as this rots back into the trunk. the three cut method for a heavy branch, use the three cut method to avoid tearing the bark. first, cut the underside of the branch 30cm from the trunk, a third of the way through. second, cut the top of the branch 5cm further out, until the branch falls. third, cut the remaining stub back to the branch collar. this avoids the branch tearing a strip of bark off the trunk as it falls. rejuvenation pruning for an old, overgrown shrub, you can either cut the whole thing back hard in spring (suitable for buddleia, fuchsia, dogwood, and most group 2 shrubs) or remove one third of the oldest stems each year for three years (suitable for forsythia, philadelphus, and most group 1 shrubs). the second method is slower but safer for shrubs that do not respond well to hard pruning. species specific pruning roses prune bush roses in late february or early march in southern england. reduce each stem by half to two thirds, cutting to an outward facing bud. remove dead, diseased, and crossing wood. aim for an open, goblet shaped bush with 4 to 6 strong stems. see our rose profile for full details. climbing roses are pruned in late winter, by reducing the side shoots growing from the main stems to 2 to 3 buds. the main stems are tied in horizontally to encourage flowering shoots along their length. hydrangeas mophead and lacecap hydrangeas are lightly pruned in spring. remove the dead flower heads to the first pair of strong buds below. remove a few of the oldest stems to the ground to encourage new growth. do not hard prune, as hydrangeas flower on the previous year's wood. see our hydrangea profile for more detail. lavender prune lavender after flowering in late summer. cut back to within 2cm of the current year's growth, avoiding cutting into the old wood. lavender that is not pruned becomes woody and leggy within a few years. see our lavender profile . buddleia prune buddleia hard in late february or early march. cut all stems back to within 10cm of the ground, or to a low framework of older wood. this encourages strong new growth that flowers in late summer. forsythia prune forsythia immediately after flowering in late spring. remove one third of the oldest stems to the ground, and shorten the remaining flowered stems to a strong young shoot. this keeps the shrub young and flowering well. philadelphus (mock orange) prune immediately after flowering. remove one third of the oldest stems to the ground, and shorten the remaining flowered stems by half. the flowers are produced on the previous year's growth, so pruning after flowering gives the new growth time to mature and flower the following year. camellia and rhododendron these rarely need pruning except to remove dead wood or to control size. if you must prune, do it immediately after flowering. avoid hard pruning, as camellias and rhododendrons do not respond well. ceanothus prune ceanothus lightly immediately after flowering. remove the flowered shoots to a strong side shoot. do not hard prune, as ceanothus does not recover well. hebes prune hebes lightly in late spring, removing the dead flower heads and trimming to shape. avoid cutting into old wood, as hebes do not regenerate from it. yew and box prune yew and box in late summer for shaping. both tolerate hard pruning and can be cut back into old wood if needed, particularly yew. see our yew profile for more detail. pruning common mistakes pruning at the wrong time the most common mistake. pruning a spring flowering shrub in winter removes the flowering wood, and you get no flowers that spring. pruning a group 3 shrub in autumn leaves fresh cuts exposed to winter frost. always check the right pruning time before you start. shearing everything into balls using hedge shears on shrubs produces a dense outer shell of growth with a dead interior. light cannot penetrate, and the shrub flowers only on the outside. use secateurs for individual cuts, and thin the shrub rather than shearing it. leaving stubs a stub above a bud dies back and can rot into the main stem. cut close to the bud, 5mm above it, at a slight angle. cutting flush with the trunk flush cuts damage the branch collar and slow healing. cut just outside the collar, leaving the slight swelling intact. over pruning removing more than a third of a shrub in a single year stresses the plant. it responds with vigorous, soft growth that is prone to disease and frost damage. spread major pruning over two or three years if possible. pruning in wet weather wet weather spreads disease through pruning cuts. wait for a dry day, particularly when pruning roses and fruit trees. pruning for rejuvenation an old, overgrown shrub can often be brought back to life with rejuvenation pruning. the technique depends on the shrub. for shrubs that respond well to hard pruning (buddleia, fuchsia, dogwood, willow, philadelphus, forsythia, ribes), cut the whole shrub back to 30cm from the ground in late winter. it will regrow strongly in the spring and flower the following year. for shrubs that do not respond to hard pruning (ceanothus, camellia, rhododendron, conifers), rejuvenation is done over three years. remove one third of the oldest stems to the ground each year, allowing the shrub to produce new growth from the base. by the end of three years, the shrub is completely renewed. for shrubs that do not regenerate from old wood (hebes, many conifers, lavenders), rejuvenation is not possible. if the shrub has become woody and leggy, the only option is to replace it. when not to prune do not prune in frosty weather, as the cuts are vulnerable to frost damage. do not prune in heavy rain, which spreads disease. do not prune cherries and plums in winter, as they are susceptible to silver leaf disease, prune them in summer instead. see the rhs guidance on pruning shrubs for the full reference. frequently asked questions what happens if i prune at the wrong time? usually nothing fatal. the shrub will survive but you may lose a year of flowers. spring flowering shrubs pruned in winter will not flower that spring. summer flowering shrubs pruned in autumn may produce soft growth that is damaged by frost. pruning at the wrong time rarely kills a shrub, but it does waste the plant's energy. can i hard prune an old shrub? depends on the shrub. buddleia, fuchsia, dogwood, willow, forsythia, philadelphus, and ribes respond well to hard pruning. ceanothus, camellia, rhododendron, and most conifers do not. if you are not sure, prune one third of the stems each year for three years rather than cutting the whole shrub back at once. do i need to seal pruning cuts? no. modern research shows that pruning paint does not help and may actually slow healing by trapping moisture. the plant forms its own callus over the cut. the exception is pruning cherries and plums in summer to prevent silver leaf disease, where a wound paint may help. when should i prune my hydrangea? for mophead and lacecap hydrangeas, prune in spring by removing the dead flower heads to the first pair of strong buds. remove a few of the oldest stems to the ground. do not hard prune, as the flowers form on the previous year's wood. see our hydrangea profile for more detail. why has my shrub not flowered since i pruned it? you probably pruned at the wrong time and removed the flowering wood. spring flowering shrubs pruned in winter will not flower that spring. the shrub will recover and flower the following year, but you have lost a year. check the right pruning time before you prune next time. final thoughts pruning is one of those garden jobs that is easier than it looks, once you know the principles. prune spring flowering shrubs after flowering, summer flowering shrubs in late spring, evergreens in late spring. use sharp, clean tools, cut just above an outward facing bud, and never remove more than a third of the plant in a single year. when in doubt, do not prune. if you have shrubs that need pruning and you are not sure where to start, get in touch with our team. we can prune your shrubs at the right time of year, give you advice on the right technique for each one, and rejuvenate overgrown specimens that you may have given up on. further reading when to cut hedges in the uk roses raised bed gardening guide for uk gardens complete uk raised bed gardening guide covering materials, construction, soil mixes, what to grow, drainage, crop rotation and maintenance for productive beds. raised bed gardening guide for uk gardens garden clearance & tidy-up guides raised beds are one of the most useful features you can add to a uk garden. they improve drainage on heavy clay, warm up earlier in spring, are easier on the back, and produce more food per square metre than the equivalent area of flat ground. this guide covers the materials to use, how to build raised beds, the soil mixes that work, what to grow in them, and how to maintain them year after year. it is written for gardeners on the chalk and clay soils typical of the chilterns. quick answer a raised bed is a square or rectangular frame, 15 to 30cm high, filled with a mix of topsoil and compost. build it from pressure treated timber, scaffold boards, or kit form recycled plastic. fill with a mix of 60 percent topsoil, 30 percent compost, and 10 percent sharp sand or grit. grow vegetables, herbs, and cut flowers in it. the bed will be productive for years if you top up the soil annually and rotate the crops. if you only build one raised bed, make it 1.2 metres wide and as long as you have space for. you can reach the middle from both sides without standing on the soil. why grow in raised beds raised beds have several advantages over flat ground, particularly on the heavy clay soils that are common in parts of the chilterns. better drainage raised beds drain faster than flat ground, which is useful on heavy clay. the soil in the bed warms up and dries out earlier in spring, allowing earlier sowing. in a wet year, raised beds keep the roots out of standing water. earlier spring the soil in a raised bed warms up 2 to 3 weeks earlier in spring than flat ground, because it is above the surrounding soil level and exposed to the sun on all sides. this allows earlier sowing of vegetables and an extended growing season. no soil compaction because you never stand on a raised bed, the soil stays loose and well aerated. roots grow more easily, and the soil needs less digging. after the first year, raised beds are largely no dig: just top up with compost annually and let the worms do the work. higher yields raised beds typically produce 20 to 30 percent more per square metre than flat ground, because the soil is better, the plants are closer together, and the drainage and warming are superior. a 1.2 by 2.4 metre bed can produce a surprising amount of food. easier on the back a 20 to 30cm raised bed means less bending when sowing, weeding, and harvesting. for gardeners with back or knee problems, raised beds make vegetable growing possible again. beds built 60cm high allow gardening from a wheelchair. the 1.2 metre rule never make a raised bed wider than 1.2 metres, or 4 feet. at this width, you can reach the middle from either side without standing on the soil. any wider and you will be tempted to step in, which compacts the soil and defeats the purpose of the bed. materials for raised beds pressure treated timber the most popular material for raised beds. buy 15 to 20cm wide boards, 5cm thick, in 2.4 metre lengths from a builders merchant. pressure treated timber lasts 8 to 15 years in contact with soil. cost: 10 to 15 pounds per board, so a 1.2 by 2.4 metre bed uses 3 boards and costs 30 to 45 pounds. concerns about the chemicals in pressure treated timber leaching into the soil are largely historical. modern pressure treatment uses copper based preservatives that are far less toxic than the chromated copper arsenate (cca) used until 2004. if you are concerned, line the bed with polythene before filling. scaffold boards second hand scaffold boards are 38mm thick and 225mm wide, which makes an excellent raised bed. they are stronger than ordinary timber and last longer. buy from scaffolding suppliers or second hand from building sites. cost: 10 to 15 pounds per board, similar to pressure treated timber. recycled plastic recycled plastic boards look like timber but never rot. they last indefinitely, need no treatment, and are environmentally friendly. the downside is the cost: 25 to 40 pounds per board, two to three times the price of timber. worth it for a bed you want to last a lifetime. sleepers new or reclaimed railway sleepers make substantial raised beds. they are 200mm by 100mm by 2.4m, and very heavy. new softwood sleepers cost 25 to 40 pounds each. reclaimed hardwood sleepers are more expensive and may be contaminated with tar, so avoid them for vegetable beds. brick and stone permanent and attractive but expensive and skilled work to build. a brick raised bed costs 100 to 200 pounds per linear metre built. best for ornamental beds in a formal garden, not for vegetable beds. kit form beds ready to assemble raised bed kits in timber, plastic, or metal are available from 40 pounds for a small 1 metre square bed to 200 pounds for a large 2 by 1 metre bed. they save the work of cutting timber and are a good option if you are not confident with a saw. raised bed material comparison material cost per 1.2 x 2.4m bed lifespan best for pressure treated timber 30 to 45 pounds 8 to 15 years most gardens, vegetable beds scaffold boards 30 to 45 pounds 10 to 15 years vegetable beds, strong and cheap recycled plastic 80 to 120 pounds 25+ years long term, low maintenance sleepers 100 to 160 pounds 15 to 25 years high beds, ornamental brick 200 to 400 pounds 50+ years permanent ornamental beds kit form timber 40 to 100 pounds 8 to 15 years easy assembly building a raised bed step 1: choose the site pick a sunny spot for vegetables, at least 6 hours of direct sun a day. level ground is easier, but a sloped site can be terraced. avoid low spots that collect water. check there are no underground pipes or cables where you plan to dig. step 2: mark out the bed mark the corners with pegs and run a string line between them. check the corners are square by measuring the diagonals, which should be equal. a standard bed is 1.2 metres wide by 2.4 metres long. step 3: prepare the ground cut the grass short if there is lawn on the site. you do not need to remove the turf, just cover it with a thick layer of cardboard before filling the bed. the grass will die and decompose, adding organic matter. if the site is weeds rather than grass, dig out perennial weeds such as bindweed and couch grass first. step 4: build the frame cut the timber to length: two boards 1.2 metres long and two boards 2.4 metres long. use 5cm by 5cm timber posts in the corners as uprights, 30 to 40cm long, driven into the ground. screw the boards to the corner posts with 60mm exterior screws. check the frame is level with a spirit level, adjusting the soil underneath if necessary. for beds longer than 2.4 metres, add intermediate posts every 1.2 metres to prevent the boards bowing. for beds two boards high, overlap the joints at the corners for strength. step 5: line the bed if you are using pressure treated timber and are concerned about chemicals, line the inside of the bed with heavy duty polythene. this also helps the timber last longer by keeping the wet soil off it. leave the bottom open for drainage. step 6: lay cardboard lay a thick layer of cardboard over the bottom of the bed, overlapping the edges by 15cm. this suppresses any weeds or grass underneath and decomposes over a year, adding organic matter. step 7: fill the bed fill the bed with your soil mix (see below). do not pat it down firmly, just level it with a rake. water it thoroughly and leave for a week before sowing, to allow the soil to settle. the hugelkultur alternative for a deep raised bed on a budget, fill the bottom third with rotting logs, twigs, and leaves, then top with soil. the woody material decomposes over years, holding moisture and feeding the soil. this is called hugelkultur and is a good way to fill a 60cm high bed cheaply. the best soil mix for raised beds the soil you fill a raised bed with is the single biggest factor in how well it performs. do not just use soil dug from the garden, as it will compact and drain poorly. use a mix of topsoil, compost, and drainage material. standard mix 60 percent topsoil, 30 percent compost (well rotted manure, garden compost, or bagged compost), 10 percent sharp sand or grit. this mix is free draining but moisture retentive, and rich enough to support vegetables for a season. no dig mix for a no dig approach, start with a 50/50 mix of topsoil and compost. each year, top up with 5cm of compost. over a few years, the soil structure becomes excellent and you never need to dig. budget mix if you cannot afford to fill the bed with bought materials, layer what you have. bottom layer: twigs and branches for drainage. middle layer: lawn clippings, leaves, and cardboard. top 15cm: a mix of topsoil and compost. the bed will be productive immediately and improve as the lower layers decompose. how much soil do you need a 1.2 by 2.4 metre bed, 20cm high, needs 0.58 cubic metres of soil mix, which is about 800kg. a bulk bag of topsoil is 1 cubic metre and costs 50 to 80 pounds. for two raised beds, you need about one bulk bag of topsoil plus compost and sand. buying soil in small bags from a garden centre is expensive. a 50 litre bag of topsoil costs 4 to 6 pounds, so a cubic metre costs 80 to 120 pounds. buy bulk bags from a builder's merchant or soil supplier for half the price. what to grow in raised beds raised beds are ideal for vegetables, herbs, and cut flowers. the improved drainage and warmer soil allow earlier sowing and higher yields than flat ground. vegetables leafy crops: lettuce, spinach, chard, rocket. sow in succession for continuous harvest. root crops: carrots, parsnips, beetroot, radishes. the loose soil is ideal for carrots, which fork in stony ground. brassicas: cabbage, broccoli, kale, cauliflower. need firm soil, so do not over dig the bed. legumes: peas, broad beans, runner beans, french beans. support with canes or netting. alliums: onions, shallots, garlic, leeks. easy and reliable in raised beds. cucurbits: courgettes, pumpkins, squash. need rich soil and plenty of space. herbs most herbs do well in raised beds, particularly mediterranean herbs such as thyme, oregano, sage, and rosemary, which prefer the well drained conditions. mint should be grown in a pot sunk into the bed, otherwise it will spread and take over. see our rosemary profile for more detail. cut flowers raised beds are excellent for cut flowers such as sweet peas, dahlias, cosmos, zinnias, and antirrhinums. the rich soil and good drainage produce long stems and abundant flowers. what not to grow in raised beds avoid perennials that spread, such as mint and horseradish, unless grown in pots sunk into the bed. avoid large plants that need a lot of root space, such as artichokes and asparagus, which are better in the open ground. avoid trees and large shrubs, which will outgrow the bed within a few years. crop rotation grow the same crop in the same bed year after year and you build up soil pests and diseases, and deplete specific nutrients. crop rotation avoids this. the traditional rotation is four groups, moved around four beds each year. the four year rotation year 1, bed a: legumes (peas, beans). fix nitrogen in the soil. year 1, bed b: brassicas (cabbage, broccoli). need nitrogen, follow legumes. year 1, bed c: roots (carrots, parsnips, onions). need firm soil, low nitrogen. year 1, bed d: potatoes and cucurbits (courgettes, pumpkins). need rich soil. each year, move each group to the next bed. so in year 2, bed a has brassicas, bed b has roots, bed c has potatoes, bed d has legumes. after 4 years, each group is back where it started, but the soil has had a rest from each crop. maintaining raised beds annual top up each spring, top up the bed with 5cm of compost or well rotted manure. the soil level in a raised bed drops over the year as the organic matter decomposes. just spread the compost on top and let the worms work it in. see the rhs guidance on raised beds for more detail. mulching mulch the bed in late winter with compost or leaf mould to suppress weeds and retain moisture. avoid bark mulch on vegetable beds as it ties up nitrogen. see our guide to mulching for general principles. weeding raised beds need less weeding than flat ground because the soil is loose and weeds pull out easily. hand weed regularly, removing weeds when they are small. avoid hoeing, which damages the soil structure. watering raised beds dry out faster than flat ground because they are above soil level. water deeply once a week in dry spells, more often for shallow rooted crops such as salads. see our summer tidy checklist for watering reminders. wood treatment timber raised beds need no treatment if you used pressure treated timber. untreated timber should be treated with a wood preservative every 2 to 3 years, avoiding the inside of the bed where the soil is. replacing timber eventually the timber will rot. pressure treated timber lasts 8 to 15 years, scaffold boards 10 to 15 years. when a board rots, replace it. the soil holds its shape, so you can usually replace one board at a time without disturbing the bed. raised bed problems drying out raised beds dry out faster than flat ground. mulch heavily, water deeply, and consider installing a simple drip irrigation system. group beds together so they shade each other's sides. slugs and snails raised beds can be a slug paradise, particularly the gaps between the boards. keep the area around the beds clear of debris, and trap slugs with beer traps. see our hosta profile for slug control methods. cat damage cats love to use freshly dug raised beds as a toilet. cover bare soil with netting or twigs until the plants are established. a mulch of sharp gravel also deters them. soil depletion if you grow the same crop year after year, the soil becomes depleted of specific nutrients and builds up specific pests. rotate crops annually and top up with compost each spring. timber rot even pressure treated timber rots eventually, particularly where it touches the soil. lift the bed every 5 years and check the bottom of the boards. replace rotted boards promptly to avoid losing the soil. cost of building raised beds for a 1.2 by 2.4 metre bed, 20cm high, the cost is: pressure treated timber: 30 to 45 pounds corner posts and screws: 10 pounds soil mix (topsoil, compost, sand): 30 to 50 pounds cardboard and mulch: free or 10 pounds total: 70 to 115 pounds per bed for four beds, allowing for a four year rotation, the total cost is 280 to 460 pounds. this is a one time investment: the beds last 10 years or more, and the only ongoing cost is the annual compost top up. designing a raised bed garden for a productive vegetable garden, plan for at least four raised beds to allow crop rotation. a common layout is four beds, each 1.2 by 2.4 metres, arranged in a square with 60cm paths between. this fits in a 5 by 5 metre area and produces enough vegetables for a small family. make the paths between the beds wide enough for a wheelbarrow, at least 60cm. wider paths, 90cm, are more comfortable. cover the paths with weed suppressing membrane and gravel or wood chip to keep them clean and weed free. consider the aspect. in the uk, a south facing bed gets the most sun. taller crops such as runner beans and brassicas should be on the north side of the beds, so they do not shade shorter crops. see our low maintenance plants guide for related design ideas. frequently asked questions how deep should a raised bed be? for most vegetables, 20cm is enough. for root crops such as carrots and parsnips, 30cm is better. for deep root crops and potatoes, 40cm. anything deeper than 40cm is for ergonomic reasons, not for the plants. do i need to line a raised bed? only if you are concerned about chemicals leaching from pressure treated timber, or if you want to extend the life of the timber. use heavy duty polythene and leave the bottom open for drainage. most gardeners do not bother lining. can i put a raised bed on concrete? yes, but you need a deeper bed, at least 30cm, and you need to add drainage. lay a 5cm layer of gravel at the bottom, with drainage holes drilled in the sides of the bed just above the gravel. do not put a solid bottom on the bed. how long do timber raised beds last? pressure treated timber lasts 8 to 15 years in contact with soil. scaffold boards last 10 to 15 years. recycled plastic lasts 25 years or more. the bottom of the board rots first, where it touches the soil. lifting the bed and replacing rotted boards extends the life significantly. what is the best soil mix for raised beds? 60 percent topsoil, 30 percent compost, 10 percent sharp sand. this mix is free draining but moisture retentive, and rich enough for vegetables. avoid using only compost, which is too rich and lacks soil structure. avoid using only garden soil, which compacts. final thoughts raised beds are one of the most useful features you can add to a uk garden. they improve drainage on heavy clay, warm up earlier in spring, are easier on the back, and produce more food per square metre than flat ground. a weekend's work and a hundred pounds gets you a productive bed that will last for years. if you would like help building raised beds, sourcing materials, or setting up a vegetable garden, get in touch with our team. we work across the chilterns and can build beds to your specification and fill them with the right soil mix. further reading low maintenance plants for the chilterns garden maintenance cost guide soft landscaping service garden maintenance to 2,500 pounds. how much does a large garden tidy-up cost in oxfordshire? garden prices & costs large gardens in oxfordshire, from the chalk downland around watlington and chinnor to the clay vale around bicester and banbury, present a different challenge to small suburban plots. a tidy-up on a half-acre garden is not just a bigger version of a small garden tidy. the scale changes the equipment, the crew size, the waste volume and the time on site. this guide explains what a large garden tidy-up costs in oxfordshire in 2026, what drives the price, and how to keep the cost predictable. quick answer for a large garden tidy-up in oxfordshire in 2026, expect to pay between 350 and 2,500 pounds depending on the size of the garden and the condition it is in. a one-off tidy of a well-maintained half-acre garden costs 350 to 600. a neglected half-acre needing heavy clearance, hedge cutting and waste removal costs 1,200 to 2,500. full-acre clearances of overgrown gardens can reach 3,500 or more. ballpark price ranges for oxfordshire large garden, light tidy (half acre, maintained): 350 to 600 large garden, standard tidy (half acre, some overgrowth): 600 to 1,200 large garden, heavy tidy (half acre, neglected): 1,200 to 2,500 full acre or estate, overgrown: 2,500 to 3,500+ what counts as a large garden in oxfordshire? for pricing purposes, we class any garden over 400 square metres as large. most large gardens we work on in oxfordshire are between 500 and 2,000 square metres (roughly 0.12 to 0.5 acres). estate gardens and rural properties can be an acre or more. the oxfordshire landscape includes the cotswolds in the north west, the chalk hills of the chilterns in the south east, and the clay vales in between. garden types vary accordingly. stone village gardens in charlbury and woodstock, period town gardens in oxford itself, modern estates in bicester and didcot, and rural properties in the countryside between. each presents different challenges for a tidy-up. what is included in a large garden tidy-up our standard large garden tidy-up includes: lawn mowing and edging across the whole garden strimming of banks, edges and awkward corners weeding of all beds and borders cutting back of overgrown shrubs and perennials hedge trimming where needed (tall hedges quoted separately) clearance of leaves, dead growth and debris sweeping of all paths, patios and hard surfaces removal of all green waste from site for larger jobs we work in a team of two or three, which keeps the time on site down and the cost predictable. we bring our own tools, fuel and waste vehicle. what drives the cost of a large garden tidy-up garden size the obvious one. a 500 square metre garden takes roughly half a day for a two-person team. a 2,000 square metre garden takes one to two full days. we price by the area plus the labour and waste, so larger gardens cost more in proportion. level of overgrowth a garden that has been kept up but needs a seasonal tidy is quick. a garden that has been left for a year or more, with brambles creeping in, hedges needing a hard cut, and borders full of self-seeded weeds, takes three to four times as long for the same area. waste volume large gardens produce a lot of waste. a standard tidy on a half-acre garden can fill a 7.5 tonne tipper. waste disposal in oxfordshire costs 120 to 180 per tonne including transport. a heavy clearance can produce 3 to 5 tonnes, adding 400 to 900 to the bill. hedge length and height tall hedges are common in rural oxfordshire. a 50 metre beech hedge at 2.5 metres tall takes a full day for one person to cut both sides and top. tall conifer hedges like leylandii take longer because the cuttings have to be carried out. see our guide on hedge cutting costs in the chilterns for detail. access many large oxfordshire gardens have good access through a side gate or drive. rural properties off single-track lanes can be harder to reach with a tipper. if we cannot get a vehicle close to the garden, waste has to be barrowed further, which adds time. slopes and banks the oxfordshire countryside is not flat. gardens on slopes around the cotswold escarpment, the chiltern dip slope or the thames valley sides take longer to work on. mowing banks requires a strimmer rather than a mower, and carrying waste uphill is slow work. how to keep the cost predictable be specific about what you want the more specific you can be, the more accurate the quote. tell us exactly what you want done, what you want left alone, and what standard of finish you expect. a vague brief means we have to add a contingency and you pay more. book in the right season january and february are our quietest months. booking a large tidy-up in winter can save 10 to 15 percent compared to peak spring. the garden also looks less dramatic when cleared in winter, which makes the work feel less overwhelming. have a waste plan if you have a council green waste bin and the clearance is light, we can leave waste in the bin. for larger jobs, we charge per tonne for removal. if you have space for a bonfire (where allowed) or a compost heap, you can save on disposal. see our guide on garden waste removal costs for detail. set up regular maintenance if your garden needs a heavy tidy-up because it has been left, a one-off tidy is the right first step. but to stop it getting that bad again, consider regular maintenance . a fortnightly visit keeps the garden in check and is cheaper per month than a full annual clear-up. see our guide to maintenance costs for pricing. sample garden clearance costs in buckinghamshire: small, medium and large gardens real 2026 garden clearance costs in buckinghamshire. small, medium and large gardens priced separately. what drives the cost and how to save money on your clearance. garden clearance costs in buckinghamshire: small, medium and large gardens garden prices & costs buckinghamshire gardens range from compact courtyards in aylesbury to substantial rural plots in the chilterns around amersham, beaconsfield and princes risborough. clearance costs vary accordingly. this guide breaks down what garden clearance costs in buckinghamshire in 2026, with separate pricing for small, medium and large gardens, so you can budget accurately before you pick up the phone. quick answer garden clearance in buckinghamshire in 2026 costs between 200 and 3,500 pounds depending on garden size and condition. small courtyard clearances start at 200 to 400. medium family garden clearances are 400 to 900. large garden clearances in the chilterns run from 900 to 3,500 or more for neglected estates. buckinghamshire clearance price ranges small garden (under 100 sqm): 200 to 400 medium garden (100 to 300 sqm): 400 to 900 large garden (300 to 1,000 sqm): 900 to 2,000 estate clearance (1,000+ sqm, overgrown): 2,000 to 3,500+ what affects the cost of garden clearance in buckinghamshire garden size and layout the chiltern hills run through the south of buckinghamshire, with chalky free-draining soil. the vale of aylesbury in the north has heavier clay. garden sizes vary from compact new-build plots in bicester and aylesbury to large period gardens in amersham, beaconsfield and marlow. soil and access chalky chiltern gardens drain freely but can be steep, making waste removal harder. clay gardens in the vale can be waterlogged in winter, which slows work. properties in conservation areas like amersham old town may have restricted access for tippers and trailers. waste volume green waste disposal in buckinghamshire costs 120 to 180 per tonne including transport. a standard family garden clearance produces 1 to 2 tonnes. a neglected large garden can produce 4 to 6 tonnes. waste is the single biggest variable cost. brambles, ivy and overgrowth brambles and ivy are common in neglected buckinghamshire gardens, particularly those backing onto woodland or chalk downland. heavy bramble coverage takes three to four times as long to clear as light overgrowth for the same area. small garden clearance in buckinghamshire (under 100 sqm) small gardens in aylesbury, bicester, high wycombe and the newer estates are the most common clearance we do. a small clearance typically takes 4 to 8 with a single gardener or a half-day with a two-person team. what is included: cutting back all overgrowth to ground level weeding of beds and borders light pruning of shrubs lawn cut and edge sweeping of paths and patios removal of all green waste typical cost: 200 to 400 including waste removal. medium garden clearance in buckinghamshire (100 to 300 sqm) medium family gardens are the bulk of our clearance work in buckinghamshire. these are the back gardens of 1930s semis in high wycombe, the period properties in marlow and beaconsfield, and the family homes in amersham and chesham. what is included: everything in a small clearance, plus: cutting back brambles and ivy heavier pruning of mature shrubs hedge trimming where needed clearance of accumulated debris removal of small shed contents if required typical cost: 400 to 900 including waste removal. the upper end applies to neglected gardens that have been left for a year or more. large garden clearance in buckinghamshire (300 to 1,000 sqm) large gardens in the chilterns, around beaconsfield, gerrards cross, great missenden and princes risborough, are a different proposition. these are the gardens that have often been left for several seasons, with substantial bramble coverage, mature hedges needing renovation, and significant waste volumes. what is included: everything in a medium clearance, plus: full team of two or three gardeners multiple tipper loads of waste removal hedge renovation cutting small tree removal (under 5 metres) shed and outbuilding clearance typical cost: 900 to 2,000 including waste removal. for neglected large gardens, expect 1,500 to 2,500. estate clearance in buckinghamshire (1,000+ sqm) for substantial rural properties, farms and estates, clearance is a multi-day project. we bring a full team, a 7.5 tonne tipper and sometimes a grab lorry for soil and rubble. these jobs are priced individually after a site visit. typical cost: 2,000 to 3,500 or more, depending on size, access and waste volume. how to reduce your clearance cost book in winter january and february are how much does hedge cutting cost in the chilterns? real 2026 hedge cutting costs in the chilterns. beech, yew, leylandii, laurel and privet. per metre pricing, tall hedge costs and renovation work. how much does hedge cutting cost in the chilterns? hedges, pruning & cutting times the chilterns has more hedges per mile than almost anywhere else in southern england. beech, yew, hornbeam, hawthorn, box and leylandii all thrive on the chalk, and almost every garden has at least one. this guide explains what hedge cutting costs in the chilterns in 2026, with pricing per metre for each common species, so you can budget accurately for your hedge work. quick answer hedge cutting in the chilterns in 2026 costs between 150 and 800 pounds per cut for a typical garden hedge. a 20 metre low hedge costs 150 to 250. a 20 metre tall hedge at 2.5 metres costs 300 to 500. hedge renovation (hard reduction) costs 200 to 600 depending on length and species. chilterns hedge cutting price ranges low hedge (1m tall, 20m long): 150 to 250 per cut medium hedge (1.5m tall, 20m long): 200 to 350 per cut tall hedge (2.5m tall, 20m long): 300 to 500 per cut very tall hedge (3m+, 20m long): 450 to 800 per cut hedge renovation (1m height reduction): 200 to 600 why chiltern hedges cost what they cost the chilterns has a particular hedge culture. beech is the classic hedge on the chalk, kept for its russet winter leaves. yew is the formal hedge of choice for the larger gardens around henley, marlow and beaconsfield. leylandii is the fast-growing conifer used for quick screening, and it is the one that causes the most problems when it gets out of hand. hedge cutting cost depends on four things: length and height of the hedge (more hedge means more cutting time) species (some cut faster than others) access (can we get a platform or ladder to it?) waste removal (cuttings have to go somewhere) hedge cutting prices by species in the chilterns beech (fagus sylvatica) the classic chiltern hedge. cut once a year in late august. a 20 metre beech hedge at 1.8 metres tall costs 200 to 300 per cut including waste removal. beech responds well to hard pruning, so renovation work is straightforward. yew (taxus baccata) formal yew hedges are the backbone of the larger chiltern gardens. cut twice a year, in june and september. a 20 metre yew hedge at 1.8 metres tall costs 250 to 400 per cut. yew regenerates from old wood, so overgrown hedges can be cut back hard in february. leylandii (x hesperotropsis leylandii) the fast-growing conifer that gets everywhere. cut twice a year to keep in check. a 20 metre leylandii hedge at 2.5 metres tall costs 350 to 500 per cut. leylandii cannot be cut back into old brown wood, so if it has got too tall the only option is removal and replanting. hornbeam (carpinus betulus) similar to beech in habit and pricing. cut once a year in late august. a 20 metre hornbeam hedge at 1.8 metres tall costs 200 to 300 per cut. privet (ligustrum ovalifolium) fast growing. cut 2 to 3 times through the growing season. a 20 metre privet hedge at 1.5 metres tall costs 150 to 250 per cut. laurel (prunus laurocerasus) cut once in late summer. a 20 metre laurel hedge at 2 metres tall costs 250 to 400 per cut. laurels are best cut with secateurs rather than a trimmer to avoid cutting leaves in half. tall hedge cutting in the chilterns many chiltern gardens have tall hedges for privacy, particularly on the exposed hillsides. cutting a hedge over 2.5 metres requires a platform or ladder, which adds time and cost. for hedges over 3 metres, we use a tractor-mounted platform or a cherry picker, which adds 150 to 300 to the cost. hedge renovation in the chilterns overgrown hedges can usually be renovated rather than removed. the technique depends on the species: beech, hornbeam, yew, hawthorn: can be cut back hard to within 30cm of the ground in february. they regrow over 2 to 3 years. privet, laurel: can be cut back hard in late winter. leylandii: cannot be cut back into old brown wood. if it has got too tall, removal is the only option. renovation work is priced per metre. a 20 metre hedge reduced by 1 metre in height costs 300 to 600 depending on species and access. bird nesting season in the chilterns under the wildlife and countryside act 1981, it is an offence to damage or destroy the nest of any wild bird while it is being built or used. the main nesting season is 1 march to 31 august. we can cut hedges during this period if we check carefully for nests first, but if nests are present we wait until september. see our full guide on when to cut hedges in the uk . waste removal for hedge cuttings hedge cuttings are bulky and hard to compost at home. we remove all cuttings as part of the quote. for a 20 metre tall hedge, expect 2 to 4 bin bags of cuttings per cut. waste removal adds 50 to 150 to the cost depending on volume. get a hedge cutting quote in the chilterns contact us on whatsapp with a photo of your hedge and approximate length and height. we will give you a price range the same day and confirm a fixed price after a site visit. whatsapp get a quote frequently asked questions how often should my hedge be cut? most formal hedges need one or two cuts a year. fast-growing hedges like leylandii and privet need two or three. see our hedge cutting calendar for detail by species. can you cut hedges in the rain? we can, but we prefer not to. wet cuttings are heavier, tools slip, and the finish is not as clean. we reschedule if the weather is very bad. my leylandii has got too tall. can you reduce it? leylandii cannot be cut back into old brown wood. if the hedge has got too tall or too wide, the only option is to remove it and replant with a slower-growing species. further reading when how much does lawn renovation cost in the uk? real 2026 uk lawn renovation costs. scarification, aeration, top dressing, overseeding and full renovation. per square metre pricing and what is included. how much does lawn renovation cost in the uk? garden prices & costs lawn renovation is the middle ground between a simple feed and a full returf. it is the right job when your lawn is more than 30 percent moss or weeds, or when it has gone thin and tired but the soil underneath is sound. this guide explains what lawn renovation costs in the uk in 2026, with per square metre pricing for each stage of the process. quick answer lawn renovation in the uk in 2026 costs between 4 and 15 pounds per square metre depending on the level of work involved. a basic scarify and overseed costs 4 to 7 per square metre. a full renovation with aeration, top dressing, overseeding and feeding costs 8 to 12 per square metre. premium lawn renovation with soil improvement costs 12 to 15 per square metre. lawn renovation price ranges (50 sqm lawn) basic (scarify and overseed): 200 to 350 standard (scarify, aerate, top dress, overseed, feed): 400 to 600 premium (full renovation with soil improvement): 600 to 900 full returf (strip and returf): 500 to 1,200 what is lawn renovation? lawn renovation is the process of bringing a tired, mossy or weedy lawn back to health without stripping it and starting again. it is a staged process that addresses the underlying problems (compaction, thatch, moss, poor drainage) and then re-establishes healthy grass through overseeding. the full process is: moss treatment (kill the moss) scarification (remove thatch and dead moss) aeration (relieve compaction) top dressing (improve surface and fill holes) overseeding (sow new grass seed) feeding (give the new grass what it needs to establish) aftercare (watering and mowing regime) cost of each stage per square metre moss treatment applying iron sulphate to kill moss. costs 1 to 2 per square metre including the chemical and application. done 2 weeks before scarification. scarification using a powered scarifier to remove thatch and dead moss. costs 2 to 4 per square metre. a 50 sqm lawn produces 4 to 6 bin bags of material. aeration hollow-tine or solid-tine aeration to relieve compaction. costs 1.50 to 3 per square metre. hollow-tine is more expensive but more effective on compacted clay soils. top dressing applying a sand and soil mix to the surface, brushed into the aeration holes. costs 2 to 4 per square metre including material. top dressing material is 70/30 sand/soil. overseeding sowing new grass seed suited to the conditions. costs 1 to 2 per square metre including seed. see our lawn planning tool for seed variety recommendations. feeding applying a balanced fertiliser to help the new grass establish. costs 0.50 to 1 per square metre including fertiliser. full renovation versus returfing if your lawn is more than 50 percent moss or weeds, or if the soil is severely compacted, a full renovation may be more cost effective than recovery. but if the lawn is beyond renovation (less than 30 percent grass), returfing is the better option. returfing costs 10 to 25 per square metre including turf, soil preparation and laying. for a 50 sqm lawn, that is 500 to 1,250. see our guide on returf vs reseed for detail. best time for lawn renovation lawn renovation is best done in either spring (march to may) or autumn (september to october) when the soil is warm and there is regular rain. avoid summer renovation because the new seed will struggle in dry conditions. see our guide on lawn renovation in spring . common lawn problems that need renovation moss: caused by shade, compaction, poor drainage or acidic soil. see our moss guide . thatch: dead grass and moss at the base, preventing water and air reaching the roots. compaction: hard soil from foot traffic, children or heavy mowing. poor drainage: standing water after rain, often on clay soils. thin grass: from under-feeding, drought, shade or disease. use our lawn planning tool for a free seed variety recommendation and renovation plan, use our lawn planning tool . it takes 2 minutes, asks about your sun exposure, soil type and main problem, and recommends a seed variety plus a step by step plan. the full seed mix percentages are available in our chiltern lawn care guide pdfs . get a lawn renovation quote contact us on whatsapp with a photo of your lawn and we will aim to respond within one day. we arrange all quotes during a free site visit so we can assess the lawn and give you an accurate price. whatsapp get a quote frequently asked questions how long does lawn renovation take? the work itself takes 1 to 2 days for a 50 sqm lawn. the full recovery takes 4 to 8 weeks for the new grass to establish. can i walk on the lawn after renovation? avoid walking on the lawn for 3 to 4 weeks after overseeding, while the new grass establishes. light walking is fine after 2 weeks. will the renovation fix my moss problem permanently? only if the underlying cause is addressed. if moss is caused by shade, compaction or poor drainage, the renovation will fix it. if the cause is not addressed, moss will return. see our moss guide . further reading why is my lawn full of moss? lawn renovation in spring should you returf or reseed? aeration, overseeding and top how much does it cost to clear brambles, ivy and overgrowth? real 2026 uk costs for clearing brambles, ivy and overgrowth. per square metre pricing, what drives the cost and how to keep the price down. how much does it cost to clear brambles, ivy and overgrowth? garden prices & costs brambles and ivy are the two plants that take over a neglected garden fastest. a single season of neglect can turn a tidy border into an impenetrable thicket. this guide explains what it costs to clear brambles, ivy and overgrowth in the uk in 2026, with per square metre pricing and the factors that push the cost up or down. quick answer clearing brambles, ivy and overgrowth in the uk in 2026 costs between 8 and 25 pounds per square metre depending on the density and how established the roots are. a small patch of 10 square metres costs 80 to 250. a full garden of 100 square metres of heavy bramble costs 800 to 2,500. bramble and ivy clearance price ranges light bramble (10 sqm): 80 to 150 medium bramble (50 sqm): 400 to 900 heavy bramble (100 sqm): 800 to 2,500 ivy on walls and fences (per 10m run): 100 to 300 ivy in trees (per tree): 80 to 250 why brambles and ivy cost what they cost brambles and ivy are the most time-consuming plants to clear. brambles have thorny stems that tangle into dense thickets, and the roots have to be dug out or they regrow. ivy clings to walls, fences and trees with tiny aerial roots, and pulling it off can damage the surface underneath. the cost is driven by: density (how thick the growth is) root establishment (how deep the roots go) access (can we get to the base of the plants?) waste volume (brambles produce a lot of bulk) bramble clearance costs light bramble (1 to 2 years growth) stems up to 1.5 metres, roots not yet well established. cut with a brush cutter, then rake up. roots can be left to rot or dug out. cost: 8 to 12 per square metre. medium bramble (3 to 5 years growth) stems 1.5 to 2.5 metres, thickets forming, roots well established. cut with a brush cutter, then dig out root crowns with a mattock. cost: 12 to 18 per square metre. heavy bramble (5+ years growth) dense thickets over 2.5 metres, deep woody root systems. cut with a brush cutter or chainsaw, then dig out every root crown. cost: 18 to 25 per square metre. ivy clearance costs ivy on walls and fences ivy clings to surfaces with aerial roots that damage mortar and render. cut the stems at the base, let the top growth die for 2 to 3 weeks, then pull it off. cost: 100 to 300 per 10 metre run of wall or fence. ivy in trees ivy climbing into trees shades the canopy and adds weight. cut the stems at the base of the tree, let the top growth die, then pull it out of the lower branches. cost: 80 to 250 per tree depending on size. ivy as ground cover ivy spreading across the ground smothers other plants. cut with a strimmer, then dig out the roots. cost: 8 to 15 per square metre. what is included in our bramble and ivy clearance cutting all top growth to ground level digging out root crowns (brambles) or main stems (ivy) removal of all cut material from site disposal of waste basic tidy of the cleared area what is not included chemical treatment of regrowth (quoted separately if needed) soil improvement or replanting (see our soft landscaping service) wall or fence repair if ivy has damaged the surface how to keep the cost down book in winter brambles die back in winter, making them easier to cut and lighter to carry. the lack of leaves also means we can see the root crowns more clearly. do the easy bits yourself if you are able to cut the top growth yourself with secateurs or loppers, we can focus on the root digging. this is the hardest part and the part most people want help with. have a waste plan bramble cuttings are bulky. if you have a bonfire area or a compost heap, you can save on waste removal. see our waste removal cost guide . use our clearance level assessment for a quick estimate of for your bramble or ivy clearance, use our clearance level assessment .. it accounts for bramble density and gives you a clearance level. get a quote contact us on whatsapp with a photo of the overgrowth and we will aim to respond within one day. whatsapp get a quote frequently asked questions will brambles grow back if i just cut them down? yes. brambles regrow from root crowns. to stop regrowth, the root crowns have to be dug out. cutting alone is a temporary fix. will ivy damage my wall? ivy aerial roots damage mortar and render over time. if the wall is sound, ivy is fine. if the mortar is already weak, ivy will make it worse. can i burn bramble cuttings? yes, once they have dried out for a few weeks. check local rules on bonfires before burning. further reading how to clear brambles properly how to remove ivy safely
