how much should you budget for a full garden reset? real 2026 uk costs for a full garden reset. from clearance to soil improvement to replanting. what to budget and how to phase the work. how much should you budget for a full garden reset? garden prices & costs a full garden reset is the big one. it is what you do when the garden has been neglected for years, or when you have just moved into a property and want to start from scratch. this guide explains what to budget for a full garden reset in the uk in 2026, broken down by stage, so you can plan the work and the cost in phases. quick answer a full garden reset in the uk in 2026 costs between 2,000 and 15,000 pounds depending on the size of the garden and the level of work. a small courtyard reset costs 2,000 to 4,000. a family garden reset costs 4,000 to 8,000. a large garden or estate reset costs 8,000 to 15,000 or more. full garden reset budget ranges small courtyard (50 sqm): 2,000 to 4,000 family garden (150 sqm): 4,000 to 8,000 large garden (400 sqm): 8,000 to 15,000 estate (1,000+ sqm): 15,000 to 35,000+ what is a full garden reset? a full garden reset is a complete transformation. it includes: clearance of all existing vegetation and structures soil improvement across the whole garden hard landscaping (paths, patios, edging) if needed new lawn (seed or turf) new planting schemes for all borders mulching and finishing it is not the same as a clearance, which just removes the old. a reset removes the old and puts in the new. budget breakdown by stage stage 1: clearance (25 to 35 percent of budget) clearing the existing garden. see our clearance cost guide for detail. for a family garden, expect 800 to 2,500. stage 2: soil improvement (10 to 15 percent of budget) digging in compost, manure, grit or sand to improve the soil. cost: 3 to 8 per square metre including material. for a 150 sqm garden, 450 to 1,200. stage 3: hard landscaping (20 to 35 percent of budget) new paths, patios, edging, raised beds, fencing. this is the most variable cost. a new patio costs 80 to 150 per square metre installed. new fencing costs 50 to 120 per metre. see our fencing service for detail. stage 4: new lawn (10 to 15 percent of budget) seed or turf. seeding costs 2 to 4 per square metre. turf costs 10 to 25 per square metre installed. see our returf vs reseed guide . stage 5: planting (15 to 25 percent of budget) new plants for all borders. cost depends on plant size and quantity. a typical family garden border (20 sqm) planted with a mix of shrubs and perennials costs 500 to 1,500 including plants, compost and planting. see our soft landscaping service . stage 6: mulching and finishing (5 percent of budget) bark or compost mulch across all beds. cost: 2 to 4 per square metre including material. sample budgets small courtyard reset (50 sqm) clearance: 400 to 800 soil improvement: 150 to 400 hard landscaping (small patio): 800 to 2,000 new lawn or planting: 300 to 600 mulch and finish: 100 to 200 total: 1,750 to 4,000 family garden reset (150 sqm) clearance: 800 to 2,000 soil improvement: 450 to 1,200 hard landscaping (patio and paths): 1,500 to 4,000 new lawn: 300 to 1,500 planting (3 borders): 1,500 to 4,500 mulch and finish: 200 to 400 total: 4,750 to 13,600 how to phase the work to spread the cost not everyone can afford to do a full reset in one go. the work can be phased over 1 to 3 years: year 1: clearance and soil improvement. get the garden back to a blank canvas and improve the soil. year 2: hard landscaping and lawn. put in the structure and the new lawn. year 3: planting. fill the borders with new plants. phasing the work spreads the cost and lets you see the garden take shape over time. it also means you can adjust the plan as you go. should i do a full reset or renovate? if your garden has good bones (mature shrubs, established hedges, sound hard landscaping) but looks tired, a renovation is better value than a full reset. a renovation keeps what works and replaces what does not. if the garden is a jungle, or if the layout does not work for you, a full reset is the right choice. see our guide on restoring an overgrown garden for the renovation approach. get a quote for your garden reset contact us on whatsapp to discuss your garden reset. we can do the whole job or phase it over time. we arrange all quotes during a free site visit. whatsapp get a quote frequently asked questions how long does a full garden reset take? a small courtyard takes 1 to 2 weeks. a family garden takes 2 to 4 weeks. a large garden takes 4 to 8 weeks. phased over years, each phase takes 1 to 2 weeks. do i need a garden designer? for a full reset, a design plan is worth the investment. it ensures the layout works and the planting suits the conditions. see our garden design service or browse our chiltern garden design plan pdfs . can i do some of the work myself? yes. many clients do the planting themselves to save money, and we do the clearance, soil work garden waste removal costs: what affects the price? real 2026 uk garden waste removal costs. per tonne pricing, skip hire, hippobag and man-with-van options. what drives the cost and how to save. garden waste removal costs: what affects the price? garden prices & costs garden waste removal is often the hidden cost of any clearance or tidy-up. the cutting and digging is the visible work, but getting rid of the waste can add 20 to 40 percent to the bill. this guide explains what garden waste removal costs in the uk in 2026, the different options available, and how to keep the price down. quick answer garden waste removal in the uk in 2026 costs between 120 and 200 per tonne including transport and disposal. for a typical family garden clearance producing 1 to 2 tonnes, expect 120 to 400. for larger clearances producing 4 to 6 tonnes, expect 480 to 1,200. garden waste removal options man with van (per tonne): 120 to 200 skip hire (6 yard): 200 to 350 hippobag (1 tonne megabag): 150 to 200 council green waste bin (annual): 60 to 100 trip to council tip (free but time): 0 what affects the cost of garden waste removal volume of waste the biggest factor. waste is charged per tonne. a standard family garden clearance produces 1 to 2 tonnes. a heavy clearance of a neglected garden can produce 4 to 6 tonnes. type of waste green waste (leaves, grass, branches) is cheapest to dispose of. mixed waste (green waste plus soil, rubble, timber) is more expensive because it has to be sorted. soil and rubble are classed as inert waste and are cheaper per tonne but heavier. access if we can get a tipper truck to the garden, loading is quick. if waste has to be barrowed through the house or up a long path, loading takes longer and costs more. distance to the disposal site the further the garden is from a licensed disposal site, the higher the transport cost. in the chilterns, most gardens are within 20 minutes of a disposal site, so transport is not a major factor. waste removal options compared man with van (us) we bring a tipper truck, load the waste, and take it to a licensed disposal site. you get a record of the disposal. cost: 120 to 200 per tonne. best for: any clearance where you want us to handle everything. skip hire you hire a skip, we fill it, the skip company collects it. cost: 200 to 350 for a 6 yard skip (holds about 3 tonnes). you need a permit if the skip goes on the road (30 to 80). best for: large clearances where you have space for a skip. hippobag a large bag you fill yourself, then a company collects it. cost: 150 to 200 for a 1 tonne megabag. best for: small clearances where you want to do the loading yourself. council green waste bin an annual subscription to your council green waste scheme. cost: 60 to 100 per year. best for: ongoing light waste from regular maintenance. too slow for clearance work. trip to the council tip free for household waste, but most tips limit you to 6 bags per visit. a typical clearance produces why cheap garden clearance quotes often cost more later why the cheapest garden clearance quote is rarely the best value. hidden costs, fly-tipping risks, and what to check before accepting a cheap quote. why cheap garden clearance quotes often cost more later garden prices & costs we have all seen the advertisements. garden clearance from 99 pounds. cheapest in the area. no job too small. but cheap garden clearance quotes often cost more in the long run. this guide explains why, what to check before accepting a cheap quote, and how to spot the warning signs of a clearance that will end in tears. quick answer cheap garden clearance quotes (under 200 for a full garden) are usually cheap for a reason. the most common problems are: waste fly-tipped and traced back to you (fine up to 5,000 pounds), incomplete work requiring a second clearance, hidden charges added on the day, and damage to your property. a proper clearance from an insured gardener costs more upfront but is better value. warning signs of a cheap clearance no insurance no waste disposal records cash only no business address pressure to decide on the day quote significantly cheaper than others the hidden costs of cheap garden clearance 1. fly-tipping risk (the big one) if a gardener offers to take your waste away for 50, ask yourself: how are they disposing of it legally? licensed disposal costs 120 to 200 per tonne. if they are charging 50 for a job that produces 2 tonnes of waste, the maths does not work. the waste is being fly-tipped. if fly-tipped waste is traced back to you (and it often is, through letters and receipts in the bags), you can be fined up to 5,000 pounds under the environmental protection act. you are legally responsible for ensuring your waste is disposed of legally, even if you paid someone else to do it. 2. incomplete work a cheap quote often means the gardener plans to do less work. they cut the top growth but leave the roots. they take the easy waste but leave the heavy stuff. they sweep the visible areas but leave the corners. you end up needing a second clearance to finish the job, which costs more than a proper clearance would have. 3. hidden charges the quote is 150. but on the day, there is a 50 charge for waste removal, a 30 charge for heavy bramble, a 40 charge for access, and a 20 charge for sweeping. the final bill is 290. this is a common tactic with cheap clearance companies. 4. damage to your property cheap clearers often work fast and carelessly. they drag brambles through the house, scratch doors, damage lawns with heavy wheelbarrows, and break fence panels. if they have no insurance, you have no recourse. 5. no aftercare or guarantee a cheap clearer takes the money and disappears. if the brambles grow back in 3 months because the roots were not dug out, there is no one to call. a proper gardener will come back and sort out any issues. what to check before accepting any clearance quote 1. public liability insurance ask for proof of public liability insurance. this covers damage to your property. any reputable gardener will have it and will be happy to show you. 2. waste disposal ask how they dispose of the waste. the answer should be a licensed disposal facility. ask for the disposal record after the job. if they cannot or will not provide this, do not use them. 3. what is included get the quote in writing and check what is included. does it include waste removal? root digging? sweeping? a fixed price quote means no surprises on the day. 4. reviews and references ask for references from previous clients. check online reviews. a gardener with no reviews and no references is a risk. 5. business address and landline a gardener with only a mobile number and no business address is hard to track down if something goes wrong. a proper business has a traceable presence. the real cost of a proper clearance a proper garden clearance from an insured gardener costs more than a cheap quote, but it is better value. for a family garden, expect 400 to 900. this includes: all cutting, digging and clearance work all waste removed and disposed of legally disposal records provided public liability insurance a fixed price with no hidden charges someone to call if there are issues see our garden clearance cost guide for detail on what a proper clearance should cost. how to spot a reputable gardener they have insurance and can prove it they dispose of waste legally and provide records they give fixed price quotes in writing they have reviews and references they have a business address and landline they are happy to answer questions they do not pressure you to decide on the day their price is in line with other reputable gardeners get a proper clearance quote we are fully insured, dispose of all waste legally, and give fixed price quotes in writing. contact us on whatsapp and we will aim to respond within one day. whatsapp get a quote frequently asked questions why are some clearance quotes so cheap? usually because they are cutting corners: fly-tipping the waste, doing less work, or adding hidden charges on the day. a proper clearance costs what it costs. can i be fined if my gardener fly-tips my waste? yes. under the environmental protection act, you are responsible for ensuring your waste is disposed of legally, even if you paid someone else to do it. fines can be up to 5,000 pounds. what should a proper clearance cost? for a family garden, 400 to 900 including waste removal. see our clearance cost guide for detail. further reading how much does garden clearance cost? diy how much does estate garden maintenance cost in buckinghamshire and oxfordshire? real 2026 estate garden maintenance costs in buckinghamshire and oxfordshire. what makes estate gardens different, pricing and what is included. how much does estate garden maintenance cost in buckinghamshire and oxfordshire? garden prices & costs estate gardens are a different proposition to family gardens. they are larger, more complex, and require a different approach to maintenance. this guide explains what estate garden maintenance costs in buckinghamshire and oxfordshire in 2026, what makes estate gardens different, and what to expect for your money. quick answer estate garden maintenance in buckinghamshire and oxfordshire in 2026 costs between 400 and 1,200 per month for a typical 1 to 3 acre garden. larger estates of 3 to 5 acres cost 1,200 to 2,500 per month. estates over 5 acres are priced individually. most estate clients have weekly or twice-weekly visits. estate garden maintenance price ranges 1 to 2 acres (weekly visits): 400 to 800 per month 2 to 3 acres (weekly visits): 800 to 1,200 per month 3 to 5 acres (twice weekly): 1,200 to 2,500 per month 5+ acres (full time gardener): 2,500 to 5,000+ per month what makes estate gardens different scale an estate garden is typically 1 acre or more. this means more lawn to mow, more hedge to cut, more border to weed, and more path to sweep. a 2 acre garden is not twice the work of a 1 acre garden, it is more like 2.5 times, because the logistics get more complex. complexity estate gardens usually have multiple distinct areas: formal lawns, woodland, wildflower meadows, kitchen gardens, orchards, ponds, tennis courts. each area needs different care at different times of year. mature planting estate gardens often have mature trees, specimen shrubs and established hedges that need specialist care. a 100 year old yew hedge needs different treatment to a 5 year old privet. staff and equipment estate gardens need bigger equipment: ride-on mowers, tractor-mounted flails, cherry pickers for tall hedges. they also need more staff on each visit. year-round interest estate owners expect the garden to look good all year, not just in summer. this means careful planning of winter structure, spring bulbs, summer flowering and autumn colour. what is included in estate garden maintenance lawn mowing and edging (all lawns, weekly in season) hedge cutting (formal hedges 2 to 3 times per year, informal hedges annually) border maintenance (weeding, deadheading, pruning, dividing) tree work (small tree pruning, fruit tree care) path and driveway maintenance (sweeping, pressure washing, weed control) leaf clearance (autumn) mulching (annual) lawn feeding and treatment pond maintenance greenhouse and kitchen day, 2 gardeners): 24,000 to 33,600 per year hedge cutting (3 times per year): 2,000 to 4,000 per year tree work: 1,000 to 3,000 per year seasonal planting: 1,000 to 2,000 per year total: 28,000 to 42,600 per year 5 acre garden, twice weekly visits twice weekly maintenance (2 days, 2 gardeners): 48,000 to 67,200 per year hedge cutting: 4,000 to 8,000 per year tree work: 3,000 to 6,000 per year of high-maintenance features. replace formal lawns with wildflower meadows. replace tender bedding with perennial borders. replace high hedges with lower ones. invest in equipment a ride-on mower, a lawn renovation in spring: what uk gardens need after winter spring lawn renovation guide for uk gardens. what to do after winter, when to scarify, aerate and overseed in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire and berkshire. lawn renovation in spring: what uk gardens need after winter lawn care & lawn repair spring is the natural time for lawn renovation. after a wet uk winter, most lawns have accumulated thatch, moss and compaction. a spring renovation addresses these problems and sets the lawn up for a healthy growing season. this guide explains what to do, when to do it, and how to get the best results from a spring lawn renovation in the chilterns, oxfordshire, buckinghamshire and berkshire. quick answer spring lawn renovation in the uk is best done in march to may when the soil is warming up and there is regular rain. the process is: moss treatment, scarification, aeration, top dressing, overseeding and feeding. a full renovation on a 50 sqm lawn costs 400 to 600 pounds if we do it, or 80 to 150 pounds in materials if you do it yourself. lawns in the chilterns often need extra attention because the chalky free-draining soil dries out fast in summer. why spring is the right time spring is the best time for lawn renovation because the conditions are ideal for grass seed germination. the soil is warming up, there is regular rainfall, and the grass is coming out of winter dormancy and ready to grow. autumn is the other good time, but spring is better if the lawn has suffered winter damage from frost, waterlogging or disease. in the chilterns, spring soil temperatures typically reach 8 to 10 degrees celsius by mid-march, which is warm enough for grass seed to germinate. gardens on the chalk hills around high wycombe, princes risborough and wendover tend to warm up earlier than those in the clay vale around aylesbury, where cold, wet soil persists longer into spring. what winter does to a uk lawn a typical uk winter causes several problems that need addressing in spring: moss growth: wet, cold conditions favour moss over grass. lawns in shaded gardens around marlow and henley-on-thames are particularly vulnerable thatch accumulation: dead grass and moss build up at the base, preventing water and air reaching the roots compaction: wet soil gets compacted by foot traffic, children playing and mowing. clay lawns in aylesbury and didcot are especially prone drainage issues: waterlogging damages grass roots and encourages moss. low-lying gardens near the thames in reading and wallingford often suffer bare patches: from frost damage, disease, dog urine or waterlogging over winter the spring lawn renovation process step 1: moss treatment (2 weeks before scarifying) apply iron sulphate at 5g per square metre to kill the moss. the moss will turn black within 2 weeks, ready for scarification. iron sulphate also greens up the grass and slightly acidifies the surface, which most grasses tolerate well. on chalky chiltern soils, this mild acidification is actually beneficial as it helps unlock nutrients that are locked up by the high ph. step 2: scarification (march to april) use a powered scarifier to remove the dead moss and thatch. a 50 sqm lawn will produce 4 to 6 bin bags of material. this looks drastic but is essential for a healthy lawn. after scarifying, the lawn will look bare and thin, but this is normal. the overseeding in step 4 will fill it back in. step 3: aeration (march to may) hollow-tine or solid-tine aeration relieves compaction and improves drainage. push the tines 10cm deep every 10cm. fill the holes with a 70/30 sand and soil mix. on heavy clay soils in aylesbury and bicester, hollow-tine aeration is particularly important because it physically removes plugs of compacted clay and allows air into the root zone. step 4: top dressing (april) apply a thin layer (5mm) of 70/30 sand and soil mix across the lawn. brush it into the surface with a stiff broom. this improves drainage and levels minor hollows. on chalky soils around high wycombe and amersham, a heavier top dressing can help build up thin soil over the chalk. step 5: overseeding (april to may) sow new grass seed at 25g per square metre. choose a seed mix suited to your conditions. for shaded lawns in beaconsfield and gerrards cross, use a shade-tolerant fescue mix. for family lawns in oxford and witney, use a hard-wearing ryegrass. for chalky chiltern gardens, use a drought-tolerant mix. see our lawn planning tool for seed recommendations, or buy our chiltern lawn care guide pdfs for full seed mix percentages. step 6: feeding (may) apply a spring lawn fertiliser at 30g per square metre. water it in if no rain within 2 days. a spring feed gives the grass the nutrients it needs to recover from renovation and grow strongly through the summer. on chalky soils, choose a fertiliser with iron for moss control and avoid high-nitrogen feeds that produce soft growth vulnerable to drought. spring lawn renovation calendar month job notes for chiltern gardens march moss treatment, first scarification chalk soils warm up first on south-facing slopes april aeration, top dressing, overseeding clay soils in the vale may still be too wet may spring feed, first cut of renovated lawn water regularly if the chilterns have a dry spring june regular mowing, watering if dry chalky soils dry out fast, water deeply once a week aftercare water the lawn regularly for 3 weeks after overseeding avoid walking on the lawn for 2 weeks after overseeding do not mow until the new grass is 7 to 8cm tall set the mower high for the first cut, then gradually lower on chalky chiltern soils, water deeply once a week through dry spells diy vs professional spring renovation diy costs 80 to 150 pounds in materials (iron sulphate, seed, fertiliser, top dressing) plus tool hire (scarifier 50 pounds per day, aerator 50 pounds per day). professional costs 400 to 600 pounds for a 50 sqm lawn including everything. for larger gardens in the chilterns, professional renovation is often better value because we have the equipment, the experience and the seed knowledge to get it right first time. see our lawn renovation cost guide for detail. get a quote for spring lawn renovation if your lawn in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire needs spring renovation, contact us on whatsapp with a few photos and we will aim to respond within one day. whatsapp get a quote frequently asked questions can i renovate my lawn in february? it is too early. the soil is too cold for seed germination. wait until march at the earliest, when soil temperatures reach 8 degrees celsius. how long until i see results? moss treatment works in 2 weeks. new grass appears 2 to 3 weeks after overseeding. full recovery takes 6 to 8 weeks. on chalky chiltern soils, recovery may take longer if spring is dry. will the renovation fix my moss problem permanently? only if the underlying cause is addressed. if moss is caused by shade, compaction or poor drainage, the renovation will fix it. if the cause is not addressed, moss will return. see our moss guide . further reading why is my lawn full of moss? aeration, should you returf or reseed your lawn? returf vs reseed: which is better for your uk lawn? cost comparison, time to results, and when each option makes sense for chiltern gardens. should you returf or reseed your lawn? lawn care & lawn repair if your lawn is beyond renovation, the choice is between returfing and reseeding. both have their place, but the right choice depends on your budget, your timeline and how much disruption you can tolerate. this guide compares the two options with real 2026 uk pricing and specific advice for gardens in the chilterns, oxfordshire, buckinghamshire and berkshire. quick answer reseeding costs 2 to 4 pounds per square metre and takes 6 to 12 weeks to establish. returfing costs 10 to 25 pounds per square metre and gives an instant lawn. reseeding is better for large areas and budget-conscious gardeners in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire. returfing is better when you need an instant result or have a small area to repair in berkshire or bedfordshire. cost comparison (50 sqm lawn) reseed: 100 to 200 pounds in seed, plus soil preparation returf: 500 to 1,250 pounds including turf and laying professional reseeding: 300 to 600 pounds professional returfing: 700 to 1,500 pounds reseeding: pros and cons pros cheaper (2 to 4 pounds per sqm) - ideal for large gardens in the chilterns wider choice of seed varieties - choose drought-tolerant mixes for chalky soils in high wycombe or shade-tolerant mixes for wooded gardens in beaconsfield better root establishment - roots grow into the native soil rather than being drought-tolerant fescue mix. on clay soils in aylesbury and didcot, use a ryegrass mix that tolerates wetter conditions. in shaded gardens in beaconsfield and marlow, use a shade-tolerant fescue laying turf on top of old grass will result in drainage problems and the old grass rotting underneath. what is the best time to reseed or returf? autumn (september to october) is ideal for both. why your lawn is patchy and how to fix it patchy lawn? identify the cause and fix it. dog damage, shade, compaction, disease and pests all cause patches in uk lawns. here is how to fix each one. why your lawn is patchy and how to fix it lawn care & lawn repair a patchy lawn is one of the most common complaints we hear from garden owners across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire. patches can be caused by anything from dog urine to shade to disease. the fix depends on the cause. this guide walks through the most common causes of patchy lawns in the chilterns and how to fix each one. quick answer patchy lawns are caused by shade, compaction, dog damage, drought, disease, pests, poor mowing or poor feeding. identify the cause first, then fix it. most patches can be repaired by overseeding with the right seed mix. for widespread patches, a full lawn renovation may be needed. common causes of patchy lawns in the chilterns 1. shade the most common cause in our area. grass needs at least 4 hours of direct sun. under trees, along north-facing walls and beside fences in gardens around beaconsfield, gerrards cross and great missenden, grass thins and patches develop. the mature trees that make chiltern gardens so attractive are also the main cause of shade-related lawn patches. fix: prune trees to let in light, or overseed with a shade-tolerant fescue mix. see our lawn planning tool for seed recommendations. in heavily shaded areas, consider replacing the lawn with shade-tolerant ground cover. 2. compaction heavy foot traffic, children playing and heavy mowing compact the soil. grass roots cannot penetrate compacted soil, so the grass thins. this is particularly common on clay soils in aylesbury, bicester and didcot, where the soil compacts easily when wet. fix: aerate annually with a hollow-tine aerator. see our aeration guide . 3. dog damage dog urine contains nitrogen that burns the grass, creating circular patches. female dogs cause more damage because they squat rather than lift. this is a common problem in family gardens across oxfordshire and buckinghamshire. fix: water the area immediately after the dog urinates to dilute the nitrogen. for existing patches, rake out the dead grass, add soil and overseed with a hard-wearing ryegrass mix. 4. drought in dry summers, grass goes dormant and patches appear. this is natural and the grass usually recovers when rain returns. however, on the free-draining chalk soils of the chilterns around high wycombe, stokenchurch and watlington, drought patches can become permanent if the dry spell is long enough. fix: water deeply once a week during drought. see our dry summer lawn guide . overseed drought-damaged areas in autumn with a drought-tolerant fescue mix. 5. disease fungal diseases like red thread, fairy rings and snow mould cause patches. these are more common in wet conditions and on lawns that are over-fed with nitrogen. lawns in the thames valley around reading and twyford can be particularly vulnerable to fungal diseases in wet autumns. fix: improve drainage, reduce thatch, and apply a fungicide if needed. see rhs lawn disease guidance . 6. pests leatherjackets (crane fly larvae) and chafer grubs eat grass roots, causing patches. birds pecking at the soil for the grubs make it worse. this is an increasing problem across the chilterns and thames valley. fix: apply a biological nematode treatment in autumn. see rhs leatherjacket guidance . 7. poor mowing cutting too short scalps the grass and weakens it. this is a common problem when homeowners cut the lawn themselves and set the mower too low, especially during the hot chiltern summers when the grass is already stressed. fix: never cut more than one third of the grass height in one mowing. set the mower to 30 to 40mm for family lawns, higher in dry weather. 8. poor feeding under-fed grass is weak and patchy. on chalky chiltern soils, nutrients are easily leached out, so regular feeding is even more important than on heavier soils. fix: apply a balanced fertiliser in spring and a low-nitrogen feed in autumn. how to repair patches rake out the dead grass with a spring-tine rake loosen the soil surface with a garden fork add a thin layer of topsoil or compost sow seed at 30g per square metre rake in lightly and water keep moist for 3 weeks protect from birds with netting use our lawn planning tool for a seed variety recommendation based on your lawn conditions, use our lawn planning tool . it recommends the right seed for your soil type, sun exposure and main problem. get help with your patchy lawn if your lawn in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire is patchy and you want professional help, contact us on whatsapp with a few photos. whatsapp get a quote frequently asked questions how do i know if my patches are caused by pests? lift a section of turf. if you see white grubs or grey-brown larvae in the soil, you have chafer grubs or leatherjackets. birds pecking at the lawn is also a sign. can i repair patches in winter? no. grass seed will not germinate in cold soil. wait until march or april when the soil warms up. how long do patches take to fill in? with the right seed and regular watering, patches fill in within 3 to 6 weeks in spring or autumn. further reading why is my lawn full of moss? how to repair a lawn after (60/40) to improve water retention. step 4: overseeding sow a hard-wearing ryegrass mix at 30g per square metre. ryegrass tolerates children, dogs and heavy footfall better than fine grasses. for lawn scarification: when is it worth doing? is lawn scarification worth it? what it does, when to do it, and how it benefits lawns in the chilterns, oxfordshire, buckinghamshire and berkshire. lawn scarification: when is it worth doing? lawn care & lawn repair scarification is one of those lawn jobs that looks brutal but is essential for a healthy lawn. it removes the thatch and moss that build up over time, letting air, water and nutrients reach the roots. but is it always worth doing? this guide explains when scarification is worth it for lawns in the chilterns and when you can skip it. quick answer scarification is worth doing if your lawn has more than 5mm of thatch, has moss, or feels spongy underfoot. it costs 2 to 4 pounds per square metre professionally, or 50 pounds per day to hire a scarifier. for lawns in good condition with no thatch or moss, scarification is not necessary. lawns on chalky chiltern soils around high wycombe and princes risborough often need scarification because the free-draining soil encourages thatch buildup. what is scarification? scarification uses a powered machine with vertical blades to cut into the lawn surface, pulling out thatch (dead grass and moss) and opening up the soil. it looks drastic, and the lawn will look terrible for a week or two afterwards, but it is essential for long-term lawn health. when scarification is worth it your lawn has visible moss (common in shaded chiltern gardens in beaconsfield and amersham) the lawn feels spongy underfoot (a sign of thatch) water puddles on the surface instead of soaking in (common on clay soils in aylesbury) the grass is thinning despite feeding you have not scarified for 2 or more years you are planning to overseed (scarification prepares the seedbed) when scarification is not worth it the lawn is in good condition with no thatch or moss the lawn was scarified in the last 12 months the lawn is newly laid (under 12 months old) the soil is very dry (scarifying dry soil damages the grass) when to scarify in the chilterns the best times are mid-spring (april) or early autumn (september to october). the soil needs to be warm and moist so the grass can recover quickly. on chalky soils in the chilterns, spring scarification is often better because the soil warms up early. on clay soils in the vale around aylesbury, autumn scarification may be better because the soil is too wet in spring. avoid scarifying in summer (too dry, especially on chalky soils) or winter (too cold for recovery). the scarification process mow the lawn short apply moss killer 2 weeks before (if moss is present) scarify in two directions (north-south, then east-west) rake up all the removed material aerate if needed overseed bare patches apply a balanced feed water and keep moist for 3 weeks how much thatch does scarification remove? a 50 sqm lawn with moderate thatch will produce 4 to 6 bin bags of material. it is surprising how much comes out. this is normal and means the scarification has done its job. diy vs professional scarification diy: hire a scarifier for 50 pounds per day, plus your time. professional: 100 to 200 pounds for a 50 sqm lawn including waste removal. professional is better value if you include aeration, overseeding and feeding in the same visit. see our lawn recovery service . get a quote for scarification if your lawn in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire needs scarifying, contact us on whatsapp. whatsapp get a quote frequently asked questions will scarification kill my lawn? no. it looks bad for 1 to 2 weeks but the grass recovers quickly with watering and feeding. the lawn will be healthier afterwards. how often should i scarify? once a year for lawns with moss or thatch. every 2 years for lawns in good condition. lawns on chalky chiltern soils may need annual scarification because thatch builds up faster in free-draining soil. further reading aeration, overseeding and top dressing explained why is my lawn full of moss? aeration, overseeding and top dressing explained what is aeration, overseeding and top dressing? why they matter, when to do them, and how they work together for healthier lawns in the chilterns. aeration, overseeding and top dressing explained lawn care & lawn repair aeration, overseeding and top dressing are the three pillars of lawn renovation. they work together to relieve compaction, introduce new grass and improve the soil surface. this guide explains what each one does, when to do it, and how they work together for lawns across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire. quick answer aeration relieves compaction by creating holes in the lawn. overseeding sows new grass seed into the existing lawn. top dressing adds a thin layer of soil mix to improve the surface. together, they form the core of lawn renovation. cost: 200 to 500 pounds for a professional treatment on a 50 sqm lawn in the chilterns. aeration what it does aeration creates holes in the lawn to relieve compaction, improve drainage and let air, water and nutrients reach the roots. on clay soils in aylesbury, bicester and didcot, aeration is the single most beneficial treatment you can do. when to do it spring (march to may) or autumn (september to october) when the soil is moist but not waterlogged. on chalky chiltern soils, spring aeration is ideal because the soil is already free-draining. on clay soils, autumn aeration is better because spring soil is often too wet. how to do it use a garden fork for small lawns, or hire a hollow-tine aerator for larger lawns. push the tines 10cm deep every 10cm. hollow-tine removes plugs of soil, which is more effective than solid-tine on compacted clay. cost 1.50 to 3 pounds per square metre professionally, or 50 pounds per day to hire an aerator. overseeding what it does overseeding sows new grass seed into the existing lawn to thicken it, fill bare patches and introduce improved grass varieties. for chiltern gardens, overseeding with drought-tolerant fescues on chalk or hard-wearing ryegrass on clay can dramatically improve lawn quality. when to do it spring (april to may) or autumn (september to october) when the soil is warm enough for germination. on chalky soils around high wycombe and amersham, autumn overseeding is often better because the soil retains some warmth from summer. how to do it sow seed at 25g per square metre after scarification and aeration. rake in lightly and water. keep moist for 3 weeks. protect from birds with netting. seed choice for chiltern gardens choose a seed mix suited to your conditions. see our lawn planning tool for recommendations, or buy our chiltern lawn care guide pdfs for full seed mix percentages. cost 1 to 2 pounds per square metre including seed. top dressing what it does top dressing adds a thin layer of soil mix to the lawn surface to improve drainage, level minor hollows and fill aeration holes. on chalky chiltern soils, top dressing helps build up thin soil over the chalk. on clay soils in the vale, top dressing with sand improves drainage. when to do it spring or autumn, after aeration and before overseeding. how to do it apply a 70/30 sand and soil mix at 3 to 5kg per square metre. brush it into the surface with a stiff broom. do not smother the grass. on chalky soils, use a 60/40 soil/sand mix. on clay soils, use an 80/20 sand/soil mix. cost 2 to 4 pounds per square metre including material. how they work together the three treatments work best when done together as part of a lawn renovation: scarify to remove thatch and moss aerate to relieve compaction top dress to improve the surface and fill aeration holes overseed to introduce new grass feed to help the new grass establish see our spring lawn renovation guide for the full process. get a quote for lawn renovation if your lawn in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire needs aeration, overseeding and top dressing, contact us on whatsapp. whatsapp get a year? no. top dressing every 2 to 3 years is sufficient for most lawns. aeration and overseeding can be done annually. what is the best seed for chalky chiltern soil? a drought-tolerant fescue and how to keep a lawn green during dry summers in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire keep your lawn green during dry summers in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire. watering, mowing, feeding and drought-tolerant seed for chiltern chalk soil lawns. how to keep a lawn green during dry summers in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire lawn care & lawn repair dry summers are becoming more common in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire, and lawns suffer first. the chalky chiltern soils drain freely, which is good in winter but means lawns dry out fast in summer. this guide explains how to keep your lawn green during dry spells in the chilterns without wasting water. quick answer to keep a lawn green during dry summers in the chilterns: water deeply once a week (not daily), mow high (40mm+), leave clippings on the lawn, and overseed with a drought-tolerant fescue mix in autumn. if you cannot water, let the lawn go dormant and it will recover when rain returns. most lawns on chalky soil around high wycombe, princes risborough and watlington can survive 4 to 6 weeks of dormancy. why lawns go brown in summer grass goes dormant when water is scarce. this is a survival mechanism, not a sign the grass is dead. the grass stops growing and turns brown to conserve moisture. when rain returns, the grass greens up within 2 weeks. most lawns can survive 4 to 6 weeks of dormancy without permanent damage. however, on the very thin chalky soils of the chiltern escarpment, prolonged drought can kill patches of grass permanently. watering strategy for chiltern lawns how much water? lawns need 25mm of water per week in dry weather. that is about 25 litres per square metre. a sprinkler delivers about 10mm per 30 minutes, so run it for 1 to 1.5 hours per week per area. how often? water deeply once a week, not lightly every day. deep watering encourages deep roots, which makes the grass more drought-resistant. daily light watering encourages shallow roots, which is the worst thing for a chiltern lawn on free-draining chalk. when to water? early morning (5am to 8am) is best. watering in the evening leaves the grass wet overnight, which encourages disease. watering in the middle of the day loses too much to evaporation. watering restrictions check for hosepipe bans in your area. during a ban, you can only water with a watering can. prioritise newly seeded areas and newly planted trees and shrubs. mowing strategy for dry weather raise the mower to 40mm or higher never cut more than one third of the grass height leave clippings on the lawn to act as mulch and retain moisture mow less frequently in dry weather keep the mower blade sharp to reduce water loss from cut ends feeding for drought resistance apply a potash-rich feed in late spring to strengthen the grass for summer. potash helps grass cope with drought stress. avoid high-nitrogen feeds in summer, as they produce soft growth that is vulnerable to drought. on chalky chiltern soils, a low-nitrogen, high-potash feed is ideal. overseeding with drought-tolerant grass the best long-term solution is to overseed with a drought-tolerant grass mix. deep-rooting fescues survive dry chiltern summers better than shallow-rooted ryegrass. overseed in autumn (september to october) so the new grass establishes before the next summer. see our lawn planning tool for seed recommendations. when to let the lawn go dormant if you cannot water (hosepipe ban, water meter, or environmental concerns), let the lawn go dormant. most uk lawns survive 4 to 6 weeks of dormancy without permanent damage. avoid walking on a dormant lawn, as the grass is fragile. it will green up when rain returns. lawns on chalky soil in the chilterns may not recover if the dormancy lasts longer than 6 weeks, so monitor the forecast. get help with your lawn if your lawn in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire is struggling in dry weather, contact us on whatsapp. whatsapp get a quote frequently asked questions will my lawn die if it goes brown? in most cases, no. grass goes dormant, not dead. it will recover when rain returns. on very thin chalky soils, prolonged drought (6+ weeks) can kill patches. how much water does my lawn need? 25mm per week, which is about 25 litres per square metre. on chalky chiltern soil, you may need more as the water drains through quickly. further reading aeration, overseeding and top dressing explained best drought-tolerant plants the 2026/27 chilterns heatwave guide ant ants rat drought dry disease may september sep sept october oct spring summer autumn winter late spring lawn care mulch feeding aeration overseeding seeding mowing mow shrub shrubs tree trees plant plants soil chilterns chiltern oxfordshire oxford buckinghamshire buckingham berkshire berks bedfordshire high wycombe wycombe princes risborough reading iver lawn leaf root seed sun cost how best maintenance

chalk soil in the chilterns: what grows well and what struggles chalk soil gardening in the chilterns. what plants thrive on alkaline chalky soil in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire, what struggles, and how to improve it. chalk soil in the chilterns: what grows well and what struggles soil, compost & mulch the chilterns are made of chalk, and the soil reflects it. chalky soil is alkaline, free-draining and often shallow. this is great news for some plants and bad news for others. this guide explains what grows well on chalk soil in the chilterns, what struggles, and how to improve chalk soil for plants that need different conditions in gardens across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire. quick answer chalk soil is alkaline (ph 7.5 to 8.5), free-draining and often shallow. plants that thrive: lavender, rosemary, salvias, alliums, beech, yew, clematis and most mediterranean plants. plants that struggle: rhododendrons, camellias, blueberries, heathers and other acid-loving plants. improve chalk soil by adding organic matter and growing acid-loving plants in pots of ericaceous compost. what is chalk soil? chalk soil is formed from the calcium carbonate remains of marine organisms. it is alkaline (ph 7.5 to 8.5), free-draining and often shallow (sometimes only 10 to 20cm deep over solid chalk). the chiltern hills are made of chalk, so gardens in high wycombe, amersham, princes risborough, wendover, stokenchurch and surrounding areas have chalky soil. what grows well on chalk soil herbs and mediterranean plants lavender - the classic chiltern plant, loves chalk rosemary - evergreen, drought-tolerant salvia - long-flowering perennial thyme - low-growing ground cover sage - evergreen culinary herb flowering perennials alliums - dramatic球形 flowers in late spring hardy geraniums - long-flowering ground cover verbena bonariensis - tall and airy, butterflies love it nepeta (catmint) - perfect partner for roses poppies - bright colour in early summer shrubs and trees photinia - red new growth in spring choisya - evergreen, scented flowers pittosporum - evergreen structure roses - thrive on chalk if well fed beech - the classic chiltern hedge yew - formal hedges, long-lived climbers clematis - plant deep to avoid wilt star jasmine - evergreen, scented wisteria - needs twice-yearly pruning honeysuckle - native, wildlife-friendly what struggles on chalk soil acid-loving plants cannot tolerate the high ph of chalk soil. they will go yellow (chlorosis) and die. these include: rhododendrons - need ph 4.5 to 6.0 camellias - need acid soil pieris - needs acid soil blueberries - need ph 4.5 to 5.5 heathers (most varieties) - need acid soil if you want to grow these plants on chalk soil in the chilterns, grow them in large pots of ericaceous compost and feed with an ericaceous plant food twice a year. how to improve chalk soil add organic matter chalk soil is often thin and low in organic matter. add a 5cm layer of compost or well-rotted manure each spring and dig it in. this improves water retention and adds nutrients. see our soil improvement guide . mulch annually a 5cm mulch of bark or compost in spring suppresses weeds and retains moisture. chalk soil dries out fast in summer, so mulch is essential for gardens in high wycombe, princes risborough and wendover. water in summer chalk soil drains freely, which means plants can suffer in dry summers. water newly planted specimens regularly through their first summer. grow in raised beds for plants that need deeper soil or different conditions, build raised beds and fill with topsoil. this gives you control over the soil without having to replace the chalk. testing your soil test your soil ph with a simple kit (5 pounds from a garden centre). if the ph is above 7.5, you have chalky soil and should choose plants accordingly. see our guide to the best plants for chalk soil for more recommendations. get help with your chalk soil how to improve soil before planting a new border improve soil before planting a new border and your plants will thrive for years. digging, compost, manure, grit and ph adjustment for chiltern gardens. how to improve soil before planting a new border soil, compost & mulch the single most important thing you can do for a new border is to improve the soil before you plant. plants in good soil establish faster, grow stronger and need less ongoing care. this guide explains how to improve soil before planting a new border in gardens across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire. quick answer to improve soil before planting a new border: dig to 30cm deep, remove weeds and stones, add a 5cm layer of compost or well-rotted manure, add grit on clay soils, and adjust ph if needed. cost: 2 to 5 pounds per square metre in materials, or 8 to 15 pounds per square metre if we do it for you. why soil improvement matters plants spend their whole lives in the soil you give them. if the soil is poor, the plants will be poor. improving the soil before planting is 10 times more effective than trying to improve it afterwards, when the plants are in the way. in the chilterns, where chalky soils are often thin and low in organic matter, soil improvement is particularly important. step 1: dig over the border dig the soil to a depth of 30cm (a spade's depth). break up any compacted layers. remove perennial weed roots (bindweed, couch grass, ground elder). remove large stones. on chalky chiltern soils, you may hit solid chalk within 20cm - dig as deep as you can and consider raised beds if the chalk is too close to the surface. step 2: add organic matter add a 5cm layer of compost or well-rotted manure across the border and dig it in. this improves soil structure, water retention and nutrient content. use garden compost, bagged compost, or well-rotted farmyard manure (not fresh, as it burns plants). on chalky soils around high wycombe and amersham, extra organic matter is essential to improve water retention. step 3: add grit on clay soils on heavy clay soils in aylesbury, bicester and didcot, add horticultural grit at 5kg per square metre and dig it in. this improves drainage and prevents the soil from becoming waterlogged in winter. step 4: adjust ph if needed test the soil ph. most plants prefer ph 6.0 to 7.0. on chalky chiltern soils (ph 7.5 to 8.5), do not try to acidify the soil - it does not work. instead, choose chalk-tolerant plants or grow acid-loving plants in pots. on acidic soils, apply garden lime at 50g per square metre. see our chalk soil guide for detail on alkaline soils. step 5: level and consolidate rake the soil to a level surface. gently firm it by walking on it on your heels. leave it to settle for 2 weeks before planting. this allows the soil to find its natural level and prevents plants from sinking. step 6: mulch after planting after planting, apply a 5cm mulch of bark or compost. this suppresses weeds, retains moisture and continues to improve the soil as it breaks down. soil improvement by soil type in the chilterns chalk soil (high wycombe, amersham, princes risborough, wendover) add compost and grow in raised beds if shallow. chalk soil is alkaline and free-draining, so add organic matter to improve water retention. see our chalk border guide . clay soil (aylesbury, bicester, didcot) add compost and grit. dig in 5cm of compost and 5kg of grit per square metre. clay soil is fertile but needs opening up. sandy soil (some areas of berkshire) add compost and manure. sandy soil drains too freely and is low in nutrients. add extra organic matter to improve water retention. cost of soil improvement compost: 5 to 10 pounds per 100 litres farmyard manure: 6 to 12 pounds per 50 litres horticultural grit: 5 to 8 pounds per 25kg total for 10 sqm border: 30 to 80 pounds in materials get help with soil improvement if you need help improving soil for a new border in oxfordshire, topsoil vs compost: what should you use in your garden? topsoil vs compost: which should you use for lawns, borders, raised beds and pots in the chilterns? real uk advice from working gardeners. topsoil vs compost: what should you use in your garden? soil, compost & mulch topsoil and compost are both soil improvers, but they do different things. topsoil adds bulk and structure. compost adds nutrients and organic matter. using the wrong one wastes money and can harm your plants. this guide explains the difference and when to use each in gardens across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire. quick answer use topsoil for levelling lawns, filling raised beds and creating new borders. use compost for improving existing soil, mulching and feeding. for most garden jobs in the chilterns, a mix of both is best. cost: topsoil 30 to 60 pounds per tonne, compost 5 to 10 pounds per 100 litres. what is topsoil? topsoil is the upper layer of soil, typically the top 15 to 30cm. it contains mineral particles (sand, silt, clay) and some organic matter. bagged topsoil is screened and graded. it is used for bulk: levelling lawns, filling raised beds, creating new borders. in the chilterns, local topsoil is often chalky and alkaline, so imported topsoil may be needed for acid-loving plants. what is compost? compost is decomposed organic matter. it is dark, crumbly and rich in nutrients. garden compost is made from kitchen and garden waste. bagged compost is commercially produced. it is used for improving soil structure, feeding plants and mulching. when to use topsoil levelling a lawn: spread topsoil over hollows and level filling raised beds: mix topsoil with compost for a balanced growing medium creating new borders: dig in topsoil to add depth on shallow chalky chiltern soils building up soil level: raising the grade around a property repairing after building work: replacing soil removed during construction when to use compost improving existing soil: dig in compost to add organic matter mulching: spread on the surface to suppress weeds and retain moisture feeding: compost releases nutrients slowly as it breaks down potting: mix with other ingredients for potting compost lawn top dressing: mix with sand for lawn top dressing when to use both for most garden jobs, a mix of topsoil and compost is best: raised beds: 70% topsoil, 30% compost new borders: dig in 5cm of compost, then top up with topsoil if needed lawn top dressing: 70% topsoil (sandy), 30% compost cost comparison topsoil: 30 to 60 pounds per tonne (bulk), 3 to 5 pounds per 25kg bag compost: 5 to 10 pounds per 100 litres (bagged) farmyard manure: 6 to 12 pounds per 50 litres mushroom compost: 4 to 8 pounds per 50 litres see our guide to compost types for detail on manure, mushroom compost and bark mulch. what not to use subsoil: the layer beneath topsoil. low in nutrients and organic matter. fresh manure: too strong, burns plants. must be rotted for at least 6 months. peat: environmentally damaging. use peat-free alternatives. get help with soil improvement if you need help choosing or sourcing soil improvers in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, should you use mushroom compost, manure or bark mulch? soil, compost & mulch mushroom compost, farmyard manure and bark mulch are the three most common soil improvers, but they do different things and suit different situations. using the wrong one can waste money or even harm your plants. this guide explains the pros and cons of each and when to use them in gardens across the chilterns. quick answer use mushroom compost for alkaline chalk soils and vegetable gardens. use farmyard manure for heavy feeding plants like roses and vegetables. use bark mulch for suppressing weeds and retaining moisture on the surface. cost: mushroom compost 4 to 8 pounds per 50 litres, manure 6 to 12 pounds per 50 litres, bark mulch 5 to 10 pounds per 100 litres. mushroom compost what it is spent compost from mushroom farms. a mix of straw, horse manure, gypsum and chalk. alkaline (ph 7.5 to 8.0). pros cheap and widely available good for alkaline soils (chalk and limestone) - ideal for chiltern how to take control of a large garden without redesigning everything practical uk guide to managing a large garden without a full redesign. zoning, prioritising, phasing work and keeping on top of maintenance. how to take control of a large garden without redesigning everything garden clearance & tidy-up guides large gardens can feel overwhelming. if you have inherited a big garden that has got away from you, the thought of bringing it under control can be daunting. the good news is that you do not need a full redesign. this guide explains how to take control of a large garden in stages, without ripping everything out and starting again. quick answer to take control of a large garden without redesigning: divide the garden into zones, tackle one zone at a time, keep the visible areas tidy first, set up regular maintenance for the managed areas, and leave the wild areas until you have time for them. most large gardens in the chilterns can be brought under control in 1 to 2 years with this approach, working in phases rather than all at once. why large gardens get out of hand large gardens in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire tend to slip out of control for predictable reasons. a typical story: a couple buys a house with a half-acre garden near marlow or henley-on-thames, plans to "do it up next spring", and three years later the brambles have reached the lawn, the hedges have grown 2 metres too tall, and the borders are a thicket of self-seeded sycamores and nettles. the scale of the garden means a single missed season has a multiplier effect. a small garden that is neglected for a year looks untidy. a large garden neglected for a year looks abandoned. the good news is that the same scale works in your favour once you have a plan. a large garden has room for zones, which means you can prioritise visibly and not try to fix everything at once. this guide walks through the zoning method we use when taking on new large-garden clients across the chilterns. step 1: divide the garden into zones a large garden is not one garden, it is several gardens. divide it into zones based on use and visibility. walk the garden with a notebook and a phone full of photos. mark zones on a simple sketch, you do not need a scaled plan at this stage. zone 1: high visibility, high use (patio, front garden, view from the house, the bit visitors see first) zone 2: regular use (lawn, family areas, vegetable garden, children's play area) zone 3: lower use (borders, shrubbery, orchard, side returns) zone 4: wild or unused (woodland edge, bottom of the garden, banks, the bit behind the shed) the boundaries between zones are not always physical. a change of level, a hedge, or a path can mark a zone change. the point is to give yourself permission to leave zone 4 alone while you sort out zone 1. step 2: tackle zone 1 first start with the areas you see and use every day. a tidy patio, mowed front lawn and clean view from the kitchen window make the whole garden feel more manageable, even when zone 4 is still a jungle. this is not just psychology: the visible improvement gives you the motivation to keep going. for most chiltern gardens, zone 1 work is a one-off tidy: mow the lawn, weed the patio, sweep paths, cut back anything overhanging the house, and clear any rubbish. a typical half-day tidy on a zone 1 area costs 150 to 300 pounds if we do it, or a weekend of your own time. see our one-off tidy vs maintenance guide for what is included. step 3: set up regular maintenance for zones 1 and 2 once zone 1 is tidy, set up regular maintenance to keep it that way. fortnightly visits from march to october keep the lawn mowed, borders weeded and paths swept. this stops the visible areas from getting out of hand while you work on the rest. for a large chiltern garden (half an acre plus), fortnightly visits typically run 4 to 6 hours per visit , so 160 to 240 pounds per visit. monthly visits over winter keep the garden ticking. the total annual maintenance cost for zones 1 and 2 of a large garden is usually 2,500 to 4,500 pounds. use our garden care level assessment for a cgm care level assessment. step 4: tackle zone 3 in phases choose one border or area at a time. do not try to do all the borders in one go. spend a weekend on one border: weed, prune, mulch, replant if needed. then move to the next. on chalk soils around high wycombe, princes risborough and wendover, mulching is especially important because the soil dries out fast and mulch keeps moisture in. see our overgrown garden guide for the full clearance process. a phased approach also lets you spread the cost. a typical border renovation of 20 square metres costs 300 to 600 pounds including plants and mulch. if you have four tired borders, that is 1,200 to 2,400 pounds spread over a year, which is more manageable than doing all four at once. step 5: decide what to do with zone 4 wild areas have value for wildlife. consider keeping some of zone 4 as a wild area, mowed once a year in late summer. this is genuinely the lowest-effort option and it supports butterflies, bees and ground-nesting birds. in the chilterns aonb, a wildflower strip at the bottom of the garden can look stunning in june and july with minimal input. if you do want to bring zone 4 into the managed garden, budget for a clearance first (see our clearance service ) and then a phased replanting. a typical zone 4 clearance of 200 square metres costs 600 to 1,500 pounds depending on the density of growth. see our guide to leaving part of your garden wild for the case for keeping it wild. how to prioritise when the garden feels overwhelming, ask yourself four questions: what do i see every day from the house? (fix this first.) what do i use most? (fix this second.) what causes the most stress when it looks untidy? (fix this third.) what is cheapest and quickest to fix? (quick wins build momentum.) this simple test almost always puts zone 1 first and zone 4 last, which is exactly the right order. how to phase the work over time timeframe what to do typical cost month 1 to 2 zone 1 tidy, set up regular maintenance 300 to 600 pounds one-off, plus monthly maintenance month 3 to 6 zone 2 renovation (lawn, borders) 500 to 1,500 pounds month 7 to 12 zone 3 phased border work 1,200 to 2,400 pounds spread over the year year 2 zone 4 decision (wild area or renovation) 0 if kept wild, 600 to 1,500 if cleared common mistakes trying to do everything in one weekend. this leads to exhaustion, half-finished jobs, and giving up. pick one zone and finish it. planting before clearing. new plants in a weedy border will be swamped. always clear and mulch first. skipping the maintenance setup. without regular visits, zone 1 will be back to square one in six months. planting thirsty plants on chalk soil. large-leaved plants like hostas and rodgersias struggle on chalky chiltern soils without constant watering. choose drought-tolerant plants instead. see our drought-tolerant plant guide . frequently asked questions how long does it take to bring a neglected large garden under control? typically 12 to 24 months. the first 3 months get the visible areas tidy and set up maintenance. the next 9 to 12 months are phased border work. after that, the garden is in a steady state and just needs regular maintenance to stay that way. is it cheaper to do it myself or hire a professional? a mix is usually best. do the easy bits yourself (weeding, light pruning, sweeping) and hire a professional for the heavy work (clearance, large hedge cutting, lawn renovation). our base rate is 40 pounds . see our maintenance cost guide for detail. what if i only want to manage part of the garden? that is exactly what zoning is for. many of our large-garden clients only maintain zones 1 and 2 and leave zone 4 as a wild area. this is a sensible, low-cost option. get help with your large garden if you have a large garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire that has got out of hand, send us a few whatsapp photos with your postcode and we will aim to respond within one day with a plan and a quote. whatsapp get a quote further reading how to restore an overgrown garden regular maintenance for large gardens garden zoning ideas garden design mistakes that make maintenance expensive hosta hostas weed bramble brambles nettle nettles ant ants rat drought pest march mar june jun july jul october oct nov dec spring summer winter late summer maintenance garden clearance clearance hedge cutting pruning lawn renovation garden design mulching mulch rubbish sweeping mow hedge hedges shrub planting plant plants soil chilterns chiltern oxfordshire oxford buckinghamshire buckingham berkshire berks bedfordshire high wycombe wycombe marlow princes risborough wendover thame henley henley-on-thames reading weeds sun frost cost when how best overgrown

how to clear brambles properly without them coming back clear brambles properly and stop them regrowing. cut, dig out root crowns, treat regrowth and prevent return. real uk advice from working gardeners. how to clear brambles properly without them coming back garden clearance & tidy-up guides brambles are the gardeners' enemy. they grow fast, tangle into impenetrable thickets, and regrow from any root left in the ground. cutting them down is easy. stopping them coming back is harder. this guide explains how to clear brambles properly so they do not return. quick answer to clear brambles properly: cut the top growth to ground level, dig out every root crown with a mattock, rake up all debris, and monitor for regrowth. treat any regrowth with a glyphosate-based weedkiller in late summer when the plants are sending energy to the roots. on chalky chiltern soils, bramble roots are easier to dig out than on heavy clay in the vale around aylesbury, where the root crowns sit in sticky, wet soil. why brambles are so persistent brambles (rubus fruticosus) are native uk plants with a survival strategy that makes them hard to kill. they spread by seed (bird-dropped), by tip-rooting (the long stems root where they touch the ground), and by suckering from the root crown. a single missed root crown can produce a new thicket in a single growing season. cutting the top growth without digging out the root is a waste of time, the plant will simply regrow from the base. in the chilterns, brambles thrive along woodland edges, in neglected corners, and on the banks of fields and gardens. they are particularly aggressive on the edges of gardens near beech woods around high wycombe, marlow and henley-on-thames, where birds drop seeds and the semi-shade conditions suit them perfectly. step 1: cut the top growth use a brush cutter, secateurs or loppers to cut all the top growth to ground level. wear thick gloves, a long-sleeved top and eye protection, the thorns are vicious and can cause nasty scratches that get infected. cut the stems into manageable lengths and pile them up for removal. a 10 square metre bramble thicket will produce 4 to 6 bin bags of cut material. if the brambles are tangled with fences or trees, work carefully to avoid damaging the underlying structure. pull the cut stems out a handful at a time, rather than yanking whole armfuls which can pull fence panels with them. step 2: dig out the root crowns this is the most important step and the one most people skip. brambles regrow from the root crown, the woody swelling where the stems meet the roots. use a mattock to dig out every crown. go 15 to 20cm deep to get the whole crown. any fragment left will regrow. on chalky soils in the chilterns, the root crowns sit in relatively free-draining soil and come out cleanly with a mattock. on heavy clay in the vale of aylesbury or around didcot, the soil sticks to the roots and you may need to use a spade as well as a mattock. either way, the goal is to remove the entire woody crown, not just chop through it. push the mattock into the soil about 15cm from the crown, lever it back, and lift the crown out. check the hole for any broken-off root fragments. these will regrow if left. step 3: rake up all debris rake up all the cut stems, leaves and root fragments. bramble stems can root where they touch the ground, so do not leave any lying around. burn the waste if you have a burn permit, take it to the tip, or have it removed by us as part of a clearance (see our clearance service ). we take all waste away. step 4: monitor for regrowth check the area every 2 weeks for the first 3 months. any root fragment you missed will produce a new shoot within 4 to 6 weeks in the growing season. dig out any regrowth immediately, do not let it get established. a small shoot is easy to dig out, a 6-month-old thicket is not. step 5: treat stubborn regrowth if regrowth persists after 2 or 3 digs, treat with a glyphosate-based weedkiller in late summer (august to september). at this time of year, brambles are sending energy down to the roots for winter, so the weedkiller is carried down to kill the root system. spray on a dry, calm day when no rain is forecast for 6 hours. follow the label instructions carefully. note: glyphosate is the only weedkiller that genuinely works on brambles. contact weedkillers burn the leaves but do not kill the root. persistent organic gardeners can keep digging out regrowth for 2 to 3 seasons instead, which eventually exhausts the root system. when to clear brambles winter (november to february) is the best time. the brambles have died back, making them easier to cut and lighter to carry. the lack of leaves also means you can see the root crowns more clearly. the ground is softer after winter rain, which makes digging easier. avoid clearing brambles during bird nesting season (march to august) if the thicket is being used by nesting birds, as it is an offence under the wildlife and countryside act 1981 to damage or destroy an active nest. tools you need brush cutter or secateurs and loppers mattock for digging out root crowns spade for heavy clay soils thick gloves, long-sleeved top, and eye protection wheelbarrow for waste rake glyphosate weedkiller and sprayer (for stubborn regrowth only) cost of bramble clearance bramble clearance is priced by area and density. light bramble (mostly top growth, easy roots) costs 8 to 12 per square metre. heavy bramble (mature thicket, root crowns established) costs 15 to 25 per square metre. a typical 20 square metre patch costs 200 to 500 cleared and waste removed. see our bramble clearance cost guide for more detail. common mistakes cutting without digging. just cutting the top growth leaves the root crown in the ground. the bramble will be back within weeks. leaving cut stems on the ground. bramble stems root where they touch soil. always remove every piece. using contact weedkiller. contact weedkillers burn the leaves but do not kill the root. use glyphosate, or just dig. not wearing protection. bramble thorns cause nasty scratches that can get infected. always wear thick gloves and long sleeves. clearing in nesting season without checking. bramble thickets are popular nest sites. always check carefully before clearing between march and august. frequently asked questions will brambles come back if i just cut them? yes. cutting removes the top growth but leaves the root crown, which will regrow within weeks. you must dig out the root crown to permanently remove brambles. is there a weedkiller that kills brambles but not grass? glyphosate will kill any plant it touches, including grass. there is no selective weedkiller for brambles in lawns. the only options are to dig them out, or to carefully paint glyphosate onto the bramble leaves without touching the grass. how long until i can replant the area? immediately after digging out the root crowns. if you have used glyphosate, wait 4 weeks for it to break down before replanting. see our soil preparation guide for what to do next. get help with bramble clearance if you have a bramble problem in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with photos of the area and approximate size, and we will aim to respond within one day with a price range. whatsapp get a how to remove ivy from fences, walls and trees safely remove ivy from fences, walls and trees without causing damage. cut at base, let it die, then pull it off. real uk advice from working gardeners. how to remove ivy from fences, walls and trees safely garden clearance & tidy-up guides ivy is a useful plant for wildlife and shade, but it can damage fences, walls and trees if left unchecked. removing it safely requires care, as pulling live ivy off can damage the surface underneath. this guide explains how to remove ivy from fences, walls and trees without causing damage. quick answer to remove ivy safely: cut the stems at the base, leave the top growth to die for 2 to 3 weeks, then pull it off. never pull live ivy off walls or fences, as the aerial roots will damage the surface. treat the cut stump with a glyphosate-based weedkiller to prevent regrowth. on older properties in the chilterns, check the pointing and render before you start, ivy can hide serious damage. why ivy needs careful removal ivy (hedera helix) is a native uk climber that clings to surfaces with aerial roots. these roots secrete a weak cement-like substance that grips onto brickwork, render, fence panels and tree bark. when you pull live ivy off a surface, the aerial roots come away with chunks of mortar, render or wood, causing real damage. dead ivy, on the other hand, releases its grip as it dries out and can be pulled off without damage. ivy is not always a problem. on sound brick walls it can provide insulation, wildlife habitat and late-season nectar for bees. but on older properties across the chilterns, particularly pre-1920s houses with lime mortar, ivy can loosen the mortar and cause damp. on fences, it adds wind load and shortens the fence's life. in trees, it can shade the canopy and make the tree top-heavy in winter storms. removing ivy from walls step 1: cut at the base cut all the ivy stems at the base of the wall, about 30cm above ground level. use secateurs or loppers. cut a 15cm section out of each stem to prevent them rejoining. this is essential: a single uncut stem will keep the whole plant alive. step 2: let it die leave the top growth to die for 2 to 3 weeks. the leaves will go brown and crisp. this is essential, as the aerial roots will release their grip as the plant dies. in a hot chilterns summer this can take as little as 10 days. in a wet autumn it can take 4 weeks. test a small section first: if the ivy pulls away easily, it is ready. step 3: pull it off once dead, the ivy should pull away from the wall easily. start at the top and work down. use a stiff brush to remove the dried root stubs. if it resists, leave it another week. forcing it will damage the wall. step 4: treat the stump treat the cut stump with a glyphosate-based weedkiller to prevent regrowth. paint it on with a brush rather than spraying, to avoid drift onto other plants. or dig out the root if accessible. on chalky chiltern soils, ivy roots come out fairly easily with a fork. step 5: check the wall check the wall for damage. ivy aerial roots can loosen mortar and render. on older properties around henley, marlow and beaconsfield, repointing may be needed. if the wall has render, check for cracks where water could get in. repoint or repair as needed. removing ivy from fences ivy on fences is easier to remove than on walls, as fence panels are replaceable. cut at the base, let it die, then pull it off. if the fence is old and the ivy has grown into the panels, you may need to replace the affected panels. see our fencing service for fence replacement. a common problem in chiltern gardens is ivy growing through a hedge. in this case, cut the ivy stems at the base and let it die in the hedge. the dead ivy will gradually fall out over a year or two. trying to pull live ivy out of a hedge damages the hedge. removing ivy from trees ivy in trees is not always a problem. it provides shelter and food for wildlife, and it does not parasitise the tree (it uses the tree as a support, not as a food source). but if it is shading the canopy, making the tree top-heavy, or weighing down branches in winter storms, it should be removed. step 1: cut at the base cut all the ivy stems at the base of the tree. cut a 15cm section out of each stem to prevent them rejoining. this kills the ivy in the tree without having to climb up and pull it out. step 2: let it die in the tree leave the ivy to die in the tree. it will gradually fall off over 1 to 2 years. do not try to pull live ivy out of the canopy, as it can break branches. the dead ivy looks untidy for a season but is harmless. step 3: treat the stump treat the cut stump with weedkiller or dig out the root. on clay soils in aylesbury and didcot, ivy roots are easier to dig out than on chalk where they can run along the surface for metres. when not to remove ivy if it is providing valuable wildlife habitat (ivy is one of the latest nectar sources for bees, flowering in october) if it is on a sound wall and not causing damage if it is screening an ugly structure if it is on a tree where it is not shading the canopy or making the tree top-heavy ivy is not always the enemy. it is one of the best plants for wildlife, providing late-season nectar and winter berries for birds. see our honeysuckle profile for an alternative wildlife-friendly climber that is easier to control. common mistakes pulling live ivy off walls. this rips off mortar and render. always cut and let it die first. cutting only some stems. a single uncut stem keeps the whole plant alive. cut every stem at the base. not treating the stump. ivy regrows vigorously from a cut stump. treat with glyphosate or dig out the root. removing ivy during nesting season. dense ivy on walls and trees is a popular nest site. check for nests before cutting between march and august. frequently asked questions does ivy damage brickwork? on sound modern brickwork with hard mortar, ivy usually causes no damage. on older properties with lime mortar (pre-1920s), ivy can loosen the mortar and cause damp. always check the condition of the wall before letting ivy grow on it. will ivy kill my tree? no. ivy is not a parasite, it uses the tree as a support but does not take food or water from it. however, ivy can shade the tree's canopy and make it top-heavy in winter storms. if your tree is healthy and the ivy is not excessive, leave it for the wildlife. how do i stop ivy coming back? dig out the root, or treat the stump with glyphosate. even then, birds drop ivy seeds and new plants will appear. monitor and remove seedlings when small. get help with ivy removal if you have ivy on walls, fences or trees in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with photos and we will aim to respond within one day. whatsapp get a quote further reading how to get rid of ivy without damaging fences and walls how much does bramble and ivy clearance cost? dealing with
