how to tidy a garden after building work tidy a garden after building work. remove rubble, decompact soil, improve soil and replant. real uk advice for post-renovation garden recovery. how to tidy a garden after building work garden clearance & tidy-up guides building work can devastate a garden. builders' vehicles compact the soil, rubble gets buried, and plants are trampled. but with the right approach, a post-building garden can be brought back to life. this guide explains how to tidy a garden after building work, from rubble removal to replanting. quick answer to tidy a garden after building work: remove all rubble and builders' waste, decompact the soil with a fork or aerator, improve the soil with compost, relevel the surface, and replant. cost: 300 to 1,500 pounds depending on the size of the garden and the extent of the damage. in the chilterns, where chalky subsoils are often exposed by builders' diggings, additional topsoil is usually needed before replanting. why building work damages gardens building work is hard on gardens. builders' vehicles compact the soil, sometimes to the density of concrete. excavations leave piles of subsoil, rubble and broken bricks buried in the borders. cement washings kill soil microbes and raise ph. plants are trampled, lawns are destroyed, and drainage is broken. by the time the builders leave, the garden is often in worse shape than if it had been neglected for years. this is a common problem across the chilterns. new builds around bicester, didcot and aylesbury come with thin topsoil over compacted clay or chalk. extensions in marlow, henley and beaconsfield leave established gardens compacted and rubble-strewn. the good news is that with the right approach, a post-building garden can be brought back to life within a single season. step 1: remove all builders' waste builders often leave rubble, offcuts of timber, broken tiles and cement bags in the garden. walk the garden with a wheelbarrow and remove all visible waste. hire a skip (200 to 350 pounds for a 6 yard skip) or use our waste removal service (120 to 200 pounds per tonne). see our waste removal guide for detail. check behind sheds, in borders, and under hedges. builders are notorious for hiding waste where it cannot be seen. a quick probe with a fork will reveal buried rubbish. step 2: remove buried rubble builders sometimes bury rubble in the garden rather than paying to remove it. this is illegal under environmental regulations but it happens. probe the soil with a fork to find buried rubble. dig it out and remove it. this is hard work but essential for healthy plant growth. plants will not grow through a layer of broken bricks. on new-build gardens, the rubble layer is often just below the thin layer of topsoil the developer spread. see our new build soil guide for dealing with this systematically. step 3: decompact the soil builders' vehicles compact the soil, making it hard for plants to grow. use a garden fork to dig over the compacted areas, pushing the fork in 15 to 20cm deep and rocking it back and forth. for large areas, hire a rotavator (50 pounds per day). on heavy clay soils in aylesbury, didcot and bicester, decompaction is particularly important because the clay holds compaction for years. on chalky soils around high wycombe and amersham, the soil is naturally free-draining and recovers faster, but still needs a good fork-over. step 4: improve the soil add a 5cm layer of compost or well-rotted manure and dig it in. this restores the organic matter that was lost during construction. on chalky chiltern soils, use compost rather than manure (manure can be too rich and burn young roots). on clay soils, add horticultural grit as well to improve drainage. if the topsoil has been stripped entirely, you will need to import new topsoil. budget 80 to 120 pounds per cubic metre for screened topsoil delivered. a 10 square metre border with 15cm of new topsoil needs 1.5 cubic metres, so 120 to 180 pounds. see our soil improvement guide for detail. step 5: relevel the surface rake the soil to a level surface. gently firm it by walking on it on your heels. leave it to settle for 2 weeks before planting. this settling time is important: if you plant immediately, the soil will sink and the plants will end up at the wrong depth. for lawns, the surface needs to be perfectly level. use a long straight edge (a 2 metre timber batten) to check for hollows and bumps. fill hollows with topsoil, knock down bumps with the back of a rake. step 6: replant choose plants suited to the soil and light conditions. see our chiltern plant library for recommendations. plant in drifts of 3, 5 or 7 for impact. mulch after planting with a 5cm layer of bark or compost. see our soft landscaping service . on chalky soils, choose mediterranean plants, herbs and drought-tolerant perennials. see our chalk soil planting guide . on clay, choose plants that tolerate wet winters and dry summers. step 7: lawn recovery if the lawn has been damaged, it will need re-establishing. the cheapest option is to reseed (5 to 10 pounds per square metre for seed and preparation). the quicker option is to returf (15 to 25 pounds per square metre laid). see our lawn renovation guide or use our lawn planning tool . common post-building garden problems compacted soil: dig over and add organic matter. plants will not grow in concrete-like soil. buried rubble: probe with a fork and remove. builders often bury rather than skip. changed levels: relevel and reinstate borders. builders often change the ground level without telling you. damaged lawn: reseed or returf. see our turf vs seed guide . cement contamination: remove contaminated soil (cement raises ph and kills microbes). on chalky chiltern soils this is less obvious but still harmful. damaged drainage: check french drains and land drains. builders often break drainage without realising. thin topsoil: common on new builds. import new topsoil before planting. cost of post-building garden recovery job diy cost professional cost waste removal (1 tonne) 200-350 (skip) 120-200 (we remove) soil decompaction (50 sqm) 50 (fork) + weekend 200-400 soil improvement (50 sqm) 150-250 (compost) 300-500 new topsoil (5 cubic metres) 400-600 (delivered) 600-900 (laid) lawn reseed (50 sqm) 100-200 300-500 replanting (50 sqm border) 300-600 (plants) 600-1,200 (plants + labour) frequently asked questions how soon after building work can i plant? as soon as the soil has been decompacted and improved. allow 2 weeks after levelling for the soil to settle before planting. the best planting time is autumn (october to december) when the soil is warm and rain is reliable. will grass grow on compacted soil? not well. compacted soil stops root growth and causes waterlogging. always decompact before seeding or turfing. a lawn on compacted soil will be patchy, mossy and prone to drought. how do i know if there is buried rubble? probe the soil with a garden fork every 30cm or so. if the fork hits something solid, dig down and check. builders often bury rubble in corners and under hedges. see our new build soil guide for a systematic check. get help with post-building garden recovery if your garden has been damaged by building work in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with photos and we how to prepare a garden for professional maintenance how to prepare your garden before starting professional maintenance. what to do, what to leave, and how to get the most from your gardener. how to prepare a garden for professional maintenance garden clearance & tidy-up guides starting with a professional gardener is a big step. the right preparation means your gardener can focus on keeping the garden looking good, rather than dealing with chaos. this guide explains what to do before your gardener starts, so you get the most from the relationship. quick answer to prepare for professional maintenance: have a one-off clearance if the garden is overgrown, agree what is included in each visit, provide access, mark any plants you want kept, and discuss your preferences for pruning and tidying. good preparation means your gardener can focus on keeping the garden looking good rather than dealing with chaos. in the chilterns, where soils vary from chalk to clay within a single garden, this conversation is especially important. why preparation matters starting with a professional gardener is a big step. the first few visits set the tone for the whole relationship. if the gardener arrives to find an overgrown garden with no clear brief, they will spend the first visit just clearing up, which is expensive and frustrating. if they arrive to find a tidy garden with a clear agreement about what is included, they can focus on the maintenance work that keeps the garden looking good long-term. this guide walks through the preparation we ask new clients across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire to do before our first maintenance visit. most of it takes an hour or two of your time and saves many hours of gardener time over the first year. step 1: have a clearance if needed if the garden is overgrown, a one-off clearance before maintenance starts is the right first step. it brings the garden back to a manageable state so the maintenance gardener can focus on keeping it tidy. a clearance typically costs 350 to 750 pounds for a standard family garden, or 750 to 1,500 for a heavily overgrown one. see our clearance service . without a clearance first, the first few maintenance visits are spent just getting on top of the overgrowth, which is more expensive than a single clearance. once the garden is cleared, regular maintenance keeps it that way. step 2: agree what is included before maintenance starts, agree what is included in each visit. a typical maintenance visit includes: lawn mowing and edging weeding of borders deadheading of flowering plants light pruning of shrubs and perennials hedge trimming (usually quoted separately for large hedges) waste removal sweeping of paths and patios things that are usually not included in a standard maintenance visit: tree surgery (large tree work needs a specialist) lawn renovation (scarification, aeration, overseeding) hard landscaping (patio, fence, deck work) planting schemes (quoted separately as soft landscaping) pest and disease treatment (quoted as needed) see our cgm method page for detail on what our standard visit includes. step 3: provide access most maintenance gardeners work while you are out. provide access via a side gate, key safe or key. make sure the gate is unlocked on the agreed day. if you have a dog, ensure it is secured or that the gardener knows the arrangement. in rural parts of the chilterns, access can be more complicated. long driveways, electric gates and rural locations all need clear instructions. we always ask for what3words or a postcode plus detailed directions for rural clients. step 4: mark plants you want kept if there are plants you particularly want kept, mark them with a ribbon or label. this prevents the gardener from pulling up self-seeded plants you want to keep. common confusions include: self-seeded foxgloves (look like weeds in spring) native primroses (often mistaken for weeds) hellebore seedlings (look like weeds around the parent plant) volunteer foxgloves and hollyhocks a simple coloured ribbon tied around the base of the plant is enough. tell your gardener what the ribbons mean. step 5: discuss your preferences tell your gardener how you like the garden to look. this is the most important conversation you will have. be specific about: how high you like the lawn cut (most clients want 25-40mm in summer, 40-50mm in spring and autumn) whether you like a formal or informal look whether you want seed heads left for wildlife (some clients love the look, others find it messy) whether you want leaves left on borders as mulch or removed any plants you do not like (we will not plant them again) any areas you want left undisturbed (wildlife areas, bulbs in grass) whether you want weeds sprayed or hand-pulled the more specific you are, the better the result. vague instructions lead to a garden that looks like the gardener's taste, not yours. step 6: set up a communication channel agree how you want to communicate with your gardener. whatsapp is usually best for quick messages and photos. agree what to do if the gardener spots a problem (pest, disease, broken fence, leak). most maintenance gardeners will whatsapp photos of issues they spot during a visit, with a recommendation. for chiltern clients, weather-related rescheduling is common. wet weather stops mowing. high winds stop hedge cutting. a whatsapp group with your gardener means you know what is happening without having to chase. step 7: agree a schedule most gardens need fortnightly visits from march to october and monthly through winter. small courtyard gardens can be monthly year-round. large or formal gardens may need weekly visits in the growing season. see our guide to visit frequency . typical maintenance costs in the chilterns (2026 prices): small garden (up to 100 sqm): 60-100 pounds per fortnightly visit medium garden (100-300 sqm): 100-200 pounds per fortnightly visit large garden (300-1000 sqm): 200-400 pounds per fortnightly visit estate (1000 sqm plus): 400-800 pounds per weekly visit common mistakes not having a clearance first. trying to maintain an overgrown garden wastes money. have a clearance, then maintain. vague instructions. "just keep it tidy" means different things to different people. be specific. not marking plants to keep. self-seeded plants look like weeds. mark them. expecting the gardener to do everything. tree surgery, lawn renovation and hard landscaping are usually separate. agree what is included. changing the schedule constantly. maintenance works best on a regular schedule. rescheduling constantly makes it hard for the gardener to plan. frequently asked questions do i need to be home for maintenance visits? no. most clients are not home. provide access via a side gate or key safe, and the gardener will work and leave a note or whatsapp photos of what was done. what if i am not happy with the work? tell your gardener immediately. most issues are communication problems, not competence problems. a quick whatsapp with a photo of what you want changed is usually all it takes. can i change the schedule? yes, with notice. we ask for 30 days notice to change a regular schedule. one-off reschedules (holidays, weather) can be done with a few days notice. get started with professional maintenance if you are in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire and want to set up professional garden maintenance, send us a whatsapp with a few photos of your garden and your postcode, and we will aim to respond within one day. whatsapp get a quote further reading how much does regular garden maintenance cost? best plants for chalk soil in the chilterns best plants for chalk soil in the chilterns. lavender, rosemary, salvias, alliums, beech, yew, clematis and more. real recommendations from local gardeners. best plants for chalk soil in the chilterns plants, borders & planting ideas chalk soil is the dominant soil type across the chilterns, from high wycombe to princes risborough. rather than fighting it, choose plants that thrive in alkaline, free-draining conditions. this guide lists the best plants for chalk soil in the chilterns, all of which we plant regularly in client gardens. quick answer the best plants for chalk soil in the chilterns are: lavender, rosemary, salvia, alliums, hardy geraniums, beech, yew, box, clematis, star jasmine and wisteria. avoid rhododendrons, camellias, blueberries and heathers, which need acid soil. chalk soils in the chilterns typically have a ph of 7.5 to 8.5 and are free-draining, which suits mediterranean plants but not acid-lovers. why chalk soil suits mediterranean plants chalk soil is alkaline (ph 7.5 to 8.5), free-draining and often shallow. these are exactly the conditions that mediterranean plants have evolved for. lavender, rosemary, salvia and other mediterranean herbs thrive on chalk. the chalky hills of the chilterns, from high wycombe to princes risborough, wendover and amersham, are some of the best places in the uk to grow mediterranean planting. the downside of chalk soil is that it dries out fast in summer and is low in organic matter. plants need to be drought-tolerant and the soil needs annual mulching to keep it productive. but the upside is huge: mediterranean plants flower better, suffer fewer pests and diseases, and need less watering than on heavier soils. see our chalk soil guide for more detail on soil identification and improvement. best herbs for chalk soil herbs are the natural choice for chalky chiltern gardens. they love the alkaline, free-draining conditions and most are evergreen, providing year-round structure. plant them in full sun near the kitchen door for easy picking. lavender - the classic chiltern plant, loves chalk and full sun rosemary - evergreen, drought-tolerant, flowers in late winter salvia - long-flowering perennial, aromatic foliage thyme - low-growing ground cover, plant between paving sage - evergreen culinary herb, silvery foliage best perennials for chalk soil perennials die down in winter and come back each spring. on chalk, choose drought-tolerant varieties that will cope with dry summers without constant watering. alliums - dramatic spherical flowers in late spring, love chalk hardy geraniums - long-flowering ground cover, suppress weeds verbena bonariensis - tall and airy, butterflies love it nepeta (catmint) - perfect partner for roses, blue flowers for months poppies - bright colour in early summer, self-seed gently echinacea - pink daisy flowers, drought-tolerant once established best shrubs for chalk soil shrubs provide the backbone of a chalk garden. choose evergreen shrubs for year-round structure and flowering shrubs for seasonal colour. photinia red robin - bright red new growth in spring, evergreen choisya - glossy green leaves, scented white flowers in spring pittosporum - small wavy leaves, good for clipping into shapes roses - thrive on chalk if well fed, mulch annually japanese anemone - late summer flowers, tolerates chalk best hedges for chalk soil hedges on chalk need to be drought-tolerant. the classic chiltern hedge is beech, which holds its brown leaves through winter and looks stunning against the chalky soil. beech - the classic chiltern hedge, holds brown leaves in winter yew - dense, formal, long-lived, loves chalk box - low formal hedges (but watch for box blight and box moth) hornbeam - similar to beech, tolerates wetter soil privet - fast-growing, tolerates chalk, semi-evergreen best climbers for chalk soil climbers are useful for covering fences and walls on chalk gardens. most popular climbers tolerate chalk well. clematis - plant deep to avoid wilt, loves chalk star jasmine - evergreen, scented, loves a sunny wall wisteria - dramatic flowers in spring, needs twice-yearly pruning honeysuckle - native, wildlife-friendly, scented plants to avoid on chalk soil acid-loving plants cannot tolerate chalk. they will go yellow (chlorosis) and die within a year or two. the high ph locks up iron and other nutrients, so the plants starve even when the soil is fertile. avoid: rhododendrons camellias pieris blueberries heathers (most varieties, except winter-flowering heaths which tolerate some chalk) magnolias (most varieties) if you want to grow these, use large pots of ericaceous compost and water with rainwater. do not try to grow them in the ground on chalk. how to improve chalk soil chalk soil is often thin and low in organic matter. the topsoil layer can be as little as 10cm deep over solid chalk. to improve it: add a 5cm layer of compost each spring and dig it in mulch annually with bark or compost to retain moisture grow green manures (phacelia, mustard) on empty beds over winter use raised beds for vegetables and thirsty plants install irrigation for new plants in their first summer see our guide to building better borders on chalky ground for a full soil improvement plan. common mistakes planting acid-lovers on chalk. rhododendrons, camellias and blueberries will die. use pots of ericaceous compost instead. not watering new plants in the first summer. chalk drains fast. new plants need regular watering for the first growing season. not mulching. chalk soil bakes hard in summer. mulch keeps moisture in and weeds down. planting too deep. on shallow chalk soil, plant slightly proud of the surface to avoid crown rot. frequently asked questions how do i know if my soil is chalk? dig a small hole 30cm deep. if the soil is pale, contains white fragments, and fizzes when you add vinegar, it is chalky. a ph test kit from a garden centre will confirm a ph of 7.5 or above. most gardens in the chilterns above the escarpment (high wycombe, amersham, wendover, princes risborough) are on chalk. gardens in the vale (aylesbury, bicester) are on clay. can i grow vegetables on chalk soil? yes, with preparation. add plenty of compost each year and use raised beds for shallow soils. most vegetables do well on chalk except potatoes (which scab) and brassicas (which prefer slightly acid soil). see our chalk border guide . will lavender grow on chalk? yes, lavender loves chalk. it is one of the best plants for chalky chiltern gardens. plant in full sun, do not overwater, and trim annually after flowering. get help with planting on chalk soil if you have a chalky garden in the chilterns and want help with planting, best drought-tolerant plants for south east england gardens best drought-tolerant plants for south east england. lavender, rosemary, salvia, stipa, sedum and more. climate-resilient planting for dry summers. best drought-tolerant plants for south east england gardens plants, borders & planting ideas dry summers are becoming the norm in south east england. rather than watering constantly, choose plants that can cope with drought. this guide lists the best drought-tolerant plants for south east england gardens, all of which we plant regularly in client gardens across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire. quick answer the best drought-tolerant plants for south east england are: lavender, rosemary, salvia, stipa, sedum, eryngium, verbena, nepeta, perovskia and alliums. these plants have evolved in dry climates and can survive weeks without water once established. south east england is the driest part of the uk, with annual rainfall as low as 600mm in parts of essex and the thames valley. climate change is making summers hotter and drier, so drought-tolerant planting is the long-term answer. why drought-tolerant planting matters south east england is the driest part of the uk. annual rainfall in the thames valley around reading, maidenhead and slough averages just 600 to 650mm, less than jerusalem or beirut. the chilterns get slightly more (650 to 750mm) but the free-draining chalk soil means the water runs through fast, leaving plants dry within days of rain. climate change is making summers hotter and drier. the 2022, 2023 and 2025 heatwaves saw temperatures over 35 degrees celsius across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire, with no rain for 6 to 8 weeks. gardens with traditional planting schemes (hydrangeas, hostas, rodgersias) needed constant watering just to survive. gardens with drought-tolerant planting sailed through without irrigation. watering lawns and borders is expensive and often restricted during hosepipe bans. drought-tolerant planting is the long-term solution. it looks good, supports pollinators, and needs a fraction of the water of traditional planting. best drought-tolerant herbs mediterranean herbs are the most drought-tolerant plants you can grow. they have evolved in hot, dry climates with free-draining soil, exactly the conditions in a chilterns summer. lavender - the ultimate drought-tolerant plant, silver foliage reflects sun rosemary - evergreen, thrives on chalk, flowers in late winter salvia - long-flowering, aromatic, comes in many colours thyme - low ground cover, plant between paving sage - silvery foliage reflects sun, evergreen best drought-tolerant perennials these perennials come back year after year and cope with dry spells without watering. plant them in spring so they establish before their first dry summer. verbena bonariensis - tall, airy, butterfly magnet, flowers for months nepeta (catmint) - blue flowers, grey foliage, drought-tolerant echinacea - pink daisy flowers, deep tap root rudbeckia - yellow daisy flowers, late summer colour alliums - dramatic spherical flowers in late spring sedum (ice plant) - fleshy leaves store water, late flowers for bees eryngium (sea holly) - spiky blue flowers, deep roots best drought-tolerant grasses ornamental grasses are excellent in drought-tolerant gardens. their deep roots find water far below the surface, and their foliage moves beautifully in the wind. stipa tenuissima - soft, flowing grass, loves dry soil miscanthus - tall, architectural, autumn flowers pennisetum - fountain grass, soft flower heads calamagrostis - feather reed grass, upright habit best drought-tolerant shrubs shrubs provide the structure of a drought-tolerant garden. choose evergreen shrubs for year-round interest. photinia - red new growth, evergreen, tough choisya - evergreen, scented, tolerant of drought pittosporum - evergreen structure, good for clipping escallonia - evergreen, flowering, prefers milder areas ceanothus - blue flowers in spring, evergreen buddleja - butterfly magnet, drought-tolerant once established tips for drought-tolerant planting plant in autumn (october to december) so roots establish before summer. spring planting works but needs more watering. water regularly for the first season until roots are deep. after that, most drought-tolerant plants need no watering. mulch annually with bark or compost to retain moisture and suppress weeds. do not fertilise - drought-tolerant plants prefer poor soil. rich soil produces soft growth that is vulnerable to drought. group plants with similar needs - do not mix drought-tolerant with thirsty plants. the thirsty plants will need watering, which will rot the drought-tolerant ones. plant in drifts of 3, 5 or 7 for impact. single plants look lost. use gravel mulch in mediterranean-style planting. it reflects light, suppresses weeds, and looks the part. drought-tolerant lawn alternatives lawns are the thirstiest part of most gardens. in a dry chilterns summer, a lawn needs 25mm of water per week just to stay green. that is 250 litres per 10 square metres per week. over a 6-week dry spell, that is 1,500 litres per 10 square metres. if you want to reduce your lawn area, consider replacing it with: drought-tolerant ground cover: thyme, sedum, ajuga gravel planting: mediterranean herbs and perennials in gravel wildflower meadow: native wildflowers cope with drought once established artificial lawn: no watering, but no wildlife either patio or deck: hard landscaping with drought-tolerant plants in pots see our chalk soil planting guide and our guide to gravel and permeable surfaces . common mistakes planting in spring and not watering. new plants need regular watering for the first growing season, even drought-tolerant ones. mixing drought-tolerant and thirsty plants. one group will suffer. group by water needs. fertilising. rich soil produces soft growth that drought kills. drought-tolerant plants prefer poor soil. not mulching. bare soil bakes hard and loses moisture. mulch is essential. frequently asked questions can i have a drought-tolerant garden in shade? yes, but the plant choice is smaller. try bergenia, epimedium, hellebores, vinca and pulmonaria. these tolerate dry shade once established. how often should i water drought-tolerant plants? for the first growing season, water weekly from april to september. after that, only water in extreme drought (4 weeks plus with no rain and high temperatures). will drought-tolerant plants survive a wet chilterns winter? most will. the key is free-draining soil. on heavy clay, drought-tolerant plants can rot in wet winters. add grit when planting, or build raised beds. get help with best plants for privacy along fences and boundaries best plants for privacy along fences and boundaries in the uk. climbers, hedges and screening plants that create privacy without planning permission. best plants for privacy along fences and boundaries plants, borders & planting ideas privacy is one of the most common reasons people plant along boundaries. the right plant can screen an ugly view, block overlooking neighbours and create a sense of seclusion. this guide lists the best plants for privacy along fences and boundaries in uk gardens. quick answer the best plants for privacy along fences are: star jasmine, clematis, honeysuckle, climbing roses, beech hedges, yew hedges, bamboo and tall ornamental grasses. choose climbers for fences, hedges for boundaries, and grasses for seasonal screening. in the chilterns, where gardens are often overlooked from neighbouring properties on slopes, privacy planting is one of the most common requests we get. why planting is better than fences for privacy fences have their place, but they have limitations. a 2 metre fence is the legal maximum without planning permission in most areas, and even that may not block overlooking from upstairs windows. fences also blow down in storms, rot after 15 to 20 years, and look stark. plants, on the other hand, can grow taller than 2 metres (in the case of trees), filter wind rather than block it (which reduces storm damage), and provide habitat for wildlife. in chiltern villages like princes risborough, wendover and great missenden, gardens on the hillside are often overlooked from properties above. a 2 metre fence will not solve this. a hedge of beech or yew at 2.5 metres, or a row of pleached trees at 3 metres, will. the planning rules allow hedges and trees over 2 metres as long as they are not a "high hedge" causing unreasonable loss of light to a neighbouring property. best climbers for fence privacy climbers are the quickest way to add privacy to a fence. they use the fence as a support and grow up to cover it within a few seasons. plant climbers 30 to 60cm from the fence, train them in with wires or trellis. star jasmine - evergreen, scented, sun or shade, slow to establish then vigorous clematis - fast-growing, wide range of colours, prune by group honeysuckle - native, wildlife-friendly, scented, tolerates shade wisteria - dramatic flowers in spring, needs strong support climbing roses - thorny, good for security, long flowering season ivy - evergreen, but can damage fences and walls best hedges for boundary privacy hedges are the best long-term solution for boundary privacy. they are more expensive than climbers initially but provide better privacy and last for decades. beech - holds brown leaves through winter on chalky chiltern soil yew - dense, evergreen, long-lived, formal laurel - large leaves, fast-growing, evergreen leylandii - fast but needs regular cutting, can cause neighbour disputes privet - semi-evergreen, fast-growing, tolerates chalk hornbeam - similar to beech, better on clay soils see our hedging plants guide for detailed recommendations by soil type. best plants for quick privacy if you need privacy quickly, choose fast-growing plants. be prepared to maintain them properly, as fast growers can get out of hand. bamboo (clumping varieties only, avoid running bamboo) - 3 to 4 metres in 2 years leylandii - 1 metre per year, but needs cutting twice a year laurel - 30 to 60cm per year, evergreen privet - 30 to 60cm per year, semi-evergreen pleached trees - instant screening at 3 to 4 metres, expensive but effective a warning about bamboo: running bamboo (phyllostachys) spreads aggressively and can damage paving and foundations. always choose clumping bamboo (fargesia) and never plant running bamboo in the ground. best plants for privacy in shade many boundary spots are shaded by neighbouring buildings or trees. these plants will give privacy in shade: ivy - evergreen, tolerates deep shade honeysuckle - tolerates shade, scented yew - tolerates deep shade, evergreen laurel - tolerates shade, large leaves hornbeam - tolerates shade, holds brown leaves in winter photinia - tolerates light shade, evergreen pleached trees for above-fence privacy when you need privacy above the 2 metre fence line, pleached trees are the answer. these are trees trained on a clear stem (usually 1.5 to 2 metres) with a flat panel of branches on top. they give privacy at 2.5 to 3.5 metres without blocking light lower down. common choices for pleached trees in the chilterns: hornbeam (best for clay soils) beech (best for chalk soils) lime (tilia) photinia (evergreen, red new growth) holly (evergreen, slow-growing) pleached trees cost 200 to 500 pounds each (mature specimens). a row of 5 trees for a 10 metre boundary costs 1,000 to 2,500 pounds planted. they are not cheap, but they are the most effective solution for overlooking from above. tips for privacy planting check boundary ownership before planting on a boundary. the fence may belong to your neighbour. choose the right height - over 2 metres may need planning permission if it is a fence or wall. hedges and trees can be taller. plant at the right distance - 30 to 60cm from the fence for climbers, 60 to 90cm for hedges. support climbers with trellis or wire attached to the fence. water regularly for the first season - new plants need weekly watering from april to september. discuss with neighbours - a tall hedge can cause disputes. talk first, plant second. consider pleached trees for above-fence privacy. common mistakes planting running bamboo. it spreads into neighbouring gardens and can cause serious disputes. always choose clumping bamboo. planting leylandii too close to the boundary. it will grow wide as well as tall, and neighbours can complain. plant at least 1 metre from the boundary. not supporting climbers. climbers need wires or trellis. without support they fall off the fence. planting too close to the fence. plant 30 to 60cm from the fence to allow for growth and maintenance. not watering in the first year. new plants need regular watering. a dead hedge is an expensive mistake. frequently asked questions how high can a privacy hedge be? there is no height limit for hedges in most areas. however, under the high hedges act 2003, a neighbour can complain to the council about a hedge over 2 metres that is blocking light to their property. the council can order you to reduce the height. discuss with neighbours before planting a tall hedge. what is the fastest-growing plant for privacy? leylandii is the fastest, growing up to 1 metre per year. but it needs cutting twice a year and can cause disputes. laurel and privet are slightly slower (30 to 60cm per year) but easier to manage. can i grow a privacy hedge in shade? yes. yew, hornbeam and laurel all tolerate shade. see our hedging guide for more. get help with privacy planting if you need privacy planting in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with photos of the area and we will aim to respond within one day. whatsapp get a quote further best evergreen shrubs for year-round structure best evergreen shrubs for year-round structure in uk gardens. photinia, choisya, pittosporum, mahonia, viburnum and more. premium planting choices. best evergreen shrubs for year-round structure plants, borders & planting ideas evergreen shrubs are the backbone of a garden. they provide structure, colour and shelter all year, even in the depths of winter. this guide lists the best evergreen shrubs for year-round structure in uk gardens, all of which we plant regularly in client gardens. quick answer the best evergreen shrubs for year-round structure are: photinia, choisya, pittosporum, mahonia, viburnum, sarcococca, skimmia, pieris and euonymus. these provide foliage, flowers and structure through every season. in chiltern gardens, where winter can be long and grey, evergreen shrubs are the backbone that stops the garden looking dead from november to march. why evergreen shrubs matter evergreen shrubs are the skeleton of a garden. they provide structure, colour and shelter all year, even in the depths of winter. without evergreens, a garden looks bare and dead from november to march. with evergreens, the garden has shape and interest through every season. in the chilterns, where winters are cold and the chalky soil can dry out even in winter, evergreen shrubs are particularly valuable. they provide shelter for wildlife, screening for privacy, and a backdrop for spring bulbs and summer perennials. a well-placed evergreen shrub can transform a border from a summer-only feature to a year-round feature. the trick is to use evergreens as the structural plants (about 30 to 40 percent of the border) and fill in with deciduous shrubs and perennials for seasonal colour. too many evergreens and the border red robin - bright red new growth in spring, evergreen, can be clipped into a hedge choisya - glossy green leaves, scented white flowers in spring, rounded shape pittosporum - small wavy leaves, good that perennials demand, and they look good in winter too with their woody structure. why flowering shrubs are the low-maintenance secret flowering shrubs are the secret to a low-maintenance border. they provide colour, structure and interest for years with minimal pruning. once established, they need only an annual prune after flowering and a mulch in spring. compare that to perennials, which need staking, dividing, deadheading and cutting back twice a year. in the chilterns, where summers are getting drier and the gardening season is getting longer, flowering shrubs are a smart choice. they have deep roots that find water in dry spells, woody stems that survive winter without protection, and a long lifespan (20 to 50 years for most shrubs, compared to 5 to 10 years for many perennials). plant a shrub border once and it will look good for decades. the trick is to choose the right shrubs for the right spots. this guide lists the best flowering shrubs for low-maintenance borders in uk gardens, with specific recommendations for chalk and clay soils in the chilterns. best spring-flowering shrubs spring-flowering shrubs bloom on last year's wood, so prune them of chalk deutzia - tolerant of chalk forsythia - tolerant of chalk buddleja - thrives on chalk best flowering shrubs for clay soil clay soils in the vale of aylesbury and around didcot need shrubs that tolerate wet winters and dry summers: viburnum - tolerates clay weigela - tolerates clay philadelphus - tolerates clay hydrangea - loves clay, but needs water in summer spiraea - tolerant of most best hedging plants for privacy in berkshire, oxfordshire and buckinghamshire best hedging plants for privacy in berkshire, oxfordshire and buckinghamshire. beech, yew, laurel, leylandii, hornbeam and the right hedge screens out neighbours, reduces noise and provides wildlife habitat. this guide lists the best hedging plants for privacy in berkshire, oxfordshire and buckinghamshire, with specific recommendations for different soil types and situations. quick answer the best hedging plants for privacy in berkshire, oxfordshire and buckinghamshire are: beech, yew, laurel, leylandii, hornbeam, hawthorn and holly. choose beech and yew for chalk soils in the chilterns, hornbeam for clay in the vale of aylesbury, and laurel for fast evergreen screening. a mixed native hedge is best for wildlife. why hedges beat fences for privacy hedging is the most natural way to create privacy in a garden. the right hedge screens out neighbours, reduces noise, and provides wildlife habitat. unlike fences, hedges filter wind rather than block it (which reduces turbulence and storm damage), they last for decades, and they look better every year as they mature. in the chilterns, where gardens range from chalky hillside plots in high wycombe and princes risborough to clay vale gardens in aylesbury and bicester, the right hedge choice depends on the soil. plant beech on chalk and it will thrive. plant beech on wet clay and it will struggle. this guide gives specific recommendations for each soil type across berkshire, oxfordshire and buckinghamshire. best hedges for chalk soil (chilterns) chalky soils in the chilterns (high wycombe, amersham, wendover, princes risborough, marlow, henley) are alkaline, free-draining and often shallow. these hedges thrive on chalk: beech - the classic chiltern hedge, holds brown leaves through winter yew - dense, formal, long-lived, loves chalk box - low formal hedges (but watch for box blight and box moth) privet - fast-growing, semi-evergreen, tolerates chalk holly - evergreen, berries, slow-growing but very dense rosemary - can be clipped as a low hedge, loves chalk best hedges for clay soil (vale of aylesbury, didcot, bicester) clay soils in the vale are wet in winter and dry in summer. beech struggles on wet clay. choose these instead: hornbeam - similar to beech, tolerates wet soil, holds brown leaves in winter hawthorn - native, wildlife-friendly, tolerates clay laurel - large leaves, evergreen, tolerates clay blackthorn - sloes, dense, thorny, native hazel - native, catkins in spring, nuts in autumn best fast-growing hedges for quick privacy if you need privacy quickly, choose fast-growing plants. be prepared to maintain them properly, as fast growers need more cutting. leylandii - up to 1 metre per year, but needs cutting twice a year. can cause neighbour disputes. laurel - 30 to 60cm per year, evergreen, large leaves privet - 30 to 60cm per year, semi-evergreen hawthorn - 30 to 60cm per year, deciduous but dense escallonia - 30 to 50cm per year, evergreen, flowering a warning about leylandii: it is the fastest-growing hedge plant, but it is also the most common cause of neighbour disputes. it needs cutting twice a year, it casts heavy shade, and it kills anything growing under it. use it only if you have space and are committed to the maintenance. best evergreen hedges for year-round privacy evergreen hedges keep their leaves all year, providing privacy in winter as well as summer. they are more expensive to plant but give year-round screening. yew - dense, formal, slow-growing, the longest-lived hedge plant laurel - large leaves, fast-growing, less formal leylandii - fast but needs regular cutting escallonia - flowering, prefers milder areas like windsor and maidenhead holly - slow but very dense, berries on female plants privet - semi-evergreen, may lose leaves in cold winters best deciduous hedges that hold leaves in winter beech and hornbeam are deciduous but hold their brown leaves through winter, providing privacy all year. they are cheaper than evergreens and look better in a rural setting. beech - holds brown leaves until spring, loves chalk hornbeam - similar to beech, better on clay best hedges for wildlife a mixed native hedge is the best for biodiversity. plant a mix of hawthorn, blackthorn, hazel, holly, field maple and dogwood. it will provide flowers, berries, nuts and nesting sites for a wide range of wildlife. hawthorn - flowers in may, berries in autumn, native blackthorn - sloes, dense, thorny, native hazel - catkins in spring, nuts in autumn, native holly - berries for birds, evergreen shelter dogwood - coloured stems in winter, native field maple - native, good autumn colour mixed native hedge - the best for biodiversity see our native shrubs for birds guide for more wildlife-friendly options. hedge planting tips plant bare-root in winter (november to march) for the best value. bare-root plants cost 30 to 50 percent less than pot-grown. plant 3 to 5 per metre for a single row, 5 to 7 for a double staggered row (better for wildlife and density). water regularly for the first season - new hedges need weekly watering from april to september. mulch annually to suppress weeds and retain moisture. prune at the right time - see our hedge cutting guide . plant a wide hedge rather than a thin one - a 60cm wide hedge is healthier and denser than a 30cm wide one. avoid planting in frost or drought. wait for a mild, damp day in winter. hedge planting costs hedge type cost per metre (planted) growth rate beech (bare common mistakes planting the wrong hedge for the soil. beech on wet clay will struggle. choose hornbeam instead. planting too close. hedges need width as well as height. plant at least 60cm from the boundary. planting leylandii too close to the boundary. it will grow wide as well as tall, causing neighbour disputes. not watering in the first year. new hedges need regular watering. a dead hedge is january garden jobs for oxfordshire, berkshire and buckinghamshire january garden jobs for oxfordshire, berkshire and buckinghamshire. what to plant, prune, clear and protect during the coldest month of the year. january garden jobs for oxfordshire, berkshire and buckinghamshire seasonal garden jobs january is the quietest month in the garden, but there is still plenty to do. the garden is dormant, which makes it the perfect time for structural work, pruning and planning. this guide lists the key january garden jobs for gardeners in oxfordshire, berkshire and buckinghamshire. quick answer key january garden jobs in oxfordshire, berkshire and buckinghamshire: prune deciduous trees and shrubs, clear dead growth from borders, check fences and structures, plan the year ahead, service tools, and protect tender plants from frost. avoid walking on frosty lawns and do not cut hedges if birds are nesting early. january is the quietest month but also the best month for structural work and planning. why january matters in the garden january is the quietest month in the garden, but there is plenty to do. the garden is dormant, which makes it the perfect time for structural work, pruning and planning. in the chilterns, january is typically cold (3 to 6 degrees celsius average) with frosts most nights, but the soil is rarely frozen solid for more than a few days at a time. this means you can work outside on mild days, and the dormant plants will not be disturbed by your activity. the biggest january mistake is doing nothing. gardens that are ignored in january become overwhelming in march, when the growth starts and everything needs doing at once. a few hours a week in january sets you up for an easier spring. this guide lists the key january garden jobs for gardeners in oxfordshire, berkshire and buckinghamshire, with specific notes for chalk and clay soils. pruning in january january is the best time to prune many deciduous trees and shrubs while they are dormant. without leaves, you can see the structure clearly, and the plants will not bleed sap from pruning cuts. prune on a dry, mild day, and disinfect your tools between plants to avoid spreading disease. apple and pear trees: prune to an open goblet shape, removing crossing branches and any dead or diseased wood. this lets light into the centre and improves fruit quality. wisteria: shorten summer-pruned shoots to 2 to 3 buds. this encourages flowering spurs rather than leafy growth. vines and climbers: prune to control size. virginia creeper and boston ivy can be cut back hard. deciduous shrubs: remove dead, diseased and crossing wood. do not prune spring-flowering shrubs (forsythia, philadelphus) or you will lose the spring display. roses: prune bush roses by half, to an outward-facing bud. climbing roses: tie in new shoots, shorten side shoots to 3 buds. do not prune stone fruits (plums, cherries, apricots) in winter, as it risks silver leaf disease. wait until april when the sap is rising. see our pruning guide for detail. border work in january clear dead stems and seed heads from perennials (leave some for wildlife if you can) cut back ornamental grasses before new growth starts in march weed on mild days when the soil is workable. dig out perennial weeds like bindweed and couch grass. dig over new borders if the soil is not frozen or waterlogged. add compost as you dig. apply a mulch of compost or well-rotted manure to borders. on chalky chiltern soils, mulch is especially important to build organic matter. lift and divide overcrowded perennials. replant the outer sections, discard the tired centre. lawn care in january avoid walking on frosty lawns (it bruises the grass and leaves black footprints for weeks) avoid walking on waterlogged lawns (it compacts the soil and damages roots) clear leaves and debris to stop them smothering the grass service the mower ready for spring. sharpen the blade, change the oil, replace the spark plug. plan any lawn renovation for march or april. see our lawn renovation guide . check for moss. if moss is taking over, plan an iron sulphate treatment for february. on heavy clay soils in aylesbury and didcot, january lawns are often waterlogged. avoid any work until the water drains. on chalky soils in high wycombe and amersham, the lawn may be firm enough to walk on, but still avoid frost. structural work in january january is the best time for structural work because the garden is dormant and plants are not in leaf. you can see the structure clearly, and access is easier without herbaceous growth in the way. check and repair fences, trellis and arches. winter storms loosen posts and panels. check garden sheds for leaks. replace felt roofs before next winter. clean and treat garden furniture. wooden furniture needs an annual oil or stain. service garden tools and machinery. sharpen secateurs, loppers and shears. plan new borders, paths and patios for spring. order materials now to avoid spring rush. check garden lighting. replace bulbs in outside lights. see our fencing service for fence repairs and replacements. wildlife in january january is the toughest month for wildlife. food is scarce, water is frozen, and shelter is critical. a few simple things in your garden can make a big difference. put out bird food and water. high-energy foods like suet and sunflower hearts are best. clean bird feeders and tables to prevent disease transmission. leave seed heads for birds if you have not already cleared them. goldfinches love thistle and teasel seeds. check hedgehog boxes are dry and insulated. add dry leaves if needed. plant bare-root hedging for wildlife (hawthorn, blackthorn, hazel). see our hedging guide . provide a shallow dish of water and break the ice daily. planting in january plant bare-root trees and shrubs while dormant. cheaper than pot-grown and establish well. plant bare-root hedges (see our hedge planting guide ) plant winter bedding (pansies, violas, cyclamen) if not already done force rhubarb by covering with a forcing jar. stems will be ready in 6 to 8 weeks. chit early potatoes in a cool, light place. stand them eye-end up in egg boxes. plant garlic if you missed the autumn planting window. use varieties suited to spring planting. planning in january january is the month to plan the year ahead. the seed catalogues arrive, the garden is dormant, and you can see the structure clearly. a few hours planning now saves weeks of effort later. order seeds for the vegetable garden. plan your rotation to avoid building up soil pests. plan new borders and order plants for spring planting. bare-root plants are best value. plan any hard landscaping projects for spring. order materials now to avoid the spring rush. review your garden and decide what to change. take photos to remind yourself what needs doing. buy our chiltern guides for lawn care, bedding design and garden design plans. book a garden maintenance schedule for the year. see our maintenance service . common january mistakes walking on frosty lawns. it bruises the grass and leaves black footprints. pruning stone fruits in winter. it risks silver leaf disease. wait until april. leaving fallen leaves on the lawn. they smother the grass and cause moss. not protecting tender plants. move tender plants to a greenhouse or wrap them in fleece. not ordering seeds and plants early. popular varieties sell out by march. frequently asked questions is it ok to plant in january? yes, for bare-root trees, shrubs and hedges. avoid planting pot-grown evergreens in frozen or waterlogged soil. wait for a mild, dry day. should i cut my hedge in january? only if it is a deciduous hedge (beech, hornbeam) and there are no nesting birds. most hedges are best cut in late summer. see our hedge cutting guide . what if the soil is frozen? wait. frozen soil is impossible to dig and walking on it damages the structure. use frozen days for indoor tasks: planning, seed ordering, tool maintenance. get help with january garden jobs if you need help with january garden jobs in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a march garden jobs in the chilterns march garden jobs in the chilterns. first lawn cut, border clearing, mulching, pruning and planting. real local advice for the start of the growing season. march garden jobs in the chilterns seasonal garden jobs march is when the garden wakes up. the soil is warming, the days are lengthening, and growth is starting. this is the month to get ahead of the season before everything starts growing at once. this guide lists the key march garden jobs for the chilterns. quick answer key march garden jobs in the chilterns: first lawn cut (with blades high), mulch borders, plant summer bulbs, sow hardy annuals, prune late-summer shrubs, divide perennials, and start weed control. march is the start of the growing season, and what you do now sets the tone for the whole year. on chalky chiltern soils, march is also the time to start watering newly-planted trees and shrubs if the spring is dry. why march is the busiest month march is the month when the garden wakes up. buds break, bulbs flower, weeds germinate, and lawns start growing. it is also the month when most gardeners realise how much they did not do in autumn and winter. the result is that march is the busiest month in the gardening calendar, and the one where a clear plan makes the biggest difference. in the chilterns, march typically sees soil temperatures reach 8 to 10 degrees celsius by mid-month, which is warm enough for grass seed germination and active root growth. the chalky soils around high wycombe, princes risborough and wendover warm up faster than the clay soils in aylesbury and bicester, which can stay cold and wet into april. this means chiltern gardeners can usually start spring jobs a week or two earlier than vale gardeners. lawn care in march the first cut is the most important cut of the year. do it right and the lawn will recover strongly. do it wrong and you set the lawn back by weeks. make the first cut with the mower blades high (40 to 50mm). just trim the top off, do not scalp. do not cut if the soil is wet and soft. wait for a dry day. edge the lawn with a half-moon edger for a crisp finish. apply a spring lawn feed at the end of march if the lawn is established. plan scarification and aeration for april if the lawn has a lot of moss or thatch. see our spring lawn renovation guide for a full programme. border work in march mulch borders with a 5cm layer of compost or bark. this suppresses weeds and retains moisture for the whole season. cut back any remaining dead growth from perennials before new growth starts. divide overcrowded perennials. lift, split, replant the outer sections. weed thoroughly. march weeds are small and easy to remove. may weeds are a nightmare. plant new perennials and shrubs while the soil is moist. feed borders with a balanced fertiliser like growmore or blood, fish and bone. pruning in march late-summer flowering shrubs (buddleja, hydrangea, fuchsia): prune hard now. they flower on this year's wood. hydrangeas: remove dead flower heads back to a strong pair of buds. do not prune hard if it is a mophead hydrangea. roses: finish pruning bush roses by mid-march. shrub roses need only light pruning. summer-flowering clematis (group 3): cut back to 30cm from the ground. dogwood (cornus): cut back hard for coloured stems next winter. do not prune spring-flowering shrubs (forsythia, cherry, japonica) until after they have flowered. see our pruning guide for detail. planting in march plant summer-flowering bulbs (dahlia, gladioli, lilies) in pots in a frost-free greenhouse. plant out in may. plant bare-root trees, shrubs and hedges while still dormant. last chance before they break. plant onion sets and shallots. plant early potatoes at the end of march (mid-march in mild areas like windsor and maidenhead). plant new perennials. they will establish well in the moist spring soil. sowing in march sow hardy annuals direct where they are to flower (cosmos, calendula, nigella, sweet peas). sow half-hardy annuals (tomatoes, peppers, aubergines) indoors with heat. sow vegetable seeds: parsnips, carrots, beetroot, spinach, radish, lettuce. sow salad crops in modules under cover for planting out in april. chit second-early and maincrop potatoes. weed control in march march is the most efficient month for weed control. annual weed seedlings are small and easy to hoe off. perennial weeds (bindweed, couch grass, ground elder) are just starting into growth and are vulnerable to glyphosate. hoe annual weeds on a dry day. leave them to dry out on the surface. dig out perennial weeds with a fork. get every bit of root. apply a mulch after weeding to suppress new growth. treat paths and drives with a path weedkiller before weeds set seed. wildlife in march keep feeding birds. they are starting to nest and need energy. put up nest boxes before birds start nesting. plant nectar-rich plants for early bees (hellebores, pulmonaria, mahonia). leave a corner of the garden undisturbed for ground-nesting bees. clean out old nest boxes before the new season. common march mistakes cutting the lawn too short. the first cut should be high. scalping weakens the grass. planting tender plants too early. dahlias and tomatoes need frost protection until late may. not weeding early enough. march weeds are easy. may weeds are not. pruning spring-flowering shrubs. you will lose the flowers. wait until after they flower. not watering new plantings. spring can be dry. new plants need weekly watering. frequently asked questions when should i make the first lawn cut? when the grass is dry and growing, usually mid to late march in the chilterns. set the mower high for the first cut. is it safe to plant in march? yes, for hardy plants, trees, shrubs and hedges. wait until may for tender plants like dahlias and tomatoes. when can i sow seeds outdoors? hardy annuals and vegetables (parsnips, carrots, beetroot) can be sown from mid-march. half-hardy plants need to wait until after the last frost, usually late may. get help with march garden jobs if you need help with march garden jobs in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with june garden jobs: what to cut, feed and tidy june garden jobs. what to cut, feed and tidy in the peak growing month. hedge cutting, deadheading, feeding and watering advice for uk gardens. june garden jobs: what to cut, feed and tidy seasonal garden jobs june is the peak growing month. everything is growing fast, including the lawn, the hedges and the weeds. this guide lists the key june garden jobs to keep on top of the growth and enjoy the garden at its best. quick answer key june garden jobs in the chilterns: mow the lawn weekly, deadhead flowering plants, water new plantings, feed containers and baskets, prune spring-flowering shrubs, and keep on top of weeds. june is the peak growing month, and the garden needs regular attention to look its best. in the chilterns, june can be dry, so watering is essential for new plants and containers. why june is the peak month june is the month when the garden is at its peak. roses are flowering, perennials are blooming, lawns are growing fast, and weeds are germinating daily. it is also the month when the weather is finally warm enough to enjoy the garden in the evening. but june is not a month to sit back and admire. the garden needs regular attention to keep it looking good, and a week of neglect can lead to a month of recovery. in the chilterns, june typically sees 50 to 70mm of rain, but this can fall in a few heavy showers rather than steady rain. the chalky soils around high wycombe, amersham and wendover drain fast, so plants can be drought-stressed within a week of dry weather. on clay soils in aylesbury and bicester, the soil holds moisture better, but surface watering is still needed for containers and new plantings. lawn care in june mow weekly with the blades at 25 to 40mm. lower than 25mm weakens the grass in dry weather. leave the clippings on the lawn in dry spells to retain moisture. water the lawn in dry spells. 25mm per week is enough. water deeply once a week, not lightly every day. feed with a summer lawn fertiliser if the lawn is looking yellow. treat weeds with a spot weedkiller rather than a blanket spray. edge the lawn regularly for a crisp finish. if the lawn goes brown in a drought, do not panic. grass goes dormant and recovers when the rain returns. watering a lawn through a drought uses 250 litres per 10 square metres per week, which is a lot of water. consider letting it go dormant if you can. deadheading in june deadheading is the most important june job for flowering plants. removing spent flowers encourages the plant to produce more, rather than putting energy into seed production. roses: deadhead weekly to encourage repeat flowering. cut back to a leaf with 5 leaflets. perennials: deadhead delphiniums, lupins, hardy geraniums, salvia. some will repeat flower. annuals: deadhead cosmos, calendula, sweet peas. sweet peas especially need daily deadheading. containers and baskets: deadhead regularly to keep them flowering all summer. watering in june june is the month when watering becomes a daily job for containers and new plantings. established borders and lawns need less, but new plants need weekly watering at least. water in the early morning or evening to reduce evaporation. water deeply once a week rather than lightly every day. deep watering encourages deep roots. use a watering can for containers. hosepipes waste water. install a water butt to collect rainwater. plants prefer rainwater to tap water. group containers together to make watering easier. use water-retaining gel in containers and baskets to reduce watering frequency. feeding in june feed containers and hanging baskets weekly with a liquid tomato feed or a balanced fertiliser. feed roses with a rose fertiliser after the first flush of flowers. feed vegetables weekly with a high-potash feed once they start flowering. do not feed shrubs and trees in summer. they do not need it and soft growth is vulnerable to drought. pruning in june spring-flowering shrubs (forsythia, philadelphus, weigela): prune after flowering. remove old wood to the ground. evergreen shrubs: trim lightly to shape. avoid hard pruning in summer. cherry and plum trees: prune now to avoid silver leaf disease. wisteria: shorten long whippy shoots to 5 to 6 leaves. topiary: trim box and yew in late june for a crisp shape. see our pruning guide for detail. weed control in june june weeds are tough. the small annual weeds of march have grown into large, flowering plants setting seed. stay on top of them. hoe annual weeds on dry days. dig out perennial weeds (bindweed, couch grass). get every bit of root. mulch bare soil to suppress weed germination. do not let weeds set seed. one chickweed plant can produce 10,000 seeds. pest and disease control in june june is the peak month for pests and diseases. aphids, slugs, snails, sawfly and blackspot are all active. check roses for aphids and blackspot. treat with insecticidal soap and a fungicide. check lilies for lily beetle. pick off the red adults and squish the larvae. check box for box moth caterpillars. pick off by hand or treat with a biological control. check for slugs and snails after rain. use organic slug pellets or beer traps. check for sawfly on solomon's seal, geum, and berberis. pick off by hand. planting in june june is not the best planting month, but you can still plant if you water well. plant out tender annuals and vegetables (dahlias, tomatoes, courgettes) after the last frost. plant summer bedding in containers and baskets. plant vegetable plug plants for late crops. water new plantings daily for the first two weeks, then weekly. common june mistakes not watering new plants enough. new plants need daily watering for two weeks, then weekly for the rest of the summer. not deadheading. flowering plants stop flowering if you let them set seed. cutting the lawn too short. short grass is vulnerable to drought. keep blades at 25 to 40mm. watering at midday. most of the water evaporates. water in the morning or evening. ignoring pests. a small aphid problem in june is a big problem in july. act early. frequently asked questions how often should i water my containers in june? daily in hot weather, every other day in mild weather. hanging baskets may need watering twice a day in a heatwave. should i feed my lawn in june? only if it is looking yellow. a spring feed should last until mid-summer. use a summer feed with low nitrogen if needed. when should i prune my philadelphus? immediately after flowering, usually late june or early july. remove the old flowering stems to the ground. get help with june garden jobs if you need help with june garden jobs in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with photos and august garden jobs during dry weather august garden jobs during dry weather. watering, mowing, deadheading and drought management for uk gardens in the hottest month. august garden jobs during dry weather seasonal garden jobs august is often the hottest and driest month in the uk. the garden needs careful management to get through without damage. this guide lists the key august garden jobs, with a focus on drought management and keeping the garden looking good. quick answer key august garden jobs in the chilterns: water containers and new plantings, deadhead flowering plants, mow the lawn less often in dry spells, prune wisteria and summer-flowering shrubs, harvest vegetables, and plan autumn planting. august is often hot and dry in the chilterns, so watering and drought management are the priority. why august is a tricky month august is the month when the garden can start to look tired. the flush of early summer is over, the weather is hot, and many plants are running out of steam. but august is also a month of opportunity: with careful management, the garden can look good right through to october. the trick is to stay on top of watering, deadheading, and to start planning for autumn. in the chilterns, august is typically the driest month, with 40 to 50mm of rain on average. temperatures reach 22 to 25 degrees celsius regularly, with heatwaves pushing 30 degrees or more. the chalky soils around high wycombe, marlow and henley-on-thames drain fast and need more watering than the clay soils in aylesbury and bicester, which retain moisture better. watering in august august is the peak watering month. containers, hanging baskets, new plantings and vegetables all need regular water. water in the early morning or evening to reduce evaporation. water deeply once a week rather than lightly every day. prioritise containers, new plantings, and vegetables. established shrubs and trees usually do not need watering. use grey water (bath and washing-up water) for established ornamental plants. do not use on edibles. install a water butt if you have not already. even a small one helps. use a drip irrigation system for containers and beds. it is more efficient than a hosepipe. mulch bare soil to retain moisture. see our chilterns heatwave guide for drought management. lawn care in august mow less often in dry spells. the grass is not growing much, and cutting stresses it. set the mower blades high (40 to 50mm) to shade the soil and retain moisture. leave clippings on the lawn in dry spells to act as mulch. water the lawn only if you can do it regularly. sporadic watering does more harm than good. if the lawn goes brown, accept it. grass goes dormant and recovers when the rain returns. do not feed the lawn in august. soft growth is vulnerable to drought. deadheading in august keep deadheading to extend the flowering season. many plants will keep flowering into september if you stay on top of it. roses: deadhead weekly. cut back to a strong outward-facing bud. dahlias: deadhead regularly. the flowers keep coming until the first frost. cosmos and annuals: deadhead to keep them flowering. sweet peas: keep picking. do not let them set seed or they stop flowering. perennials: deadhead salvia, hardy geraniums, echinacea. some will repeat flower. pruning in august wisteria: shorten the long whippy shoots to 5 to 6 leaves from the main framework. summer-flowering shrubs (philadelphus, weigela, deutzia): prune immediately after flowering. trained fruit trees (espaliers, fans): summer prune to control growth. evergreen hedges: final trim of the year in late august. lavender: trim after flowering to keep compact. do not cut into old wood. see our hedge cutting guide and pruning guide for detail. harvesting in august august is the peak harvest month for vegetables and fruit. tomatoes, courgettes, cucumbers, beans: pick daily to keep plants productive. soft fruit: autumn raspberries, blackberries, plums, apples (early varieties). onions and shallots: harvest when the tops fall over and yellow. garlic: harvest when the lower leaves are yellow. maincrop potatoes: harvest as needed. leave the rest in the ground until october. herbs: cut and dry or freeze for winter use. sowing in august august is the time to sow for autumn and winter crops. sow salad crops (lettuce, rocket, mizuna) for autumn picking. sow spring cabbage for planting out in september. sow oriental greens (pak choi, mibuna, mizuna) for autumn harvest. sow spinach for autumn and winter cropping. sow green manures (phacelia, mustard) on empty beds to dig in before winter. pest and disease control in august watch for blight on tomatoes and potatoes. remove affected leaves immediately. watch for wasps on ripening fruit. trap them or pick fruit early. watch for vine weevil adult damage on rhododendrons and camellias (notched leaf edges). watch for powdery mildew on susceptible plants (asters, monarda, roses). improve air circulation. watch for ants farming aphids on plants. control the ants to control the aphids. planning for autumn in august august is the month to plan autumn planting. order bulbs, trees and shrubs now for delivery in september and october. order spring-flowering bulbs (daffodils, tulips, alliums) for september planting. order bare-root trees and hedges for november delivery. plan new borders for autumn planting. autumn is the best planting time. plan lawn renovation for september. see our lawn renovation guide . buy our chiltern guides for autumn lawn care and bulb planting. common august mistakes not watering enough. containers and new plantings need daily watering in hot weather. watering at midday. most water evaporates. water in the morning or evening. not deadheading. plants stop flowering if allowed to set seed. cutting the lawn too short. short grass is vulnerable to drought. not harvesting vegetables. plants stop producing if fruit is left on. frequently asked questions should i water my lawn in a drought? only if you can commit to watering regularly. sporadic watering does more harm than good. letting the lawn go dormant is fine. it will recover when the rain returns. can i still plant in august? yes, but you must water daily for the first two weeks. autumn planting (september to november) is better. when should i order spring bulbs? order in august for september delivery. popular varieties sell out by october. get help with august garden jobs if you need help with august garden jobs in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us september garden jobs before autumn takes over september garden jobs before autumn. lawn renovation, hedge cutting, leaf clearance, bulb planting and border work for the change of season. september garden jobs before autumn takes over seasonal garden jobs september is the transition month. the garden is still growing but starting to slow down. this is the time to prepare for autumn and get ahead of the leaf fall. this guide lists the key september garden jobs. quick answer key september garden jobs in the chilterns: start lawn renovation, plant spring bulbs, divide perennials, sow winter vegetables, harvest fruit, and tidy borders. september is the start of the autumn planting season, often the best month of the year for gardening in the chilterns. the soil is warm, the rain is returning, and plants establish quickly before winter. why september is the best planting month september is often the best month of the year for gardening. the soil is warm from the summer, the rain is returning, and the air is cooler. plants establish quickly before winter, and the gardener can work comfortably without the heat of august. in the chilterns, september typically sees temperatures of 16 to 19 degrees celsius and 50 to 60mm of rain, which is ideal for new plantings. september is also the start of the autumn planting season, which runs from september to november. autumn planting is better than spring planting for most trees, shrubs and perennials because the soil is warm enough for root growth through october and november, giving plants a head start on spring-planted specimens. lawn care in september september is the best month for lawn renovation. the soil is warm, the rain is reliable, and the grass has time to establish before winter. scarify to remove thatch and moss. aerate with a hollow-tine or solid-tine aerator to relieve compaction. top dress with a 70/30 sand and soil mix. overseed with a seed mix suited to your conditions. apply an autumn lawn feed (low nitrogen, high potash). treat moss with iron sulphate if needed. see our lawn renovation guide for a full programme. use our lawn planning tool for a cgm care level assessment. planting spring bulbs in september september is the start of the spring bulb planting season. plant daffodils, crocus and alliums now. wait until november for tulips (they prefer cooler soil). daffodils: plant in september, 10 to 15cm deep, 10cm apart. crocus: plant in september, 5 to 8cm deep, 8cm apart. alliums: plant in september, 10 to 15cm deep, 15cm apart. tulips: wait until november. plant 10 to 15cm deep, 10cm apart. hyacinths: plant in september, 10cm deep, 15cm apart. wear gloves, they can irritate skin. plant bulbs in drifts of 7 to 15 for natural-looking groups. avoid single rows, which look unnatural. dividing perennials in september september is a good month to divide overcrowded perennials. the soil is warm, the rain is returning, and divisions have time to establish before winter. lift the clump with a fork. divide into 3 or 4 sections with a spade or knife. discard the tired centre, replant the vigorous outer sections. replant at the same depth, water well, and mulch. divide: hardy geraniums, astrantia, hostas, daylilies, phlox, sedum, ornamental grasses. do not divide: oriental poppies, echinops, monkshood (they have tap roots and do not divide well). planting in september plant new perennials. they will establish before winter and get off to a fast start in spring. plant container-grown trees and shrubs. water well. plant strawberry runners for next year's crop. plant autumn onion sets for early harvest next year. plant spring cabbage and other winter vegetables. sowing in september sow winter lettuce and salad crops under cover. sow spinach for winter cropping. sow overwintering onions. sow green manures (phacelia, mustard, field beans) on empty beds. sow hardy annuals (sweet peas, calendula, cornflower) for early flowers next year. harvesting in september september is the main harvest month for fruit and vegetables. apples and pears: harvest as they ripen. test by lifting the fruit gently, if it comes away easily it is ready. autumn raspberries: pick daily. blackberries: pick as they turn black. tomatoes: harvest before the frost. ripen green ones indoors. maincrop potatoes: harvest on a dry day. store in hessian sacks. squash and pumpkins: harvest before the first frost. leave to cure in the sun for a week. onions and shallots: harvest and dry before storing. tidying borders in september cut back perennials that have finished flowering. leave some seed heads for wildlife. lift and store tender perennials (dahlias, cannas, gladioli) after the first frost. cut back diseased or mildewed growth to reduce overwintering problems. clear fallen leaves from borders. compost them or use as leaf mould. apply a mulch of compost or manure to feed the soil over winter. hedge cutting in september september is the last month to cut hedges before the bird nesting season ends and winter sets in. most hedges can have their final trim of the year in september. trim beech and hornbeam hedges for the last time this year. trim evergreen hedges (yew, box, privet, leylandii). do not cut native hedges (hawthorn, blackthorn) until january or february. see our hedge cutting guide for detail. common september mistakes not planting bulbs early enough. daffodils and crocus need to be planted in september or october for spring flowers. cutting hedges too late. new growth will not harden off before frost. cut by mid-september. not harvesting fruit. windfall apples should be picked up daily to prevent disease. not tidying diseased leaves. blackspot on roses and mildew on perennials will overwinter if you do not clear affected leaves. not watering new plantings. september can still be dry. water new plants weekly. frequently asked questions can i plant in september? yes, september is one of the best months for planting. the soil is warm and the rain is returning. plant trees, shrubs, perennials and bulbs now. when should i scarify my lawn? september is ideal. the grass recovers quickly in the warm soil. see our scarification guide . should i cut my hedge in september? yes, mid-september is the last chance to cut hedges before winter. new growth needs time to harden off before frost. get
