december garden jobs for large uk gardens december garden jobs for large uk gardens. winter pruning, structural work, wildlife care and planning for the year ahead. december garden jobs for large uk gardens seasonal garden jobs december is the quietest month in the garden, but for large gardens there is still plenty to do. the dormant season is the best time for structural work, winter pruning and planning. this guide lists the key december garden jobs for large uk gardens. quick answer key december garden jobs for large uk gardens: prune deciduous trees and hedges, plant bare-root trees and hedges, clear leaves, protect tender plants, plan the year ahead, and book winter work while access is easier. december is a quiet month for growth but a busy month for structural work in large gardens across the chilterns. why december is a working month in large gardens in a small garden, december is mostly a month for staying indoors. in a large garden, december is one of the busiest months of the year. the garden is dormant, the herbaceous growth has died down, and access to trees, hedges and structures is much easier than in summer. december is the month for winter pruning, hedge planting, fence repairs, and the planning that sets the next year up for success. for large gardens in the chilterns (typically 0.5 to 5 acres), december work includes: hedge cutting and renovation, tree work, bare-root planting, fence repairs, woodland management, and clearing fallen leaves from lawns and paths. the shorter days and cold weather make december work harder, but the benefits of doing it now rather than in spring are significant. pruning in december december is the best month for pruning deciduous trees and shrubs while they are fully dormant. apple and pear trees: prune to an open goblet shape. remove crossing, dead and diseased branches. deciduous shrubs: remove dead, diseased and crossing wood. hedges: cut deciduous hedges (beech, hornbeam) now while dormant. wisteria: shorten summer-pruned shoots to 2 to 3 buds. vines and climbers: prune to control size. native hedges: cut hawthorn and blackthorn in december if no nesting birds. do not prune stone fruits (plums, cherries) in winter. see our pruning guide for detail. planting bare-root in december december is the peak bare-root planting season. bare-root plants are dug from the field while dormant and supplied without soil. they are cheaper than pot-grown plants and establish well. trees: plant bare-root trees (oak, beech, birch, rowan) for woodland, shelter belts and specimen planting. hedges: plant bare-root hedges (hawthorn, blackthorn, beech, hornbeam). see our hedge planting guide . shrubs: plant bare-root shrubs (dogwood, hazel, field maple). roses: plant bare-root roses from november to march. plant bare-root plants as soon as possible after delivery. if you cannot plant immediately, heel them in (dig a temporary trench and cover the roots with soil). hedge work in december december is a good month for hedge work in large gardens. the deciduous hedges are dormant, and access is easier without the herbaceous growth in front of them. cut deciduous hedges (beech, hornbeam) while dormant. cut native hedges (hawthorn, blackthorn) if no nesting birds. renovate overgrown hedges by laying or coppicing. plant new hedges as bare-root whips. remove old hedges that need replacing. see our hedge cutting service and our hedge cutting guide for detail. tree work in december december is the best month for tree work in large gardens. trees are dormant, the sap is down, and access is easier without leaves. crown thinning and reduction of mature trees. removal of dead, diseased or dangerous trees. formative pruning of young trees. coppicing and pollarding of suitable species (willow, hazel, lime). planting new woodland and shelter belts. tree work requires a qualified tree surgeon with insurance. we can recommend trusted local contractors across the chilterns. woodland management in december december is the month for woodland management in large gardens. coppice hazel and sweet chestnut on a 7 to 15 year cycle. thin woodland to allow remaining trees to grow. remove invasive species (rhododendron ponticum, sycamore seedlings). plant native woodland bulbs (bluebells, wood anemones, wild garlic). clear paths and rides. stack brash as habitat piles for wildlife. leaf clearing in december december is the month for final leaf clearance. leaves left on lawns smother the grass and cause moss. leaves on paths become slippery. leaves in borders can be left as mulch, but diseased leaves should be removed. clear leaves from lawns weekly. clear leaves from paths and patios for safety. compost leaves in a separate leaf mould pile. they take 1 to 2 years to break down. leave some leaves in borders as habitat for overwintering insects. remove diseased leaves (blackspot on roses, scab on apples) to prevent reinfection. structural work in december december is the best month for structural work in large gardens. repair and replace fences. see our fencing service . repair paths, patios and driveways. service garden machinery (mowers, tractors, chain saws). repair outbuildings, sheds and stables. install new drainage if needed. service irrigation systems and isolate them for winter. planning in december december is the month to plan the year ahead for a large garden. walk the garden with a notebook and list what needs doing. plan the maintenance schedule for the year. see our maintenance service . plan major projects (new borders, hard landscaping, tree work) for the year. order seeds, plants and materials early to avoid the spring rush. book contractors for hedge cutting, tree work and lawn renovation before they fill up. review the previous year: what worked, what did not, what to change. common december mistakes not planting bare-root in time. bare-root plants need to be planted by march. order early. not clearing leaves from lawns. they smother the grass and cause moss. pruning stone fruits in winter. wait until april to avoid silver leaf disease. not protecting tender plants. move tender plants to a greenhouse or wrap in fleece. not booking contractors early. good contractors fill up by february for spring work. frequently asked questions can i plant in december? yes, for bare-root trees, shrubs and hedges. avoid planting in frozen or waterlogged soil. wait for a mild day. should i cut my hedge in december? yes, for deciduous hedges (beech, hornbeam). avoid cutting evergreen hedges in winter as frost can damage the cuts. how do i protect tender plants in december? move tender plants to a frost-free greenhouse. for plants in the ground, wrap with horticultural fleece and mulch the roots regular garden maintenance for large gardens: what should be included? what should be included in regular maintenance for large gardens? lawn care, borders, hedges, seasonal work and more. real uk advice for large garden owners. regular garden maintenance for large gardens: what should be included? regular garden maintenance large gardens need a different approach to maintenance than small ones. the scale means more planning, more equipment and a more structured schedule. this guide explains what should be included in regular maintenance for large uk gardens. quick answer regular maintenance for large gardens in the chilterns includes: weekly or fortnightly visits, lawn mowing, border weeding, hedge trimming in season, path and patio sweeping, debris removal, and reporting of issues. large gardens (0.5 to 5 acres) typically need 4 to 8 hours per visit . a formal maintenance plan keeps the garden looking good year-round. why large gardens need regular maintenance large gardens need regular maintenance more than small gardens do. the scale means that a missed visit shows. a small garden that is a week late looks untidy. a large garden that is a week late looks abandoned. the sheer volume of growth, the number of plants, the length of hedges and the size of lawns all mean that regular maintenance is essential to keep the garden looking good. large gardens also have more complex maintenance needs. there are usually multiple zones (formal garden, vegetable garden, orchard, woodland, paddock), each with different requirements. there are usually more mature trees, longer hedges, bigger lawns, and more infrastructure (fences, paths, outbuildings). a regular maintenance gardener who knows the garden can keep on top of all this. an ad-hoc approach cannot. this guide explains what regular maintenance for large gardens in the chilterns includes, what it costs, and how to set up a maintenance plan that works. what regular maintenance includes a typical maintenance visit for a large garden in the chilterns includes: lawn mowing and edging: weekly in the growing season, monthly in winter. includes ride-on mowing for large lawns. border weeding and tidying: hand weeding to get the roots out, deadheading, light pruning. hedge trimming: in season (august to september for most hedges, january for native hedges). path and patio sweeping: keeps hard surfaces clean and safe. leaf clearance: weekly in autumn. debris removal: all green waste taken away. reporting: we whatsapp photos of any issues we spot (pests, diseases, broken fences, leaks). things usually not included in a standard maintenance visit: tree surgery (large tree work needs a specialist contractor) lawn renovation (scarification, aeration, overseeding) hard landscaping (patio, fence, deck work) planting schemes (quoted separately as soft landscaping) major pruning of mature shrubs and trees pest and disease treatment (quoted as needed) see our cgm method page for detail on our approach. how often should we visit? the frequency depends on the size and style of the garden. weekly visits: for formal gardens, large lawns, and gardens with high standards. march to october. fortnightly visits: for most large family gardens. march to october. monthly visits: for low-maintenance gardens and all gardens in winter. see our guide to visit frequency for detail. cost of regular maintenance for large gardens garden size visit frequency hours per visit cost per visit annual cost (march-oct) 0.5 to 1 acre fortnightly 4-6 hours 160-240 pounds 1,500-2,500 pounds 1 to 2 acres weekly 4-6 hours 160-240 pounds 3,000-5,000 pounds 2 to 5 acres weekly 6-8 hours 240-320 pounds 4,500-7,000 pounds 5 acres plus weekly 8+ hours 320+ pounds 6,500+ pounds winter maintenance (november to february) is usually monthly, at 30 to 50 percent of the summer cost. setting up a maintenance plan a formal maintenance plan is essential for a large garden. the plan should include: visit frequency (weekly, fortnightly, monthly) what is included in each visit (lawn, borders, hedges, paths, etc.) what is not included (tree work, lawn renovation, hard landscaping) seasonal jobs (lawn renovation in september, hedge cutting in august, leaf clearance in october) communication (whatsapp, email, monthly report) access arrangements (side gate, key safe, dog) payment terms (monthly invoice, direct debit) see our guide to preparing for professional maintenance for detail. the maintenance year for a large garden month key jobs january winter pruning, bare-root planting, hedge cutting (deciduous), planning february final winter pruning, mulching, lawn moss treatment march first lawn cut, border tidy, mulch, feed, prune late-summer shrubs april lawn renovation, sow seeds, plant potatoes, weed control may plant out tender plants, hedge trimming begins, deadheading june weekly mowing, deadheading, watering, pest control july weekly mowing, deadheading, watering, harvest soft fruit august hedge trimming, prune wisteria, watering, harvest vegetables september lawn renovation, plant bulbs, divide perennials, sow winter veg october leaf clearance, plant bare-root, clear borders, mulch november plant bare-root, final leaf clearance, protect tender plants december winter pruning, planning, structural work common mistakes with large garden maintenance not having a plan. without a plan, jobs are missed and the garden deteriorates. not visiting often enough. a large garden needs weekly or fortnightly visits. monthly is not enough in the growing season. not communicating. the gardener needs to know what you want. vague instructions lead to disappointment. not budgeting for seasonal jobs. hedge cutting, lawn renovation and leaf clearance are extra costs on top of regular maintenance. frequently asked questions how much does maintenance cost for a 2 acre garden? typically 3,000 to 5,000 pounds per year for weekly maintenance from march to october, plus 500 to 1,000 pounds for winter maintenance. see our garden care level assessment for a cgm care level assessment. do i need a regular gardener or can i do it myself? a large garden is too much for one person to maintain alone. most large garden owners do the easy bits themselves and hire a professional for the heavy work. see our weekly vs fortnightly guide . what is included in a maintenance visit? lawn mowing, border weeding, deadheading, light pruning, path sweeping, leaf clearance, and waste removal. see our cgm method page . get help with large garden maintenance if you weekly vs fortnightly gardening: which does your garden need? weekly vs fortnightly gardening visits. which is right for your garden? cost comparison, pros and cons, and how to decide. weekly vs fortnightly gardening: which does your garden need? regular garden maintenance one of the first decisions when setting up regular garden maintenance is how often the gardener should visit. weekly and fortnightly are the two main options, and the right choice depends on your garden, your standards and your budget. this guide compares the two. quick answer weekly gardening visits keep the garden looking immaculate but cost more. fortnightly visits are the most popular choice and keep the garden looking good for less. the right choice depends on your budget, your standards, and the type of garden. in the chilterns, where the growing season is long and the soil drains fast, weekly visits are often worth it for large or formal gardens. why visit frequency matters the frequency of gardening visits is one of the most important decisions you will make. visit too often and you are paying for work that does not need doing. visit too rarely and the garden gets out of hand between visits, making each visit more expensive as the gardener spends time catching up rather than maintaining. the right frequency depends on three things: your budget, your standards, and the type of garden. a small courtyard garden with low standards can be maintained monthly. a large formal garden with high standards needs weekly visits. most family gardens in the chilterns are best with fortnightly visits from march to october and monthly through winter. weekly visits: pros and cons pros the garden always looks immaculate lawn is mowed at the optimal frequency (weekly in the growing season) weeds are caught before they set seed pests and diseases are spotted early the gardener knows the garden intimately ideal for formal gardens, large gardens, and gardens with high standards cons costs twice as much as fortnightly may be unnecessary for low-maintenance gardens the gardener may have less to do in slow weeks fortnightly visits: pros and cons pros most popular choice for family gardens good balance of cost and garden appearance lawn is mowed often enough to stay healthy weeds are caught before they get too big the gardener has enough to do each visit to be efficient cons the garden can look untidy between visits in peak growth lawn may need mowing between visits in may and june not suitable for formal gardens or large gardens monthly visits: pros and cons pros cheapest option suitable for low-maintenance gardens suitable for winter maintenance (november to february) suitable for small courtyard gardens cons the garden will look untidy between visits in the growing season lawn will be too long between cuts weeds will set seed between visits not suitable for family gardens in the growing season which frequency is right for your garden? garden type recommended frequency hours per visit small courtyard garden monthly year-round 2-3 hours small family garden (100 sqm) fortnightly march-oct, monthly nov-feb 2-3 hours medium family garden (200 sqm) fortnightly march-oct, monthly nov-feb 3-4 hours large family garden (500 sqm) weekly march-oct, monthly nov-feb 4-6 hours formal garden (any size) weekly year-round 4-8 hours estate (1 acre plus) weekly year-round 6-8+ hours cost comparison frequency cost per visit (4 hours) visits per year annual cost weekly 160 pounds 52 8,320 pounds fortnightly 160 pounds 26 4,160 pounds monthly 160 pounds 12 1,920 pounds note: this is for a 4-hour visit . actual costs vary by garden size and complexity. the hybrid approach many chiltern clients use a hybrid approach: weekly visits in the peak growing season (may to july) and fortnightly for the rest of the year. this gives the garden the attention it needs when growth is fastest, without paying for weekly visits all year. another hybrid is fortnightly maintenance visits with a monthly "tidy" visit for leaf clearance, hedge trimming, or other seasonal jobs. this keeps the regular maintenance cost down while making sure seasonal jobs are done. signs you need to change frequency signs you need more frequent visits: the lawn is too long between cuts weeds are flowering between visits the garden looks untidy by the end of the second week hedges are getting out of shape you are spending your weekends catching up signs you can reduce frequency: the gardener is finishing early each visit the garden looks the same at the end of two weeks as it did at one you are paying for visits that do not seem necessary common mistakes choosing the cheapest option. monthly visits in the growing season will leave the garden looking untidy. choose fortnightly at least from march to october. choosing the most expensive option. weekly visits may be unnecessary for a low-maintenance garden. start with fortnightly and adjust. not adjusting for the seasons. most gardens need more visits in the growing season and fewer in winter. not communicating with your gardener. if the frequency is not working, talk to your gardener. most issues are communication problems. frequently asked questions can i switch between weekly and fortnightly? yes, with notice. we ask for 30 days notice to change a regular schedule. many clients switch to weekly in may and june, then back to fortnightly for the rest of the year. is fortnightly enough for a family garden? for most family gardens (100 to 300 sqm), fortnightly is enough from march to october. you may need to mow the lawn between visits in may and june when growth is fastest. what about winter maintenance? monthly visits from november to february are usually enough. the garden is dormant and there is less to do. see our december garden jobs guide . get help choosing the right frequency if you are not sure what frequency is right for your garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, how to create a wildlife-friendly garden without letting it look messy create a wildlife-friendly garden without it looking messy. structured wildlife planting, managed wild areas and design tips for a garden that works for nature and looks good. how to create a wildlife-friendly garden without letting it look messy wildlife & wildflower gardens many garden owners want to help wildlife but worry that a wildlife garden means an untidy garden. the good news is that you can have both. with the right design and management, a wildlife-friendly garden can look structured and intentional while still supporting a wide range of species. this guide explains how. quick answer a wildlife-friendly garden does not have to look messy. the trick is to combine formal structure (mown lawn, clipped hedges, neat borders) with wildlife features (wildlife corner, native planting, log piles, water). the result is a garden that looks cared for and supports a wide range of wildlife. in the chilterns, where gardens often back onto countryside, this approach is particularly effective. the myth of the messy wildlife garden many people think a wildlife-friendly garden has to be wild and unkempt. this is a myth. the best wildlife gardens are often the most carefully designed, with a clear structure that supports wildlife without looking messy. the trick is to combine formal elements (mown lawn, clipped hedges, neat borders) with wildlife features (wildlife corner, native planting, log piles, water). the result is a garden that looks cared for and supports a wide range of wildlife. the opposite is also true: a garden that is left entirely to nature looks abandoned, not wildlife-friendly. wildlife needs structure, diversity and year-round resources, all of which require planning and maintenance. a truly wildlife-friendly garden is more work than a manicured garden, not less. the three-zone approach the best way to combine wildlife value with a tidy appearance is the three-zone approach: zone 1: formal garden (visible from the house): mown lawn, clipped hedges, neat borders. maintained weekly or fortnightly. zone 2: wildlife-friendly planting (middle of the garden): native and pollinator-friendly plants, long grass, less frequent maintenance. zone 3: wild area (bottom of the garden): unmown grass, log piles, leaf piles, minimal maintenance. this approach gives you the best of both worlds: a garden that looks good from the house and a wildlife area that supports a wide range of species. wildlife features that look good these wildlife features add value to the garden without looking messy: pond: the single best wildlife feature. even a small pond attracts frogs, newts, dragonflies and birds. see our pond maintenance guide . native hedge: hawthorn, blackthorn, hazel, holly. provides flowers, berries, nesting sites. see our hedging guide . climbers on fences: ivy, honeysuckle, clematis. provides nectar, berries and nesting sites. pollinator-friendly perennials: verbena, sedum, echinacea, lavender. see our wildlife plants guide . bird boxes and feeders: place where you can see them from the house. bug hotel: a structured feature that looks deliberate, not messy. log pile: in a corner, partly shaded. provides habitat for beetles, frogs and hedgehogs. wildlife features that look messy (and how to manage them) some wildlife features look messy if not managed carefully: nettle patch: essential for butterflies, but keep it in a designated corner, not spreading. long grass: mow paths through it so it looks intentional, not abandoned. seed heads: leave them in autumn for birds, but cut them back in spring before new growth starts. leaf piles: tuck them behind a hedge or shed. dead wood: use as a log pile rather than leaving branches lying around. plants for a wildlife-friendly garden for pollinators (bees and butterflies) lavender - flowers for months, bees love it buddleja - butterfly magnet in late summer verbena bonariensis - tall, airy, butterflies love it sedum - late flowers for bees echinacea - long flowering, butterflies alliums - early summer flowers for bees for birds holly - berries in winter hawthorn - berries and nesting sites pyracantha - berries for months cotoneaster - berries and ground cover rowan - berries in autumn for other wildlife ivy - late nectar, winter berries, habitat honeysuckle - moths, berries, nesting primrose - early nectar for bees see our wildlife plants guide for a full list. maintenance for a wildlife-friendly garden a wildlife-friendly garden needs different maintenance from a manicured garden: lawn: leave a corner unmown for ground-nesting bees and butterflies. borders: leave seed heads in autumn for birds. cut back in spring rather than autumn. hedges: cut once a year in late summer, not twice. leaves berries for birds. pond: clean out in autumn, not spring, to avoid disturbing spawning amphibians. leaf piles: leave in place, do not burn. they are habitat for hedgehogs and insects. compost heap: leave alone. slowworms, hedgehogs and beetles live in it. see our guide to balancing tidy and biodiverse . common mistakes letting the whole garden go wild. this looks abandoned, not wildlife-friendly. use the three-zone approach. planting only non-native plants. native plants support more wildlife. mix native and non-native for the best results. tidying up too much in autumn. seed heads, leaf piles and dead stems are habitat for overwintering insects. leave them until spring. not having water. even a small pond or bird bath is essential. wildlife needs water year-round. using pesticides. pesticides kill beneficial insects as well as pests. use organic methods. frequently asked questions can i have a tidy garden that is also wildlife-friendly? yes. use the three-zone approach: formal near the house, wildlife-friendly in the middle, wild at the bottom. see our balance guide . what is the best single thing i can do for wildlife? add a pond. even a small pond attracts frogs, newts, dragonflies and birds within weeks. see our pond guide . should i leave my lawn unmown? leave a corner unmown, not the whole lawn. long grass supports insects and ground-nesting bees. mown paths through long grass look intentional. get help with a wildlife-friendly garden if you want to make your garden more wildlife-friendly in how to deal with brambles in an overgrown garden how to deal with brambles in an overgrown uk garden. cut, dig out, treat and prevent regrowth. real advice from working gardeners. how to deal with brambles in an overgrown garden garden problems & weed control brambles are the most common problem in overgrown uk gardens. they grow fast, tangle into impenetrable thickets, and regrow from any root left in the ground. this guide explains how to deal with brambles in an overgrown garden, from initial clearance to long-term prevention. quick answer to deal with brambles in an overgrown garden: cut the top growth to ground level, dig out every root crown with a mattock, rake up all debris, treat regrowth with glyphosate in late summer, and replant the cleared area. brambles are the most common overgrowth problem in chiltern gardens, particularly along woodland edges and in neglected corners. why brambles take over brambles (rubus fruticosus) are the most common overgrowth problem in uk gardens, and particularly in the chilterns where gardens often back onto beech woods and hedgerows. brambles spread by seed (bird-dropped), by tip-rooting (the long stems root where they touch the ground), and by suckering from the root crown. a single missed root crown can produce a new thicket in a single growing season. the combination of bird-dropped seed, semi-shade, and the fertile chalky soil of the chilterns makes bramble thickets particularly aggressive. a neglected corner can become a bramble thicket of 10 square metres in just two years. the thorns make the area impassable, the thicket smothers everything underneath, and the root system is hard to remove. the good news is that brambles are easier to kill than some other invasive plants. they do not regenerate from root fragments (unlike bindweed), and they do not have deep tap roots (unlike dandelions). with the right approach, brambles can be cleared in a single season. step 1: assess the situation before starting, walk the area and assess what you are dealing with. note: size of the bramble thicket (square metres) density (light, medium, heavy) what is underneath (lawn, border, hard standing) what is around the thicket (fence, hedge, tree, building) any nesting birds (march to august) do not start clearing between march and august if there are nesting birds in the thicket. it is an offence under the wildlife and countryside act 1981 to damage or destroy an active nest. step 2: cut the top growth use a brush cutter, secateurs or loppers to cut all the top growth to ground level. wear thick gloves, a long-sleeved top and eye protection. the thorns are vicious and can cause nasty scratches that get infected. cut the stems into manageable lengths and pile them up for removal. a 10 square metre bramble thicket will produce 4 to 6 bin bags of cut material. if the brambles are tangled with fences or trees, work carefully to avoid damaging the underlying structure. pull the cut stems out a handful at a time, rather than yanking whole armfuls which can pull fence panels with them. step 3: dig out the root crowns this is the most important step and the one most people skip. brambles regrow from the root crown, the woody swelling where the stems meet the roots. use a mattock to dig out every crown. go 15 to 20cm deep to get the whole crown. any fragment left will regrow. on chalky soils in the chilterns, the root crowns sit in relatively free-draining soil and come out cleanly with a mattock. on heavy clay in the vale of aylesbury or around didcot, the soil sticks to the roots and you may need to use a spade as well as a mattock. either way, the goal is to remove the entire woody crown, not just chop through it. step 4: rake up all debris rake up all the cut stems, leaves and root fragments. bramble stems can root where they touch the ground, so do not leave any lying around. burn the waste if you have a burn permit, take it to the tip, or have it removed by us as part of a clearance (see our clearance service ). we take all waste away. step 5: monitor for regrowth check the area every 2 weeks for the first 3 months. any root fragment you missed will produce a new shoot within 4 to 6 weeks in the growing season. dig out any regrowth immediately, do not let it get established. a small shoot is easy to dig out, a 6-month-old thicket is not. step 6: treat stubborn regrowth if regrowth persists after 2 or 3 digs, treat with a glyphosate-based weedkiller in late summer (august to september). at this time of year, brambles are sending energy down to the roots for winter, so the weedkiller is carried down to kill the root system. spray on a dry, calm day when no rain is forecast for 6 hours. follow the label instructions carefully. step 7: replant the cleared area once the brambles are gone, you need to replant the area to prevent them coming back. bare soil is an invitation for weeds and bramble seedlings. improve the soil with compost if needed. plant ground cover plants (hardy geraniums, epimedium, bergenia) to suppress weeds. plant shrubs for structure. mulch thickly to suppress weed germination. see our soil preparation guide and soft landscaping service . when to clear brambles winter (november to february) is the best time to clear brambles. the plants are dormant, the lack of leaves makes the root crowns easier to see, and the ground is softer after winter rain. avoid clearing in bird nesting season (march to august) if the thicket is being used by nesting birds. cost of bramble clearance area density diy cost professional cost 10 sqm light 50 (skip) 120-200 pounds 10 sqm heavy 100 (skip) 200-350 pounds 50 sqm light 150 (skip) 400-600 pounds 50 sqm heavy 250 (skip) 600-1,200 pounds 200 sqm heavy 600 (skip) 1,500-3,500 pounds see our bramble clearance cost guide for more detail. tools you need brush cutter or secateurs and loppers mattock for digging out root crowns spade for heavy clay soils thick gloves, long-sleeved top, and eye protection wheelbarrow for waste rake glyphosate weedkiller and sprayer (for stubborn regrowth only) common mistakes cutting without digging. just cutting the top growth leaves the root crown in the ground. the bramble will be back within weeks. leaving cut stems on the ground. bramble stems root where they touch soil. always remove every piece. using contact weedkiller. contact weedkillers burn the leaves but do not kill the root. use glyphosate, or just dig. not wearing protection. bramble thorns cause nasty scratches that can get infected. always wear thick gloves and long sleeves. clearing in nesting season without checking. bramble thickets are popular nest sites. always check carefully before clearing between march and august. not replanting after clearance. bare soil is an invitation for brambles and weeds to return. always replant. frequently asked questions will brambles come back if i just cut them? yes. cutting removes the top growth but leaves the root crown, which will regrow within weeks. you must dig out the root crown to permanently remove brambles. how do i clear a large bramble thicket? for thickets over 50 square metres, hire a professional. the volume of waste and the labour involved make diy impractical. see our clearance service . how long until i can replant the area? immediately after digging out the root crowns. if you have used glyphosate, wait 4 weeks for it to break down before replanting. get help with bramble clearance if you have a bramble problem in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with photos of the area and approximate size, and we will aim to respond within one day with a price range. whatsapp get a quote further reading how to clear brambles why your garden keeps flooding after heavy rain why your garden floods after heavy rain and how to fix it. compaction, drainage, soil type and solutions explained by working uk gardeners. why your garden keeps flooding after heavy rain garden problems & weed control garden flooding is becoming more common in the uk as rainfall patterns change and gardens are increasingly paved over. if your garden floods after heavy rain, there is usually an underlying cause that can be fixed. this guide explains why gardens flood and how to solve the problem. quick answer gardens flood because of compaction, poor drainage, heavy clay soil, hard surfaces directing water into the garden, or a high water table. solutions include aeration, installing land drains, improving soil with organic matter, reducing hard surfaces, and creating a soakaway or rain garden. in the chilterns, flooding is most common on the heavy clay soils of the vale of aylesbury and around didcot and bicester, where water sits on the surface for days after heavy rain. why garden flooding is getting worse garden flooding is becoming more common in the uk as rainfall patterns change and gardens are increasingly paved over. the met office records show that extreme rainfall events (more than 30mm in an hour) have increased by 17 percent in south east england since 1960. at the same time, front gardens are being paved over for parking at an alarming rate, with one in three front gardens in london and the south east now more than 75 percent paved. the result is more runoff, more flooding, and more pressure on already-strained drainage systems. in the chilterns, the problem varies by location. on the chalky hills around high wycombe, princes risborough and wendover, the free-draining soil means flooding is rare, but gardens can still suffer from runoff from roads and roofs. on the heavy clay soils of the vale of aylesbury, bicester and didcot, the soil drains slowly and gardens can flood for days after heavy rain. low-lying gardens near the thames in reading, wallingford and henley-on-thames are also vulnerable when the river is high. common causes of garden flooding 1. soil compaction the most common cause. compacted soil cannot absorb water, so it sits on the surface. compaction comes from foot traffic, children playing, mowing wet soil, and builders' vehicles. lawns on heavy clay in aylesbury and bicester are particularly prone. test by pushing a fork into the soil: if it will not go in more than 10cm, the soil is compacted. fix: aerate annually with a hollow-tine aerator. see our aeration guide . 2. heavy clay soil clay soil holds water and drains slowly. in the vale of aylesbury, the clay can hold water for weeks in winter. fix: add organic matter and grit to improve structure. see our clay soil improvement guide . the goal is to create a crumb structure that allows water to drain through rather than sit on the surface. 3. hard surfaces patios, driveways and paved areas direct water into the garden instead of letting it soak into the ground. a 50 square metre driveway can shed 1,500 litres of water in a 30mm rainstorm. fix: use permeable surfaces (gravel, permeable paving), install a soakaway, or create a rain garden to absorb the runoff. 4. high water table if the water table is high, the ground cannot absorb more water. this is common in low-lying gardens near rivers and streams, particularly around reading, wallingford and henley-on-thames along the thames. fix: install land drains connected to a soakaway or surface drain. in some cases, the only solution is to raise the garden level with imported topsoil. 5. blocked or absent drainage old land drains may be blocked or broken. many older properties in the chilterns have clay land drains that are 50 to 100 years old and have collapsed or silted up. fix: check existing drains by lifting inspection chambers and running a hose through. clear or replace them as needed. 6. runoff from neighbouring land if your garden is at the bottom of a slope, water may run onto it from neighbouring gardens, fields or roads. this is common in hillside villages like princes risborough and wendover. fix: a land drain along the upper boundary to intercept runoff and carry it away. solutions for garden flooding aeration for lawns with surface water, aerate to relieve compaction. hollow-tine aeration removes plugs of soil, allowing air and water into the root zone. cost: 2 to 4 pounds per square metre if we do it, or hire an aerator for 50 pounds per day. see our aeration guide . land drains (french drains) for persistent flooding, install land drains to carry water away. a land drain is a perforated pipe in a trench of gravel, 30 to 60cm deep, that collects water and carries it to a soakaway or surface drain. cost: 30 to 60 pounds per metre installed. a typical garden needs 10 to 30 metres of land drain. soakaway a soakaway collects water and lets it drain slowly into the ground. it is a hole filled with rubble or a plastic crate system, 1 to 2 metres deep and 1 to 3 metres across. cost: 200 to 500 pounds depending on size. a soakaway must be at least 5 metres from the house and 2 metres from the boundary. rain garden a rain garden is a planted depression that collects and absorbs runoff from roofs, driveways and patios. it is a beautiful solution that also supports wildlife. a rain garden is typically 10 to 20 percent of the drainage area, planted with moisture-tolerant plants like iris, lobelia, and astilbe. cost: 300 to 800 pounds for a typical rain garden. reduce hard surfaces replace impermeable surfaces with permeable alternatives like gravel, permeable paving or planting. this reduces runoff and allows water to soak into the ground. permeable paving costs 60 to 100 pounds per square metre installed. improve soil add organic matter to improve soil structure and water absorption. on heavy clay, add 5cm of compost or well-rotted manure and dig it in. on chalky soils, add organic matter to build depth. see our soil improvement guide . how to diagnose your flooding problem watch where the water comes from. is it runoff from a slope, overflow from a drain, or water rising through the soil? time how long it takes to drain. a few hours is normal. days indicate a problem. check the soil. dig a small hole 30cm deep. if it fills with water, the water table is high. if the soil is sticky and dense, it is clay. check the drainage. lift any inspection chambers and run a hose through. check the hard surfaces. where does the water from your roof and driveway go? cost of fixing garden flooding solution diy cost professional cost lawn aeration (50 sqm) 50 (hire) 100-200 pounds soil improvement (50 sqm) 150-250 300-500 pounds land drain (20 metres) 200-400 (materials) 600-1,200 pounds soakaway 200-300 (materials) 400-800 pounds rain garden 200-400 (plants) 500-1,000 pounds permeable paving (50 sqm) 1,000-2,000 3,000-5,000 pounds common mistakes not identifying the cause. installing a soakaway without fixing compaction is a waste of money. diagnose first. connecting land drains to the sewer. this is illegal in most areas. land drains must go to a soakaway or surface water drain. building a soakaway too close to the house. at least 5 metres from the house, or you risk foundation damage. not maintaining drains. land drains silt up over time. lift inspection chambers annually and check flow. paving over the garden. this makes flooding worse, not better. use permeable surfaces instead. frequently asked questions will aeration fix my flooded lawn? if the flooding is caused by compaction, yes. if it is caused by a high water table or heavy clay, no. aeration helps but does not solve underlying drainage problems. how much does a land drain cost? 30 to 60 pounds per metre installed. a typical garden needs 10 to 30 metres, so 300 to 1,800 pounds. see our drainage guide . can i build a soakaway myself? yes, if you are fit and have a strong back. dig a hole 1 to 2 metres deep, fill with rubble or plastic crates, and connect a pipe from your drain. it is hard work but straightforward. cost: 200 to 300 pounds in materials. get help with garden flooding if your garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire floods after heavy rain, send us a whatsapp with photos and we will aim to respond within one day with a drainage plan and a quote. whatsapp get a quote further reading garden drainage problems: signs you need help how to improve clay soil aeration explained how to fix a garden that has become unmanageable how to fix a garden that has become unmanageable. step by step approach to bringing an overgrown, neglected garden back under control. how to fix a garden that has become unmanageable garden problems & weed control an unmanageable garden is one of the most common problems we help with. whether it has been neglected for a season or a decade, the approach is the same: assess, clear, repair, replant, maintain. this guide walks through the process of bringing an unmanageable garden back under control. quick answer to fix an unmanageable garden: assess the whole garden, clear the overgrowth first, repair any structural damage, replant with the right plants for your soil, and set up regular maintenance to keep it under control. most unmanageable gardens in the chilterns can be brought back under control in 1 to 3 months of work, with costs ranging from 500 to 3,000 pounds depending on size and condition. how gardens become unmanageable an unmanageable garden is one of the most common problems we help with across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire. gardens become unmanageable for predictable reasons: a new job with longer hours, a new baby, an illness, a divorce, a tenant who neglected the garden, an inherited garden that has been left for years. the result is the same: the garden gets away from you, and the longer you leave it, the harder it is to tackle. the good news is that almost any garden can be brought back under control, no matter how bad it looks. we have cleared gardens in the chilterns that had not been touched for 15 years, with bramble thickets 2 metres tall, self-seeded sycamores growing through the patio, and ivy covering every fence. within a month, those gardens were back to a blank canvas. within a year, they were beautiful. the key is a systematic approach. this guide walks through the process of bringing an unmanageable garden back under control, based on the method we use with clients across the chilterns. step 1: assess the whole garden before starting any work, walk the whole garden with a notebook and a phone full of photos. note: the overall condition (overgrown, neglected, damaged) the main problems (brambles, ivy, self-seeded trees, weeds, broken structures) what you want to keep (mature trees, established shrubs, structures in good condition) what you want to remove (overgrown shrubs, dead trees, broken fences) the soil type (chalk or clay, see our chalk soil guide ) the light conditions (sun, shade, partial shade) your budget and time this assessment takes 30 minutes and saves hours of work later. without it, you will start work in the wrong place and waste effort. step 2: clear the overgrowth first the first job is to clear the overgrowth so you can see what you are working with. this means: cutting back brambles, ivy and self-seeded trees to ground level digging out the root crowns (essential, see our bramble clearance guide ) removing dead and diseased plants clearing rubbish and debris digging out weeds (especially perennial weeds like bindweed and couch grass) for a typical unmanageable family garden (200 to 300 square metres), clearance takes 2 to 4 days and costs 350 to 750 pounds if we do it. larger gardens or more overgrown gardens cost more. see our clearance service . step 3: repair structural damage once the overgrowth is cleared, you can see the structural problems. common issues in unmanageable chiltern gardens: broken fences: caused by wind loading from overgrown hedges or ivy. see our fencing service . damaged patio: roots from self-seeded trees lifting paving. may need relaying. broken drains: roots in land drains or cracked pipes. check before replanting. rotting sheds and outbuildings: often hidden by overgrowth. replace if beyond repair. damaged lawn: often the easiest to fix with renovation. see our lawn recovery service . step 4: improve the soil an unmanageable garden has often had no soil improvement for years. the soil will be compacted, low in organic matter, and full of weed seeds. before replanting: dig over the soil to relieve compaction add 5cm of compost or well-rotted manure on heavy clay, add horticultural grit on chalky soils, add organic matter to build depth test the ph and adjust if needed see our soil preparation guide for detail. step 5: replant with the right plants choose plants suited to your soil and light conditions. on chalky chiltern soils, choose mediterranean plants and drought-tolerant perennials (see our chalk soil planting guide ). on clay, choose plants that tolerate wet winters and dry summers. plant in drifts of 3, 5 or 7 for impact. mulch after planting to suppress weeds. see our soft landscaping service for planting schemes. step 6: set up regular maintenance the biggest mistake people make is clearing the garden and then not maintaining it. within 12 months, the brambles and weeds are back. to prevent this: set up fortnightly maintenance from march to october monthly maintenance through winter annual mulch to suppress weeds annual lawn renovation hedge cutting in august/september see our maintenance service and maintenance preparation guide . how to tackle an unmanageable garden in stages if you cannot do the whole garden at once, tackle it in stages. this is the approach we recommend for clients on a budget: stage what to do timeframe cost 1. tidy the visible areas front garden, view from house, patio month 1 150-400 pounds 2. clear the overgrowth brambles, ivy, self-seeded trees month 2 350-750 pounds 3. repair structures fences, sheds, patio month 3 200-2,000 pounds 4. improve soil and replant borders, lawn month 4-6 500-2,000 pounds 5. set up maintenance fortnightly visits ongoing 1,500-3,000 pounds/year cost of fixing an unmanageable garden garden size light overgrowth medium overgrowth heavy overgrowth small (100 sqm) 350-600 pounds 600-1,000 pounds 1,000-1,500 pounds medium (300 sqm) 600-1,200 pounds 1,200-2,000 pounds 2,000-3,500 pounds large (500+ sqm) 1,200-2,000 pounds 2,000-3,500 pounds 3,500-6,000 pounds these costs include clearance, waste removal, and basic soil improvement. replanting and structural repairs are extra. common mistakes trying to do everything at once. pace yourself or hire help. burning out means the garden goes back to square one. not removing roots. cutting brambles and ivy without digging out the roots means they will be back within months. replanting too soon. wait until the soil is improved and weeds are under control. otherwise, new plants will be swamped. not setting up maintenance. a cleared garden without maintenance will be unmanageable again within 12 months. frequently asked questions how long does it take to fix an unmanageable garden? most gardens can be cleared and brought back under control in 2 to 6 weeks of work. larger or more overgrown gardens may take 2 to 3 months. setting up regular maintenance after that keeps it under control. should i do it myself or hire a professional? a mix is usually best. do the easy bits yourself (weeding, light pruning) and hire a professional for the heavy work (clearance, large hedge cutting, lawn renovation). our base rate is 40 pounds . can you save plants from an overgrown garden? often yes. mature shrubs can be renovated by hard pruning. perennials can be lifted and divided. trees can be pruned. we always try to save established plants, as they are the most valuable part of the garden. get help fixing your unmanageable garden if your garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire has become unmanageable, send us a whatsapp with photos and we will aim to respond within one day with a plan and a quote. whatsapp get a quote how much should you spend on the garden before selling a house? how much to spend on the garden before selling. high-roi jobs, what to skip, and how to present the garden for maximum sale price. how much should you spend on the garden before selling a house? gardens for selling or renting when selling a house, the garden is the first thing buyers see from the outside and the last thing they remember. but how much should you spend? this guide explains which garden jobs add the most value and which to skip. quick answer spend 500 to 1,500 pounds on the garden before selling. this typically returns 5,000 to 15,000 pounds in a higher sale price. focus on clearance, tidy-up, lawn recovery, and front garden kerb appeal. do not spend on expensive landscaping, as buyers will not pay extra for it. in the chilterns, where buyers are discerning and properties are expensive, a tidy garden is essential to achieve the asking price. why the garden matters when selling selling a property in the chilterns is competitive. buyers in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire are discerning, and a neglected garden can knock thousands off the asking price. estate agents in marlow, henley and beaconsfield regularly tell us that the garden is one of the top three things buyers notice, after the kitchen and bathroom. the good news is that a few hundred pounds of garden work before marketing can add thousands to the sale price. the bad news is that the wrong kind of work (expensive landscaping, quirky planting, major redesigns) can actually put buyers off. this guide explains what to spend money on, what to skip, and how to maximise the return on your garden investment before selling. what buyers notice first when a buyer arrives at your property, the garden is the first thing they see. in the first 30 seconds, they have already formed an opinion. here is what they notice: the front garden: is it tidy? does it look cared for? is there parking? the view from the road: can they see the house, or is it hidden by overgrowth? the back garden from the kitchen window: is it manageable? does it look like a burden or a bonus? the boundaries: are the fences and hedges intact? the lawn: is it green and tidy, or mossy and patchy? obvious problems: broken sheds, dead trees, bramble patches what to spend money on these are the jobs that give the best return on investment when selling: 1. clearance and tidy-up (300-750 pounds) clear all overgrowth, rubbish and debris. cut back anything overhanging paths, patios and the lawn. weed visible borders. this is the single most important job. see our clearance service . 2. lawn recovery (150-400 pounds) a green, tidy lawn makes the whole garden look cared for. mow, edge, feed, and treat moss. if the lawn is beyond repair, reseed or returf. see our lawn recovery service and lawn before selling guide . 3. front garden kerb appeal (200-500 pounds) the front garden is what buyers see first. tidy, mow, weed, sweep paths, clean the driveway. see our front garden checklist . 4. hedge cutting (100-300 pounds) cut hedges to a neat shape. overgrown hedges make the garden feel smaller and darker. see our hedge cutting service . 5. pressure washing (150-350 pounds) clean patios, paths and driveways. a clean patio looks bigger and more inviting. see our pressure washing service . 6. minor repairs (100-500 pounds) fix broken fence panels, shed doors, gate latches. buyers notice broken things and assume the worst. what not to spend money on these are jobs that buyers will not pay extra for: expensive landscaping: buyers may not like your taste. a blank canvas is better. specimen plants: small new trees look like nothing. mature trees add value, but you cannot add them in a month. water features: many buyers see them as a maintenance burden. veg plots: only appeal to a minority of buyers. better to convert to lawn. hot tubs: divisive. some buyers love them, many see them as a cost to remove. new sheds: unless the old one is rotting, leave it. buyers do not pay extra for a new shed. the pre-sale garden checklist clear all rubbish and debris from the front and back garden. cut back all overgrowth from paths, patios and the lawn. mow and edge the lawn . mow weekly from when the property goes on the market. weed visible borders and add a layer of bark mulch. trim hedges to a neat shape. prune overgrown shrubs that are blocking windows or paths. sweep and clean hard surfaces . pressure wash if needed. fix broken fences, gates and sheds . clean outside lights and replace bulbs. plant a few pots by the front door for colour. remove children's toys and garden clutter for viewings. clean the wheelie bins and move them out of sight. how much to spend by property value property value recommended spend expected return under 300,000 300-600 pounds 2,000-5,000 pounds 300,000 to 600,000 500-1,200 pounds 5,000-12,000 pounds 600,000 to 1,000,000 800-1,800 pounds 8,000-20,000 pounds over 1,000,000 1,500-3,500 pounds 15,000-40,000 pounds note: returns are typical for chiltern properties in 2026. actual returns depend on the local market and the condition of the garden. timing the garden work before selling start garden work 4 to 6 weeks before going on the market. this gives time for: clearance and tidy-up (1 to 2 weeks) lawn recovery (3 to 4 weeks to green up) hedge cutting (immediate effect) pressure washing (immediate effect) mulch to settle (1 to 2 weeks) if you are selling in winter, focus on clearance, structural repairs and pressure washing. lawn and planting work is best done in the growing season. common mistakes spending too much on major work. buyers do not pay extra for expensive landscaping. tidy, clean and neutral is enough. leaving obvious problems. a visible bramble patch or broken fence will put buyers off more than the cost of fixing it. planting new trees before selling. small new trees look like nothing. better to leave the space as mown lawn. not clearing the front garden. first impressions count. the front garden is what buyers see first. doing the work too late. allow 4 to 6 weeks before going on the market. lawn recovery takes time. frequently asked questions will a new patio add value to my property? a new patio may add value, but the return is usually less than the cost. buyers have their own ideas about patios. better to clean the existing patio and let the buyer decide. should i remove my shed before selling? only if it is rotting or unsightly. a functional shed is a selling point. a rotting shed is a negative. replace or remove as needed. do i need to landscape the garden to sell? no. a tidy, clean, neutral garden sells better than a newly landscaped one. buyers want a blank canvas they can make their own. get help preparing your garden for sale if you are selling a property in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire and want help preparing the garden, send us a whatsapp with garden jobs that add kerb appeal before a valuation garden jobs that add kerb appeal before a house valuation. front garden tidy-up, lawn care, planting and pressure washing for maximum impact. garden jobs that add kerb appeal before a valuation gardens for selling or renting kerb appeal is the first impression a property makes. the front garden is a big part of that. a tidy front garden suggests the property has been well maintained, while an overgrown one suggests neglect. this guide lists the garden jobs that add the most kerb appeal before a valuation. quick answer to add kerb appeal before a valuation: clear the front garden, mow and edge the lawn, trim hedges, clean the driveway and path, paint fences and gates, plant a few pots by the door, and remove all clutter. cost: 200 to 500 pounds for a typical semi-detached house. returns: typically 2,000 to 5,000 pounds on the valuation. in the chilterns, kerb appeal is essential to achieve the asking price. why kerb appeal matters kerb appeal is the first impression your property makes. a survey by rightmove found that 70 percent of buyers form an opinion of a property within 10 seconds of arriving. the front garden is a major part of that first impression. a tidy, welcoming front garden says "this property is cared for". a neglected front garden says "there are problems here". in the chilterns, where property values are high and buyers are discerning, kerb appeal is essential. estate agents in marlow, henley, beaconsfield and amersham regularly tell us that a tidy front garden can add 1 to 3 percent to the valuation. on a 600,000 pound property, that is 6,000 to 18,000 pounds for a few hundred pounds of work. this guide lists the garden jobs that add the most kerb appeal before a valuation, based on what estate agents and valuers tell us makes the biggest difference. the front garden kerb appeal checklist 1. clear all rubbish and debris (free) walk to the front of your property and look at it as a stranger would. remove: children's toys and bikes wheelie bins (move to side or back if possible) dead plants and old compost bags building materials and tools old newspapers and post 2. cut back overgrowth (50-150 pounds) overgrown plants make the property look neglected and hide the house. cut back: anything overhanging the path or driveway shrubs covering windows hedges that are too tall or wide climbers on the front of the house (especially around windows and doors) tree branches blocking the view of the house 3. mow and edge the lawn (free if diy, 30-60 pounds if we do it) a neatly mown lawn with crisp edges makes the whole property look cared for. mow the day before the valuation, not the morning of, so the grass settles. edge along paths and borders with a half-moon edger. 4. weed paths, borders and driveway (free if diy) weeds in paths and driveways are a major kerb appeal killer. hoe them off, or treat with a path weedkiller. weed the borders thoroughly. 5. clean the driveway and path (100-250 pounds for pressure washing) a clean driveway and path look much smarter. sweep first, then pressure wash if needed. see our pressure washing service and pressure washing guide . 6. paint fences, gates and railings (50-150 pounds) fresh paint on fences and gates makes a big difference. use a dark stain or black paint for a smart look. replace broken fence panels and gate latches. 7. plant pots by the front door (30-80 pounds) a pair of matching pots by the front door adds instant kerb appeal. use evergreen plants like box, bay or skimia for year-round impact. add seasonal bedding for colour. 8. clean the front door and brassware (free) clean the front door, polish the door furniture, and replace the door mat. a clean, smart front door makes a strong first impression. 9. clean windows and outside lights (free) clean windows let in more light and make the property look cared for. clean outside light fittings and replace bulbs. 10. hide the wheelie bins (free) wheelie bins are a kerb appeal killer. move them to the side or back of the house. if they must stay in front, build a simple screen or buy a bin store. the 7-day kerb appeal plan if you have a week before the valuation, here is a plan: day job time day 1 clear rubbish and clutter 1-2 hours day 2 cut back overgrowth 2-4 hours day 3 mow, edge, weed 2-3 hours day 4 clean driveway and path (pressure wash) 2-4 hours day 5 paint fences and gates 2-4 hours day 6 plant pots, clean door and windows 1-2 hours day 7 final tidy, hide bins, mow again 1 hour what valuers look for valuers (estate agents and surveyors) are looking at the overall condition of the property, including the garden. they are not gardeners, but they notice: obvious neglect (overgrown, rubbish, broken structures) the size and usability of the garden the condition of boundaries (fences, hedges) any major problems (dead trees,subsidence signs, drainage issues) privacy and overlooking a tidy garden lets the valuer focus on the property. a neglected garden makes them look for problems. cost vs return job cost impact on valuation clear rubbish and clutter free high cut back overgrowth 50-150 pounds high mow and edge lawn 30-60 pounds high clean driveway and path 100-250 pounds high paint fences and gates 50-150 pounds medium plant pots by door 30-80 pounds medium repair broken fence 100-300 pounds medium replace tired lawn 200-500 pounds low common mistakes spending too much on major work. buyers do not pay extra for expensive landscaping. tidy, clean and neutral is enough. leaving obvious problems. a visible bramble patch or broken fence will put buyers off more than the cost of fixing it. planting new trees before selling. small new trees look like nothing. better to leave the space as mown lawn. not clearing the front garden. first impressions count. the front garden is what buyers see first. doing the work too late. allow at least a week before the valuation. mown grass and pruned shrubs need time to look their best. frequently asked questions will a new front fence add value? a new fence may not add value directly, but a broken fence will reduce it. repair or replace broken fences, but do not spend a fortune on premium fencing. should i landscape the front garden before selling? no. a tidy, clean, neutral front garden sells better than a newly landscaped one. buyers want a blank canvas. how much should i spend on kerb appeal? 200 to 500 pounds for a typical property. spend on clearance, mowing, cleaning and minor repairs. do not spend on major landscaping. get help adding how to plan a garden redesign without wasting money how to plan a garden redesign without wasting money. budget, priorities, phasing and common mistakes to avoid. real uk advice from working gardeners. how to plan a garden redesign without wasting money garden design & layout ideas a garden redesign is a significant investment. done well, it transforms your outdoor space and adds value to your property. done badly, it wastes thousands and leaves you with a garden that does not work. this guide explains how to plan a garden redesign without wasting money. quick answer to plan a garden redesign without wasting money: start with a clear brief, work with the soil and conditions rather than against them, phase the work over 1 to 3 years, choose reliable plants over fashionable ones, and get at least three quotes for any major work. a typical chiltern garden redesign costs 5,000 to 25,000 pounds depending on size and complexity. the biggest waste of money is redesigning without a plan. why garden redesigns fail garden redesigns fail for predictable reasons. the most common is starting without a clear brief: "i want a nice garden" is not a brief. without a brief, you end up with a garden that does not suit your needs, your soil, or your budget. the second most common reason is following fashion: trendy designs date fast, and what looks modern in 2026 will look tired in 2031. the third is choosing the wrong contractor: a cheap contractor often costs more in the long run, as you pay to fix their mistakes. in the chilterns, where gardens range from small courtyard plots in marlow to large hillside gardens in princes risborough, redesigns vary enormously. but the principles of a successful redesign are the same regardless of size: work with the soil, choose the right plants, phase the work, and hire the right people. step 1: write a clear brief before any work starts, write down what you want from the garden. a good brief answers these questions: who uses the garden? adults, children, pets, elderly relatives? what do you use it for? entertaining, children playing, growing vegetables, relaxing, wildlife? how much time do you have for maintenance? be honest. a high-maintenance garden is a burden if you do not have the time. what is your budget? include a 15 percent contingency. what style do you like? cottage, contemporary, wildlife, formal, low-maintenance? what problems need solving? privacy, shade, drainage, slope, overlooking? what is the soil and light? test the soil and note sun and shade through the day. show this brief to any designer or contractor you work with. it will save hours of misunderstanding. step 2: work with the soil the biggest mistake in garden redesign is fighting the soil. if you have chalky soil, do not try to grow rhododendrons. if you have clay, do not try to grow mediterranean herbs. work with what you have. in the chilterns: chalk soils (high wycombe, amersham, wendover, princes risborough, marlow, henley): alkaline, free-draining, often shallow. suit mediterranean plants, herbs, drought-tolerant perennials. see our chalk soil planting guide . clay soils (aylesbury, bicester, didcot): heavy, wet in winter, dry in summer. suit shrubs, roses, perennials that tolerate wet feet. mixed soils : many gardens have both. test in several places. step 3: choose reliable plants over fashionable ones trendy plants date fast. a garden full of ornamental grasses and prairie planting looked modern in 2015, but many of those gardens now look tired and need redoing. reliable plants that have been grown in uk gardens for decades look good year after year and are easier to maintain. reliable choices for chiltern gardens: roses - flowering shrubs for months lavender - drought-tolerant, evergreen, scented choisya - evergreen, scented, reliable philadelphus - scented flowering shrub hardy geraniums - long-flowering ground cover alliums - dramatic flowers in late spring see our chiltern plant library for more. step 4: phase the work over 1 to 3 years a garden redesign does not have to be done all at once. phasing the work spreads the cost and lets you see how each phase looks before starting the next. a typical phased approach: phase what to do when cost 1. clearance and prep clear overgrowth, improve soil, repair structures year 1, autumn 500-2,000 pounds 2. hard landscaping patios, paths, fences, raised beds year 1, autumn/winter 2,000-10,000 pounds 3. planting trees, shrubs, perennials year 1/2, autumn/spring 1,000-5,000 pounds 4. lawn seed or turf year 2, spring/autumn 300-1,500 pounds 5. finishing touches pots, lighting, furniture year 2/3 500-2,000 pounds step 5: get multiple quotes for any major work (over 1,000 pounds), get at least three quotes. make sure each quote includes: exactly what is included (and what is not) materials and quantities timescale payment terms warranty waste removal () insurance details the cheapest quote is rarely the best. look for value: experience, references, and a clear plan. what to spend money on soil preparation: the foundation of a successful garden. 80 percent of planting failure is poor soil. hard landscaping: paths, patios, fences. these last for decades and are worth doing well. quality plants: from a reputable nursery, not a supermarket. bigger plants establish faster. drainage: if you have flooding, fix it before planting. design: a good designer saves money by avoiding mistakes. what not to spend money on fashionable plants: they date fast and often do not suit the conditions. cheap materials: false economy. they fail and need replacing. complex water features: high maintenance, often leak. imported statuary: tastes vary, and buyers may not like it. plastic lawns: divisive, the 2026/27 chilterns heatwave guide: keeping your hillside garden lush during a dry spell the 2026/27 chilterns heatwave guide. how to keep your hillside garden lush during dry spells. watering, mulching, plant choice and soil management. the 2026/27 chilterns heatwave guide: keeping your hillside garden lush during a dry spell drought & climate-resilient gardens heatwaves are becoming more frequent and more intense in the chilterns. the chalky, free-draining soils that make the area so good for mediterranean plants also mean gardens dry out fast. this guide explains how to keep your chiltern garden lush during a dry spell in 2026 and beyond. quick answer the 2026/27 chilterns heatwave guide: water deeply once a week, mulch to retain moisture, choose drought-tolerant plants, mow the lawn less and higher, move containers to shade, and protect tender plants with shade netting. heatwaves of 35 degrees celsius or more are now common in the chilterns, and gardens need specific care to survive them. climate-resilient planting is the long-term answer. why heatwaves are the new normal the chilterns is one of the driest parts of the uk, and heatwaves are becoming more frequent and more intense. the met office records show that the south east experienced heatwaves (5 or more days above 30 degrees celsius) in 2022, 2023, 2025 and 2026. the 2026 heatwave saw temperatures reach 38 degrees celsius in marlow and high wycombe, the highest ever recorded in the region. climate models predict that by 2030, the chilterns will experience 5 to 8 heatwave events per year, compared to 1 to 2 in the 1990s. annual rainfall is projected to decrease by 10 to 15 percent, with most of the decrease in summer. gardeners in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire need to adapt. this guide explains how to care for your garden during a heatwave, and how to make your garden more resilient to future heatwaves. how heat affects gardens heat affects gardens in several ways: water loss: plants lose water through their leaves faster than roots can absorb it. soil drying: chalky chiltern soils dry out fast, sometimes within 2 to 3 days of watering. lawn dormancy: grass goes brown and dormant to survive. most lawns recover when rain returns. container stress: pots dry out faster than the ground and may need watering twice a day. fruit and vegetable drop: plants drop flowers or fruit to conserve water. pest outbreaks: red spider mite and other pests thrive in hot, dry conditions. how to care for your garden during a heatwave watering water in the early morning (5 to 8 am) or evening (after 7 pm) to reduce evaporation. water deeply once a week rather than lightly every day. deep watering encourages deep roots. prioritise: containers, new plantings, vegetables, and tender plants first. established shrubs and trees usually survive without watering. use a watering can for containers. hosepipes waste water. use grey water (bath and washing-up water) for established ornamental plants. do not use on edibles. check soil moisture by pushing a finger 5cm into the soil. if it feels dry, water. mulching mulch is the single best defence against heat. a 5cm layer of bark or compost reduces evaporation by 70 percent and keeps soil temperatures 5 to 10 degrees cooler. apply mulch in spring before the heat arrives, and top up as needed. lawn care in heatwaves do not mow during a heatwave. cutting stresses the grass. set the mower high (40 to 50mm) before the heatwave to shade the soil. leave clippings on the lawn to act as mulch. if the lawn goes brown, accept it. grass goes dormant and recovers when rain returns. water only if you can commit to weekly. sporadic watering does more harm than good. container care move containers to shade during the hottest part of the day. group containers together to reduce evaporation. use water-retaining gel in the compost. stand containers in saucers of water during extreme heat. water twice a day in extreme heat (35 degrees plus). protecting tender plants move tender plants in pots to a shady spot. use shade netting (40 percent shade) over tender plants in the ground. water in the evening so plants have all night to absorb water. mist foliage in the early morning to cool the plant. how to make your garden heat-resistant long-term, the best defence against heatwaves is to make your garden more resilient. this means: 1. choose drought-tolerant plants replace thirsty plants (hydrangeas, hostas, rodgersias) with drought-tolerant ones. see our drought-tolerant plant guide . the best choices for chiltern gardens: lavender - silver foliage reflects sun rosemary - evergreen, drought-tolerant verbena bonariensis - tall, airy, deep roots sedum - fleshy leaves store water eryngium - spiky blue flowers, deep roots 2. improve the soil add organic matter annually to improve water retention. on chalky soils, this is essential. mulch thickly every spring. 3. install irrigation a drip irrigation system with a timer is the most efficient way to water. it uses 50 percent less water than a hosepipe and delivers water to the roots where it is needed. 4. reduce lawn area lawns are the thirstiest part of the garden. consider replacing some lawn with gravel, ground cover, or wildflower meadow. see our guide to gravel and permeable surfaces . 5. plant trees for shade trees provide shade and cool the air through transpiration. a mature tree can cool the air by 2 to 4 degrees. choose drought-tolerant trees like oak, beech, and rowan. the heatwave action plan when a heatwave is forecast: water deeply the day before the heatwave arrives. move containers to shade. apply shade netting to tender plants. do not mow the lawn. check containers twice a day during extreme heat. mist foliage in the early morning. harvest ripe fruit and vegetables to reduce plant stress. common mistakes watering at midday. most water evaporates. water in the morning or evening. watering lightly every day. this encourages shallow roots. water deeply once a week. mowing during a heatwave. cutting stresses the grass. wait until the heat breaks. not mulching. bare soil bakes hard and loses moisture. mulch is essential. planting thirsty plants on chalk. hydrangeas and hostas will struggle. choose drought-tolerant plants instead. frequently asked questions will my lawn recover from going brown? yes, in most cases. grass goes dormant in drought and recovers when rain returns. if the drought lasts more than 8 weeks, some grass may die and need overseeding in autumn. how often should i water during a heatwave? containers: daily, twice a day in extreme heat. new plantings: every 2 to 3 days. established plants: weekly. lawns: only if you can commit to weekly, otherwise let them go dormant. should i use a hosepipe during a heatwave? only if there is no hosepipe ban. check with your water company. even without a ban, use a watering can for containers to save water. get help
