beyond mulch: how we use gravel and permeable surfaces to conserve water in local landscapes beyond mulch: how gravel and permeable surfaces conserve water in chiltern gardens. gravel gardens, permeable paving and drought-resistant design. beyond mulch: how we use gravel and permeable surfaces to conserve water drought & climate-resilient gardens mulch is the first line of defence against drought, but it is not the only one. gravel gardens and permeable surfaces are increasingly popular in the chilterns as a way to conserve water while creating beautiful, low-maintenance gardens. this guide explains how we use these surfaces in local landscapes. quick answer beyond mulch: use gravel, permeable paving, and drought-tolerant planting to conserve water in your garden. gravel gardens need 50 to 70 percent less water than traditional borders. permeable paving reduces runoff and allows rain to soak into the ground. in the chilterns, where summers are getting drier, these techniques are essential for sustainable gardening. why water conservation matters in the chilterns the chilterns is one of the driest parts of the uk. annual rainfall in the thames valley around reading, maidenhead and slough averages just 600 to 650mm, less than jerusalem. the chalky soils drain fast, so even after heavy rain the soil can be dry within days. climate change is making summers hotter and drier, with hosepipe bans now common in south east england. water conservation in gardens is not just about saving water. it is about creating gardens that thrive without constant watering, that survive heatwaves, and that are sustainable in the long term. this guide explains the techniques we use to create water-efficient gardens across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire. mulch: the first line of defence mulch is the single most effective water conservation technique. a 5cm layer of bark or compost reduces evaporation by 70 percent, keeps soil temperatures 5 to 10 degrees cooler, and suppresses weeds. apply mulch in spring when the soil is moist, and top up annually. types of mulch: bark: cheap, long-lasting, looks good. best for ornamental borders. compost: feeds the soil as well as mulching. best for vegetables and roses. well-rotted manure: rich in nutrients. best for hungry plants like roses and vegetables. gravel: looks good with mediterranean plants. permanent, but does not feed the soil. leaf mould: free if you make it. best for woodland plants. see our mulching guide for detail. gravel gardens gravel gardens are the ultimate water-efficient garden. they need 50 to 70 percent less water than traditional borders, look good all year, and support a wide range of drought-tolerant plants. the classic gravel garden is the beth chatto garden in essex, but the style works equally well in the chilterns. how to build a gravel garden clear the area of all weeds, especially perennial weeds. improve the soil with compost if needed. on chalk, no improvement is usually needed. plant drought-tolerant plants in autumn so they establish before summer. apply a 5cm layer of gravel over the soil. use 10 to 20mm angular gravel, not pea shingle. water regularly for the first season until plants are established. after the first year, do not water. drought-tolerant plants do not need it. best plants for gravel gardens lavender rosemary salvia stipa tenuissima verbena bonariensis eryngium sedum alliums see our drought-tolerant plant guide for more. permeable paving permeable paving allows rain to soak into the ground rather than running off. this reduces flooding, recharges groundwater, and is required by planning regulations for front gardens over 5 square metres in england. types of permeable paving gravel: the cheapest and most permeable. 30 to 50 pounds per square metre. permeable block paving: blocks with wider joints filled with gravel. 60 to 100 pounds per square metre. porous asphalt: looks like normal asphalt but is porous. 50 to 80 pounds per square metre. grass reinforcement: plastic grids filled with soil and grass. 40 to 70 pounds per square metre. resin-bound gravel: smooth surface, fully permeable. 80 to 120 pounds per square metre. benefits of permeable paving reduces flooding by allowing rain to soak into the ground required for front gardens over 5 square metres in england reduces runoff into drains and sewers recharges groundwater often cheaper than traditional paving with drainage rain gardens a rain garden is a planted depression that collects and absorbs runoff from roofs, driveways and patios. it is a beautiful solution that also supports wildlife and reduces flooding. a rain garden is typically 10 to 20 percent of the drainage area, planted with moisture-tolerant plants. how to build a rain garden choose a location at least 5 metres from the house, where water naturally collects. dig a shallow depression 10 to 20cm deep. improve the soil with compost and grit. plant with moisture-tolerant plants (iris, lobelia, astilbe, primula). connect a pipe from your roof or driveway to the rain garden. mulch with gravel to retain moisture. cost: 300 to 800 pounds for a typical rain garden. water butts and irrigation every garden should have a water butt. a 200-litre water butt costs 50 to 100 pounds and provides free, chlorine-free water for plants. connect it to a downpipe from the roof. for larger gardens, consider a drip irrigation system. it uses 50 percent less water than a hosepipe and delivers water directly to the roots. a typical system costs 200 to 500 pounds for a small garden, 500 to 1,500 pounds for a medium garden. drought-tolerant lawns lawns are the thirstiest part of the garden. consider replacing some lawn with: wildflower meadow: needs no watering, supports wildlife. see our wildflower guide . clover lawn: stays green in drought, needs less mowing. gravel or planting: replace the lawn with gravel and drought-tolerant plants. artificial lawn: no watering, but no wildlife value. divisive. cost of water conservation measures measure cost water saved mulch (50 sqm) 50-150 pounds 30-50% gravel garden (50 sqm) 500-1,500 pounds 50-70% permeable paving (50 sqm) 1,500-5,000 pounds reduces runoff rain garden 300-800 pounds reduces runoff water butt (200 litres) 50-100 pounds free water drip irrigation 200-1,500 pounds 50% vs hosepipe clover lawn (50 sqm) 100-200 pounds 50% vs grass common mistakes not mulching. bare soil bakes hard and loses moisture. mulch is essential. planting in spring and not watering. new drought-tolerant plants need regular watering for the first growing season. mixing drought-tolerant with thirsty plants. one group will suffer. group by water needs. fertilising. rich soil produces soft growth that drought kills. drought-tolerant plants prefer poor soil. using impermeable paving. it increases runoff and flooding. use permeable surfaces instead. frequently asked questions do gravel gardens attract cats? cats sometimes use gravel as a litter tray. deterrents include citrus peels, coffee grounds, or commercial cat repellents. planting densely also deters cats. is permeable paving more expensive than regular paving? slightly more, but it avoids the cost of drainage systems. for front gardens over 5 square metres, permeable paving is required by planning regulations. how much water does a drip irrigation system save? about 50 percent compared to a hosepipe. drip irrigation delivers water directly to the roots, with no evaporation or runoff. get help when to pressure wash a patio when to pressure wash a patio in the uk. best time of year, weather conditions, and how often to clean your patio for best results. when to pressure wash a patio patio & pressure washing pressure washing transforms a green, slippery patio into a clean, safe surface. but timing matters. do it at the wrong time of year and the results will not last. this guide explains when to pressure wash a patio in the uk for the best, longest-lasting results. quick answer the best time to pressure wash a patio is in late spring (april to may) or early autumn (september to october). avoid pressure washing in winter (frost can damage surfaces) or in high summer (water evaporates too fast, leaving tide marks). most patios need pressure washing once a year. in the chilterns, where winters are wet and patios get green with algae, an annual wash in april or may is ideal. why timing matters pressure washing at the right time gives better results and avoids damaging the surface. the wrong time can leave tide marks, damage the surface, or cause efflorescence (white salt deposits). in the chilterns, where the climate is wet and patios are prone to algae, timing is particularly important. the best time to pressure wash late spring (april to may) this is the ideal time. the winter algae and grime have built up, the weather is warm enough to dry the patio quickly, and you have the whole summer to enjoy the clean patio. avoid pressure washing in march if there is still a risk of frost. early autumn (september to october) the second best time. the summer is over, the patio has accumulated grime from bbqs and foot traffic, and you want it clean before winter. avoid october if frost is forecast, as water in cracks can freeze and damage the surface. when not to pressure wash winter (november to march) avoid pressure washing in winter. frost can damage the surface, especially sandstone and limestone. water in cracks can freeze and split the stone. the patio will not dry properly, leaving tide marks. if you must pressure wash in winter, choose a mild, dry day with no frost forecast for 48 hours. high summer (july to august) avoid pressure washing in the heat of summer. water evaporates too fast, leaving tide marks and dissolved salts on the surface. if you must pressure wash in summer, do it early in the morning or in the evening when it is cooler. during or after rain the patio should be dry before you start. a wet patio will not show you which areas need cleaning, and the water will not penetrate the dirt. wait 24 to 48 hours after rain before pressure washing. during bird nesting season (if near hedges) if your patio is near a hedge with nesting birds, the noise of the pressure washer can disturb them. avoid pressure washing between march and august if nesting birds are present. how often to pressure wash most patios need pressure washing once a year. patios in shaded, damp positions (under trees, north-facing) may need washing twice a year. patios in sunny, well-ventilated positions may only need washing every 18 months. signs your patio needs pressure washing: green algae on the surface black or green staining slippery when wet weeds growing in the joints visible dirt and grime surface-specific timing surface when to wash pressure notes concrete any time, avoid frost high tough surface, can take high pressure block paving late spring medium re-sand after washing indian sandstone late spring, early autumn low soft stone, easy to damage limestone late spring, early autumn low very soft, hand clean if possible tarmac late spring low only high pressure damages tarmac timber decking late spring low use a decking cleaner, not high pressure the pressure washing process clear the area: remove furniture, pots, and obstacles. sweep: remove loose dirt and leaves. treat stains: apply a chemical cleaner for oil, rust, or organic stains. test a small area: check the pressure does not damage the surface. pressure wash: work in sections, keep the lance moving. re-sand block paving: brush kiln-dried sand into the joints. allow to dry: wait 24 to 48 hours before replacing furniture. consider sealing: a sealant keeps the surface cleaner for longer. cost of pressure washing area diy cost professional cost small patio (20 sqm) 50 (hire) + weekend 120-220 pounds standard patio (40 sqm) 70 (hire) + weekend 200-350 pounds driveway (50 sqm) 70 (hire) + weekend 250-450 pounds large drive (100 sqm) 100 (hire) + 2 days 450-750 pounds see our pressure washing cost guide and pressure wash calculator . common mistakes pressure washing too often. pressure washing wears the surface. once a year is enough for most patios. using too high a pressure. high pressure damages sandstone, tarmac and pointing. use the right pressure for the surface. not re-sanding block paving. pressure washing removes the joint sand. always re-sand with kiln-dried sand. pressure washing in winter. frost can damage the surface. wait for spring or autumn. not treating stains first. oil and rust stains need chemical treatment before pressure washing. frequently asked questions can i pressure wash in the rain? no. the patio should be dry before you start. a wet patio will not show you which areas need cleaning, and the water will not penetrate the dirt. how long does a pressure washed patio stay clean? 6 to 12 months, depending on shade, dampness, and foot traffic. shaded, damp patios go green faster. sealing the patio after washing keeps it cleaner for longer. will pressure washing damage my patio? not if done correctly. use the right pressure for the surface, keep the lance moving, and do not get too close. soft stones like sandstone and limestone need low pressure. get how to clean patio slabs with or without a pressure washer how to clean patio slabs with or without a pressure washer. chemical cleaners, manual methods and when to hire a professional. real uk advice. how to clean patio slabs with or without a pressure washer patio & pressure washing a green, slippery patio is dangerous and looks terrible. but you do not necessarily need a pressure washer to clean it. this guide explains how to clean patio slabs with or without a pressure washer, and when to call in a professional. quick answer to clean patio slabs: sweep first, treat stains with the right chemical, then either pressure wash (for hard surfaces) or hand clean with a stiff brush and patio cleaner (for soft stones like sandstone). on chalky chiltern soils, patios get particularly green with algae in winter. most patios need cleaning once a year, in late spring or early autumn. why patios need cleaning patios in the chilterns take a beating. the wet winters, the chalk dust that blows onto surfaces, and the shade from mature trees all encourage algae and moss. a slippery green patio is not just unsightly, it is a safety hazard, especially for elderly relatives and young children. regular cleaning keeps patios safe and looking good. the right cleaning method depends on the surface. hard surfaces like concrete and block paving can take pressure washing. soft stones like indian sandstone and limestone need gentler treatment to avoid damage. this guide explains how to clean each type of patio safely. step 1: sweep and clear before any cleaning, sweep the patio thoroughly to remove loose dirt, leaves and debris. remove all furniture, pots and obstacles. check the joints for weeds and dig them out. a clean, clear surface is essential for effective cleaning. step 2: treat stains different stains need different treatments: oil stains: apply a dedicated oil remover, brush in, leave for 30 minutes, rinse off. rust stains: apply a rust remover (oxalic acid based), leave for 15 minutes, rinse off. organic stains (leaves, berries): apply a patio cleaner with sodium hypochlorite, leave for 30 minutes, rinse off. barbecue grease: apply a degreaser, brush in, rinse off. bird droppings: scrape off, then wash with soapy water. chewing gum: freeze with a freeze spray, then chip off. step 3: choose your cleaning method method a: pressure washing (for hard surfaces) suitable for: concrete, block paving, brick, flagstones. hire or buy a pressure washer (110 to 150 bar is enough for most patios). test a small inconspicuous area first. work in sections of 2 to 3 square metres. keep the lance 20 to 30cm from the surface. keep the lance moving to avoid damaging the surface. for block paving, re-sand the joints with kiln-dried sand after washing. method b: hand cleaning with a stiff brush (for soft stones) suitable for: indian sandstone, limestone, slate, tarmac. mix a patio cleaner with water according to the instructions. apply the cleaner to the patio with a watering can. leave for 15 to 30 minutes (do not let it dry). scrub with a stiff-bristled brush. rinse with a hose or bucket of clean water. this method takes longer than pressure washing but is safer for soft stones. method c: chemical-only cleaning (for delicate surfaces) suitable for: very old or delicate stone, rendered walls, painted surfaces. apply a patio cleaner with a sprayer or watering can. leave for 30 to 60 minutes. rinse with a hose on low pressure. repeat if necessary. surface-specific cleaning guide concrete tough surface that can take pressure washing. use high pressure, but keep the lance moving. re-sand any joints. cost: 50 to 100 pounds diy, 150 to 350 pounds professional. block paving pressure wash on medium pressure. always re-sand the joints with kiln-dried sand after washing. consider sealing to keep the surface cleaner for longer. cost: 60 to 120 pounds diy, 200 to 450 pounds professional. indian sandstone soft stone that can be damaged by high pressure. hand clean with a stiff brush and patio cleaner. if pressure washing, use low pressure (under 100 bar) and keep the lance 30cm away. cost: 70 to 150 pounds diy, 250 to 500 pounds professional. limestone very soft stone. hand clean only. use a dedicated limestone cleaner (acid-free). never use bleach or acid-based cleaners on limestone. cost: 80 to 150 pounds diy, 250 to 500 pounds professional. timber decking use a dedicated decking cleaner, not pressure washing. pressure washing raises the grain and damages the timber. scrub with a stiff brush, rinse with a hose. cost: 50 to 100 pounds diy, 150 to 300 pounds professional. tarmac use low pressure only. high pressure damages tarmac. better to hand clean with a stiff brush and tarmac cleaner. cost: 50 to 100 pounds diy, 150 to 300 pounds professional. how to prevent patio staining seal the surface: a sealant keeps the surface cleaner for longer. apply every 2 to 3 years. improve drainage: standing water encourages algae. fix drainage problems. trim overhanging plants: shade encourages algae. let more light in. sweep regularly: leaves left on the patio cause staining. treat algae annually: apply an algae killer in autumn to prevent winter growth. cost of patio cleaning method diy cost professional cost pressure wash (40 sqm) 50-70 (hire) 200-350 pounds hand clean (40 sqm) 30-50 (cleaner) 250-450 pounds chemical clean (40 sqm) 40-60 (chemicals) 200-400 pounds sealing (40 sqm) 80-150 (sealant) 200-500 pounds common mistakes using high pressure on soft stone. indian sandstone and limestone can be permanently damaged. use low pressure or hand clean. using bleach on limestone. bleach etches limestone and leaves white marks. use a dedicated limestone cleaner. not re-sanding block paving. pressure washing removes the joint sand. always re-sand with kiln-dried sand. not treating stains first. pressure washing alone will not remove oil or rust stains. treat with the right chemical first. cleaning in winter. frost can damage wet surfaces. clean in spring or autumn. frequently asked questions can i use bleach to clean my patio? on concrete and block paving, yes, diluted 1:10 with water. on sandstone and limestone, no. bleach etches soft stones and leaves white marks. use a dedicated patio cleaner instead. will pressure washing remove weeds from the joints? it will remove the tops, but not the roots. dig out weeds with a knife or weed tool first, then pressure wash. how do i stop my patio going green again? improve drainage, trim overhanging plants to let in light, and apply an algae killer in autumn. sealing the patio also helps. get help cleaning your patio if you need help cleaning your patio in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp why patios go green and slippery in wet weather why patios go green and slippery in wet weather. algae, moss and lichen growth explained, plus how to prevent and remove them. why patios go green and slippery in wet weather patio & pressure washing a green, slippery patio is one of the most common complaints we hear, especially in autumn and winter. the green film is algae, and it thrives in the wet, shady conditions of a uk winter. this guide explains why patios go green and slippery, and how to prevent and remove it. quick answer patios go green and slippery because of algae, moss and lichen that thrive in damp, shaded conditions. the wet chilterns winters and the shade from mature trees create ideal conditions for algae growth. solutions: pressure wash annually, improve drainage, trim overhanging plants, apply an algae killer in autumn, and consider sealing the surface. why patios go green the green slime on your patio is algae, a simple plant that thrives in damp, shaded conditions. algae reproduce by spores that are everywhere in the air. when the conditions are right (damp, shaded, with a rough surface to cling to), the spores germinate and form a green film. within weeks, the patio is slippery and dangerous. in the chilterns, the conditions for algae growth are perfect. the wet winters (200 to 250mm of rain from november to february), the mature trees that shade many gardens, and the chalky dust that provides nutrients all encourage algae. patios in marlow, henley and beaconsfield, with their mature beech and oak trees, are particularly prone. moss and lichen also grow on patios, but they prefer slightly different conditions. moss likes damp, shaded, compacted surfaces. lichen likes sunny, exposed, porous surfaces. all three make the patio slippery and unsightly. the conditions that cause algae damp: algae need moisture. patios that stay wet for long periods are most affected. shade: algae prefer shade. patios under trees or on the north side of the house are most affected. rough surface: algae cling to rough surfaces better than smooth. concrete and sandstone are more affected than polished stone. poor drainage: standing water encourages algae. fix drainage to fix the algae. organic matter: leaves, compost and bird droppings feed algae. sweep regularly. chalk dust: in the chilterns, chalk dust provides calcium that algae love. how to get rid of algae 1. pressure wash the most effective way to remove algae. use a pressure washer (110 to 150 bar) on a dry day in late spring or early autumn. see our pressure washing guide . 2. chemical treatment if pressure washing is not enough, apply a patio cleaner with sodium hypochlorite or benzalkonium chloride. these chemicals kill algae and prevent regrowth for 6 to 12 months. apply in spring or autumn, leave for 30 minutes, rinse off. 3. vinegar solution for an organic approach, mix white vinegar with water 1:1 and spray on the algae. leave for 15 minutes, scrub with a stiff brush, rinse off. less effective than chemical cleaners but safer for pets and children. 4. boiling water for small areas, boiling water kills algae on contact. pour directly on the algae, scrub with a stiff brush, rinse off. best for small patches, not large patios. how to prevent algae returning 1. improve drainage standing water encourages algae. fix drainage problems by: re-leveling sunken areas where water collects installing a land drain to carry water away creating a soakaway to absorb runoff using permeable paving to allow water to drain through see our garden flooding guide . 2. trim overhanging plants shade encourages algae. trim trees and shrubs that overhang the patio to let in more light and air. even thinning the canopy by 20 percent can make a big difference. 3. apply an algae killer in autumn apply a preventative algae killer in october, before the wet winter weather sets in. this kills spores before they can establish. reapply in spring if needed. 4. seal the surface a sealant makes the surface less hospitable to algae. it fills the pores in the stone, leaving nowhere for spores to take hold. apply every 2 to 3 years. cost: 5 to 10 pounds per square metre. 5. sweep regularly leaves and organic matter feed algae. sweep the patio weekly in autumn to remove leaves, and as needed through the year. 6. use the right surface if you are laying a new patio, choose a surface that resists algae: polished stone is less prone to algae than rough stone resin-bound gravel is less prone than block paving composite decking is less prone than timber avoid tarmac in shaded areas (it stays wet and grows algae fast) the slippery surface test to test if your patio is dangerously slippery, walk across it in wet shoes. if you slip or feel insecure, it needs cleaning. a safe patio should give good grip even when wet. the health and safety executive recommends a slip resistance of 36 or above (measured with a pendulum tester). most patios with algae score below 20, which is dangerously slippery. a clean, dry patio scores 60 to 80. cost of treating algae treatment diy cost professional cost pressure wash (40 sqm) 50-70 (hire) 200-350 pounds chemical treatment (40 sqm) 20-40 (chemicals) 100-200 pounds sealing (40 sqm) 80-150 (sealant) 200-500 pounds tree trimming (per tree) 0 (diy) 100-300 pounds drainage improvement 200-400 (materials) 600-1,200 pounds common mistakes not treating the cause. cleaning algae without fixing drainage or shade means it comes back. diagnose first. using bleach on soft stone. bleach etches sandstone and limestone. use a dedicated patio cleaner instead. pressure washing too often. pressure washing wears the surface. once a year is enough. not applying a preventative treatment. an autumn algae killer prevents winter growth. ignoring the slip risk. a slippery patio is a safety hazard, especially for elderly relatives. clean it before someone falls. frequently asked questions why does my patio go green in winter? winter in the chilterns is wet and shady, perfect conditions for algae. the algae spores are always in the air, but they only grow when conditions are right. will pressure washing damage my patio? not if done correctly. use the right pressure for the surface (low for sandstone, medium for block paving, high for concrete) and keep the lance moving. how do i stop my patio going green again? improve drainage, trim overhanging plants to let in light, apply an algae killer in autumn, and seal the surface every 2 to 3 years. get help with a slippery patio if your patio in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire has gone green and slippery, send us a whatsapp with photos and we will aim to respond within one day. whatsapp get a quote further reading when to pressure rewilding with intent: how professional maintenance keeps a wild garden looking refined rewilding with intent. how professional maintenance keeps a wild garden looking refined and managed, not messy. structured wildlife gardening. rewilding with intent: how professional maintenance keeps a wild garden looking refined drought & climate-resilient gardens rewilding is a popular trend, but an unmanaged wild garden quickly looks messy. the answer is rewilding with intent: a structured approach that supports wildlife while keeping the garden looking intentional and cared for. this guide explains how we do it. quick answer rewilding with intent means creating a wild garden that is deliberately designed and maintained, not just abandoned. the result is a garden that supports wildlife, looks intentional, and is a joy to spend time in. in the chilterns, where gardens often back onto countryside, rewilding with intent is particularly effective. the key is a clear structure, careful plant selection, and regular maintenance to keep the wild look refined rather than chaotic. what is rewilding with intent? rewilding is a buzzword in gardening, but it is often misunderstood. many people think rewilding means abandoning the garden and letting nature take over. the result is usually a bramble thicket and a neighbour dispute. true rewilding, the kind that produces a beautiful and wildlife-rich garden, is the opposite of abandonment. it is deliberate, designed, and maintained. rewilding with intent means: choosing native plants that support local wildlife creating habitats (pond, log piles, long grass, wildflowers) maintaining the garden to keep it looking intentional managing invasive species so they do not take over providing year-round resources for wildlife (nectar, berries, shelter) in the chilterns, with its mix of chalk downland, beech woods and farmland, rewilding with intent can produce stunning results. the garden becomes a bridge between the cultivated and the wild, supporting a wider range of wildlife than either alone. the three-zone approach the best way to combine rewilding with a tidy appearance is the three-zone approach: zone 1: formal garden (visible from the house): mown lawn, clipped hedges, neat borders. maintained weekly or fortnightly. zone 2: wildlife-friendly planting (middle of the garden): native and pollinator-friendly plants, long grass, less frequent maintenance. zone 3: wild area (bottom of the garden): unmown grass, log piles, leaf piles, minimal maintenance. this approach gives you the best of both worlds: a garden that looks good from the house and a wildlife area that supports a wide range of species. designing a rewilded garden structure a rewilded garden needs structure to look intentional. this means: mown paths through long grass, so it looks deliberate clipped hedges around the wild area, to frame it defined edges between zones, using mowing strips or edging focal points like a specimen tree or a bench, to draw the eye plant selection choose native plants that support local wildlife. for the chilterns: trees: oak, rowan, holly, hawthorn, field maple shrubs: hazel, dogwood, spindle, guelder rose perennials: foxglove, ox-eye daisy, knapweed, scabious, yarrow grasses: tufted hair grass, false oat grass, yorkshire fog bulbs: bluebell, snowdrop, wild daffodil, wild garlic see our wildlife plants guide for more. habitats create a range of habitats to support different species: pond: the single best wildlife feature. even a small pond attracts frogs, newts, dragonflies and birds. long grass: supports insects, ground-nesting bees, and butterflies. log piles: habitat for beetles, frogs, slowworms and hedgehogs. leaf piles: habitat for hedgehogs, insects, and frogs. bee hotels: for solitary bees. bird boxes and feeders: for nesting and feeding birds. compost heap: habitat for slowworms, hedgehogs, and beetles. maintenance for a rewilded garden a rewilded garden needs different maintenance from a manicured garden, but it still needs maintenance. the key jobs are: mow paths through long grass monthly, to keep it looking intentional. cut long grass once a year in late summer (august to september), after the wildflowers have set seed. remove invasive species (bramble, nettle, dock) before they take over. manage ponds: clear excess vegetation in autumn, not spring. prune shrubs in late winter, after the berries have been eaten. leave seed heads in autumn for birds, cut back in spring. maintain habitats: top up log piles, leaf piles, and bee hotels as needed. the maintenance calendar for a rewilded garden month job january prune shrubs, leave seed heads for birds february clean bird boxes, top up log piles march mow paths through long grass, plant bare-root trees april mow paths monthly, weed invasive species may mow paths, monitor pond for spawn june mow paths, deadhead early wildflowers july mow paths, prepare for summer cut august cut long grass after seed set, rake off hay september clean pond, top up leaf piles october collect leaves for leaf piles, plant bulbs november plant bare-root trees and shrubs december leave the garden alone, wildlife needs winter shelter common mistakes letting the whole garden go wild. this looks abandoned, not rewilded. use the three-zone approach. planting only non-native plants. native plants support more wildlife. mix native and non-native. tidying up too much in autumn. seed heads, leaf piles and dead stems are winter habitat. leave them until spring. not having water. even a small pond is essential. wildlife needs water year-round. not managing invasive species. bramble, nettle and dock will take over a rewilded garden if not managed. frequently asked questions is a rewilded garden less work than a manicured garden? no, it is different work. less mowing and weeding, but more monitoring and managing invasive species. a rewilded garden is not a low-maintenance garden, it is a different-maintenance garden. will a rewilded garden annoy my neighbours? not if it is well-maintained. the three-zone approach, with a tidy zone 1 visible from the road, prevents the "abandoned" look that causes complaints. how long does it take for a rewilded garden to establish? 3 to 5 years for the plants to establish and the wildlife to find it. the first year looks untidy, the second year looks promising, and by the third year it looks intentional. get help with rewilding if you want to rewild your garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with photos and we will aim to respond within one day. whatsapp get a quote further reading best wildlife plants for chiltern gardens balancing tidy and biodiverse leaving part of your garden wild best wildlife plants for chiltern gardens best wildlife plants for chiltern gardens. native shrubs, pollinator flowers, berry plants and trees that support birds, bees and butterflies. best wildlife plants for chiltern gardens wildlife & wildflower gardens wildlife gardening is about choosing the right plants. the right plants provide nectar, pollen, berries, shelter and nesting sites for a wide range of species. this guide lists the best wildlife plants for chiltern gardens, all of which we plant regularly in client gardens across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire. quick answer the best wildlife plants for chiltern gardens include: lavender, buddleja, verbena bonariensis, sedum, echinacea, alliums, holly, hawthorn, rowan, pyracantha, ivy and honeysuckle. choose a mix of nectar-rich flowers for bees and butterflies, berries for birds, and evergreens for shelter. in the chilterns, where gardens often back onto countryside, a wildlife-friendly garden can support a remarkable range of species. why wildlife planting matters in the chilterns the chilterns is one of the most wildlife-rich areas of southern england. the chalk downland, beech woods, wildflower meadows and farmland all support a wide range of species. but intensive farming, development and pesticide use have all reduced the wildlife in our gardens. gardeners in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire can help by planting for wildlife. the good news is that wildlife-friendly plants are often the most beautiful garden plants. lavender, buddleja, echinacea and verbena are garden classics that also support pollinators. holly, rowan and pyracantha provide berries for birds and structure for the garden. a well-designed wildlife garden looks better than a manicured one, and it supports the local ecosystem. best plants for bees and butterflies bees and butterflies need nectar-rich flowers throughout the year. choose plants that flower at different times to provide a continuous food source. spring nectar (march to may) crocus - early nectar for bumblebees hellebore - flowers from february to april primrose - native, early nectar mahonia - winter flowers for early bumblebees rosemary - flowers in late winter forsythia - bright yellow flowers in march summer nectar (june to august) lavender - the best plant for bees, flowers for months buddleja - the butterfly bush, flowers from july to september verbena bonariensis - tall, airy, butterflies love it sedum - late summer flowers for bees echinacea - long-flowering, butterflies alliums - early summer flowers for bees salvia - long-flowering, bees love it marjoram - herb, flowers for bees autumn nectar (september to november) sedum - late flowers for bees preparing for winter ivy - late nectar for bees and butterflies japanese anemone - flowers into october aster - late flowers for butterflies fuchsia - flowers until first frost best plants for birds birds need berries, seeds and shelter. plant a mix of berrying shrubs and evergreens for year-round support. berrying shrubs holly - berries in winter, evergreen shelter hawthorn - berries in autumn, nesting sites rowan - berries in late summer, autumn colour pyracantha - berries for months, thorny shelter cotoneaster - berries from autumn to winter berberis - berries and thorny shelter guelder rose - native, berries in autumn seed plants sunflowers - seeds for finches in autumn teasel - seeds for goldfinches in winter thistle - seeds for goldfinches (if you can tolerate it) cornflower - seeds for small birds phlox - seeds for finches shelter plants ivy - evergreen shelter for nesting birds holly - dense evergreen shelter hawthorn - thorny nesting sites privet - dense evergreen hedge best plants for other wildlife ivy - late nectar, winter berries, habitat for insects and birds honeysuckle - moths, berries, nesting sites for dormice primrose - early nectar for bees, food for primrose moth caterpillars nettle - food for butterfly caterpillars (peacock, small tortoiseshell, red admiral) native grasses - food for moth caterpillars, seeds for birds plants to avoid some plants offer little or nothing to wildlife: double flowers: no nectar or pollen (e.g. double roses, double dahlias) highly bred cultivars: often have no scent or nectar annual bedding: short flowering period, no value to wildlife invasive species: rhododendron ponticum, snowberry, three-cornered leek how to design a wildlife border choose a sunny spot - bees and butterflies prefer sun plant in drifts of 3, 5 or 7 - easier for pollinators to find mix flower shapes - tubular (for long-tongued bees), flat (for butterflies), composite (for short-tongued bees) plan for year-round flowering - spring bulbs, summer perennials, autumn sedum and ivy include evergreens for winter shelter add water - even a bird bath makes a difference leave seed heads in autumn for birds common mistakes planting only non-native plants. native plants support more wildlife. mix native and non-native for the best results. using double flowers. double flowers have no nectar or pollen. choose single flowers instead. tidying up too much in autumn. seed heads and leaf piles are winter habitat. leave them until spring. not having water. even a small pond or bird bath is essential for wildlife. using pesticides. pesticides kill beneficial insects as well as pests. use organic methods. frequently asked questions what is the best single plant for wildlife? lavender. it flowers for months, bees love it, butterflies visit it, and it is evergreen. plant a swathe of lavender and you will have wildlife all summer. are native plants better for wildlife than non-native? native plants generally support more species, but non-native plants can also be valuable (e.g. buddleja for butterflies). a mix is best. how do i attract butterflies to my garden? plant buddleja, verbena bonariensis and sedum. provide a sunny, sheltered spot. avoid pesticides. leave a nettle patch for caterpillars. get help with wildlife planting if you want to plant for wildlife in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with how to make a hedge better for birds and pollinators how to make a garden hedge better for birds and pollinators. mixed native hedges, berry plants, flowering shrubs and timing of cutting. how to make a hedge better for birds and pollinators hedges, pruning & cutting times hedges are one of the most valuable wildlife features in any garden. a well-managed hedge provides nesting sites, berries, nectar and shelter for a huge range of species. this guide explains how to make your hedge better for birds and pollinators. quick answer to make a hedge better for birds and pollinators: plant a mix of native species (hawthorn, blackthorn, hazel, holly), let it flower and berry before cutting, cut once a year in late winter rather than twice a year, and plant climbers (ivy, honeysuckle) at the base. a mixed native hedge supports 10 times more wildlife than a single-species evergreen hedge. why hedges matter for wildlife hedges are the single best wildlife feature in a garden. a mixed native hedge provides flowers for pollinators, berries for birds, nesting sites, shelter from wind and predators, and corridors for wildlife to move through the landscape. a well-managed hedge can support more than 50 species of bird, 30 species of butterfly and moth, and hundreds of other insects. in the chilterns, where farmland hedges have been removed over decades, garden hedges are increasingly important for wildlife. a garden hedge in marlow or henley can be a vital link between isolated patches of countryside. the more gardeners who plant wildlife-friendly hedges, the more connected the landscape becomes. why most garden hedges are bad for wildlife most garden hedges are bad for wildlife. the typical garden hedge is a single species (leylandii, beech, yew, privet), cut twice a year for a neat, formal look. this means: no flowers for pollinators (cut before they bloom) no berries for birds (cut before they form) single species supports fewer insects than a mix dense evergreen shades out the ground flora formal shape offers no nesting sites a leylandii hedge cut twice a year supports almost no wildlife. a mixed native hedge cut once a year is a wildlife haven. the best hedge plants for wildlife hawthorn the best single hedge plant for wildlife. flowers in may (hawthorn blossom, or "may"), berries in autumn (haws), thorny nesting sites. supports over 300 species of insect. native to the uk. blackthorn flowers in march before the leaves (sloe berries in autumn). thorns provide dense nesting cover. supports many moth caterpillars. native. hazel catkins in spring (yellow pollen for early bees), hazelnuts in autumn. supports dormice (in some areas) and many moth caterpillars. native. holly evergreen, berries in winter (on female plants), nesting sites. slow-growing but very long-lived. native. field maple native tree, good for hedges. winged seeds in autumn. supports many moth caterpillars. dogwood native shrub, white flowers in spring, black berries in autumn, coloured stems in winter. good for wet soils. spindle native shrub, unusual pink and orange berries in autumn. supports many moth caterpillars. guelder rose native shrub, white flowers in spring, red berries in autumn. good for damp soils. other wildlife-friendly hedge plants beech: not native to the chilterns chalk but widely planted. holds brown leaves in winter, supports some insects. hornbeam: similar to beech, native to clay soils. good for wildlife. privet: semi-evergreen, flowers for bees if not cut. good for nesting. escallonia: non-native but flowers for bees. good for coastal and mild areas. how to plant a wildlife hedge choose a mix of species: 50 percent hawthorn, 25 percent blackthorn, 25 percent other (hazel, holly, field maple, dogwood). plant bare-root in winter (november to march) for best value. 5 to 7 plants per metre in a double staggered row. prepare the soil: dig in compost, remove perennial weeds. plant at the right depth: the same as they were in the nursery. water regularly for the first season. mulch annually to suppress weeds. cut once a year in late winter (february) for the first 3 years to encourage bushy growth. after 3 years, cut once every 2 to 3 years to allow flowering and berrying. see our hedge planting guide and hedging plants guide . how to manage a hedge for wildlife cut once a year, not twice. once-a-year cutting lets the hedge flower and berry. cut in late winter (february) after the berries have been eaten. avoid cutting in bird nesting season (march to august). cut on a 2 to 3 year rotation: cut one side one year, the other side the next, the top the third year. this leaves some berries and shelter each winter. let some plants grow into trees. a holly or rowan allowed to grow to 4 metres adds structure and value. plant climbers at the base: ivy, honeysuckle, dog rose, clematis. these add nectar, berries and nesting sites. leave the base untidy: leaf litter and log piles at the base support hedgehogs, frogs and insects. the cost of a wildlife hedge item cost bare-root plants (60-80cm, 5 per metre) 2-4 pounds per plant planting (10 metre hedge) 100-200 pounds diy, 200-400 pounds professional mulch 20-50 pounds annual cut 50-150 pounds professional a 10 metre mixed native hedge costs 200 to 400 pounds planted, plus 50 to 150 pounds per year for cutting. common mistakes planting a single species. a single-species hedge supports far less wildlife than a mix. plant at least 3 species. cutting twice a year. this removes flowers and berries. cut once a year, in late winter. cutting in nesting season. it is an offence to damage active nests. cut in february or september. planting leylandii. leylandii supports almost no wildlife. choose native species instead. not planting climbers. ivy, honeysuckle and dog rose add huge wildlife value to a hedge. frequently asked questions what is the best hedge plant for birds? hawthorn. it flowers in may, berries in autumn, and provides thorny nesting sites. plant 50 percent hawthorn in any wildlife hedge. when should i cut my wildlife hedge? once a year, in late winter (february). avoid cutting between march and august when birds are nesting. see our hedge cutting guide . how wide should a wildlife hedge be? at least 60cm wide. a wider hedge (90cm to 1.2m) supports more wildlife and is healthier than a thin hedge. how to get rid of ivy without damaging fences and walls how to get rid of ivy without damaging fences and walls. cut at base, let it die, then pull it off. treat stumps to prevent regrowth. how to get rid of ivy without damaging fences and walls garden problems & weed control ivy can damage fences, walls and trees if left unchecked. but pulling live ivy off a surface can cause more damage than the ivy itself. this guide explains how to get rid of ivy safely without damaging the surface underneath. quick answer to get rid of ivy without damaging fences and walls: cut the stems at the base, leave the top growth to die for 2 to 3 weeks, then pull it off. never pull live ivy off walls or fences, as the aerial roots will damage the surface. treat the cut stump with glyphosate to prevent regrowth. in older chiltern properties with lime mortar, check the pointing before you start. why ivy removal needs care ivy (hedera helix) is a native uk climber that clings to surfaces with aerial roots. these roots secrete a weak cement-like substance that grips onto brickwork, render, fence panels and tree bark. when you pull live ivy off a surface, the aerial roots come away with chunks of mortar, render or wood, causing real damage. dead ivy, on the other hand, releases its grip as it dries out and can be pulled off without damage. the key is patience: cut the stems, wait for the ivy to die, then remove it. in the chilterns, where many properties are older (pre-1920s) with lime mortar, ivy can cause particular damage. the aerial roots work into the soft mortar and loosen it over years. removing ivy from these walls needs extra care, and you should expect to do some repointing afterwards. the ivy removal process step 1: cut the stems at the base cut all the ivy stems at the base of the wall or fence, about 30cm above ground level. use secateurs or loppers. cut a 15cm section out of each stem to prevent them rejoining. this is essential: a single uncut stem will keep the whole plant alive. step 2: let the top growth die leave the top growth to die for 2 to 3 weeks. the leaves will go brown and crisp. in a hot chilterns summer this can take as little as 10 days. in a wet autumn it can take 4 weeks. test a small section first: if the ivy pulls away easily, it is ready. step 3: pull the dead ivy off start at the top and work down. pull gently, working the stems away from the surface. use a stiff brush to remove the dried root stubs. if it resists, leave it another week. forcing it will damage the surface. step 4: treat the stump treat the cut stump with a glyphosate-based weedkiller to prevent regrowth. paint it on with a brush rather than spraying, to avoid drift onto other plants. or dig out the root if accessible. on chalky chiltern soils, ivy roots come out fairly easily with a fork. step 5: check the surface check the wall or fence for damage. ivy aerial roots can loosen mortar and render. on older properties around henley, marlow and beaconsfield, repointing may be needed. if the wall has render, check for cracks where water could get in. ivy on fences ivy on fences is easier to remove than on walls, as fence panels are replaceable. cut at the base, let it die, then pull it off. if the fence is old and the ivy has grown into the panels, you may need to replace the affected panels. a common problem in chiltern gardens is ivy growing through a hedge. in this case, cut the ivy stems at the base and let it die in the hedge. the dead ivy will gradually fall out over a year or two. trying to pull live ivy out of a hedge damages the hedge. see our fencing service for fence replacement. ivy on trees ivy in trees is not always a problem. it provides shelter and food for wildlife, and it does not parasitise the tree (it uses the tree as a support, not as a food source). but if it is shading the canopy, making the tree top-heavy, or weighing down branches in winter storms, it should be removed. cut all the ivy stems at the base of the tree. cut a 15cm section out of each stem. leave the ivy to die in the tree. it will gradually fall off over 1 to 2 years. do not try to pull live ivy out of the canopy, as it can break branches. treat the cut stump with weedkiller or dig out the root. when not to remove ivy ivy is not always the enemy. it is one of the best plants for wildlife: flowers in october, providing late nectar for bees and butterflies berries in winter, food for birds when little else is available evergreen shelter for nesting birds and roosting bats insulates walls, reducing heat loss in winter consider leaving ivy on sound walls where it is not causing damage, especially if it is providing valuable wildlife habitat. see our wildlife plants guide . the cost of ivy removal job diy cost professional cost ivy removal from 10 sqm wall 0 (tools) 150-300 pounds ivy removal from 20 sqm fence 0 (tools) 200-400 pounds ivy removal from tree 0 (tools) 100-300 pounds repointing (per sqm) 20-40 (materials) 60-120 pounds fence panel replacement 30-60 (panel) 80-150 pounds common mistakes pulling live ivy off walls. this rips off mortar and render. always cut and let it die first. cutting only some stems. a single uncut stem keeps the whole plant alive. cut every stem at the base. not treating the stump. ivy regrows vigorously from a cut stump. treat with glyphosate or dig out the root. removing ivy during nesting season. dense ivy on walls and trees is a popular nest site. check for nests before cutting between march and august. not checking the wall afterwards. ivy can hide cracks and damage. always inspect the wall after removal. frequently asked questions does ivy damage brickwork? on sound modern brickwork with hard mortar, ivy usually causes no damage. on older properties with lime mortar (pre-1920s), ivy can loosen the mortar and cause damp. always check the condition of the wall before letting ivy grow on it. will ivy kill my tree? no. ivy is not a parasite, it uses the tree as a support but does not take food or water from it. however, ivy can shade the tree's canopy and make it top-heavy in winter storms. if your tree is healthy and the ivy is not excessive, leave it for the wildlife. how do i stop ivy coming back? dig out the root, or treat the stump with glyphosate. even then, birds drop ivy seeds and new plants will appear. monitor and remove seedlings when small. get help with ivy removal if you have ivy on walls, fences or trees in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with photos and we will aim to respond what to do when hedges have been left too long what to do when hedges have been left too long. renovation pruning, height reduction and when to replace a neglected hedge. what to do when hedges have been left too long hedges, pruning & cutting times a hedge that has been left too long is one of the most common problems we deal with. whether it has grown too tall, too wide, or has gone bare at the base, there are usually options beyond removal. this guide explains what to do when a hedge has been left too long. quick answer if hedges have been left too long, you can renovate most species by cutting one side hard one year, the other side the next year, and the top the third year. yew, beech, hornbeam, hawthorn, privet and laurel all respond well to hard pruning. leylandii does not: it cannot regrow from old brown wood, so an overgrown leylandii hedge usually needs to be removed and replaced. why hedges get overgrown hedges get overgrown for predictable reasons. a year or two of missed cuts, an elderly owner who cannot manage the trimming, a tenant who did not maintain the garden, or simply a hedge that was planted in the wrong place and has outgrown its space. in the chilterns, where hedges grow vigorously on the fertile soils, an overgrown hedge can become a 4 metre tall, 2 metre wide monster within 5 years of neglect. the good news is that most overgrown hedges can be renovated rather than removed. yew, beech, hornbeam, hawthorn, privet and laurel all respond well to hard pruning. with a 3-year renovation plan, you can bring an overgrown hedge back to a manageable size without killing it. the main exception is leylandii, which cannot regrow from old brown wood. which hedges can be renovated? hedge type can be renovated? how hard can you cut? yew yes, very well cut to 30cm from ground beech yes, well cut to 60cm from ground hornbeam yes, very well cut to 60cm from ground hawthorn yes, very well cut to 60cm from ground privet yes, very well cut to 30cm from ground laurel yes, well cut to 1m from ground holly yes, slowly cut to 1m from ground leylandii no cannot regrow from old wood box yes, but watch for blight cut to 30cm from ground the 3-year renovation plan the trick with hedge renovation is to do it over 3 years, not all at once. cutting the whole hedge hard in one go shocks the plant and can kill it. cutting one side at a time leaves enough foliage for the plant to recover. year 1: cut one side hard in february or march (before nesting season), cut one side of the hedge back hard. for yew and privet, cut to 30cm from the main stems. for beech and hornbeam, cut to 60cm. for laurel, cut to 1 metre. leave the other side and the top untouched. year 2: cut the other side the following february or march, cut the other side of the hedge back hard. the side you cut in year 1 should be regrowing with fresh shoots. year 3: cut the top in the third february or march, cut the top of the hedge back to the desired height. by now, both sides should be regrowing strongly. year 4 onwards: regular maintenance from year 4, cut the hedge once a year to keep it in shape. the hedge will look thin and bare for the first 2 years, but by year 4 it should be a dense, healthy hedge at the size you want. when to renovate hedges the best time to renovate a hedge is in late winter (february to early march), before the bird nesting season starts and before the spring growth begins. this gives the hedge the whole growing season to recover. never renovate a hedge between march and august, when birds may be nesting. it is an offence under the wildlife and countryside act 1981 to damage or destroy an active nest. check the hedge carefully before starting work. what to do with leylandii leylandii cannot be renovated. it does not regrow from old brown wood, so if you cut it back beyond the green growth, you are left with brown stems that will never recover. the only options for an overgrown leylandii hedge are: reduce the height by 30 to 60cm, if there is enough green growth below. remove the hedge entirely and replace with a slower-growing species like yew or beech. live with it at its current size, but commit to cutting it twice a year to prevent further growth. removing a leylandii hedge costs 200 to 600 pounds for a 10 metre hedge, depending on height and access. stump grinding is extra. tools for hedge renovation petrol hedge trimmer: for cutting back the sides and top loppers: for cutting thick stems pruning saw: for cutting very thick stems chainsaw: for cutting the main trunks of large hedges (qualified users only) step ladder or platform: for reaching the top of tall hedges safety gear: gloves, goggles, ear defenders for tall hedges (over 3 metres), consider hiring a cherry picker or a professional hedge cutting service. see our hedge cutting service . the cost of hedge renovation job diy cost professional cost renovate 10m hedge (3 year plan) 0 (tools) 500-1,500 pounds remove 10m leylandii hedge 200 (skip) 400-800 pounds stump grinding (10m) 100 (hire) 200-400 pounds replant with yew (10m, 60-80cm) 400-800 (plants) 800-1,500 pounds common mistakes cutting the whole hedge at once. this can kill the hedge. do one side at a time over 3 years. cutting leylandii into old wood. leylandii does not regrow from brown wood. avoid cutting back beyond the green growth. renovating in nesting season. it is an offence to damage active nests. cut in february or september. not feeding after renovation. a renovated hedge needs a feed in spring to help it recover. not watering in dry spells. a newly renovated hedge needs water to recover. water weekly in dry weather. frequently asked questions can i cut my beech hedge back hard? yes. beech responds well to hard pruning. cut one side to 60cm in february, the other side the following february, and the top in the third february. will my yew hedge recover from being cut to 30cm? yes. yew is one of the few conifers that regrows from old wood. a yew hedge cut to 30cm will look thin for a year, then regrow thick and dense. how do i get rid of a leylandii hedge? cut it down to ground level (we can do this for you), then grind out the stumps. replace with a slower-growing hedge like yew or beech. get help with hedge renovation if you have an overgrown hedge in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with photos and we will aim to respond within one day. whatsapp get a quote further reading when should hedges be cut in the uk? best hedging plants for privacy our hedge cutting service how to deal with bindweed in uk gardens how to deal with bindweed in uk gardens. identify, dig out, smother and treat persistent bindweed with real advice from working gardeners. how to deal with bindweed in uk gardens garden problems & weed control bindweed is one of the most persistent weeds in uk gardens. its roots can go 3 metres deep, and any fragment left in the ground will regrow. this guide explains how to deal with bindweed using a combination of digging, smothering and targeted treatment. quick answer to deal with bindweed in uk gardens: dig out the roots repeatedly, smother with cardboard and mulch, or treat with glyphosate in summer when the plant is sending energy to the roots. bindweed is one of the most persistent weeds in chiltern gardens, particularly on the fertile soils around high wycombe, marlow and henley. total eradication takes 2 to 3 years of consistent effort. why bindweed is so persistent bindweed (calystegia sepium, hedge bindweed, and convolvulus arvensis, field bindweed) is one of the most persistent weeds in uk gardens. it twines through other plants, smothering them, and regrows from any fragment of root left in the soil. the roots can go 3 metres deep and 5 metres sideways, making total removal almost impossible in one go. in the chilterns, bindweed is particularly common in established gardens with hedges and shrubs. the fertile soils and the partial shade of hedges provide ideal conditions. a garden that has been neglected for a season can have bindweed threaded through every shrub and perennial. the good news is that bindweed can be controlled with persistence. it takes 2 to 3 years of consistent effort, but it is possible to get on top of it. this guide explains the methods we use in client gardens across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire. how to identify bindweed bindweed is easy to identify once you know what to look for: leaves: arrow-shaped, bright green, 5 to 10cm long stems: thin, twining, can grow 2 to 3 metres in a season flowers: white or pink trumpet flowers, june to september roots: white, fleshy, can go 3 metres deep bindweed is often confused with morning glory (ipomoea), but morning glory is an annual with larger flowers, while bindweed is a perennial with smaller flowers. methods for controlling bindweed method 1: dig out the roots the most effective organic method. dig out as much of the root system as you can, then dig out any regrowth as soon as it appears. dig carefully around the bindweed, following the white roots. remove every bit of root you can find. even a 2cm fragment will regrow. use a sieve to remove small root fragments from the soil. do not compost the roots. burn them or put them in the bin. check weekly for regrowth and dig out any new shoots. this method takes 2 to 3 years of consistent digging. each year, the regrowth is weaker as the root system is exhausted. method 2: smother with cardboard and mulch block out the light to weaken the plant. this works best for large areas. cut the bindweed to ground level. lay thick cardboard over the area, overlapping by 30cm. cover with a 10cm layer of bark or compost mulch. leave for 12 to 18 months. check the edges for regrowth escaping. this method takes 1 to 2 years, but it is less work than digging and improves the soil at the same time. method 3: glyphosate treatment the most effective chemical method. glyphosate is a systemic weedkiller that travels through the plant to the roots. wait until june or july, when the bindweed is in full leaf and sending energy to the roots. untangle the bindweed from desirable plants and lay it on bare soil. spray with glyphosate at the recommended rate. leave for 3 to 4 weeks for the glyphosate to work. dig out the dead roots. repeat in september if necessary. warning: glyphosate will kill any plant it touches. do not spray on windy days, and protect desirable plants with plastic sheeting. method 4: the "glove method" for bindweed growing through desirable plants, where spraying is not possible. put on a rubber glove, then a cotton glove over the top. dip the cotton glove in glyphosate solution. wipe the bindweed leaves with the glove, coating them with glyphosate. avoid touching desirable plants. leave for 3 to 4 weeks. this is slow but effective, and avoids spray drift. the 3-year bindweed control plan whatever method you choose, plan for 3 years of control. bindweed cannot be eradicated in one season. year what to do expected result year 1 dig out roots, or glyphosate in june and september significant reduction (60-80%) year 2 dig out regrowth, or glyphosate in june further reduction (80-95%) year 3 dig out any remaining shoots near eradication (95-99%) year 4 onwards monitor and dig out any regrowth maintenance how to prevent bindweed returning dig out roots completely when you find bindweed. do not let it establish. mulch thickly to suppress seed germination. avoid importing soil that may contain bindweed roots. check new plants for bindweed roots before planting. monitor edges of the garden where bindweed may spread from neighbouring land. common mistakes composting bindweed roots. compost heaps do not kill bindweed roots. burn them or put them in the bin. leaving root fragments in the soil. even a 2cm fragment will regrow. sieve the soil to remove fragments. expecting one treatment to work. bindweed takes 2 to 3 years to control. be persistent. spraying glyphosate in spring. glyphosate works best in summer when the plant is sending energy to the roots. pulling bindweed instead of digging it. pulling breaks the roots, leaving fragments that regrow. dig gently. frequently asked questions can i compost bindweed? no. compost heaps do not get hot enough to kill bindweed roots. burn them, put them in the bin, or drown them in water for 6 weeks first. will glyphosate kill my other plants? glyphosate will kill any plant it touches. use the "glove method" for bindweed growing through desirable plants, and protect nearby plants with plastic sheeting. how long does it take to get rid of bindweed? 2 to 3 years of consistent effort. you can reduce it by 80 percent in the first year, but total eradication takes longer because of the deep root system. get help with bindweed control if you have a bindweed problem in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, how to make a large garden look manageable to buyers how to make a large garden look manageable to house buyers. simplify, define areas, reduce maintenance and present the garden as an asset not a burden. how to make a large garden look manageable to buyers gardens for selling or renting a large garden can be a selling point or a deterrent. buyers who see an overgrown, complex garden see a burden. buyers who see a tidy, simple, well-defined garden see an asset. this guide explains how to make a large garden look manageable to buyers. quick answer to make a large garden look manageable to buyers: mow the lawn and edge it crisply, cut back all overgrowth, define the garden with mown paths through longer grass, create clear zones (lawn, patio, border), remove wild areas, and add a seating area. buyers want a garden they can enjoy, not one that will be a burden. a tidy, zoned garden looks more manageable than a wild, undefined one. why large gardens scare buyers large gardens can be a selling point, but they can also be a liability. many buyers, especially older downsizers and busy professionals, see a large garden as a maintenance burden. estate agents in marlow, henley, beaconsfield and amersham regularly tell us that buyers are put off by gardens that look unmanageable, even when they love the house. the trick is to make a large garden look manageable. a tidy, zoned garden with clear structure feels under control. a wild, undefined garden feels overwhelming. the same garden can look manageable or unmanageable depending on how it is presented. this guide explains how to make a large garden look manageable to buyers. what buyers want in a large garden clear structure: defined zones, clear boundaries, mown paths manageable maintenance: no bramble patches, no overgrown hedges, no neglected corners usable space: a patio or terrace for sitting, a lawn for children, clear paths for walking privacy: hedges and fences that provide screening without being too tall mature planting: established trees and shrubs that add value without adding maintenance a blank canvas: room for the buyer to make their own mark the large garden makeover before selling 1. mow the lawn and edge it crisply (free if diy, 50-150 pounds professional) a neatly mown lawn with crisp edges makes the whole garden look cared for. mow the day before the photos, and again before each viewing. edge along paths and borders with a half-moon edger. 2. define the garden with mown paths (free if diy) if you have long grass or a wild area, mow paths through it. this makes the garden look intentional and managed, rather than abandoned. a simple mown path through a meadow area says "this is deliberate, not neglected". 3. cut back all overgrowth (100-400 pounds professional) cut back anything overhanging paths, patios and the lawn. trim hedges to a neat shape. prune overgrown shrubs. remove self-seeded trees. the garden should look open and easy to navigate. 4. create clear zones (free if diy) make the zones of the garden obvious: patio or terrace: clean, with furniture set out lawn: mown, edged, clear of weeds borders: weeded, mulched, with defined edges vegetable garden: tidy, with raised beds if possible wildlife area: small, defined, with a sign or feature 5. remove wild areas (free if diy) if you have a wild area at the bottom of the garden, consider mowing it before the sale. a wild area looks like a maintenance burden to some buyers. if you want to keep it, define it with a mown path and a sign saying "wildflower meadow". 6. add a seating area (200-500 pounds) a seating area helps buyers imagine using the garden. a simple bench at the end of a path, or a table and chairs on the patio, makes the garden feel like a place to relax, not a burden. 7. repair boundaries (200-1,000 pounds) buyers notice boundaries. repair broken fences, replace missing panels, cut hedges to a neat shape. a tidy boundary makes the whole garden feel cared for. 8. tidy outbuildings (free if diy) clean and tidy sheds, greenhouses and garages. a tidy shed is a selling point; a rotting shed full of clutter is a negative. 9. remove clutter (free) remove children's toys, garden tools, empty pots, half-used bags of compost. a clutter-free garden looks bigger and more manageable. 10. add a few pots (50-150 pounds) a few well-placed pots of flowers by the patio or front door add colour and make the garden feel loved. do not overdo it, too many pots look cluttered. the psychology of garden presentation buyers form an opinion of a garden in the first 30 seconds. the key psychological triggers are: tidy: a tidy garden suggests the property is well cared for manageable: clear zones and mown paths suggest the garden is easy to maintain usable: a seating area helps buyers imagine using the garden private: screening from neighbours makes the garden feel like a retreat mature: established trees and shrubs add value without adding maintenance cost of making a large garden look manageable job diy cost professional cost mow and edge (1 acre) 0 (own mower) 100-200 pounds cut back overgrowth 0 (tools) 200-600 pounds hedge trimming (50m) 0 (tools) 200-500 pounds repair fences (20m) 200-400 (materials) 500-1,200 pounds pressure wash patio (50 sqm) 70 (hire) 250-450 pounds remove wild area 0 (strimmer) 100-300 pounds add seating area 200-500 (furniture) 500-1,200 pounds plants and pots 50-200 200-500 pounds total for a typical large garden makeover before selling: 1,500 to 4,000 pounds. expected return: 10,000 to 30,000 pounds on the sale price. common mistakes leaving wild areas. a wild area looks like a maintenance burden. mow it or define it with a sign. not defining zones. an undefined garden looks bigger but more overwhelming. define zones with mowing and edging. leaving obvious problems. broken fences, dead trees, bramble patches will put buyers off. not adding a seating area. buyers want to imagine using the garden. a seating area helps. doing too much landscaping. buyers want a blank canvas, not your personal taste. tidy, clean and neutral is enough. frequently asked questions will a large garden put buyers off? some buyers (older downsizers, busy professionals) may be put off. but others (families, keen gardeners) see a large garden as a major selling point. the key is to make it look manageable. should i reduce the size of the lawn? no. a large lawn is a selling point for families. mow it, edge it, and keep it tidy. should i remove my vegetable garden? probably. a vegetable garden only appeals to keen gardeners. convert it to lawn or a simple border for the sale. get help preparing your large garden for sale if you are selling a property with a large garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, garden design ideas for large gardens in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire garden design ideas for large gardens in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire. zoning, structure, planting schemes and hard landscaping for substantial plots. garden design ideas for large gardens in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire garden design & layout ideas large gardens in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire offer space and opportunity, but they also need careful design to avoid looking empty or unmanageable. this guide provides garden design ideas for large gardens, with specific reference to the soils and conditions of the chilterns and thames valley. quick answer garden design ideas for large gardens in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire: divide the garden into zones (formal, informal, wild), use structure (hedges, paths, trees) to define spaces, choose plants suited to the soil (chalk or clay), create focal points (statue, water, specimen tree), and plan for year-round interest. a well-designed large garden in the chilterns combines formal structure with naturalistic planting. why large gardens need design a large garden without a design is just a field with a fence around it. the same garden, with a clear design, becomes a series of distinct spaces each with its own character. design is what makes a large garden feel manageable, usable and beautiful. in the chilterns, large gardens range from 0.5 to 5 acres, often on sloping ground, with mixed soil (chalk on the hills, clay in the vale) and mature trees. the design challenge is to create a garden that feels cohesive despite its size, suits the local conditions, and works for the owner's lifestyle. this guide gives design ideas that work specifically for large gardens in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire. the zoned approach the best way to design a large garden is to divide it into zones. each zone has a different purpose and character, but they all connect through paths, views and structure. zone 1: formal garden near the house. patio, lawn, borders. the most maintained part of the garden. zone 2: family garden. lawn, vegetable garden, play area. functional and durable. zone 3: ornamental garden. mixed borders, specimen trees, garden room. the decorative heart of the garden. zone 4: wild garden. meadow, orchard, woodland. low maintenance, high wildlife value. structure: the bones of the garden structure is what holds a large garden together. without structure, a large garden looks like a random collection of plants. with structure, it looks designed. hedges hedges define spaces in a large garden. use them to: separate zones screen ugly views provide windbreaks create garden rooms choose beech or yew for chalk soils, hornbeam for clay. see our hedging guide . paths paths connect the zones and lead the eye. use different materials for different zones: stone paving near the house gravel for informal paths grass for wild areas bark for woodland paths trees trees provide structure and scale in a large garden. plant: specimen trees at focal points (oak, beech, rowan, ornamental cherry) avenue trees along drives and main paths (lime, beech, hornbeam) orchard trees in the wild garden (apple, pear, plum) woodland edge at the boundary (native mix) focal points every zone needs a focal point, something that draws the eye and gives the space a purpose. focal points can be: a specimen tree (e.g. a mature oak in the lawn) a statue or sculpture a water feature (pond, fountain, rill) a garden building (summerhouse, gazebo, pavilion) a view (a gap in the hedge framing a view of the countryside) a bench at the end of a path planting schemes for large gardens formal borders near the house, plant formal borders with a mix of shrubs and perennials. choose reliable plants that look good for months: roses - flowering shrubs for months lavender - evergreen, scented, drought-tolerant hardy geraniums - long-flowering ground cover alliums - dramatic flowers in late spring naturalistic planting in the middle zones, use naturalistic planting with grasses and perennials. this style looks good year after year with minimal maintenance: stipa tenuissima - soft, flowing grass verbena bonariensis - tall, airy echinacea - pink daisy flowers sedum - late flowers for bees wildflower meadow in the wild garden, plant a wildflower meadow. this is the lowest maintenance option and supports the most wildlife. see our wildflower guide . orchard a small orchard (5 to 10 trees) is a beautiful feature in a large garden. choose heritage varieties on semi-dwarfing rootstocks (m26 for apples). plant in winter, 5 metres apart. designing for the chilterns chiltern gardens have specific conditions that influence design: chalk soil: choose mediterranean plants, herbs, drought-tolerant perennials. see our chalk soil guide . clay soil: choose shrubs, roses, perennials that tolerate wet winters and dry summers. sloping ground: use terraces, retaining walls, and steps to create usable spaces. mature trees: design around them, do not remove them. they add value. views: frame the best views, screen the worst. cost of designing a large garden item cost garden design plan (1 acre) 1,500-4,000 pounds hedges (50m, planted) 750-2,500 pounds paths (50m, gravel) 1,000-2,500 pounds patios (50 sqm, sandstone) 3,000-6,000 pounds trees (5 specimen trees) 500-2,500 pounds borders (200 sqm, planted) 2,000-6,000 pounds lawn (500 sqm, seeded) 500-1,500 pounds garden building (summerhouse) 2,000-8,000 pounds total for a large garden design and implementation: 15,000 to 50,000 pounds. see our garden design service and chiltern garden design plan pdf guides . common mistakes not having a design. a large garden without a design is just a field. get a design before you start. fighting the soil. acid-lovers die on chalk. match plants to your soil. not using structure. without hedges, paths and trees, a large garden looks random. over-planting. plants need space to grow. a garden that looks full in year 1 will be a jungle in year 3. not planning for maintenance. a high-maintenance design is a burden. plan for the time you actually have. frequently asked questions do i need a garden designer for a large garden? yes, almost certainly. a designer saves money by avoiding mistakes and creates a cohesive plan. see our garden design service . how long does it take to design and build a large garden? 3 to 12 months for the design and construction, plus 2 to 5 years for the plants to establish. should i phase the work? yes. phasing spreads the cost and lets you see how each phase looks before starting the next. get help designing your large garden if you have a large garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire and want help with design, send us a
