should you leave part of your garden wild? should you leave part of your garden wild? pros, cons and how to do it well. managed wild areas, wildlife value and maintenance. should you leave part of your garden wild? wildlife & wildflower gardens leaving part of your garden wild is one of the best things you can do for wildlife. but it needs to be done deliberately, not just by neglect. this guide explains the pros and cons of leaving part of your garden wild, and how to do it well. quick answer yes, you should leave part of your garden wild. a wild area supports 10 times more wildlife than a manicured lawn, requires minimal maintenance, and adds visual interest. aim for 10 to 20 percent of the garden as a wild area, in the least visible corner. in the chilterns, where gardens often back onto countryside, a wild area can be a vital wildlife corridor. why leave part of the garden wild the uk has lost 97 percent of its wildflower meadows since 1930. half of our native pollinators are in decline. gardens cover 1 million hectares of the uk, more than all the nature reserves combined. what gardeners do matters. leaving part of the garden wild is one of the most effective things you can do for wildlife. the good news is that a wild area is not just good for wildlife. it is also low-maintenance, looks beautiful, and adds interest to the garden. a wild area at the bottom of the garden, with long grass and wildflowers, can be the most beautiful part of the garden in june and july. in the chilterns, where gardens often back onto countryside, a wild area can act as a wildlife corridor, connecting isolated patches of habitat. the more gardeners who leave wild areas, the more connected the landscape becomes. the benefits of a wild area supports 10 times more wildlife than a manicured lawn low maintenance: cut once a year, that is it beautiful: wildflowers in summer are stunning good for soil: long grass builds organic matter carbon storage: long grass stores more carbon than short grass pest control: a wild area supports predators that eat garden pests education: children love exploring wild areas privacy: a wild area screens the bottom of the garden how much to leave wild aim for 10 to 20 percent of the garden as a wild area. in a typical 300 sqm family garden, that is 30 to 60 sqm, or a 5 by 6 to 8 metre patch at the bottom of the garden. you can leave more if you have a large garden. estates of an acre or more can have 30 to 50 percent wild, often as a meadow or woodland edge. where to put the wild area put the wild area in the least visible part of the garden, usually the bottom corner. this keeps the visible parts of the garden tidy while still providing wildlife habitat. avoid putting the wild area in the front garden, where it may look untidy to passers-by and cause neighbour disputes. how to create a wild area option 1: just stop mowing the simplest option. stop mowing a section of lawn and let the grass grow. within a year, you will have a patch of long grass. within 2 to 3 years, wildflowers will appear (from seeds in the soil). within 5 years, you will have a mini meadow. this is the lowest-effort option, but the results are unpredictable. you may get a beautiful wildflower meadow, or you may get a patch of coarse grass and nettles. option 2: plant a wildflower meadow for more reliable results, plant a wildflower meadow. see our wildflower meadow guide for detail. the basic process: reduce soil fertility (wildflowers prefer poor soil). stop fertilising for a year before planting. sow wildflower seed in autumn or spring. cut once a year in late summer (august to september). remove the cuttings to keep the soil poor. cost: 50 to 150 pounds for seed for a 50 sqm meadow. option 3: plant a wildlife corner if you do not want long grass, plant a wildlife corner with native shrubs and perennials: hawthorn - flowers, berries, nesting sites blackthorn - sloes, nesting sites hazel - catkins, nuts foxglove - nectar for bees nettles - food for butterfly caterpillars add a log pile and a leaf pile for habitat. see our balance guide . option 4: let nature take over if you have a large garden, you can simply fence off a section and let nature take over. within 5 years, it will be a thicket of brambles, self-seeded trees, and nettles. within 10 years, it will be a young woodland. this is the lowest-effort option but the most visually wild. how to manage a wild area a wild area is not a no-maintenance area. it needs annual management to prevent it becoming a bramble thicket: meadow: cut once a year in late summer (august to september). remove the cuttings. long grass: cut once a year in late summer. remove the cuttings. wildlife corner: prune shrubs every 2 to 3 years. remove invasive species. nature area: cut paths through it annually to allow access. how to make a wild area look intentional a common complaint about wild areas is that they look messy. the trick is to make them look intentional: mow paths through the wild area. this shows it is deliberate. define the edges with a mown strip or edging. add a feature like a bench, sculpture, or bird bath. frame it with a hedge or fence. add a sign like "wildflower meadow" or "wildlife area". the cost of a wild area option cost maintenance per year just stop mowing 0 1 hour (annual cut) wildflower meadow (50 sqm) 50-150 pounds (seed) 2 hours (cut and remove) wildlife corner 200-500 pounds (plants) 4 hours (prune and weed) nature area (large) 0 4 hours (cut paths) common mistakes letting the whole garden go wild. this looks abandoned, not wildlife-friendly. keep 10 to 20 percent wild, the rest maintained. not cutting the wild area annually. without an annual cut, the wild area becomes a bramble thicket. not removing cuttings. cuttings rot down and fertilise the soil, which favours grass over wildflowers. putting the wild area in the front garden. this can cause neighbour disputes. keep wild areas in the back garden. not making it look intentional. a wild area without mown paths looks abandoned. mow paths to show it is deliberate. frequently asked questions will a wild area annoy my neighbours? not if it is in the back garden and looks intentional. mow paths through it and frame it with a hedge. the three-zone approach (formal near the house, wildlife-friendly in the middle, wild at the bottom) prevents neighbour disputes. will a wild area attract rats? not usually. rats are attracted by food waste, not long grass. a wild area without food waste will not attract rats. how long does it take for a wild area to establish? 1 to 3 years. the first year looks untidy, the second year looks promising, and by the third year it should look intentional and support a wide range of wildlife. get help creating a wild area if you want to create a wild area in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with photos and we will aim to respond within one day. how to create a wildflower area in a large lawn how to create a wildflower area in a large lawn. strip the turf, sow native seed, mow once a year. real uk advice for wildflower meadows. how to create a wildflower area in a large lawn wildlife & wildflower gardens converting part of a large lawn to a wildflower area is one of the best things you can do for wildlife and one of the most rewarding garden projects. but it needs to be done properly. this guide explains how to create a wildflower area in a large lawn. quick answer to create a wildflower area in a large lawn: reduce soil fertility, sow wildflower seed in autumn or spring, cut annually in late summer, and remove the cuttings. a wildflower meadow supports 50 times more wildlife than a manicured lawn and needs just one cut per year. in the chilterns, the chalky soils are perfect for native wildflowers. why create a wildflower area a wildflower meadow is one of the most beautiful and wildlife-friendly features you can create in a garden. it supports 50 times more pollinators than a manicured lawn, looks stunning in summer, and needs just one cut per year. for a large lawn, converting part of it to a wildflower meadow saves mowing time and adds huge wildlife value. in the chilterns, the chalky soils are perfect for native wildflowers. cowslips, orchids, scabious, knapweed and bird's-foot trefoil all thrive on chalk. a wildflower meadow on chalk can be as beautiful as any traditional garden border, and far more valuable for wildlife. the benefits of a wildflower meadow supports 50 times more pollinators than a manicured lawn low maintenance: cut once a year, that is it beautiful: wildflowers in summer are stunning climate resilient: drought-tolerant once established good for soil: deep roots build soil structure carbon storage: meadows store more carbon than lawns saves mowing time: cut once a year vs weekly for a lawn how to create a wildflower meadow step 1: choose the right spot wildflowers need sun. choose a sunny spot, at least 6 hours of sun a day. avoid shaded areas under trees. the spot should be in the least visible part of the garden, as the meadow will look untidy in winter and early spring. step 2: reduce soil fertility wildflowers prefer poor soil. on rich soil, grass outcompetes the wildflowers. to reduce fertility: stop fertilising for 1 to 2 years before sowing remove the top 5 to 10cm of topsoil (the most fertile layer) or sow a crop of mustard (which takes nutrients from the soil) and dig it in on chalky chiltern soils, fertility is usually low enough already. on clay or rich garden soil, you will need to reduce fertility. step 3: prepare the seed bed prepare a fine, weed-free seed bed: kill existing vegetation (with glyphosate or by covering with black plastic for 3 months) cultivate the soil to a fine tilth remove weed roots and stones firm the soil gently rake to a fine surface step 4: sow the seed sow in autumn (september to november) or spring (march to april). autumn sowing is best, as some wildflower seeds need cold to germinate. mix seed with sand (1 part seed to 4 parts sand) for even sowing sow at 4 to 5 grams per square metre rake lightly to cover the seed firm the soil with a roller or by walking on it water if no rain within a week choose a native wildflower mix suited to your soil. for chalk, choose a chalk grassland mix. for clay, choose a clay meadow mix. see our chiltern plant library for suggestions. step 5: manage the meadow annual management is essential: first year: cut the meadow every 6 to 8 weeks to control annual weeds. remove the cuttings. subsequent years: cut once a year in late summer (august to september). remove the cuttings. spot-treat weeds: dig out any invasive weeds (thistle, dock, nettle) as they appear. monitor for success: count the wildflower species each year. a good meadow has 20 to 30 species. the wildflower meadow calendar month what to do september cut the meadow. remove all cuttings. october sow new meadows. or leave the cut meadow alone. november to february leave alone. wildlife needs winter shelter. march sow new meadows (spring sowing). april to july leave alone. wildflowers growing and flowering. august plan the annual cut for late august or september. what wildflowers to sow for chalky chiltern soils: cowslip wild orchid (bee orchid, pyramidal orchid) scabious knapweed bird's-foot trefoil selfheal ox-eye daisy yarrow salad burnet wild thyme for clay soils: meadow buttercup ragged robin devil's-bit scabious meadowsweet purple loosestrife ox-eye daisy knapweed how to make the meadow look intentional a common complaint about wildflower meadows is that they look untidy. to make the meadow look intentional: mow paths through the meadow. this shows it is deliberate. mow a strip around the edge of the meadow, for a defined border. add a feature like a bench or sculpture. add a sign like "wildflower meadow" or "wildlife area". frame it with a hedge or mown strip. the cost of creating a wildflower meadow item cost wildflower seed (50 sqm) 50-150 pounds soil preparation (50 sqm) 0 (diy) to 200-400 pounds (professional) annual cut and remove (50 sqm) 0 (diy) to 100-200 pounds (professional) total to create 50-550 pounds common mistakes not reducing soil fertility. on rich soil, grass outcompetes the wildflowers. always reduce fertility first. not removing cuttings. cuttings rot down and fertilise the soil, which favours grass over wildflowers. sowing too much seed. sow at 4 to 5 grams per square metre. more seed means more competition and fewer flowers. cutting at the wrong time. cut in late summer (august to september), after the wildflowers have set seed. not mowing paths. without mown paths, the meadow looks abandoned. mow paths to show it is deliberate. frequently asked questions how long does it take for a wildflower meadow to establish? 2 to 3 years. the first year looks sparse, the second year looks promising, and by the third year it should be a dense, flowery meadow. will a wildflower meadow attract ticks? possibly, but the risk is low in a garden meadow. check for ticks after walking in long grass, especially in summer. can i have a wildflower meadow in shade? yes, but the choice of wildflowers is smaller. choose a woodland mix with wood anemone, primrose, bluebell, and red campion. get how to create a luxury-looking garden without a full redesign create a luxury-looking garden without a full redesign. strategic planting, lighting, hard surface cleaning and structural pruning for a premium feel. how to create a luxury-looking garden without a full redesign garden design & layout ideas a luxury garden is not about how much money you spend. it is about structure, simplicity and attention to detail. you can transform the feel of your garden without a full redesign by focusing on a few key areas. this guide explains how to create a luxury-looking garden on a realistic budget. quick answer to create a luxury-looking garden without a full redesign: invest in structure (hedges, paths, containers), choose a limited plant palette, add lighting, use premium materials (stone, hardwood), keep the lawn immaculate, and add statement plants. cost: 1,000 to 5,000 pounds for a typical garden. in the chilterns, where properties are expensive and gardens are visible, a luxury look adds significant value. what makes a garden look luxurious luxury in a garden is not about money. it is about restraint, structure, and quality. a garden with a few well-chosen plants, immaculate lawn, clean paths, and subtle lighting looks more luxurious than a garden crammed with expensive plants but no structure. the trick is to invest in the elements that signal quality and to remove the elements that signal neglect. in the chilterns, where property values are high and gardens are visible from the road, a luxury-looking garden adds significant value. estate agents in marlow, henley, beaconsfield and amersham tell us that a well-presented garden can add 5 to 10 percent to the sale price. this guide explains how to create a luxury-looking garden without a full redesign. the 7 elements of a luxury garden 1. structure structure is the bones of the garden. without it, no amount of planting looks luxurious. invest in: clipped hedges: yew, box, or beech. define the spaces. clean paths: stone, brick, or gravel. lead the eye. defined edges: between lawn, borders, and paths. focal points: a specimen tree, a statue, a bench. 2. a limited plant palette luxury gardens use a limited number of plant species, repeated throughout. this creates cohesion and looks intentional. choose 5 to 7 plants and repeat them in drifts. avoid the "one of everything" look, which appears chaotic. luxury plants for chiltern gardens: box - clipped into balls or cones yew - clipped hedges and topiary lavender - drifts along paths roses - in formal beds alliums - dramatic flowers in late spring verbena bonariensis - tall, airy stipa tenuissima - soft, flowing grass 3. premium materials use premium materials for paths, patios, and edging: natural stone: indian sandstone, york stone, slate hardwood: oak, iroko for decking and furniture metal edging: for crisp, clean lawn edges granite setts: for paths and edging cast iron: for benches, urns, and features avoid: concrete slabs, plastic edging, softwood decking, plastic furniture. 4. immaculate lawn the lawn is the most visible element of the garden. an immaculate lawn signals care and attention. to achieve it: mow weekly at 25 to 40mm edge crisply with a half-moon edger feed in spring and autumn treat moss annually aerate annually overseed bare patches see our lawn recovery service and lawn care plan guide . 5. lighting lighting transforms a garden at night. use: uplighting: on specimen trees and statues path lights: for safe navigation wall lights: on the house and outbuildings string lights: across patios and pergolas water feature lighting: if you have a pond use warm white (2700k) for a luxurious feel. avoid cold white, which looks harsh. 6. statement plants a few statement plants add drama and luxury: specimen trees: a mature oak, a multi-stem birch, a clipped bay topiary: clipped box or yew shapes large containers: with bay standards, olive trees, or agapanthus climbers: wisteria on the house, climbing roses on arches 7. quality containers replace plastic pots with quality containers: terracotta: frost-proof, ages beautifully stone: cast stone urns and troughs lead: traditional, weathered look wooden: oak or iroko, with metal bands zinc: modern, contemporary look the quick luxury upgrade if you have 1,000 to 2,000 pounds and a weekend, here is how to add instant luxury: cut the lawn and edge crisply (free if diy) clean paths and patio (100 to 250 pounds for pressure washing) plant 20 lavender plants along the path (100 to 200 pounds) buy 2 large terracotta pots with bay standards for the front door (300 to 600 pounds) install low-voltage path lights (200 to 400 pounds) paint fences and gates dark green or black (50 to 150 pounds) add a specimen tree if budget allows (200 to 500 pounds) the cost of a luxury garden upgrade item cost quick upgrade (lawn, clean, lavender, pots, lights) 1,000-2,000 pounds standard upgrade (above + new paths, hedges, more plants) 3,000-8,000 pounds premium upgrade (above + new patio, lighting, specimen trees) 8,000-25,000 pounds full luxury redesign 25,000-100,000+ pounds common mistakes too many plant species. a limited palette looks more luxurious than a chaotic mix. plastic pots and furniture. replace with quality materials. neglected lawn. an immaculate lawn is the foundation of a luxury garden. no lighting. lighting transforms the garden at night. cheap paths and edging. invest in natural stone and metal edging. frequently asked questions how much does a luxury garden cost? a quick luxury upgrade costs 1,000 to 2,000 pounds. a full luxury redesign costs 25,000 to 100,000 pounds or more. most clients spend 5,000 to 15,000 pounds on a significant upgrade. what is the most luxurious plant? a clipped yew or box topiary. they take years to grow and clip, and they signal care and attention. should i hire a garden designer for a luxury garden? yes, almost certainly. a designer saves money by avoiding mistakes how to design a garden for privacy without making it feel closed in how to design a garden for privacy without making it feel closed in. strategic screening, layered planting and partial screens for privacy with openness. how to design a garden for privacy without making it feel closed in garden design & layout ideas privacy is important in a garden, but a 2 metre wall of leylandii around the whole boundary makes the garden feel like a box. the trick is to screen specific sightlines while maintaining a sense of openness. this guide explains how to design a garden for privacy without making it feel closed in. quick answer to design a garden for privacy without making it feel closed in: use a mix of heights (low hedges, medium shrubs, tall trees), avoid solid walls over 1.8m, use pleached trees for above-fence screening, create gaps for views, and use climbers on pergolas and arches. in the chilterns, where gardens are often overlooked from properties on slopes, this approach is essential. the privacy dilemma privacy is one of the most common requests we get from clients in the chilterns. but there is a dilemma: tall boundaries block views but also block light and make the garden feel claustrophobic. the trick is to provide privacy without making the garden feel closed in. in hillside villages like princes risborough, wendover and great missenden, gardens are often overlooked from properties above. a 2 metre fence will not solve this. a 4 metre wall will solve it but will also block all light and make the garden feel like a prison. the solution is a mix of heights, with screening only where it is needed. the 5-principle approach 1. use a mix of heights do not use the same height all around the garden. use: low hedges (60cm to 1m): for boundary definition without blocking views medium shrubs (1.5 to 2m): for screening seating areas tall trees (3 to 5m): for screening specific overlooking windows pleached trees (2 to 3m clear stem, 1 to 1.5m panel): for above-fence screening 2. avoid solid walls over 1.8m solid walls over 1.8m block light and make the garden feel closed in. they also require planning permission in most areas. use hedges, trees, or pergolas instead. 3. use pleached trees for above-fence screening pleached trees are trees trained on a clear stem (1.5 to 2m) with a flat panel of branches on top. they screen above the fence line without blocking light lower down. common choices: hornbeam, beech, lime, photinia. cost: 200 to 500 pounds each. 4. create gaps for views a garden with no views feels claustrophobic. create gaps in the boundary planting to frame views of the countryside, a church spire, or a distant tree. this makes the garden feel connected to the wider landscape. 5. use climbers on pergolas and arches pergolas and arches with climbers provide privacy at eye level without blocking light. use: star jasmine clematis honeysuckle climbing roses wisteria privacy solutions for different overlooking problems overlooking from ground-floor windows if neighbours can see into your garden from ground-floor windows: plant a 1.8 to 2m hedge along the boundary use beech on chalk, hornbeam on clay plant 60cm from the boundary to allow for growth overlooking from first-floor windows if neighbours can see into your garden from first-floor windows: plant pleached trees along the boundary (3 to 4m tall) or plant a row of fastigiate trees (yew, hornbeam, beech) use a pergola with climbers to screen seating areas overlooking from properties on a slope if your garden is overlooked from properties on a slope above: plant tall trees (4 to 6m) along the upper boundary use a mix of evergreen and deciduous for year-round screening consider a garden room or pavilion for private seating overlooking from a path or road if your garden is overlooked from a path or road: plant a 1.8m hedge along the front boundary use a gate and arch with climbers avoid low fences, which invite looking over privacy planting for chiltern gardens for chalky soils (high wycombe, amersham, wendover) beech - holds brown leaves in winter, loves chalk yew - dense, formal, slow-growing photinia - evergreen, red new growth privet - fast-growing, semi-evergreen for clay soils (aylesbury, bicester, didcot) hornbeam - similar to beech, tolerates clay laurel - large leaves, fast-growing hawthorn - native, wildlife-friendly the cost of privacy planting solution cost (10m boundary) beech hedge (bare root, 60-80cm) 200-400 pounds yew hedge (pot grown, 60-80cm) 500-1,200 pounds pleached trees (5 trees, 3m tall) 1,500-4,000 pounds pergola with climbers 800-2,500 pounds fastigiate trees (5 trees, 3-4m) 1,000-3,000 pounds common mistakes planting leylandii. it grows too fast, causes neighbour disputes, and kills anything underneath. building a wall over 2m. needs planning permission and blocks light. using the same height all around. makes the garden feel closed in. use a mix of heights. not planting climbers. pergolas and arches with climbers provide privacy at eye level without blocking light. not considering neighbours. tall hedges can cause disputes. discuss before planting. frequently asked questions how high can a hedge be? there is no legal height limit for hedges. however, under the high hedges act 2003, a neighbour can complain about a hedge over 2m that blocks light. the council can order you to reduce the height. what is the best plant for above-fence privacy? pleached trees. they have a clear stem of 1.5 to 2m and a flat panel of branches on top, screening above the fence without blocking light lower down. should i use a wall or a hedge for privacy? a hedge is usually better. it filters wind rather than blocking it, supports wildlife, and does not require planning permission. a wall is needed only where space is very tight. get help designing for privacy if you want to design a garden for privacy in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire estate garden maintenance: what makes it different from normal gardening? what makes estate garden maintenance different from normal gardening? scale, complexity, specialist skills, equipment and year-round planning. estate garden maintenance: what makes it different from normal gardening? regular garden maintenance estate garden maintenance is a different discipline from normal garden maintenance. the scale, complexity and equipment needed are on a different level. this guide explains what makes estate garden maintenance different and what to expect if you own or manage a large estate garden. quick answer estate garden maintenance is different from normal gardening because of scale, complexity, and the need for a team rather than an individual. estate gardens (1 acre plus) need weekly visits of 6 to 8 hours, a team of 2 to 3 gardeners, specialist equipment (ride-on mowers, wood chippers), and a formal maintenance plan. cost: 6,000 to 20,000 pounds per year for a typical 2 to 5 acre estate in the chilterns. what makes estate gardens different estate gardens are different from normal gardens in several ways. the scale is larger, the complexity is higher, the equipment is specialist, and the team is bigger. a normal gardener cannot maintain an estate garden. it needs a team with the right equipment and a formal maintenance plan. in the chilterns, estates range from 1 to 20 acres, often with mature trees, formal gardens, woodland, paddocks, and driveways. maintaining these estates is a year-round job that needs careful planning and a dedicated team. this guide explains what makes estate garden maintenance different and what to expect. the 5 key differences 1. scale estate gardens are 1 acre or more. a single gardener cannot maintain this in a day. a 2 acre estate needs 6 to 8 hours of work per week in the growing season, which means a team of 2 to 3 gardeners. a 5 acre estate needs 10 to 15 hours per week, with a team of 3 to 4. 2. complexity estate gardens have multiple zones, each with different needs: formal garden (lawn, borders, hedges) vegetable garden (intensive cultivation) orchard (pruning, mowing, harvesting) woodland (coppicing, path maintenance, invasive species control) paddocks (mowing, fencing, hedging) driveways and parking (sweeping, snow clearance) ponds and water features (cleaning, plant management) each zone needs different skills and equipment. 3. specialist equipment estate gardens need equipment that normal gardens do not: ride-on mowers: for large lawns (5,000 to 15,000 pounds) tractors: for paddocks and meadows (10,000 to 30,000 pounds) wood chippers: for tree and hedge work (2,000 to 5,000 pounds) cherry pickers: for tall hedges and trees (hire 300 to 600 pounds per day) trailers: for moving plants and materials (1,000 to 3,000 pounds) chain saws: for tree work (qualified users only) 4. a team, not an individual estate gardens need a team of 2 to 4 gardeners, plus occasional specialists: head gardener: oversees the team, plans the work gardeners: do the maintenance work tree surgeon: for major tree work lawn specialist: for lawn renovation handyman: for fences, gates, paths 5. a formal maintenance plan an estate garden needs a formal maintenance plan that covers: weekly jobs (mowing, weeding, deadheading) monthly jobs (hedge trimming, pruning, planting) seasonal jobs (lawn renovation, leaf clearance, winter pruning) annual jobs (soil improvement, mulching, fence repairs) major projects (new borders, tree work, hard landscaping) the estate maintenance year season key jobs spring first cut, mulch, feed, prune late-summer shrubs, plant summer mow weekly, deadhead, water, weed, hedge trimming autumn lawn renovation, leaf clearance, plant bulbs, divide perennials winter prune deciduous trees, plant bare-root, woodland work, planning the cost of estate maintenance estate size weekly hours team size annual cost 1 to 2 acres 6-8 hours 2 gardeners 6,000-12,000 pounds 2 to 5 acres 8-12 hours 2-3 gardeners 12,000-25,000 pounds 5 to 10 acres 12-20 hours 3-4 gardeners 25,000-50,000 pounds 10+ acres 20+ hours 4+ gardeners 50,000+ pounds these costs include labour, equipment, and waste disposal. specialist work (tree surgery, lawn renovation) is extra. what is included in estate maintenance lawn mowing: weekly in the growing season with ride-on mowers border maintenance: weeding, deadheading, light pruning hedge trimming: in season (august to september, february) leaf clearance: weekly in autumn path and patio maintenance: sweeping, pressure washing tree work: minor pruning (major work by specialist tree surgeon) woodland management: coppicing, invasive species control equipment maintenance: servicing mowers, sharpening tools reporting: whatsapp updates and monthly reports what is not included major tree surgery (quoted separately) lawn renovation (quoted separately) hard landscaping (quoted separately) planting schemes (quoted as soft landscaping) building repairs (quoted separately) common mistakes hiring a single gardener for an estate. one person cannot maintain an estate garden. hire a team. not having a maintenance plan. without a plan, jobs are missed and the garden deteriorates. not investing in equipment. ride-on mowers and wood chippers are essential for efficiency. not budgeting for specialists. tree surgery and lawn renovation are extra costs. not communicating. the team needs a clear brief and regular communication with the owner. frequently asked questions how much does estate maintenance cost? 6,000 to 50,000 pounds per year depending on size. a 2 acre estate costs 12,000 to 25,000 pounds per year. do i need a full-time gardener? for estates over 5 acres, yes. for smaller estates, a weekly or fortnightly visit from a team is enough. should i employ a gardener or use a contractor? for most estates, a contractor is better. you get a team, specialist equipment, and cover for holidays and illness. get help with estate maintenance if you have an estate garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire and want help with maintenance, send us a whatsapp with photos and we front garden tidy-up checklist before house viewings front garden tidy-up checklist before house viewings. mow, edge, weed, sweep, trim hedges, add colour. real uk advice for sellers. front garden tidy-up checklist before house viewings gardens for selling or renting the front garden is the first thing a buyer sees. a tidy front garden suggests the property has been well maintained. an overgrown one suggests neglect. this checklist walks through everything you need to do to get your front garden ready for viewings. quick answer the front garden tidy-up checklist before house viewings: clear rubbish, mow and edge the lawn, weed paths and borders, trim hedges, clean the driveway and path, paint fences and gates, plant pots by the door, clean the front door and brassware, hide wheelie bins, and remove all clutter. cost: 200 to 500 pounds for a typical semi. returns: 2,000 to 5,000 pounds on the valuation. why the front garden matters the front garden is the first thing buyers see. a survey by rightmove found that 70 percent of buyers form an opinion of a property within 10 seconds of arriving. the front garden is a major part of that first impression. a tidy, welcoming front garden says "this property is cared for". a neglected front garden says "there are problems here". in the chilterns, where property values are high and buyers are discerning, the front garden is particularly important. estate agents in marlow, henley, beaconsfield and amersham tell us that a tidy front garden can add 1 to 3 percent to the valuation. on a 600,000 pound property, that is 6,000 to 18,000 pounds for a few hundred pounds of work. the front garden tidy-up checklist 1. clear all rubbish and debris (free) remove all rubbish, debris, and clutter from the front garden: children's toys and bikes wheelie bins (move to side or back if possible) dead plants and old compost bags building materials and tools old newspapers and post broken pots and planters 2. cut back overgrowth (50-150 pounds) overgrown plants make the property look neglected and hide the house. cut back: anything overhanging the path or driveway shrubs covering windows hedges that are too tall or wide climbers on the front of the house (especially around windows and doors) tree branches blocking the view of the house 3. mow and edge the lawn (free if diy, 30-60 pounds professional) a neatly mown lawn with crisp edges makes the whole property look cared for. mow the day before the viewing, not the morning of, so the grass settles. edge along paths and borders with a half-moon edger. 4. weed paths, borders and driveway (free if diy) weeds in paths and driveways are a major kerb appeal killer. hoe them off, or treat with a path weedkiller. weed the borders thoroughly. 5. clean the driveway and path (100-250 pounds for pressure washing) a clean driveway and path look much smarter. sweep first, then pressure wash if needed. see our pressure washing service . 6. paint fences, gates and railings (50-150 pounds) fresh paint on fences and gates makes a big difference. use a dark stain or black paint for a smart look. replace broken fence panels and gate latches. 7. plant pots by the front door (30-80 pounds) a pair of matching pots by the front door adds instant kerb appeal. use evergreen plants like box, bay or skimmia for year-round impact. add seasonal bedding for colour. 8. clean the front door and brassware (free) clean the front door, polish the door furniture, and replace the door mat. a clean, smart front door makes a strong first impression. 9. clean windows and outside lights (free) clean windows let in more light and make the property look cared for. clean outside light fittings and replace bulbs. 10. hide the wheelie bins (free) wheelie bins are a kerb appeal killer. move them to the side or back of the house. if they must stay in front, build a simple screen or buy a bin store. the 7-day front garden tidy-up plan if you have a week before the first viewing, here is a plan: day job time day 1 clear rubbish and clutter 1-2 hours day 2 cut back overgrowth 2-4 hours day 3 mow, edge, weed 2-3 hours day 4 clean driveway and path (pressure wash) 2-4 hours day 5 paint fences and gates 2-4 hours day 6 plant pots, clean door and windows 1-2 hours day 7 final tidy, hide bins, mow again 1 hour the 24-hour front garden tidy-up if you have just 24 hours before a viewing, focus on the highest-impact jobs: clear rubbish and clutter (1 hour) mow and edge the lawn (30 minutes) weed paths and driveway (1 hour) sweep paths and patio (30 minutes) hide wheelie bins (10 minutes) clean the front door and brassware (30 minutes) plant a pot by the front door (30 minutes) total: 4 to 5 hours, cost 50 to 100 pounds. the cost of a front garden tidy-up job diy cost professional cost clear rubbish free 50-100 pounds cut back overgrowth 0 (tools) 100-300 pounds mow and edge 0 (own mower) 30-60 pounds weed 0 (tools) 50-100 pounds clean driveway and path 50 (hire) 150-300 pounds paint fences and gates 30-80 (paint) 100-250 pounds plant pots 30-80 (plants) 50-150 pounds total 110-260 560-1,260 pounds common mistakes spending too much on major work. buyers do not pay extra for expensive landscaping. tidy, clean and neutral is enough. leaving obvious problems. a visible bramble patch or broken fence will put buyers off more than the cost of fixing it. planting new trees before selling. small new trees look like nothing. better to leave the space as mown lawn. not clearing the front garden. first impressions count. the front garden is what buyers see first. doing the work too late. allow at least a week before the first viewing. mown grass and pruned shrubs need time to look their best. frequently asked questions will a new front fence add value? a new fence may not add value directly, but a broken fence will reduce it. repair or replace broken fences, but do not spend a fortune on premium fencing. should i landscape the front garden before selling? no. a tidy, clean, neutral front garden sells better than a newly landscaped one. buyers want a blank canvas. how much should i spend on the front garden? 200 to 500 pounds for a typical property. spend on clearance, mowing, cleaning and minor repairs. do not spend on major landscaping. get help with your front garden tidy-up if you are selling a property in garden design mistakes that make maintenance more expensive garden design mistakes that make maintenance more expensive. wrong plants, too much lawn, poor access and bad layout. avoid these to save money long term. garden design mistakes that make maintenance more expensive garden design & layout ideas some gardens are harder to maintain than they need to be. the cause is usually a design mistake made when the garden was created. this guide lists the most common garden design mistakes that make maintenance more expensive, so you can avoid them. quick answer the garden design mistakes that make maintenance expensive: too much lawn, too many perennials, poor access, the wrong plants for the soil, no irrigation, no storage, and complex water features. a well-designed garden costs 1,500 to 3,000 pounds per year to maintain. a badly designed garden costs 4,000 to 8,000 pounds per year. in the chilterns, where labour rates are high, design mistakes are expensive. why design affects maintenance cost garden design and maintenance cost are linked. a well-designed garden is a pleasure to maintain. a badly designed garden is a constant battle. the same garden, designed differently, can cost twice as much to maintain. this is why it is worth investing in a good design before you start. in the chilterns, where labour rates are 40 to 50 pounds per hour, design mistakes are expensive. a garden that needs an extra 2 hours per week of maintenance costs 4,000 to 5,000 pounds per year extra. over 10 years, that is 40,000 to 50,000 pounds. this guide explains the design mistakes that make maintenance expensive, so you can avoid them. the 7 most expensive design mistakes 1. too much lawn lawns need weekly mowing from march to october. a 200 sqm lawn takes 1 hour to mow and edge. over a year, that is 30 to 35 hours of mowing, or 1,200 to 1,750 pounds at 40 pounds per hour. reduce the lawn area to reduce maintenance cost. alternatives: wildflower meadow (cut once a year) gravel garden (no mowing) ground cover planting (no mowing) extended borders (less lawn, more planting) 2. too many perennials perennials need staking, dividing, deadheading and cutting back. a 50 sqm perennial border takes 4 to 6 hours per week in the growing season. over a year, that is 80 to 120 hours, or 3,200 to 4,800 pounds at 40 pounds per hour. reduce perennials, increase shrubs and evergreens: shrubs need pruning once a year evergreens need pruning once a year perennials need attention weekly 3. poor access a garden with poor access is expensive to maintain. narrow paths, no gate, no place to store tools. the gardener spends time carrying tools and waste, not gardening. design for access: paths at least 90cm wide (1.2m for wheelbarrows) gates at least 90cm wide a shed or storage area for tools a place to park a van or truck access to all parts of the garden 4. the wrong plants for the soil plants in the wrong soil struggle and need constant attention. acid-lovers on chalk go yellow and need feeding. thirsty plants on chalk need constant watering. drought-tolerant plants on clay rot in wet winters. match plants to soil: on chalk: mediterranean plants, herbs, drought-tolerant perennials on clay: shrubs, roses, perennials that tolerate wet winters test the soil before choosing plants see our chalk soil planting guide . 5. no irrigation without irrigation, containers and new plantings need daily watering in summer. a 30-minute watering session each day is 3.5 hours per week, or 1,400 pounds per year at 40 pounds per hour. install irrigation: drip irrigation for containers and borders (200 to 1,500 pounds) timer to water automatically (50 to 150 pounds) water butt to collect rainwater (50 to 100 pounds) 6. no storage without storage, tools are carried from the house, and waste is carried to the car. this adds 30 minutes to every maintenance visit, or 1,000 pounds per year at 40 pounds per hour. provide storage: a shed for tools (500 to 2,000 pounds) a storage area for materials (compost, mulch, stakes) a place to load waste 7. complex water features complex water features need regular cleaning, pump maintenance, and winter protection. a typical water feature takes 4 to 8 hours per year to maintain, or 160 to 320 pounds at 40 pounds per hour. simplify water features: choose a simple pond with a small pump avoid fountains and complex filtration use a wildlife pond (no pump needed) the cost of maintenance for different garden designs garden type annual maintenance cost low-maintenance (evergreens, gravel, small lawn) 1,000-2,000 pounds standard family garden (lawn, borders, hedges) 2,000-4,000 pounds high-maintenance (large lawn, perennial borders, veg) 4,000-8,000 pounds formal garden (clipped hedges, topiary, rose beds) 5,000-10,000 pounds estate (1 acre plus) 6,000-25,000+ pounds how to design for low maintenance reduce lawn area: replace with gravel, ground cover, or meadow choose shrubs over perennials: less maintenance use evergreens: year-round structure with annual pruning match plants to soil: avoid constant feeding and watering install irrigation: automate watering provide storage: save time carrying tools and waste simplify water features: avoid complex pumps and filtration use gravel instead of borders: no watering, no weeding plant in drifts: fewer species, easier to maintain mulch annually: suppress weeds, retain moisture common mistakes not thinking about maintenance when designing. a beautiful design that needs 10 hours a week of maintenance is a burden. choosing plants for flowers, not for structure. perennials are high-maintenance. shrubs and evergreens are low-maintenance. not matching plants to soil. plants in the wrong soil struggle and need constant attention. not installing irrigation. daily watering in summer is expensive. not providing storage. carrying tools and waste adds time to every visit. frequently asked questions how much does a low-maintenance garden cost to maintain? 1,000 to 2,000 pounds per year for a typical family garden. this assumes a small lawn, evergreen shrubs, and a fortnightly maintenance visit. should i have a lawn or a wildflower meadow? a wildflower meadow needs less maintenance (cut once a year vs weekly mowing), but it looks untidy in winter. choose based on your taste and the garden style. how do i reduce maintenance cost without redesigning? reduce the lawn area, replace perennials with shrubs, mulch annually, and install irrigation for containers. these changes can reduce maintenance by 30 to 50 percent. get help designing for low maintenance if you want to design a low-maintenance garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with photos and we will garden maintenance for second homes and empty properties garden maintenance for second homes and empty properties. what to do between visits, security, seasonal care and keeping the garden looking lived-in. garden maintenance for second homes and empty properties regular garden maintenance second homes and empty properties present a particular garden maintenance challenge. the garden is not seen regularly, so problems can develop unnoticed. this guide explains how to maintain a garden for a second home or empty property. quick answer garden maintenance for second homes and empty properties: arrange fortnightly visits during the growing season, monthly visits in winter, secure the property, monitor for problems, and have a key holder for emergencies. cost: 1,500 to 4,000 pounds per year for a typical second home in the chilterns. the biggest risks are burglary, vandalism, and the garden becoming overgrown. the challenge of second home gardens second homes and empty properties present a unique garden maintenance challenge. the owner is not there to spot problems, the garden can become overgrown between visits, and the property can be a target for burglary and vandalism. a regular maintenance schedule is essential to keep the garden looking cared for and the property secure. in the chilterns, second homes are common in marlow, henley, beaconsfield and the surrounding countryside. many are owned by london-based professionals who visit at weekends. the garden can become overgrown between visits, and an overgrown garden signals that the property is empty. this guide explains how to maintain the garden of a second home or empty property. the 5 key issues for second home gardens 1. the garden becomes overgrown without regular maintenance, the garden becomes overgrown within weeks. lawns grow long, weeds set seed, hedges get out of shape. an overgrown garden signals that the property is empty, which is a security risk. 2. security risks an overgrown garden hides the property from passers-by, making it a target for burglary. a tidy garden signals that the property is cared for, even if the owner is not there. 3. no one to spot problems if a fence blows down, a tree falls, or a pipe leaks, no one is there to spot it. a regular maintenance visit checks for these problems and reports them to the owner. 4. access for emergencies if there is a fire, a break-in, or a flood, the emergency services need access. a maintenance gardener can be a key holder and provide access. 5. compliance with insurance many home insurance policies require that the property is checked regularly (usually every 7 to 30 days) if it is empty. a maintenance visit can serve as this check. the maintenance schedule for second homes season frequency key jobs spring (march to may) fortnightly mow, weed, check for problems summer (june to august) fortnightly mow weekly, water containers, check for problems autumn (september to november) fortnightly mow, leaf clearance, check for problems winter (december to february) monthly quick tidy, check for problems, security check what a second home maintenance visit includes mow the lawn and edge weed paths, borders, and driveway check for problems: broken fences, fallen trees, leaks, signs of break-in water containers (if applicable) sweep paths and patio check the property: windows, doors, gutters security check: gates locked, outbuildings secure report: whatsapp photos and notes to the owner how to secure an empty property install a key safe for emergency access install timer lights to make the property look occupied lock all gates and outbuildings trim hedges so the property is visible from the road remove valuables from sheds and outbuildings install a security camera or alarm have a key holder who can respond to emergencies insurance requirements most home insurance policies require that an empty property is checked regularly. the typical requirements are: check every 7 to 30 days (varies by policy) turn off the water (or keep the heating on to prevent frozen pipes) lock all doors and windows remove valuables notify the insurer that the property is empty a maintenance visit can serve as the regular check. always check your policy for specific requirements. the cost of second home maintenance garden size annual cost small (100 sqm) 1,000-2,000 pounds medium (300 sqm) 1,500-3,500 pounds large (500+ sqm) 3,000-6,000 pounds this includes fortnightly visits in the growing season, monthly visits in winter, and emergency call-out cover. common mistakes not having a maintenance schedule. an overgrown garden signals that the property is empty. not having a key holder. if there is an emergency, no one can provide access. not checking insurance requirements. most policies require regular checks. failure to do so can invalidate the policy. not securing the property. an empty property is a target for burglary and vandalism. not communicating with the owner. the owner needs to know what is happening at the property. frequently asked questions how often should a second home garden be maintained? fortnightly in the growing season (march to october), monthly in winter. this keeps the garden looking cared for and the property secure. how much does second home maintenance cost? 1,500 to 3,500 pounds per year for a typical second home. this includes regular visits and emergency call-out cover. can i use a neighbour to check the property? yes, for basic checks. but a professional maintenance gardener is better for keeping the garden under control and spotting problems. get help with second home maintenance if you have a second home or empty property in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire and want help with garden maintenance, best lawn care plan for large gardens in the chilterns best lawn care plan for large gardens in the chilterns. mowing, feeding, aeration, scarification and watering schedule for chalk soil lawns. best lawn care plan for large gardens in the chilterns lawn care & lawn repair large lawns in the chilterns need a structured care plan. the chalky, free-draining soil means lawns can dry out fast in summer and need particular attention to moss and thatch. this guide provides a month-by-month lawn care plan for large gardens in the chilterns. quick answer the best lawn care plan for large gardens in the chilterns: mow weekly at 25 to 40mm from march to october, scarify and aerate annually in september, overseed bare patches in spring or autumn, feed in spring and autumn, treat moss annually, and water deeply in dry spells. a 500 sqm lawn costs 800 to 1,500 pounds per year to maintain professionally. on chalky chiltern soils, the lawn needs extra watering and moss treatment. why large lawns need a plan a large lawn is the most visible element of a large garden. an immaculate lawn makes the whole garden look cared for. a neglected lawn makes the whole garden look untidy. but a large lawn is also expensive to maintain, and without a plan, the cost can spiral. in the chilterns, large lawns range from 200 to 2,000 sqm. the chalky soils around high wycombe, amersham and wendover dry out fast, so the lawn needs extra watering in summer. the clay soils in aylesbury and bicester hold water better, but are prone to moss and compaction. this guide gives a lawn care plan tailored to large gardens in the chilterns. the annual lawn care calendar month job january avoid walking on frosty lawns. plan the year. february apply moss treatment if needed. service the mower. march first cut with blades high (40-50mm). edge the lawn. april weekly mowing begins. apply spring feed. aerate if not done in autumn. may weekly mowing at 30-40mm. treat weeds with a spot weedkiller. june weekly mowing at 25-30mm. water in dry spells. july weekly mowing at 25-30mm. water deeply once a week. august weekly mowing. water in dry spells. treat for leatherjackets if needed. september scarify, aerate, top dress, overseed. apply autumn feed. october last cut of the year at 40-50mm. clear leaves. november clear leaves. avoid walking on wet soil. december avoid walking on frosty lawns. plan the year. mowing mowing is the most important (and most frequent) lawn job. get it right and the lawn will thrive. get it wrong and the lawn will struggle. frequency: weekly from march to october, fortnightly in november and december if mild height: 25 to 30mm in summer, 40 to 50mm in spring and autumn pattern: vary the direction each cut to prevent soil compaction clippings: leave on the lawn in dry spells to retain moisture, collect in wet spells to prevent thatch edging: edge along paths and borders with a half-moon edger every 4 to 6 weeks for large lawns (over 500 sqm), use a ride-on mower. it is 5 to 10 times faster than a walk-behind mower. scarification scarification removes thatch (dead grass and moss) from the lawn. thatch prevents water and air reaching the roots. scarify annually in september. when: september, when the grass is actively growing how: use a powered scarifier, set to cut 5 to 10mm into the soil result: the lawn will look bare and thin for 2 to 3 weeks, then recover with overseeding cost: 2 to 4 pounds per square metre if we do it, or hire a scarifier for 50 pounds per day. see our scarification guide . aeration aeration relieves compaction and improves drainage. aerate annually in september. when: september, with or after scarification how: use a hollow-tine or solid-tine aerator, 10cm deep every 10cm top dressing: brush a 70/30 sand and soil mix into the holes cost: 2 to 4 pounds per square metre if we do it. see our aeration guide . overseeding overseeding thickens the lawn and introduces new grass varieties. overseed after scarification and aeration. when: september (best) or march seed rate: 25g per square metre seed choice: shade-tolerant mix for shaded lawns, hard-wearing mix for family lawns, drought-tolerant mix for chalky soils aftercare: water daily for 3 weeks, keep off for 2 weeks, do not mow until new grass is 7 to 8cm tall feeding feed the lawn twice a year: spring (march to april) and autumn (september to october). spring feed: high nitrogen to promote growth. apply at 30g per square metre. autumn feed: low nitrogen, high potash to promote root growth. apply at 30g per square metre. on chalky soils: choose a feed with iron for moss control moss treatment moss is a common problem on chiltern lawns, especially on clay soils and in shaded areas. treat annually with iron sulphate in february or march. application: 5g per square metre, mixed with water result: moss turns black within 2 weeks, ready for scarification prevention: improve drainage, reduce shade, aerate annually see our moss guide . watering water the lawn in dry spells to keep it green. on chalky chiltern soils, the lawn can go brown within 2 weeks of dry weather. when: when the soil is dry to 5cm deep how much: 25mm per week (about 2.5 litres per square metre) how often: once a week, deeply. avoid frequent light watering. time of day: early morning (5 to 8am) to reduce evaporation if you cannot water, let the lawn go dormant. it will recover when the rain returns. the cost of large lawn care lawn size annual cost (professional) 200 sqm 400-800 pounds 500 sqm 800-1,500 pounds 1,000 sqm 1,500-3,000 pounds 2,000 sqm 3,000-6,000 pounds this includes weekly mowing, annual scarification and aeration, feeding, and moss treatment. see our lawn recovery service and lawn calculator . common mistakes cutting too short. short grass is vulnerable to drought, moss and weeds. keep blades at 25 to 40mm. not scarifying. thatch builds up and the lawn declines. scarify annually. not aerating. compacted lawns get mossy and weedy. aerate annually. over-feeding. too much fertiliser produces soft growth vulnerable to drought and disease. not treating moss. moss spreads fast if not treated. apply iron sulphate annually. frequently asked questions how often should i mow a large lawn? weekly from march to october. use a ride-on mower for lawns over 500 sqm. when should i scarify and aerate? september, when the grass is actively growing. the lawn will recover in 3 to 4 weeks with overseeding. should i water my lawn in a drought? only if you can commit to weekly watering. sporadic watering does more harm than good. letting the lawn go dormant is fine. get help with large lawn care if you have a large lawn in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire and want help with maintenance, send us a how to plan borders that look good all year how to plan borders that look good all year. structure, seasonal interest, plant choice and layout for year-round garden colour. how to plan borders that look good all year garden design & layout ideas a border that looks good for two weeks in june and then fades is a common disappointment. the secret to a border that looks good all year is structure, succession planting and choosing plants with multiple seasons of interest. this guide explains how to plan borders that look good in every month. quick answer to plan borders that look good all year: use a mix of evergreen shrubs (40 percent), deciduous shrubs (20 percent), perennials (30 percent), and bulbs (10 percent). choose plants with different flowering times, interesting foliage, and winter structure. plan for year-round interest, not just summer flowers. in the chilterns, choose plants suited to the soil (chalk or clay) for reliable year-round performance. why year-round borders are hard most borders look good for 6 to 8 weeks in summer and dull for the rest of the year. the trick to a year-round border is to plan for every season, not just summer. this means choosing plants with different flowering times, interesting foliage, and winter structure. in the chilterns, where winters can be long and grey, year-round interest is particularly important. a border that looks good in february and november as well as june is a joy. this guide explains how to plan borders that look good all year, with specific recommendations for chiltern gardens. the 4-layer border plan a well-planned border has 4 layers, each contributing to year-round interest: layer 1: evergreen shrubs (40 percent) evergreen shrubs provide year-round structure. they are the backbone of the border. choose 3 to 5 evergreen shrubs, planted in drifts of 3 to 5. photinia - red new growth in spring choisya - glossy leaves, scented flowers in spring pittosporum - small wavy leaves mahonia - yellow winter flowers viburnum tinus - winter flowers layer 2: deciduous shrubs (20 percent) deciduous shrubs provide seasonal interest (flowers, autumn colour) and winter structure (bare stems). philadelphus - scented white flowers in june weigela - trumpet flowers in pink and red deutzia - white or pink flowers forsythia - yellow flowers in march layer 3: perennials (30 percent) perennials provide summer flowers. choose varieties with different flowering times. spring: hardy geraniums , epimedium early summer: alliums , nepeta , roses mid summer: echinacea , verbena bonariensis late summer: sedum , japanese anemone , aster layer 4: bulbs (10 percent) bulbs provide early flowers before the perennials get going. spring: daffodils , tulips , crocus summer: alliums , lilies autumn: cyclamen , colchicum the year-round interest calendar month what is interesting january mahonia flowers, viburnum tinus flowers, evergreen foliage february mahonia flowers, sarcococca scent, evergreen foliage march forsythia flowers, daffodils, crocus, new growth on evergreens april tulips, choisya flowers, philadelphus foliage, spring perennials may alliums, nepeta, early roses, choisya flowers june roses, philadelphus, weigela, hardy geraniums july echinacea, verbena, sedum buds, lavender august sedum, japanese anemone, verbena, echinacea september aster, sedum, japanese anemone, autumn bulbs october aster, autumn colour on deciduous shrubs, berries november autumn colour, berries, evergreen foliage december evergreen foliage, viburnum tinus flowers, mahonia buds plants for chiltern soils for chalky soils evergreens: photinia, choisya, pittosporum deciduous: philadelphus, weigela, deutzia perennials: lavender, alliums, verbena bonariensis, sedum bulbs: alliums, tulips (darwin hybrids) for clay soils evergreens: viburnum, mahonia, holly deciduous: weigela, philadelphus, hydrangea perennials: hardy geraniums, astrantia, filipendula bulbs: daffodils, snowdrops, bluebells how to design a year-round border start with evergreens: place them first, as the backbone add deciduous shrubs: for seasonal flowers and autumn colour add perennials: for summer flowers, in drifts of 3 to 5 add bulbs: for early flowers, planted in autumn plan for every month: check that something is interesting every month use foliage: choose plants with interesting leaves, not just flowers include winter structure: seed heads, berries, evergreen foliage the cost of planting a year-round border item cost (per 10 sqm border) evergreen shrubs (5) 150-400 pounds deciduous shrubs (3) 90-200 pounds perennials (15) 150-300 pounds bulbs (50) 30-80 pounds compost and mulch 50-100 pounds planting labour 100-300 pounds total 570-1,380 pounds common mistakes planting only summer-flowering perennials. the border looks good for 8 weeks and dull for the rest of the year. not using evergreens. the border has no winter structure. planting too close. plants need space to grow. check the eventual spread. not matching plants to soil. acid-lovers die on chalk. match plants to your soil. not planning for winter. include seed heads, berries, and evergreen foliage for winter interest. frequently asked questions how many plants do i need for a 10 sqm border? about 25 plants: 5 evergreen shrubs, 3 deciduous shrubs, 15 perennials, and 50 bulbs. plant in drifts of 3 to 5. how do i plan for year-round interest? make a list of what is interesting each month, then choose plants to fill the gaps. aim for something interesting every month. should i use evergreens or deciduous shrubs? both. evergreens provide year-round structure, deciduous shrubs provide seasonal flowers and autumn colour. aim for 40 percent evergreen, 20 percent deciduous. get help planning year-round borders if you want to plan patio cost 2026 patio & pressure washing pressure washing transforms a green, slippery patio into a clean, safe surface. but what does it cost in 2026? this guide explains pressure washing patio costs with real per-square-metre pricing and the factors that push the price up or down. quick answer the cost of pressure washing a patio in 2026 ranges from 120 to 350 pounds for a standard 40 sqm patio. small patios (20 sqm) cost 120 to 220 pounds. large driveways (100 sqm) cost 450 to 750 pounds. diy costs 50 to 100 pounds for pressure washer hire. in the chilterns, prices are at the higher end due to higher labour rates. how much does pressure washing cost in 2026? pressure washing is priced by area, with a minimum charge of around 100 pounds. the cost varies depending on the size of the area, the surface type, the level of soiling, and whether waste removal is included. in the chilterns, where labour rates are 40 to 50 pounds per hour, pressure washing is at the higher end of the uk price range. this guide gives the current 2026 prices for pressure washing in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire, based on our quotes and the prices charged by other contractors in the area. pressure washing prices by area area cost (professional) diy cost (hire) small patio (20 sqm) 120-220 pounds 50-70 pounds standard patio (40 sqm) 200-350 pounds 50-70 pounds large patio (60 sqm) 300-500 pounds 70-100 pounds driveway (50 sqm) 250-450 pounds 70 pounds large drive (100 sqm) 450-750 pounds 100 pounds decking (20 sqm) 150-300 pounds 50-70 pounds walls (per sqm) 5-15 pounds n/a what affects the cost? several factors affect the cost of pressure washing: 1. size of the area most contractors charge per square metre, with a minimum charge. the cost per square metre decreases as the area increases. 2. surface type hard surfaces (concrete, block paving) are cheaper to clean than soft stones (indian sandstone, limestone) because they can take higher pressure. soft stones need lower pressure and more care. 3. level of soiling heavily soiled patios with moss, algae, and stains take longer to clean and cost more. lightly soiled patios are cheaper. 4. stain treatment oil stains, rust stains, and organic stains need chemical treatment before pressure washing. this adds 50 to 150 pounds to the cost. 5. re-sanding block paving pressure washing removes the joint sand from block paving. re-sanding with kiln-dried sand is essential. this adds 50 to 100 pounds to the cost. 6. sealing sealing the surface after cleaning keeps it cleaner for longer. sealing adds 5 to 10 pounds per square metre, or 200 to 500 pounds for a typical patio. 7. waste removal waste water and debris must be collected and disposed of. most contractors include this in the price, but check. 8. access difficult access (rear garden with no side access, narrow lanes) adds to the cost. cost breakdown for a typical patio (40 sqm) item cost pressure washing (40 sqm at 5-8 pounds/sqm) 200-320 pounds stain treatment (if needed) 50-100 pounds re-sanding (if block paving) 50-100 pounds sealing (optional) 200-400 pounds waste removal included total (basic clean) 200-320 pounds total (with re-sanding and sealing) 500-820 pounds diy vs professional pressure washing factor diy professional cost (40 sqm) 50-70 pounds (hire) 200-350 pounds time 1-2 days 2-4 hours quality variable consistently good risk of damage higher lower waste removal your problem included how to save money on pressure washing combine with neighbours: some contractors offer discounts for multiple properties in the same street book in spring or autumn: the busy season is summer. book off-peak for lower prices. skip sealing: sealing is optional. skip it if budget is tight. choose the right surface: concrete is cheaper to clean than indian sandstone maintain regularly: an annual clean is cheaper than letting the patio get badly soiled common mistakes pressure washing too often. pressure washing wears the surface. once a year is enough. using too high a pressure. high pressure damages sandstone, tarmac and pointing. use the right pressure. not re-sanding block paving. always re-sand with kiln-dried sand after pressure washing. not treating stains first. pressure washing alone will not remove oil or rust stains. choosing the cheapest quote. cheap contractors may not have insurance or may damage the surface. frequently asked questions how much does pressure washing cost per square metre? 5 to 8 pounds per square metre for hard surfaces (concrete, block paving), 7 to 12 pounds per square metre for soft stones (indian sandstone, limestone). is diy pressure washing worth it? for small areas (under 20 sqm), yes. for larger areas, the time and effort make professional cleaning better value. how long does pressure washing take? 2 to 4 hours for a 40 sqm patio. 4 to 8 hours for a 100 sqm driveway. get a quote for pressure washing if you need pressure washing in oxfordshire, how to turn a neglected corner into a wildlife area how to turn a neglected garden corner into a wildlife area. log piles, wildflowers, water and shelter for a small wildlife project. how to turn a neglected corner into a wildlife area wildlife & wildflower gardens every garden has a neglected corner. the bit behind the shed, the shady patch by the fence, the area where nothing seems to grow. instead of fighting it, turn it into a wildlife area. this guide explains how to transform a neglected corner into a haven for wildlife. quick answer to turn a neglected corner into a wildlife area: clear the brambles and rubbish, plant a native hedge (hawthorn, blackthorn, hazel), add a pond, leave a log pile and leaf pile, sow wildflowers, and leave a patch of long grass. cost: 200 to 800 pounds for a typical 20 sqm corner. the result is a wildlife haven that supports 50 times more species than a manicured lawn. why neglected corners are an opportunity every garden has a neglected corner. the bottom of the garden, behind the shed, the bit you never use. most people try to tidy it up, but the better option is to embrace it as a wildlife area. a neglected corner is already partway to being a wildlife haven. with a bit of work, it can become the most valuable part of the garden for wildlife. in the chilterns, where gardens often back onto countryside, a wildlife corner can act as a bridge between the garden and the wider landscape. the more wildlife corners there are, the more connected the landscape becomes. this guide explains how to turn a neglected corner into a wildlife area. the 6-step process step 1: clear the neglected corner before you can create a wildlife area, you need to clear the rubbish and brambles. remove: all rubbish and debris brambles (dig out the root crowns) self-seeded trees (sycamore, ash, birch) old fences and structures keep any mature trees and shrubs that are worth keeping. see our clearance service . step 2: plant a native hedge plant a native hedge around the corner to define the area and provide habitat. use: hawthorn - 50 percent of the hedge blackthorn - 25 percent hazel - 15 percent holly - 10 percent plant bare-root in winter (november to march), 5 to 7 per metre in a double staggered row. cost: 200 to 400 pounds for a 10 metre hedge. see our hedging guide . step 3: add a pond a pond is the single best wildlife feature. even a small pond (1 to 2 sqm) attracts frogs, newts, dragonflies and birds within weeks. build the pond with: a shallow edge (for wildlife to get in and out) a deeper centre (60 to 80cm, for overwintering) native plants (water lily, iris, marsh marigold) no fish (they eat the wildlife) cost: 200 to 600 pounds for a small wildlife pond. see our pond maintenance guide . step 4: add a log pile and leaf pile log piles and leaf piles provide habitat for beetles, frogs, slowworms and hedgehogs. build them in a partly shaded corner. log pile: stack logs of different sizes, partly buried leaf pile: pile autumn leaves in a corner, leave for 1 to 2 years stone pile: for reptiles and amphibians step 5: sow wildflowers sow wildflowers in the sunniest part of the corner. use a native wildflower mix suited to your soil (chalk or clay). see our wildflower guide . reduce soil fertility first sow in autumn or spring cut once a year in late summer remove the cuttings cost: 50 to 150 pounds for seed for a 20 sqm area. step 6: leave a patch of long grass leave a patch of long grass for ground-nesting bees and butterflies. mow paths through it so it looks intentional. cut once a year in late summer. what to plant in a wildlife corner native shrubs hawthorn blackthorn hazel holly dogwood spindle nectar plants foxglove ox-eye daisy knapweed scabious primrose berry plants pyracantha cotoneaster guelder rose pond plants water lily iris marsh marigold water forget-me-not how to make the wildlife corner look intentional a common complaint about wildlife corners is that they look messy. to make the corner look intentional: mow paths through the long grass define the edges with a mown strip or edging add a feature like a bench, sculpture, or bird bath frame it with a hedge add a sign like "wildlife area" or "wildflower meadow" the cost of creating a wildlife corner item cost (20 sqm corner) clearance 100-300 pounds native hedge (10m) 200-400 pounds pond (2 sqm) 200-600 pounds wildflower seed 50-150 pounds plants (nectar and berry) 100-300 pounds log pile and leaf pile 0 (free materials) total 650-1,750 pounds common mistakes not clearing the area first. brambles and rubbish will smother any new planting. clear first. not planting a hedge. a hedge defines the area and provides habitat. plant one around the corner. not adding water. a pond is the single best wildlife feature. even a small one makes a big difference. using non-native plants. native plants support more wildlife. choose native species. not making it look intentional. a wildlife corner without mown paths looks abandoned. mow paths to show it is deliberate. frequently asked questions how long does it take for a wildlife corner to establish? 1 to 3 years. the first year looks untidy, the second year looks promising, and by the third year it should be a thriving wildlife haven. will a wildlife corner attract rats? not usually. rats are attracted by food waste, not wildlife planting. keep compost heaps rodent-proof and do not leave food out. can i have a wildlife corner in a small garden? yes. even a 5 sqm corner can support a surprising amount of wildlife. plant a small hedge, add a tiny pond, and leave a patch of long grass. get help creating a wildlife corner if you want to turn a neglected corner into a wildlife area in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or how to reclaim an overgrown lawn and border area how to reclaim an overgrown lawn and border area in stages. clearance, soil improvement, lawn renovation and replanting advice for uk gardens. how to reclaim an overgrown lawn and border area garden clearance & tidy-up guides when both the lawn and the borders have been left too long, the job is bigger than either one alone. but the process is the same: clear, improve, renovate, replant. this guide explains how to reclaim an overgrown lawn and border area in stages. quick answer to reclaim an overgrown lawn and border: cut the lawn back gradually (never more than one third of the height in one cut), scarify to remove thatch and moss, aerate to relieve compaction, overseed bare patches, and clear the borders of weeds before mulching and replanting. in the chilterns, the chalky soils dry out fast after clearing, so watering is essential for the first 6 weeks. a typical 50 sqm lawn and border rescue costs 300 to 700 pounds if we do it. why lawns and borders get overgrown lawns and borders get overgrown for predictable reasons: a year or two of missed maintenance, a tenant who did not look after the garden, an illness, or simply a busy period in life. the lawn gets long and mossy, the borders get weedy and overgrown, and the whole garden looks neglected. the good news is that almost any lawn and border can be rescued in a single season. in the chilterns, the chalky soils around high wycombe and amersham dry out fast, which stresses the lawn in summer. the clay soils in aylesbury and bicester hold water, which encourages moss. both soil types need different rescue approaches. this guide explains how to reclaim an overgrown lawn and border, with specific notes for chiltern conditions. step 1: assess the damage walk the lawn and borders and note: lawn height (how long is it?) moss coverage (percentage of the lawn) bare patches (size and location) weeds in the lawn (dandelions, daisies, plantain) border weeds (bindweed, couch grass, ground elder) overgrown shrubs and perennials soil type (chalk or clay) step 2: reclaim the lawn 1. cut back gradually if the lawn is very long (over 10cm), cut it back gradually over 2 to 3 cuts, a week apart. never remove more than one third of the height in one cut, or you will shock the grass. set the mower high for the first cut, then lower it gradually. 2. treat moss apply iron sulphate at 5g per square metre to kill the moss. the moss will turn black within 2 weeks, ready for scarification. on chalky chiltern soils, this mild acidification is beneficial as it helps unlock nutrients. 3. scarify use a powered scarifier to remove dead moss and thatch. a 50 sqm lawn will produce 4 to 6 bin bags of material. the lawn will look bare and thin afterwards, but this is normal. see our scarification guide . 4. aerate aerate to relieve compaction. use a hollow-tine or solid-tine aerator, 10cm deep every 10cm. on clay soils in aylesbury and bicester, hollow-tine aeration is particularly important. 5. overseed overseed bare and thin patches at 25g per square metre. choose a seed mix suited to your conditions: shade-tolerant for shaded lawns, drought-tolerant for chalky soils. see our overseeding guide . 6. feed apply a balanced fertiliser at 30g per square metre. water in if no rain within 2 days. step 3: reclaim the borders 1. cut back dead growth cut back all dead stems and seed heads from perennials and grasses. this makes it easier to see what you are doing. 2. dig out perennial weeds dig out perennial weeds (bindweed, couch grass, ground elder) with a fork. get every bit of root. see our bindweed guide . 3. lift and divide perennials lift and divide overcrowded perennials. replant the vigorous outer sections, discard the tired centre. 4. prune overgrown shrubs prune overgrown shrubs to a manageable size. most deciduous shrubs can be cut back hard in late winter. see our pruning guide . 5. improve the soil add 5cm of compost and dig it in. on chalky soils, this builds depth. on clay soils, add grit as well to improve drainage. 6. mulch apply a 5cm layer of bark or compost mulch to suppress weeds and retain moisture. cost of reclaiming an overgrown lawn and border job diy cost professional cost lawn scarification (50 sqm) 50 (hire) 100-200 pounds lawn aeration (50 sqm) 50 (hire) 100-200 pounds overseeding (50 sqm) 30-50 (seed) 80-150 pounds border weed clearance 0 (tools) 100-300 pounds soil improvement 100-200 (compost) 200-400 pounds mulch 50-100 100-200 pounds total (50 sqm) 280-450 680-1,450 pounds common mistakes cutting the lawn too short in one go. this shocks the grass and can kill it. cut back gradually. not scarifying. thatch and moss build up without annual scarification. the lawn declines. not digging out perennial weed roots. pulling the tops leaves the roots, which regrow. not watering new seed. new grass seed needs daily watering for 3 weeks to germinate. not mulching after clearing. bare soil is an invitation for weeds. always mulch. frequently asked questions how long does it take to reclaim an overgrown lawn? 4 to 8 weeks for the lawn to recover. full recovery takes 3 to 6 months, with the best results seen the following spring. should i returf or reseed? reseed if more than 50 percent of the lawn is salvageable. returf if the lawn is beyond repair. see our turf vs seed guide . when is the best time to reclaim a lawn? spring (march to may) or autumn (september to october), when the soil is warm and there is regular rain. get help
