how to plan a garden tidy-up weekend without getting overwhelmed how to plan a garden tidy-up weekend without getting overwhelmed. prioritise, time each job and know when to hire help. how to plan a garden tidy-up weekend without getting overwhelmed garden clearance & tidy-up guides a garden tidy-up can feel overwhelming if you try to do everything at once. the key is to plan, prioritise and work in stages. this guide explains how to plan a garden tidy-up weekend that leaves you with a tidy garden rather than a bad back. quick answer to plan a garden tidy-up weekend without getting overwhelmed: focus on the visible areas first, prioritise lawn and paths, delegate tasks, take regular breaks, and do not try to do everything. a well-planned weekend tidy-up transforms the look of the garden for 200 to 500 pounds. in the chilterns, focus on mowing, weeding, and clearing paths for maximum impact. why tidy-up weekends fail the garden tidy-up weekend is a classic gardening event. the intention is good: spend a weekend tidying the garden and get it back under control. the reality is often different: you start at 8am on saturday, work until 6pm, ache all over, and on sunday you can barely move. the garden is half-done, and you are too exhausted to finish. by the next weekend, you have lost momentum. the problem is not the work, it is the planning. a well-planned tidy-up weekend is achievable and satisfying. a poorly planned one is exhausting and demoralising. this guide explains how to plan a garden tidy-up weekend that works, based on what works for our clients across the chilterns. the 7-step plan step 1: walk the garden and make a list before the weekend, walk the garden with a notebook and list everything that needs doing. be specific: mow the lawn weed the front border cut back the hedge by the patio clear leaves from the path prune the rose by the gate do not write "tidy the garden". write specific jobs. step 2: prioritise rank the jobs by impact: high impact, low effort: mow the lawn, sweep the paths, weed the visible borders high impact, high effort: cut back the hedge, clear a bramble patch low impact, low effort: weed the back border, prune the shrub by the shed low impact, high effort: dig out a tree stump, relevel the lawn do the high-impact, low-effort jobs first. these give the biggest visible improvement for the least effort. step 3: focus on visible areas the areas visible from the house and the road are the most important. a tidy patio, mown lawn, and clean view from the kitchen window make the whole garden feel more manageable. save the back of the garden for another weekend. step 4: gather tools and materials before the weekend, gather everything you need: lawn mower (and fuel if petrol) strimmer hedge trimmer secateurs and loppers fork and spade rake and broom wheelbarrow garden waste bags or a skip thick gloves nothing kills momentum like stopping to find a tool. step 5: plan the two days time saturday sunday 8:00-9:00 gather tools, plan the day review yesterday's work, plan the day 9:00-11:00 mow the lawn, sweep paths weed borders, prune shrubs 11:00-11:30 break break 11:30-13:00 cut back hedges plant new plants, mulch 13:00-14:00 lunch lunch 14:00-16:00 weed visible borders final tidy, load waste 16:00-17:00 load waste, tidy tools admire the work step 6: delegate if you have family or friends to help, delegate specific jobs. one person mows, one person weeds, one person loads waste. this is much more efficient than everyone doing the same job. step 7: take breaks take a 15-minute break every 2 hours. drink water, eat a snack, sit down. gardening is hard physical work, and exhaustion leads to mistakes and injuries. the 200 pound weekend tidy-up if you have 200 pounds and a weekend, here is what you can achieve: mow and edge the lawn (free if diy) weed the visible borders (free if diy) cut back overgrowth (free if diy) hire a skip for waste (150-200 pounds) buy 3 bags of mulch (30 pounds) plant a few bedding plants for colour (20 pounds) the result: a garden that looks transformed for 200 pounds. what not to do do not try to do everything. focus on visible areas. the back of the garden can wait. do not start with the hardest job. start with high-impact, low-effort jobs to build momentum. do not work through breaks. exhaustion leads to mistakes and injuries. do not try to lift heavy things alone. get help for heavy lifting. do not leave waste in the garden. hire a skip or arrange collection. common mistakes not having a plan. without a plan, you waste time deciding what to do next. trying to do too much. a tidy-up weekend is not a renovation. focus on tidying. not gathering tools first. stopping to find a tool kills momentum. not taking breaks. exhaustion leads to mistakes and injuries. not disposing of waste. a pile of garden waste in the corner ruins the effect of the tidy-up. frequently asked questions how much can i do in a weekend? a typical family garden (300 sqm) can be tidied in a weekend with 2 people working. focus on mowing, weeding, hedge cutting, and clearing paths. should i hire a skip? yes, if you have more than 6 bags of waste. a 6 yard skip costs 200 to 350 pounds and holds about 50 bags. can i do it myself or should i hire help? a mix is usually best. do the easy bits yourself and hire a professional for the heavy work (hedge cutting, large clearance). get help with your tidy-up if you need help with a garden tidy-up in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with damage, clear all rubbish and overgrowth, remove weeds and self-seeded trees, repair structural damage, improve the soil, and replant with low-maintenance plants. cost: 300 to 1,000 pounds for a typical rental garden. landlords in the chilterns should budget for a garden clearance between tenancies to protect the property value. the rental garden problem rental property gardens are a common problem for landlords. tenants rarely maintain the garden to a high standard, and after a few years of tenancy, the garden can be overgrown, full of rubbish, and in need of significant work. a neglected rental garden reduces the property value, makes it harder to re-let, and can cause disputes with neighbours. in the chilterns, where rental properties are common in university towns like oxford and commuter towns like high wycombe and aylesbury, landlords regularly face the problem of neglected gardens. the good news is that with a systematic approach, even the worst rental garden can be cleared and restored in 1 to 3 days. this guide explains how. step 1: assess the damage before starting, walk the garden and note: amount of rubbish and debris level of overgrowth (brambles, ivy, self-seeded trees) weed coverage (bindweed, couch grass, ground elder) structural damage (broken fences, sheds, paths) lawn condition plants worth keeping (mature trees, established shrubs) access for waste removal step 2: clear rubbish and debris remove all rubbish and debris first. this includes: tenant's rubbish (old furniture, broken toys, building materials) garden waste (leaves, branches, dead plants) broken structures (old sheds, fence panels) hire a skip (200 to 350 pounds for a 6 yard skip) or use a waste removal service (120 to 200 pounds per tonne). step 3: clear overgrowth clear all overgrowth: cut brambles and ivy to ground level, dig out root crowns remove self-seeded trees (sycamore, ash, birch) cut back overgrown shrubs clear weeds (dig out perennial weed roots) see our bramble clearance guide and clearance service . step 4: repair structural damage repair or replace: broken fence panels and posts broken gates rotting sheds (replace or remove) damaged paths and patios see our fencing service . step 5: improve the soil add 5cm of compost and dig it in. remove any buried rubble. see our soil preparation guide . step 6: replant with low-maintenance plants choose low-maintenance plants that will survive neglect: photinia choisya lavender roses hardy geraniums step 7: restore the lawn if the lawn is salvageable, scarify, aerate, overseed. if beyond repair, reseed or returf. see our lawn recovery service . cost of clearing a neglected rental garden job cost rubbish removal (1 tonne) 120-200 pounds overgrowth clearance 150-400 pounds structural repairs 100-500 pounds soil improvement 100-300 pounds replanting 200-600 pounds lawn restoration 150-400 pounds total 820-2,400 pounds common mistakes not clearing the whole garden. a half-cleared garden looks worse than a fully neglected one. clear it all. planting high-maintenance plants. tenants will not maintain them. choose low-maintenance plants. not repairing structures. broken fences and sheds put off future tenants. not budgeting for clearance between tenancies. a clearance every 3 to 5 years protects the property value. frequently asked questions how often should a rental garden be cleared? every 3 to 5 years, or between tenancies. this prevents the garden becoming overgrown and protects the property value. can i charge the tenant for garden clearance? only if the tenancy agreement makes the tenant responsible for the garden. check the agreement before deducting from the deposit. what should i plant in a rental garden? low-maintenance plants: evergreen shrubs (photinia, choisya), drought-tolerant perennials (lavender, sedum), and hard-wearing lawn grass. get help clearing a rental garden if you are a landlord in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or they need scale, structure and presence. a plant that looks substantial in a small garden can disappear in a large one. this guide lists the best plants for large country gardens in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire. quick answer the best plants for large country gardens in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire: specimen trees (oak, beech, rowan, ornamental cherry), structural shrubs (photinia, choisya, viburnum), drifts of perennials (verbena, echinacea, sedum), and native hedges (hawthorn, blackthorn, hazel). choose plants suited to the soil (chalk or clay) and plant in drifts for impact. in the chilterns, large country gardens benefit from a mix of formal and naturalistic planting. why large country gardens need special plants large country gardens need different plants from small suburban gardens. the scale is bigger, so plants need to be bigger or planted in bigger drifts. the setting is more rural, so plants need to fit the landscape. and the maintenance is more challenging, so plants need to be reliable and long-lived. in the chilterns, large country gardens range from 0.5 to 5 acres, often with mature trees, mixed soil (chalk on the hills, clay in the vale), and views of the countryside. the plant choice is critical to creating a garden that fits the setting and is manageable. this guide lists the best plants for large country gardens in oxfordshire and buckinghamshire. specimen trees for large country gardens specimen trees provide structure and scale. plant 1 to 3 specimen trees in prominent positions. oak (quercus robur): the classic english tree, lives for centuries beech (fagus sylvatica): stunning in autumn, loves chalk rowan (sorbus aucuparia): berries for birds, autumn colour ornamental cherry (prunus): spring flowers, autumn colour birch tinus - winter flowers, evergreen mahonia - winter flowers, architectural pittosporum - evergreen, good for clipping philadelphus - scented white flowers in june perennials for large country gardens perennials provide summer colour. plant in drifts of 5, 7, or 9 for impact at scale. verbena bonariensis - tall, airy, butterflies echinacea - pink daisy flowers, long flowering sedum - late flowers for bees hardy geraniums - long-flowering ground cover alliums - dramatic flowers in late spring aster - late summer flowers japanese anemone - late summer flowers grasses for large country gardens ornamental grasses add movement and structure. plant in large drifts. miscanthus - tall, architectural, autumn flowers stipa tenuissima - soft, flowing, drought-tolerant calamagrostis - upright, feather reed grass pennisetum - fountain grass, soft flower heads native hedges for large country gardens native hedges define the garden and support wildlife. plant a mixed native hedge. hawthorn - 50 percent of the hedge blackthorn - 25 percent hazel - 15 percent holly - 10 percent see our hedging guide . plants for chalk soils for chalky chiltern soils (high wycombe, amersham, wendover, deschampsia how to plant a large country garden start with trees: plant specimen trees first, as they take longest to establish add hedges: define the boundaries with native hedges plant structural shrubs: in drifts of 3, 5, or 7 add perennials: in large drifts for impact at scale add grasses: for movement and late-season interest mulch annually: to suppress weeds and retain moisture cost of best plants for windy chiltern hills gardens best plants for windy chiltern hills gardens. wind-resistant trees, shrubs and perennials for exposed hillside positions. best plants for windy chiltern hills gardens plants, borders & planting ideas many chiltern gardens are on exposed hillsides where wind is a real problem. the wrong plants get battered, broken and stunted. the right plants not only survive but thrive. this guide lists the best plants for windy chiltern hills gardens. quick answer the best plants for windy chiltern hills gardens: hawthorn, blackthorn, escallonia, sea buckthorn, lavender, rosemary, stipa grasses, and yew. windy gardens on the chiltern hills (ibstone, stokenchurch, wendover) need tough, wind-tolerant plants. avoid tall plants with large leaves, which get damaged. plant windbreaks first, then plant behind them. why windy gardens are challenging the chiltern hills are exposed to strong winds, particularly on the higher ground around stokenchurch, ibstone, wendover, and princes risborough. wind damages plants in several ways: it tears leaves, dries out the soil, breaks stems, and stunts growth. a windy garden needs different plants from a sheltered garden. the trick is to plant a windbreak first (a hedge or row of tough shrubs across the prevailing wind), then plant behind it. the windbreak reduces wind speed by 50 percent for a distance of 10 times its height. this guide lists the best plants for windy chiltern hills gardens. windbreak plants for chiltern hills plant a windbreak hedge across the prevailing wind (usually south-west in the chilterns). use tough, wind-tolerant plants: hawthorn - native, wind-tolerant, wildlife-friendly blackthorn - native, dense, thorny escallonia - evergreen, flowering, wind-tolerant sea buckthorn - silvery leaves, orange berries privet - semi-evergreen, fast-growing leylandii - fast-growing evergreen (but needs regular cutting) best shrubs for windy gardens behind the windbreak, plant tough, wind-tolerant shrubs: photinia - tough, evergreen, red new growth choisya - tough, evergreen, scented pittosporum - evergreen, wind-tolerant escallonia - evergreen, flowering berberis - thorny, colourful foliage pyracantha - thorny, berries best perennials for windy gardens choose perennials with strong stems and small leaves: lavender - drought and wind tolerant rosemary - evergreen, wind-tolerant verbena bonariensis - tall but very wind-tolerant eryngium - spiky, wind-tolerant sedum - low, wind-tolerant nepeta - low, wind-tolerant best grasses for windy gardens ornamental grasses love wind and move beautifully in it: stipa tenuissima - soft, flowing, loves wind miscanthus - tall, strong stems calamagrostis - upright, wind-tolerant hakonechloa - low, flowing best trees for windy gardens choose trees with small leaves and flexible stems: rowan - wind-tolerant, berries birch - flexible, wind-tolerant hawthorn - native, wind-tolerant pine - wind-tolerant conifer holly - evergreen, wind-tolerant plants to avoid in windy gardens tall plants with large leaves: gunnera, rodgersia, hostas (leaves get shredded) top-heavy plants: sunflowers, delphiniums (stems break) shallow-rooted plants: young trees (get blown over) tender plants: bananas, tree ferns (wind damages foliage) how to design a windy garden plant a windbreak across the prevailing wind (usually south-west) plant tough shrubs behind the windbreak choose wind-tolerant perennials with small leaves and strong stems plant in drifts to create a cohesive look use grasses for movement and structure avoid large-leaved plants that get damaged by wind cost of planting a windy garden item cost windbreak hedge (10m) 200-400 pounds shrubs (10) 300-600 pounds perennials (20) 200-400 pounds grasses (10) 100-250 pounds total 800-1,650 pounds common mistakes not planting a windbreak first. without a windbreak, most plants will struggle in a windy garden. planting large-leaved plants. large leaves get shredded by wind. choose small-leaved plants. planting tall, top-heavy plants. they get blown over. choose low, sturdy plants. not staking young trees. young trees in windy sites need staking for the first 2 to 3 years. not watering enough. wind dries out the soil faster than sun. water regularly. frequently asked questions what is the best windbreak for a chiltern hills garden? a mixed native hedge of hawthorn, blackthorn, and holly. it is wind-tolerant, wildlife-friendly, and looks natural in the chiltern landscape. will lavender grow in a windy garden? yes, lavender is excellent for windy gardens. it has small leaves, strong stems, and is drought-tolerant. how do i protect young trees from wind? stake them for the first 2 to 3 years, using a single stake at 45 degrees. remove the stake after 3 years to let the tree develop its own strength. get help planting a windy garden if you have a windy garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire best plants for clay and chalk mixed gardens best plants for clay and chalk mixed gardens in the chilterns. plants that tolerate both soil types for variable garden conditions. best plants for clay and chalk mixed gardens plants, borders & planting ideas many chiltern gardens have both clay and chalk in the same plot. the chalk is on the slopes and the clay in the valleys, or the clay-with-flints caps the chalk. this creates a challenge: you need plants that tolerate both conditions. this guide lists the best plants for clay and chalk mixed gardens. quick answer for clay and chalk mixed gardens in the chilterns: choose plants that tolerate both alkaline and heavier soils, plant in raised beds on the chalk areas, and add organic matter throughout. good plants for mixed soils include roses, viburnum, philadelphus, hardy geraniums, and hollies. test the soil in different parts of the garden before planting. the mixed soil challenge many gardens in the chilterns have mixed soils: chalk in one part, clay in another. this is particularly common at the boundary between the chalk hills and the clay vale, around princes risborough, wendover, and the edges of aylesbury. the chalk is alkaline and free-draining; the clay is also alkaline but heavy and wet. plants that suit one may not suit the other. the good news is that both soils are alkaline, so the plant choice is wider than for acid soils. the challenge is the drainage: chalk drains fast, clay drains slowly. this guide explains how to garden on mixed clay and chalk soils in the chilterns. step 1: test the soil test the soil in several places in the garden. dig small holes 30cm deep and check: colour: pale soil is chalky, dark soil is clay texture: crumbly soil is chalky, sticky soil is clay drainage: fill the hole with water and see how long it takes to drain ph: both chalk and clay in the chilterns are alkaline (ph 7.5 to 8.5) map the garden: which parts are chalky, which are clay, which are mixed. plants that tolerate both chalk and clay these plants grow well on both chalk and clay, making them ideal for mixed gardens: trees rowan birch crab apple hawthorn holly shrubs roses viburnum philadelphus weigela forsythia berberis spiraea perennials hardy geraniums aster japanese anemone sedum iris (bearded) bulbs daffodils tulips alliums plants for chalk areas on the chalky parts of the garden, plant: lavender rosemary salvia beech yew see our chalk soil guide . plants for clay areas on the clay parts of the garden, plant: hydrangea hornbeam astrantia filipendula how to manage mixed soils test the soil in several places and map the chalk and clay areas. choose versatile plants that tolerate both soils. add organic matter to both soils. this improves drainage in clay and water retention in chalk. use raised beds on the chalk areas for plants that need deeper soil. improve drainage on the clay areas with grit and land drains. plant in the right place: chalk-tolerant plants on chalk, clay-tolerant plants on clay. cost of planting a mixed soil garden item cost soil testing kit 10-20 pounds compost (1 cubic metre) 80-120 pounds grit (for clay areas) 50-100 pounds raised bed materials 200-500 pounds plants (20 versatile plants) 300-600 pounds total 640-1,340 pounds common mistakes not testing the soil. mixed soils need different management in different parts of the garden. test first. planting the wrong plant in the wrong place. match plants to the soil in each part of the garden. not adding organic matter. both chalk and clay benefit from added organic matter. not improving drainage on clay. clay needs grit and possibly land drains to prevent waterlogging. not using raised beds on chalk. shallow chalk needs raised beds for deeper-rooted plants. frequently asked questions how do i know if my soil is chalk or clay? dig a small hole 30cm deep. pale, crumbly soil is chalk. dark, sticky soil is clay. both are alkaline in the chilterns (ph 7.5 to 8.5). can i grow the same plants on chalk and clay? some plants tolerate both (roses, viburnum, geraniums). others are more specific (lavender on chalk, hydrangea on clay). test the soil and match plants accordingly. should i improve the soil or use raised beds? both. add organic matter to improve the existing soil, and use raised beds on shallow chalk or poorly-draining clay. get help with your mixed how to improve a lawn before selling your house how to improve a lawn before selling your house. quick fixes and renovation jobs that add kerb appeal and sale value. how to improve a lawn before selling your house lawn care & lawn repair a green, healthy lawn is one of the biggest selling points for a garden. a mossy, patchy lawn is one of the biggest turn-offs. this guide explains how to improve a lawn before selling your house, with quick fixes and renovation jobs. quick answer to improve a lawn before selling your house: mow and edge it regularly, treat moss, feed in spring, overseed bare patches, water in dry spells, and consider returfing if it is beyond repair. cost: 100 to 500 pounds. returns: 2,000 to 10,000 pounds on the sale price. a green, tidy lawn is one of the top three things buyers notice. why the lawn matters when selling the lawn is one of the most visible elements of the garden. a green, tidy lawn makes the whole garden look cared for. a brown, mossy lawn makes the whole garden look neglected. estate agents in the chilterns regularly tell us that the lawn is one of the top three things buyers notice, after the kitchen and bathroom. the good news is that improving a lawn is relatively cheap and fast. with 4 to 6 weeks of attention, even a poor lawn can look good. this guide explains how to improve a lawn before selling, with specific advice for chiltern soils. the 6-step lawn improvement plan step 1: mow and edge (weekly) mow weekly at 30 to 40mm. edge along paths and borders with a half-moon edger. a neatly mown lawn with crisp edges makes the whole garden look cared for. step 2: treat moss (february to march) if the lawn has moss, apply iron sulphate at 5g per square metre. the moss will turn black within 2 weeks. scarify to remove the dead moss. on chalky chiltern soils, the iron also helps green up the grass. step 3: feed (march to april) apply a spring lawn feed at 30g per square metre. this greens up the grass and encourages strong growth. water in if no rain within 2 days. step 4: overseed bare patches (april to may or september) overseed bare and thin patches at 25g per square metre. choose a seed mix suited to your conditions. water daily for 3 weeks. see our overseeding guide . step 5: water in dry spells water the lawn weekly in dry spells, applying 25mm (about 2.5 litres per square metre). a green lawn sells better than a brown one. step 6: consider returfing (if beyond repair) if the lawn is more than 50 percent moss or weeds, consider returfing. new turf costs 5 to 10 pounds per square metre laid, and gives instant results. see our turf vs seed guide . the pre-sale lawn timeline when job 6 to 8 weeks before treat moss, scarify, overseed bare patches 4 to 6 weeks before feed, water regularly 2 to 4 weeks before mow weekly, edge 1 week before mow, edge, final tidy day before photos mow and edge for the best look cost of improving a lawn before selling job diy cost professional cost moss treatment (50 sqm) 10-20 50-100 pounds scarification (50 sqm) 50 (hire) 100-200 pounds feed (50 sqm) 10-20 30-60 pounds overseeding (50 sqm) 30-50 80-150 pounds watering 0 (own hose) 0 returfing (50 sqm, if needed) 250-500 500-1,000 pounds total (without returfing) 100-140 260-510 pounds common mistakes cutting the lawn too short. short grass looks scalped and unhealthy. mow at 30 to 40mm. not treating moss. a mossy lawn looks neglected. treat moss and scarify. not feeding. a fed lawn is greener and healthier. feed in spring. not watering in dry spells. a brown lawn puts buyers off. water weekly. doing the work too late. allow 4 to 6 weeks for the lawn to look its best. frequently asked questions will a new lawn add value to my house? a green, tidy lawn adds value by making the garden look cared for. a new lawn (returfed) costs 500 to 1,000 pounds but can add 2,000 to 5,000 pounds to the sale price. should i returf or reseed before selling? returf if the lawn is beyond repair and you have less than 6 weeks before marketing. reseed if you have more than 6 weeks and the lawn is salvageable. how long before the photos should i mow? mow 1 to 2 days before the photos, not the day of. this gives the grass time to settle and the clippings time to disperse. get help improving your lawn before selling if you are selling a property in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, how to improve thin, dry soil on sloping gardens how to improve thin, dry soil on sloping chiltern gardens. terracing, organic matter, mulching and drought-tolerant planting for hillside gardens. how to improve thin, dry soil on sloping gardens soil, compost & mulch sloping gardens in the chilterns often have thin, dry soil. the chalk subsoil is close to the surface, and water runs off rather than soaking in. this makes growing difficult. this guide explains how to improve thin, dry soil on sloping gardens. quick answer to improve thin, dry soil on sloping gardens: build terraces to create flat planting areas, add organic matter to build soil depth, mulch thickly to retain moisture, choose drought-tolerant plants, and install irrigation. on chalky chiltern slopes, soil is often thin and dry. cost: 500 to 3,000 pounds for terracing and soil improvement. the challenge of sloping gardens with thin soil sloping gardens with thin, dry soil are common in the chilterns, particularly on the chalk hills around high wycombe, amersham, wendover, and princes risborough. the challenges are: thin topsoil (often just 5 to 10cm), free-draining chalk that dries out fast, and slopes that make planting and maintenance difficult. the good news is that with the right approach, even the most challenging sloping garden can be transformed. the key is to build terraces (to create flat planting areas and deeper soil), add organic matter (to build soil depth and water retention), and choose drought-tolerant plants. this guide explains how. step 1: build terraces terraces create flat planting areas on a slope. they also create deeper soil, as the retaining wall allows you to build up soil depth. build terraces with: timber sleepers: 200 x 100mm, treated. cost: 30 to 50 pounds each. stone walls: dry stone or mortared. cost: 100 to 200 pounds per square metre. brick walls: for a more formal look. cost: 80 to 150 pounds per square metre. gabions: wire cages filled with stone. cost: 60 to 100 pounds per square metre. a typical 10 metre terrace costs 500 to 2,000 pounds depending on materials. step 2: add organic matter add 10 to 15cm of compost or well-rotted manure to the terraced beds and dig it in. this builds soil depth and water retention. on chalky soils, this is essential. see our soil improvement guide . step 3: mulch thickly apply a 5 to 7cm layer of bark or compost mulch. this retains moisture and suppresses weeds. on dry slopes, mulch is essential. reapply annually. step 4: choose drought-tolerant plants choose plants that tolerate dry conditions: lavender rosemary salvia sedum eryngium verbena bonariensis stipa tenuissima see our drought-tolerant plant guide . step 5: install irrigation a drip irrigation system is the most efficient way to water a sloping garden. it delivers water directly to the roots, with no runoff. cost: 200 to 1,000 pounds for a typical system. step 6: plant for erosion control on slopes, plant ground cover plants to control erosion: hardy geraniums epimedium bergenia cotoneaster ajuga cost of improving thin, dry soil on a slope item cost (10m slope) terracing (timber sleepers) 500-1,500 pounds compost (2 cubic metres) 160-240 pounds mulch 100-200 pounds plants (20 drought-tolerant) 200-500 pounds drip irrigation 200-500 pounds planting labour 200-500 pounds total 1,360-3,440 pounds common mistakes not building terraces. without terraces, soil washes away and plants struggle. not adding organic matter. thin, dry soil needs organic matter to build depth and water retention. planting thirsty plants. hydrangeas and hostas will struggle on dry slopes. choose drought-tolerant plants. not mulching. bare soil on a slope erodes and dries out. mulch is essential. not installing irrigation. new plants on dry slopes need regular watering. install drip irrigation. frequently asked questions how do i build a terrace on a slope? dig into the slope to create a flat area, build a retaining wall at the front (timber, stone, or brick), and fill behind with soil and compost. see our soft landscaping service . what is the best plant for a dry slope? lavender. it loves sun and chalk, needs no watering once established, and the roots help bind the soil. how do i stop soil washing away on a slope? build terraces, plant ground cover, and mulch thickly. the combination of physical barriers (terraces), living roots (ground cover), and surface protection (mulch) prevents erosion. get help with your sloping garden if you have a sloping garden with thin, dry soil in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire why your borders are dry, tired and underperforming why your borders are dry, tired and underperforming. soil exhaustion, lack of mulch, wrong plants and overcrowding diagnosed and fixed. why your borders are dry, tired and underperforming soil, compost & mulch tired borders are one of the most common garden problems. the plants are not growing well, the soil looks dry and cracked, and everything looks lacklustre. this guide explains why borders become dry, tired and underperforming, and how to fix them. quick answer borders look dry, tired and underperforming because of: thin or poor soil, lack of mulch, lack of water, wrong plants for the conditions, overcrowding, and lack of feeding. to fix: add organic matter, mulch annually, water in dry spells, choose the right plants, divide overcrowded perennials, and feed in spring. on chalky chiltern soils, drought is the most common cause. why borders underperform a tired, underperforming border is a common problem. the plants look weak, the flowers are small, the leaves are yellow, and the whole border lacks vigour. the good news is that most tired borders can be revived with a few simple interventions. the key is to identify the cause and address it. in the chilterns, the most common cause of tired borders is drought. the chalky soils around high wycombe, amersham and wendover drain fast, and plants struggle in dry summers. the clay soils in aylesbury and bicester hold water better but can become compacted and nutrient-poor. this guide explains why borders underperform and how to fix them. the 6 causes of tired borders 1. thin or poor soil thin, poor soil cannot support healthy plants. the soil lacks organic matter, nutrients, and water-holding capacity. symptoms: small leaves, weak growth, yellow colour. fix: add 5 to 10cm of compost and dig it in. see our soil improvement guide . 2. lack of mulch bare soil bakes hard in summer and loses moisture. without mulch, the soil dries out fast and weeds take over. fix: apply a 5cm layer of bark or compost mulch annually. see our mulching guide . 3. lack of water plants need water to grow. in dry chiltern summers, plants can be drought-stressed within a week of dry weather. symptoms: wilting, brown leaf edges, dropped flowers. fix: water deeply once a week in dry spells. 4. wrong plants for the conditions plants in the wrong conditions struggle. acid-lovers on chalk go yellow. thirsty plants on dry soils wilt. shade-lovers in sun scorch. fix: choose plants suited to your soil and light conditions. 5. overcrowding overcrowded plants compete for water, nutrients, and light. symptoms: weak, leggy growth, small flowers. fix: lift and divide overcrowded perennials every 3 to 5 years. 6. lack of feeding plants need nutrients to grow. without annual feeding, the soil becomes depleted and the plants struggle. fix: feed in spring with a balanced fertiliser. how to revive a tired border assess the soil: dig a small hole and check the depth, structure, and moisture. add organic matter: 5 to 10cm of compost, dug in. divide overcrowded perennials: lift, divide, replant the vigorous outer sections. remove weeds: dig out perennial weeds, hoe annual weeds. feed: apply a balanced fertiliser in spring. mulch: 5cm of bark or compost to retain moisture. water: deeply once a week in dry spells. prune: remove dead wood and shape shrubs. replant gaps: with plants suited to the conditions. the cost of reviving a tired border item cost (per 10 sqm) compost (1 cubic metre) 80-120 pounds fertiliser 10-20 pounds mulch 30-60 pounds new plants (10) 100-300 pounds labour 100-300 pounds total 320-800 pounds common mistakes not adding organic matter. poor soil produces poor plants. add compost annually. not mulching. bare soil dries out and grows weeds. mulch annually. planting the wrong plants. match plants to your soil and light conditions. not dividing overcrowded perennials. overcrowded plants compete and struggle. divide every 3 to 5 years. not feeding. depleted soil produces weak plants. feed annually. frequently asked questions why are my plants yellow? yellow leaves are usually a sign of nutrient deficiency or drought stress. add compost, feed, and water regularly. how often should i water my borders? weekly in dry spells, applying 25mm (2.5 litres per square metre). water deeply to encourage deep roots. how often should i divide perennials? every 3 to 5 years, depending on the variety. overcrowded perennials produce fewer flowers and weaker growth. get help reviving your borders if your borders are how to prepare soil after garden clearance how to prepare soil after garden clearance. digging, compost, ph testing and soil improvement after clearing an overgrown garden. how to prepare soil after garden clearance soil, compost & mulch after clearing an overgrown garden, the soil is often in poor condition. compacted, depleted and full of weed fragments, it needs work before you can plant. this guide explains how to prepare soil after garden clearance. quick answer to prepare soil after garden clearance: remove all remaining roots and weeds, dig over the soil to relieve compaction, add 5 to 10cm of compost, test the ph, and leave to settle for 2 weeks before planting. on chalky chiltern soils, build depth with compost. on clay, add grit for drainage. cost: 100 to 400 pounds for a typical 50 sqm area. why soil preparation matters after clearance after a garden clearance, the soil is often in poor condition. it has been compacted by foot traffic, depleted of organic matter, and may be full of weed seeds and root fragments. planting directly into this soil will lead to poor results: weak plants, weeds returning, and wasted money. the key is to prepare the soil properly before planting. this takes 1 to 2 weeks but makes the difference between success and failure. in the chilterns, the soil varies between chalk (free-draining, shallow) and clay (heavy, wet), and the preparation is slightly different for each. this guide explains how to prepare soil after garden clearance. step 1: remove remaining roots and weeds after clearance, check for remaining roots and weeds. dig out: perennial weed roots (bindweed, couch grass, ground elder) bramble and ivy root crowns self-seeded tree roots any root fragment left in the soil will regrow. be thorough. step 2: dig over the soil dig over the soil to 20 to 30cm deep to relieve compaction. use a fork for small areas, a rotavator for large areas (50 pounds per day hire). on clay soils, this is particularly important. step 3: add organic matter add 5 to 10cm of compost or well-rotted manure and dig it in. this: improves soil structure adds nutrients improves water retention (on chalk) and drainage (on clay) encourages earthworms on chalky soils, use compost to build depth. on clay, add grit as well to improve drainage. step 4: test the ph test the soil ph with a simple kit. chiltern soils are usually alkaline (ph 7.5 to 8.5). choose plants suited to the ph. step 5: add grit if needed on heavy clay, add horticultural grit (5kg per square metre) to improve drainage. do not add sand, which makes concrete when mixed with clay. step 6: level and firm rake the soil to a level surface. firm gently by walking on it on your heels. do not over-firm, or you will compact it again. step 7: leave to settle leave the soil to settle for 2 weeks before planting. this allows the soil structure to stabilise and the organic matter to start breaking down. if you plant immediately, the soil will sink and the plants will end up at the wrong depth. step 8: mulch before planting apply a 5cm layer of mulch before planting (or after, around the plants). this suppresses weeds and retains moisture. soil preparation for chalk soils on chalky chiltern soils (high wycombe, amersham, wendover): add 10cm of compost to build depth do not add grit (chalk is already free-draining) choose chalk-tolerant plants (lavender, rosemary, salvia) use raised beds if the topsoil is very thin soil preparation for clay soils on clay soils (aylesbury, bicester, didcot): add 5cm of compost for organic matter add 5kg per square metre of horticultural grit for drainage dig deeply to relieve compaction consider installing land drains if waterlogged cost of soil preparation after clearance item diy cost professional cost weed and root removal 0 (tools) 100-200 pounds digging/rotavating (50 sqm) 50 (rotavator hire) 200-400 pounds compost (2 cubic metres) 160-240 200-300 pounds (laid) grit (for clay, 250kg) 50-100 100-200 pounds ph test kit 10-20 included mulch 50-100 100-200 pounds total 320-510 700-1,300 pounds common mistakes not removing all weed roots. any root fragment left will regrow. be thorough. not adding organic matter. poor soil produces poor plants. add compost. adding sand to clay. sand and clay make concrete. use grit, not sand. planting immediately after preparation. the soil needs 2 weeks to settle. not matching plants to soil. choose plants suited to your soil type. frequently asked questions how long after soil preparation can i plant? 2 weeks. this allows the soil to settle and the organic matter to start breaking down. should i import topsoil? if the existing topsoil is less than 10cm deep, import new topsoil. if it is 10 to 20cm deep, improve it with compost. if it is over 20cm deep, just add organic matter annually. what is the best compost for soil improvement? well-rotted farmyard manure or good quality garden compost. avoid mushroom compost on chalky soils (it raises ph further). get help preparing your soil if you need help preparing soil after garden clearance in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire, send us a whatsapp with how to improve soil around mature trees and hedges how to improve soil around mature trees and hedges. competition for water and nutrients, mulching and planting under trees. how to improve soil around mature trees and hedges hedges, pruning & cutting times soil around mature trees and hedges is some of the most difficult soil in any garden. the trees and hedges take all the water and nutrients, leaving the soil dry, thin and impoverished. this guide explains how to improve soil around mature trees and hedges. quick answer to improve soil around mature trees and hedges: mulch annually with compost or bark, water in dry spells, feed in spring, avoid digging (which damages roots), and plant ground cover to suppress weeds. the soil around mature trees and hedges is often dry and depleted because the roots take up water and nutrients. in the chilterns, this is particularly pronounced on chalky soils. the challenge of mature trees and hedges mature trees and hedges are valuable features of a garden, but they create challenging conditions for other plants. the roots of mature trees and hedges take up water and nutrients from a wide area, leaving the surrounding soil dry and depleted. the shade from the canopy further limits what can grow. the result is often bare, dry soil under trees and hedges. in the chilterns, where mature beech, oak, and yew are common, this is a familiar problem. the chalky soils are already dry, and the tree roots make them drier. but with the right approach, you can improve the soil and grow plants under mature trees and hedges. this guide explains how. why soil around mature trees is poor root competition: tree roots take up water and nutrients shade: the canopy blocks light, limiting plant growth dry conditions: the soil is often dry, especially on chalk low organic matter: the tree roots break down organic matter fast compaction: the area under trees is often walked on how to improve the soil 1. mulch annually mulch is the single best thing you can do for soil under trees and hedges. apply a 5 to 10cm layer of compost or bark mulch annually. this: retains moisture adds organic matter as it breaks down suppresses weeds protects tree roots from compaction do not pile mulch against the tree trunk, as this can cause rot. keep a 10cm gap around the trunk. 2. water in dry spells the soil under mature trees is dry because the tree roots take up the water. water the area deeply once a week in dry spells. apply 25mm (2.5 litres per square metre). 3. feed in spring apply a balanced fertiliser in spring. use growmore or blood, fish and bone at 50g per square metre. water in if no rain within 2 days. 4. avoid digging digging around mature trees damages the roots. instead, mulch and let the worms do the work. the worms will incorporate the mulch into the soil over time. 5. plant ground cover plant ground cover plants that tolerate dry shade. these suppress weeds and add organic matter as they grow. good choices: epimedium - evergreen, dry shade bergenia - large leaves, dry shade ivy - evergreen, dry shade pulmonaria - spring flowers, dry shade hardy geraniums - some varieties tolerate dry shade ajuga - low growing, tolerates dry shade plants for dry shade under trees for chalky chiltern soils under mature trees: epimedium bergenia ivy hellebore foxglove primrose bluebell how to plant under mature trees choose the right plants: plants that tolerate dry shade plant in autumn: when the soil is moist and the trees are dormant plant small: small plants establish better under trees plant between roots: do not cut tree roots. plant in the gaps. water regularly: for the first year, water weekly in dry spells mulch after planting: 5cm of bark or compost cost of improving soil under trees and hedges item cost (per 10 sqm) compost or bark mulch 50-100 pounds fertiliser 5-10 pounds ground cover plants (20) 100-300 pounds planting labour 50-150 pounds total 205-560 pounds common mistakes digging around mature trees. this damages the roots. mulch instead. not mulching. mulch is essential for soil under trees. planting thirsty plants. the soil is already dry. choose drought-tolerant plants. planting too close to the trunk. keep a 30cm gap around the trunk. not watering new plants. new plants under trees need regular watering for the first year. frequently asked questions will grass grow under mature trees? poorly. grass struggles in shade and dry soil. better to plant ground cover or mulch the area. can i grow vegetables under trees? no. vegetables need sun and fertile soil. trees provide shade and take up nutrients. how often should i mulch under trees? annually, in spring. apply 5 to 10cm of compost or bark. keep a 10cm gap around the trunk. get help improving how to mulch properly and what to buy how to mulch properly and what to buy. bark, compost, manure, gravel and leaf mould compared. application rates and timing for uk gardens. how to mulch properly and what to buy soil, compost & mulch mulching is the single most beneficial thing you can do for your garden. it suppresses weeds, retains moisture, improves soil and makes everything look tidy. but there are many types of mulch, and using the wrong one wastes money. this guide explains how to mulch properly and what to buy. quick answer to mulch properly: apply a 5cm layer of bark or compost in spring when the soil is moist, keep a 5cm gap around plant stems, and reapply annually. the best mulches for chiltern gardens are bark (for ornamental borders), compost (for vegetables and roses), and gravel (for mediterranean plants). cost: 30 to 100 pounds for a typical 50 sqm border. why mulching matters mulch is the single most effective thing you can do for your garden. a 5cm layer of bark or compost reduces evaporation by 70 percent, suppresses weeds by 80 percent, and adds organic matter to the soil. it is cheap, easy to apply, and makes a huge difference to plant health. in the chilterns, where the chalky soils dry out fast and the clay soils become compacted, mulch is essential. on chalk, mulch retains moisture and builds soil depth. on clay, mulch improves structure and drainage. this guide explains how to mulch properly and what to buy. the benefits of mulching retains moisture: reduces evaporation by 70 percent suppresses weeds: blocks light, prevents germination adds organic matter: as it breaks down, it feeds the soil improves soil structure: especially on clay soils protects roots: from heat in summer and frost in winter looks good: a fresh mulch makes the garden look cared for encourages worms: worms feed on the mulch and aerate the soil types of mulch bark the most popular mulch for ornamental borders. long-lasting, looks good, and suppresses weeds well. cost: 5 to 10 pounds per 50 litre bag (covers about 1 square metre at 5cm depth). ornamental bark: chunky, looks good, lasts 2 to 3 years composted bark: finer, breaks down faster, feeds the soil bark fines: very fine, good for paths and play areas compost best for vegetables, roses, and hungry plants. feeds the soil as well as mulching. cost: 4 to 8 pounds per 50 litre bag. garden compost: free if you make it well-rotted manure: rich in nutrients, good for roses and vegetables mushroom compost: alkaline, good for chalky soils (avoid on acid soils) bagged compost: from garden centres, consistent quality gravel best for mediterranean plants and gravel gardens. permanent, looks good, but does not feed the soil. cost: 5 to 10 pounds per 25kg bag. 10 to 20mm angular gravel: best for gravel gardens cotswold chippings: golden colour, looks good in chiltern gardens slate chippings: dark, modern look leaf mould free if you make it. best for woodland plants. takes 1 to 2 years to make. straw best for strawberries and vegetables. cheap, but blows around in wind. grass clippings free if you have a lawn. good for vegetables, but can smell as they break down. apply in thin layers. how to mulch properly weed first: remove all weeds before mulching. mulch suppresses new weeds but does not kill existing ones. water first: water the soil thoroughly before mulching. mulch locks in moisture, so the soil should be moist first. apply 5cm: a 5cm layer is enough. more than 7cm can smother plant roots. keep a gap around stems: leave a 5cm gap around plant stems to prevent rot. do not pile against tree trunks: keep a 10cm gap around tree trunks. reapply annually: bark and compost break down and need reapplying each year. when to mulch the best time to mulch is mid-spring (march to april), when the soil is moist and warming up. avoid mulching in summer (the soil is too dry) or winter (the soil is too cold). autumn mulching is also effective, as it protects the soil from winter rain and frost. apply after the autumn tidy-up. how much mulch to buy area bark (50l bags) cost 10 sqm (5cm depth) 10 bags 50-100 pounds 20 sqm (5cm depth) 20 bags 100-200 pounds 50 sqm (5cm depth) 50 bags 250-500 pounds 100 sqm (5cm depth) 100 bags (or bulk) 400-700 pounds (bulk) for large areas, buy in bulk (1 cubic metre = 1,000 litres = 20 bags). bulk bark costs 80 to 120 pounds per cubic metre delivered. cost of mulching item cost bark (50 sqm, bulk) 200-300 pounds compost (50 sqm, bulk) 200-400 pounds gravel (50 sqm) 300-600 pounds application labour 50-200 pounds total (bark, 50 sqm) 250-500 pounds common mistakes not weeding first. mulch suppresses new weeds but does not kill existing ones. weed first. not watering first. mulch locks in moisture, so the soil should be moist first. applying too thickly. more than 7cm can smother plant roots. 5cm is enough. piling mulch against stems. this causes rot. keep a 5cm gap around stems. using the wrong mulch. use bark for ornamentals, compost for vegetables, gravel for mediterranean plants. frequently asked questions what is the best mulch for chiltern gardens? bark for ornamental borders, compost for vegetables and roses, gravel for mediterranean plants. on chalky soils, all three work well. how often should i mulch? annually, in spring. bark and compost break down and need reapplying each year. gravel is permanent. can i use grass clippings as mulch? yes, but apply in thin layers (1 to 2cm) to prevent smell. good for vegetables, not for ornamentals. get help with mulching if you want help why your garden looks tired even after mowing why your garden looks tired even after mowing. soil, plant choice, pruning and feeding issues that make a garden look lacklustre. why your garden looks tired even after mowing garden problems & weed control you have mowed the lawn, but the garden still looks tired. the lawn is green but the borders are lacklustre, the shrubs are shapeless and the whole garden lacks sparkle. this guide explains why your garden looks tired even after mowing, and what to do about it. quick answer your garden looks tired even after mowing because: the lawn is mossy or weedy, the borders are unweeded, shrubs are overgrown, paths are dirty, and there is no colour or structure. mowing alone is not enough. to make the garden look fresh: mow and edge, weed the borders, prune shrubs, clean paths, and add a few pots for colour. cost: 100 to 300 pounds for a typical garden. why mowing is not enough many people mow the lawn and wonder why the garden still looks tired. the answer is that mowing is just one of many jobs. a freshly mown lawn looks good for a day, but if the borders are weedy, the shrubs are overgrown, and the paths are dirty, the garden still looks neglected. in the chilterns, where gardens range from small courtyard plots to large estates, the same principle applies. mowing is the foundation, but it is not enough on its own. this guide explains why your garden looks tired even after mowing, and what to do about it. the 6 reasons your garden looks tired 1. the lawn is mossy or weedy a mossy, weedy lawn looks tired even when mown. the moss makes the lawn look patchy, and the weeds (daisies, dandelions, plantain) break up the uniform green. fix: treat moss with iron sulphate, scarify, aerate, overseed, and treat weeds. see our moss guide . 2. the borders are unweeded weeds in the borders make the whole garden look untidy. even a freshly mown lawn looks tired next to weedy borders. fix: weed the borders thoroughly, then mulch to suppress new weeds. 3. shrubs are overgrown overgrown shrubs look messy and dominate the garden. even a tidy lawn and borders look tired next to overgrown shrubs. fix: prune shrubs to a manageable size. see our pruning guide . 4. paths are dirty dirty, mossy, weedy paths make the whole garden look neglected. fix: sweep paths regularly, pressure wash annually, and treat algae. see our pressure washing guide . 5. no colour or structure a garden with no colour (flowers) or structure (evergreens) looks flat and tired. fix: plant a few pots of seasonal colour, add evergreen shrubs for structure, and plant perennials for seasonal interest. 6. edges are not crisp a mown lawn with straggly edges looks untidy. fix: edge along paths and borders with a half-moon edger. crisp edges make a big difference. the 7-step garden refresh mow and edge the lawn: mow at 30 to 40mm, edge with a half-moon edger. weed the borders: dig out perennial weeds, hoe annual weeds. prune overgrown shrubs: cut back to a manageable size. clean paths and patio: sweep, then pressure wash if needed. deadhead flowering plants: to encourage more flowers. add a few pots of colour: by the doors and on the patio. mulch the borders: 5cm of bark or compost to suppress weeds and retain moisture. the cost of a garden refresh job diy cost professional cost mow and edge 0 (own mower) 50-100 pounds weed borders 0 (tools) 100-200 pounds prune shrubs 0 (tools) 100-300 pounds clean paths 50 (hire) 150-300 pounds deadhead 0 (tools) 50-100 pounds pots and plants 30-80 50-150 pounds mulch 50-100 100-200 pounds total 130-230 600-1,350 pounds common mistakes thinking mowing is enough. mowing is just one of many jobs. the whole garden needs attention. not weeding the borders. weedy borders make the whole garden look untidy. not pruning overgrown shrubs. overgrown shrubs dominate the garden. not cleaning paths. dirty, mossy paths make the garden look neglected. not adding colour. a garden with no colour looks flat and tired. frequently asked questions why does my garden look tired even after mowing? because mowing is just one of many jobs. the borders need weeding, shrubs need pruning, paths need cleaning, and the garden needs colour and structure. how often should i do a garden refresh? fortnightly during the growing season (march to october), with a more thorough refresh each season. how much does a garden refresh cost? 130 to 230 pounds diy, or 600 to 1,350 pounds professional, for a typical family garden. get help refreshing your garden if your garden looks tired in oxfordshire, how to rescue a garden after tenants move out how to rescue a garden after tenants move out. clearance, lawn recovery, border restoration and waste removal for landlords. how to rescue a garden after tenants move out garden clearance & tidy-up guides when tenants move out, the garden is often the last thing on their mind. the result is an overgrown, neglected garden that needs rescuing before new tenants move in. this guide explains how to rescue a garden after tenants move out. quick answer to rescue a garden after tenants move out: assess the damage, clear all rubbish and overgrowth, remove weeds and self-seeded trees, repair structural damage, improve the soil, replant with low-maintenance plants, and set up a maintenance schedule. cost: 300 to 1,000 pounds for a typical rental garden. landlords in the chilterns should budget for a garden rescue between tenancies. the tenant move-out garden problem when tenants move out, the garden is often in poor condition. tenants rarely maintain the garden to a high standard, and after a few years, the garden can be overgrown, full of rubbish, and in need of significant work. a neglected garden reduces the property value, makes it harder to re-let, and can cause disputes with neighbours. in the chilterns, where rental properties are common in university towns like oxford and commuter towns like high wycombe, aylesbury, and bicester, landlords regularly face this problem. the good news is that with a systematic approach, even the worst tenant-damaged garden can be rescued in 1 to 3 days. this guide explains how. step 1: assess the damage before starting, walk the garden and note: amount of rubbish and debris level of overgrowth (brambles, ivy, self-seeded trees) weed coverage (bindweed, couch grass, ground elder) structural damage (broken fences, sheds, paths) lawn condition plants worth keeping (mature trees, established shrubs) access for waste removal step 2: clear rubbish and debris remove all rubbish and debris first: tenant's rubbish (old furniture, broken toys, building materials) garden waste (leaves, branches, dead plants) broken structures (old sheds, fence panels) hire a skip (200 to 350 pounds) or use a waste removal service (120 to 200 pounds per tonne). step 3: clear overgrowth clear all overgrowth: cut brambles and ivy to ground level, dig out root crowns remove self-seeded trees cut back overgrown shrubs clear weeds (dig out perennial weed roots) see our clearance service and bramble clearance guide . step 4: repair structural damage repair or replace: broken fence panels and posts broken gates rotting sheds damaged paths and patios see our fencing service . step 5: improve the soil add 5cm of compost and dig it in. remove any buried rubble. see our soil preparation guide . step 6: replant with low-maintenance plants choose low-maintenance plants that will survive neglect: photinia choisya lavender roses hardy geraniums step 7: restore the lawn if the lawn is salvageable, scarify, aerate, overseed. if beyond repair, reseed or returf. see our lawn recovery service . step 8: set up a maintenance schedule to prevent the garden becoming overgrown again, set up a maintenance schedule between tenancies. monthly visits keep the garden under control. cost of rescuing a garden after tenants move out job cost rubbish removal (1 tonne) 120-200 pounds overgrowth clearance 150-400 pounds structural repairs 100-500 pounds soil improvement 100-300 pounds replanting 200-600 pounds lawn restoration 150-400 pounds total 820-2,400 pounds common mistakes not clearing the whole garden. a half-cleared garden looks worse than a fully neglected one. clear it all. planting high-maintenance plants. tenants will not maintain them. choose low-maintenance plants. not repairing structures. broken fences and sheds put off future tenants. not setting up maintenance between tenancies. the garden will become overgrown again without regular maintenance. frequently asked questions can i charge the tenant for garden clearance? only if the tenancy agreement makes the tenant responsible for the garden. check the agreement before deducting from the deposit. how often should a rental garden be cleared? every 3 to 5 years, or between tenancies. this prevents the garden becoming overgrown and protects the property value. what should i plant in a rental garden? low-maintenance plants: evergreen shrubs (photinia, choisya), drought-tolerant perennials (lavender, sedum), and hard-wearing lawn grass. get help rescuing a garden after tenants move out if you are a work, which puts off busy buyers. and it hides the true size and layout of the garden, so buyers cannot see the potential. in the chilterns, where property values are high and buyers are discerning, how to prepare a garden for professional property photos how to prepare a garden for professional property photos. mowing, cleaning, staging and timing for the best estate agent photos. how to prepare a garden for professional property photos gardens for selling or renting estate agent photos are the first thing buyers see on rightmove and zoopla. the garden needs to look its best for the photography session. this guide explains how to prepare a garden for professional property photos. quick answer to prepare a garden for professional property photos: mow and edge the lawn 1 to 2 days before, tidy all visible areas, clean the patio, plant a few pots for colour, open parasols and garden furniture, and choose a sunny day for the photos. cost: 100 to 400 pounds. the photos sell the property, so the garden needs to look its best. why garden photos matter professional property photos are the first thing buyers see on rightmove and zoopla. they determine whether a buyer clicks through to view the property. the garden photos are particularly important: they show the size, the usability, and the condition of the garden. a well-prepared garden photographs well and attracts buyers. a neglected garden photographs badly and puts buyers off. in the chilterns, where gardens are a major selling point, the photos are critical. a good garden photo can add thousands to the sale price. a bad garden photo can lose viewings. this guide explains how to prepare a garden for professional property photos. the 8-step photo preparation 1. mow and edge the lawn (1-2 days before) mow the lawn 1 to 2 days before the photos, not the day of. this gives the grass time to settle and the clippings time to disperse. mow at 30 to 40mm. edge along paths and borders with a half-moon edger. 2. tidy all visible areas weed the borders, deadhead flowering plants, sweep paths and patios. remove all rubbish and clutter. the visible areas (patio, lawn, borders near the house) should look immaculate. 3. clean the patio sweep the patio, then pressure wash if needed. a clean patio looks bigger and more inviting in photos. see our pressure washing service . 4. plant pots for colour place a few pots of seasonal colour by the doors and on the patio. this adds life and colour to the photos. avoid too many pots, which look cluttered. 5. set up garden furniture set up garden furniture: table and chairs on the patio, parasol up if sunny. this helps buyers imagine using the garden. arrange the furniture as if for a meal. 6. hide wheelie bins and clutter move wheelie bins, children's toys, garden tools, and empty pots out of sight. a clutter-free garden photographs better. 7. choose the right day choose a sunny day for the photos. sun makes the garden look bright and inviting. avoid overcast days if possible. if the photos must be taken on an overcast day, ask the photographer to use editing to brighten the images. 8. time of day the best time for garden photos is mid-morning (10 to 11 am) or mid-afternoon (3 to 4 pm), when the sun is at a good angle. avoid midday, when the sun is overhead and creates harsh shadows. the photo preparation timeline when job 1 what buyers notice first in a neglected garden what buyers notice first in a neglected garden. the warning signs that put buyers off and how to fix them before viewings. what buyers notice first in a neglected garden gardens for selling or renting buyers form an opinion within 8 seconds of arriving at a property. the garden is a big part of that first impression. this guide explains what buyers notice first in a neglected garden, so you can fix the problems before viewings. quick answer what buyers notice first in a neglected garden: overgrown lawn, broken fences, bramble patches, rubbish and clutter, dirty patio, dead plants, and overgrown hedges. these are the top 7 turn-offs that reduce the sale price. in the chilterns, where buyers are discerning, a neglected garden can knock 5 to 15 percent off the sale price. why first impressions matter when a buyer arrives at a property, they form an opinion in the first 30 seconds. the garden is a major part of that first impression. a tidy, well-presented garden says "this property is cared for". a neglected garden says "there are problems here". once a buyer has formed a negative opinion, it is hard to change it. in the chilterns, where property values are high and buyers are discerning, a neglected garden is particularly damaging. estate agents in marlow, henley, beaconsfield and amersham tell us that a neglected garden is one of the top three things that put buyers off. this guide explains what buyers notice first in a neglected garden, so you can fix the problems before marketing. the 7 things buyers notice first 1. overgrown lawn an overgrown lawn is the most obvious sign of neglect. it suggests the whole property has been neglected, not just the garden. buyers wonder what else has been neglected (the boiler? the roof? the drains?). fix: mow and edge the lawn before the photos. 2. broken fences broken fences suggest the property is not maintained. they also make the garden feel less private and secure. fix: repair or replace broken fence panels. see our fencing service . 3. bramble patches bramble patches are a clear sign of neglect. they look like a lot of work to clear, which puts buyers off. fix: clear brambles before listing. see our bramble clearance guide . 4. rubbish and clutter rubbish and clutter (old furniture, broken toys, building materials) make the garden look neglected and reduce the apparent size. fix: remove all rubbish and clutter. hire a skip if needed. 5. dirty patio a dirty, mossy patio looks neglected and is a safety hazard. it also makes the garden feel smaller. fix: sweep and pressure wash the patio. see our pressure washing service . 6. dead plants dead plants in the borders suggest the garden is not cared for. they also make the garden look untidy. fix: remove dead plants and replace with healthy ones. 7. overgrown hedges overgrown hedges make the garden feel smaller and darker. they also suggest the property is not maintained. fix: cut hedges to a neat shape. see our hedge cutting service . the cost of fixing the 7 turn-offs problem cost to fix overgrown lawn 0 (diy) to 50-100 pounds broken fences 100-500 pounds bramble patches 100-400 pounds rubbish and clutter 50 (skip) to 200-400 pounds dirty patio 50 (diy) to 150-350 pounds dead plants 50-200 pounds overgrown hedges 100-300 pounds total 450-2,250 pounds the return on investment fixing the 7 turn-offs costs 450 to 2,250 pounds and can add 5,000 to 15,000 pounds to the sale price. the return on investment is 2 to 30 times the cost. common mistakes not fixing obvious problems. buyers notice problems and assume the worst. fix them before listing. spending on the wrong things. fix the obvious problems first. do not spend on expensive landscaping. leaving it too late. allow 2 to 4 weeks before the photos for the fixes to look their best. not clearing the front garden. the front garden is what buyers see first. make sure it is tidy. frequently asked questions what is the biggest turn-off for buyers? an overgrown lawn. it is the most visible sign of neglect and suggests the whole property has been neglected. how much does it cost to fix a neglected garden? 450 to 2,250 pounds to fix the 7 main turn-offs. this can add 5,000 to 15,000 pounds to the sale price. should i fix the garden before listing? yes. fixing the garden before listing is one of the best investments you can make. the return on investment is 2 to 30 times the cost. get help fixing your neglected garden if you are selling a property in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire and need help before probate or inherited property sale gardens for selling or renting when a property is being sold through probate or as an inherited property, the garden is often overgrown and needs clearing before the sale. this guide explains the process of garden clearance before probate or inherited property sale. quick answer garden clearance before probate or inherited property sale: clear the garden to improve the valuation, remove hazards for viewings, and present the property at its best. cost: 400 to 1,500 pounds depending on size and condition. in the chilterns, where inherited properties are common, a clearance before probate can add 5 to 15 percent to the valuation. the inherited property garden problem inheriting a property is often an emotional and logistical challenge. the garden may have been neglected for months or years, particularly if the previous owner was elderly or in care. an overgrown, neglected garden reduces the property valuation, makes viewings difficult, and can cause neighbour disputes. in the chilterns, where many inherited properties are period homes with established gardens, the garden clearance is particularly important. a clearance before probate can add 5 to 15 percent to the valuation, which can mean tens of thousands of pounds on a typical property. this guide explains how to clear a garden before probate or inherited property sale. why clear before probate? higher valuation: a tidy garden increases the property valuation easier viewings: buyers can see the garden properly faster sale: a tidy garden sells faster hazard removal: clear hazards (broken sheds, dead trees) for safety neighbour relations: an overgrown garden can cause neighbour disputes the 6-step clearance process step 1: assess the garden walk the garden and note: level of overgrowth rubbish and debris structural damage (broken fences, sheds) plants worth keeping (mature trees, established shrubs) hazards (dead trees, broken structures) step 2: clear rubbish and debris remove all rubbish, debris, and broken structures. hire a skip (200 to 350 pounds) or use a waste removal service (120 to 200 pounds per tonne). step 3: clear overgrowth clear brambles, ivy, self-seeded trees, and overgrown shrubs. see our clearance service . step 4: remove hazards remove dead trees, broken sheds, and other hazards. a dead tree may need a tree surgeon. a broken shed can be dismantled and removed. step 5: tidy the lawn and borders mow the lawn, weed the borders, and cut the hedges. a tidy lawn and borders make the garden look cared for. step 6: repair obvious damage repair broken fences, gates, and paths. buyers notice broken things and assume the worst. what to keep keep: mature trees (they add value) established shrubs (they provide structure) healthy hedges (they provide privacy) structural elements (paths, walls, patios) in good condition the cost of clearance before probate job cost clearance (small garden) 400-800 pounds clearance (medium garden) 800-1,500 pounds clearance (large/heavily overgrown) 1,500-3,000 pounds tree surgery (if needed) 200-1,000 pounds minor repairs 100-500 pounds total (typical) 500-2,000 pounds the return on investment a clearance before probate can add 5 to 15 percent to the valuation. on a 400,000 pound property, that is 20,000 to 60,000 pounds. the return on investment is 10 to 100 times the cost of the clearance. common mistakes not clearing before probate. an overgrown garden reduces the valuation. removing mature trees. mature trees add value. keep them unless they are dead or dangerous. not repairing obvious damage. broken fences and paths put buyers off. doing the work too late. allow 2 to 4 weeks before the valuation for the garden to look its best. frequently asked questions can i deduct the clearance cost from the estate? yes, clearance costs are an estate expense and can be deducted from the estate before inheritance tax is calculated. check with the executor or solicitor. should i clear the garden before or after probate? before, if possible. a clearance before probate can increase the valuation. if you cannot clear before probate, clear before marketing. how much does clearance cost? 400 to 2,000 pounds for a typical inherited property garden. this can add 20,000 to 60,000 pounds to the valuation. get help clearing an inherited how to make a rental property garden presentable between tenants how to make a rental property garden presentable between tenants. quick tidy-up, lawn care and border maintenance for landlords. how to make a rental property garden presentable between tenants gardens for selling or renting when one tenant moves out and another is about to move in, the garden needs to be presentable. a tidy garden makes the property more attractive to new tenants and justifies the rent. this guide explains how to make a rental property garden presentable between tenants. quick answer to keep a rental property garden presentable between tenants: arrange monthly maintenance visits, secure the property, monitor for problems, and have a key holder for emergencies. cost: 500 to 1,500 pounds per year for a typical rental garden. landlords in the chilterns should budget for this to protect the property value and attract good tenants. the empty rental garden problem when a rental property is empty between tenants, the garden can become overgrown within weeks. an overgrown garden signals that the property is empty, which is a security risk. it also makes the property harder to let, as prospective tenants see a neglected garden and assume the property is not well maintained. in the chilterns, where rental properties are common in university towns like oxford and commuter towns like high wycombe, aylesbury, and bicester, landlords need to keep the garden presentable between tenancies. this guide explains how. the 5-point plan for empty rental gardens 1. monthly maintenance visits arrange monthly maintenance visits to: mow the lawn weed paths and borders check for problems water containers (if any) security check 2. secure the property lock all gates and outbuildings install a key safe for emergency access trim hedges so the property is visible from the road remove valuables from sheds install timer lights 3. monitor for problems check for: broken fences fallen trees or branches leaks signs of break-in pest infestations 4. have a key holder have a key holder (the maintenance gardener or a neighbour) who can provide access for emergencies. 5. comply with insurance most home insurance policies require that an empty property is checked regularly (usually every 7 to 30 days). a maintenance visit can serve as this check. the monthly maintenance schedule season frequency key jobs spring (march to may) monthly mow, weed, check for problems summer (june to august) monthly mow, water containers, check for problems autumn (september to november) monthly mow, leaf clearance, check for problems winter (december to february) monthly quick tidy, security check what a maintenance visit includes mow the lawn and edge weed paths, borders, and driveway check for problems (broken fences, fallen trees, leaks) water containers (if any) sweep paths and patio security check (gates locked, outbuildings secure) report: whatsapp photos and notes to the landlord the cost of keeping a rental garden presentable garden size annual cost small (100 sqm) 500-1,000 pounds medium (300 sqm) 800-1,500 pounds large (500+ sqm) 1,200-2,500 pounds common mistakes not having a maintenance schedule. an overgrown garden signals that the property is empty. not having a key holder. if there is an emergency, no one can provide access. not checking insurance requirements. most policies require regular checks. failure to do so can invalidate the policy. not securing the property. an empty property is a target for burglary and vandalism. not communicating with the landlord. the landlord needs to know what is happening at the property. frequently asked questions how often should an empty rental garden be maintained? monthly. this keeps the garden looking presentable and the property secure. how much does it cost to keep a rental garden presentable? 500 to 1,500 pounds per year for a typical rental garden. this is much less than the cost of a full clearance between tenancies. can i use a neighbour to check the property? yes, for basic checks. but a professional maintenance gardener is better for keeping the garden under control and spotting problems. get help
