country garden design ideas for chiltern homes country garden design ideas for chiltern homes. cottage planting, naturalistic borders, wildflower meadows and informal hedges for a rural feel. country garden design ideas for chiltern homes garden design & layout ideas the chilterns have a distinct rural character, and many homeowners want a garden that reflects it. a country garden is relaxed, naturalistic and full of life. this guide provides country garden design ideas for chiltern homes. quick answer country garden design ideas for chiltern homes: use native planting (hawthorn, hazel, beech), wildflower meadows, orchard trees, naturalistic borders, and rustic materials (stone, timber, brick). combine formal structure near the house with wilder areas further out. in the chilterns, the country garden style suits the rolling hills and chalky soils. the chiltern country garden style the chiltern country garden is a distinct style. it combines formal structure near the house with wilder, naturalistic areas further out. it uses native plants, rustic materials, and a soft, romantic colour palette. it fits the rolling hills, the chalky soils, and the rural character of the chilterns. this guide gives design ideas for country gardens in the chilterns, from small village plots to large estate gardens. the principles are the same regardless of size: work with the local conditions, use native plants, and combine formal and informal elements. the 6 elements of a chiltern country garden 1. native planting use native plants that fit the chiltern landscape: hawthorn - hedges and specimens hazel - hedges and coppice beech - hedges and specimens rowan - specimen tree holly - evergreen structure 2. wildflower meadows a wildflower meadow is the quintessential country garden feature. it supports wildlife, looks beautiful in summer, and needs just one cut per year. see our wildflower guide . 3. orchard a small orchard (5 to 10 trees) is a beautiful feature. choose heritage varieties on semi-dwarfing rootstocks. plant in winter, 5 metres apart. 4. naturalistic borders naturalistic borders use grasses and perennials in drifts, creating a soft, romantic look. choose plants that suit the soil: on chalk: lavender, salvia, verbena, stipa on clay: astrantia, filipendula, deschampsia 5. rustic materials use rustic materials for paths, patios, and structures: stone: cotswold stone, york stone timber: oak sleepers, rustic fencing brick: reclaimed bricks for paths and walls gravel: for paths and driveways 6. wildlife features include wildlife features: pond log piles bird boxes and feeders bee hotels native hedge the three-zone country garden zone 1 (near the house): formal garden with lawn, borders, patio zone 2 (middle of the garden): naturalistic borders, orchard, vegetable garden zone 3 (bottom of the garden): wildflower meadow, woodland, wildlife pond country garden plants for chiltern soils for chalky soils trees: beech, rowan, yew shrubs: philadelphus, weigela, lavender perennials: alliums, verbena, sedum, echinacea grasses: stipa, calamagrostis for clay soils trees: hornbeam, rowan, crab apple shrubs: viburnum, mahonia, hydrangea perennials: hardy geraniums, astrantia, filipendula grasses: miscanthus, deschampsia the cost of a country garden item cost garden design 1,000-4,000 pounds hard landscaping (patio, paths) 3,000-10,000 pounds native hedge (30m) 600-1,500 pounds wildflower meadow (100 sqm) 100-300 pounds orchard (5 trees) 200-500 pounds borders (200 sqm, planted) 2,000-6,000 pounds pond 300-1,500 pounds total 7,200-23,800 pounds common mistakes using non-native plants. native plants fit the chiltern landscape better. over-formal design. a country garden should be soft and naturalistic, not clipped and formal. fighting the soil. match plants to your soil (chalk or clay). not including wildlife features. a country garden should support wildlife. using modern materials. use rustic materials (stone, timber, brick) for a country look. frequently asked questions what is the chiltern country garden style? a combination of formal structure near the house with wilder, naturalistic areas further out. it uses native plants, rustic materials, and a soft colour palette. should i have a wildflower meadow? yes, if you have space. a wildflower meadow is beautiful, low-maintenance, and supports wildlife. see our wildflower guide . what is the best tree for a chiltern country garden? beech on chalk, hornbeam on clay. both are native, long-lived, and beautiful. get help designing your why your garden needs a maintenance plan, not random visits why your garden needs a maintenance plan, not random visits. structured care saves money and keeps the garden healthier long term. why your garden needs a maintenance plan, not random visits regular garden maintenance many garden owners call a gardener when the garden looks bad, then cancel when it looks good. this reactive approach costs more and produces worse results than a maintenance plan. this guide explains why your garden needs a maintenance plan, not random visits. quick answer your garden needs a maintenance plan, not random visits, because: a plan ensures all jobs are done at the right time, prevents the garden becoming overgrown, is more cost-effective, and produces better results. a maintenance plan includes weekly, monthly, seasonal, and annual jobs. in the chilterns, a plan tailored to the local climate and soil produces the best results. why random visits do not work many people call a gardener when the garden how to maintain a mature garden without losing its character how to maintain a mature garden without losing its character. pruning, soil care, plant replacement and preserving the established feel. how to maintain a mature garden without losing its character regular garden maintenance mature gardens have character that new gardens cannot match. the established trees, the clipped hedges, the self-seeded plants between paving, all contribute to a sense of place. but mature gardens also need careful maintenance to stay at their best. this guide explains how to maintain a mature garden without losing its character. quick answer to maintain a mature garden without losing its character: keep mature trees and shrubs, prune regularly to keep them healthy, underplant with shade-tolerant plants, repair rather than replace structures, and avoid over-tidying. a mature garden has decades of character that cannot be replaced. in the chilterns, where many properties have established gardens, preserving this character is essential. the value of a mature garden a mature garden is a valuable asset. the mature trees, established shrubs, and structural elements (paths, walls, hedges) cannot be replaced in a lifetime. a garden that has been tended for 20, 30, or 50 years has a character that a new garden cannot match. the temptation is to "modernise" or "tidy up", but this often destroys the very character that makes the garden special. in the chilterns, where many properties are period homes with established gardens, preserving the character is essential. mature beech, yew, and oak are irreplaceable. established mixed borders have a richness that new borders lack. this guide explains how to maintain a mature garden without losing its character. the 6 principles of mature garden maintenance 1. keep mature trees and shrubs mature trees and shrubs are the most valuable part of a mature garden. keep them, do not remove them. if they are too big, prune them rather than removing them. if they are diseased, treat them rather than removing them. only remove trees that are dead, dangerous, or causing structural damage. 2. prune regularly to keep them healthy regular pruning keeps mature trees and shrubs healthy: remove dead, diseased, and crossing wood thin the canopy to let in light reduce height if needed (by a qualified tree surgeon) prune shrubs after flowering see our pruning guide . 3. underplant with shade-tolerant plants mature trees cast shade. underplant with shade-tolerant plants: hellebores primroses foxgloves ferns epimedium bluebells 4. repair rather than replace repair existing structures rather than replacing them: repoint old walls rather than rebuilding them repair fence panels rather than replacing the whole fence restore old paths rather than laying new ones renovate old sheds rather than buying new ones this preserves the character of the garden and is often cheaper. 5. avoid over-tidying a mature garden has a softness that comes from age. avoid over-tidying: leave some seed heads in autumn for birds leave some leaf litter in borders for wildlife do not clip every shrub into a shape let some plants self-seed 6. improve the soil mature gardens have depleted soil. improve it annually: add compost each spring mulch annually feed mature trees and shrubs in spring the mature garden maintenance schedule frequency how to add wildlife value to a formal garden how to add wildlife value to a formal garden without losing the formal structure. native plants, water features and managed wild areas. how to add wildlife value to a formal garden wildlife & wildflower gardens formal gardens and wildlife gardens are not mutually exclusive. you can have crisp edges, clipped hedges and structured planting while still supporting a wide range of wildlife. this guide explains how to add wildlife value to a formal garden. quick answer to add wildlife value to a formal garden: keep the formal structure (clipped hedges, neat borders) but add wildlife features (pond, native hedge, pollinator-friendly plants, bird boxes, log pile). the result is a garden that looks formal but supports a wide range of wildlife. in the chilterns, this approach is increasingly popular among owners of formal gardens. the formal garden wildlife dilemma formal gardens are beautiful, but they often support little wildlife. the clipped hedges, neat borders, and manicured lawns provide little food or shelter for wildlife. the temptation is to "let it go wild", but this destroys the formal character that makes the garden special. the good news is that you can add wildlife value to a formal garden without losing its character. the key is to keep the formal structure but add wildlife features. a pond, a native hedge, pollinator-friendly plants, bird boxes, and a log pile can all be added without compromising the formal look. this guide explains how. the 7 wildlife features for a formal garden 1. pond a pond is the single best wildlife feature. even a small, formal pond (with a stone edge and clear water) attracts frogs, newts, dragonflies and birds. locate it where it can be seen from the house. 2. native hedge plant a native hedge (hawthorn, blackthorn, hazel, holly) along one boundary. it provides flowers, berries, and nesting sites, while still looking tidy if cut annually. see our hedging guide . 3. pollinator-friendly plants replace some formal bedding with pollinator-friendly plants: lavender - fits formal gardens, bees love it alliums - dramatic, formal, pollinator-friendly verbena bonariensis - tall, airy, butterflies sedum - late flowers for bees single roses - pollinator-friendly, formal 4. bird boxes and feeders install bird boxes and feeders where they can be seen from the house. choose designs that fit the formal garden (e.g. a bird table in the same style as the garden furniture). 5. log pile a log pile in a corner provides habitat for beetles, frogs, and hedgehogs. stack logs neatly to make it look intentional. 6. bee hotels install bee hotels for solitary bees. mount them on a south-facing wall, 1 to 2 metres off the ground. 7. water for birds a bird bath or shallow dish of water provides drinking water for birds and insects. locate it where it can be seen from the house. how to add wildlife features without losing formal character keep the formal structure: clipped hedges, neat borders, manicured lawn add a formal pond: with a stone edge and clear water plant a native hedge along one boundary: cut annually for a neat shape choose pollinator-friendly formal plants: lavender, alliums, single roses install bird boxes and feeders: in matching style add a log pile in a corner: stacked neatly install bee hotels: on a south-facing wall add a bird bath: wildlife pond maintenance: what to cut back and when wildlife pond maintenance: what to cut back and when. plant care, water quality, cleaning and seasonal tasks for a healthy wildlife pond. wildlife pond maintenance: what to cut back and when wildlife & wildflower gardens a wildlife pond needs less maintenance than a formal pond, but it still needs some care. the key is knowing what to cut back, what to leave, and when to do it. this guide explains wildlife pond maintenance throughout the year. quick answer wildlife pond maintenance: clear excess vegetation in autumn (not spring), remove dead leaves, thin out aquatic plants, keep 30 percent of the surface clear, and clean the pond every 3 to 5 years. in the chilterns, ponds on chalky soils need less maintenance than ponds on clay. the key is to clean in autumn to avoid disturbing spawning amphibians. why wildlife ponds need maintenance a wildlife pond is a valuable garden feature, but it needs maintenance to stay healthy. without maintenance, ponds become choked with vegetation, fill with silt, and become stagnant. the wildlife that depends on the pond (frogs, newts, dragonflies) declines. with proper maintenance, a wildlife pond can support a wide range of species for decades. in the chilterns, where gardens often have ponds (both formal and informal), maintenance is essential. the chalky soils produce less algae than clay soils, but all ponds need annual maintenance. this guide explains what to cut back and when. the 6 maintenance jobs 1. clear excess vegetation (autumn) clear excess aquatic vegetation in autumn (october to november). remove about 30 percent of the vegetation to keep the pond healthy. leave the removed vegetation on the side of the pond for 24 hours, so that any creatures can crawl back into the water. 2. remove dead leaves (autumn) remove dead leaves from the pond in autumn. leaves rot down and produce methane, which degrades water quality. use a net to skim leaves off the surface. 3. thin out aquatic plants (autumn) thin out aquatic plants that have become too vigorous: water lily: remove dead leaves and divide if too big iris: cut back dead leaves, divide if too big marsh marigold: cut back dead growth reeds and rushes: thin to prevent them taking over 4. keep 30 percent of the surface clear keep at least 30 percent of the pond surface clear of vegetation. this allows light into the water and lets oxygen exchange happen. if more than 70 percent of the surface is covered, thin the plants. 5. clean the pond (every 3 to 5 years) clean the pond every 3 to 5 years to remove silt. the best time is autumn (october to november), when amphibians are less active. steps: pump out half the water into a temporary container remove fish and amphibians to the temporary container remove plants to the temporary container dig out the silt (use it on the garden, it is rich in nutrients) refill with the saved water and fresh water return plants and creatures to the pond 6. check water level (summer) in dry summers, the water level can drop. top up with rainwater if possible. avoid tap water, which contains chlorine and nutrients that encourage algae. the pond maintenance calendar month job january leave alone. wildlife is hibernating. february check for frog spawn. do not disturb. march to may leave alone. frogs and newts are spawning. june to august top up water level in dry spells. september plan autumn maintenance. october clear excess vegetation, remove dead leaves. november clean pond (if due). thin aquatic plants. december leave alone. wildlife is hibernating. why clean in autumn, not spring? clean the pond in autumn (october to november), not spring. in spring, frogs and newts are spawning, and cleaning will destroy the spawn. in autumn, amphibians are less active and can be moved to a temporary container without harm. common mistakes cleaning the pond in spring. this destroys frog and newt spawn. clean in autumn. removing all vegetation. keep at least 50 percent for wildlife habitat. using tap water to top up. tap water contains chlorine and nutrients. use rainwater. removing silt every year. silt provides habitat. clean only every 3 to 5 years. not leaving removed vegetation on the side. creatures need time to crawl back into the pond. frequently asked questions how often should i clean my wildlife pond? every 3 to 5 years, in autumn. this removes silt without disturbing spawning amphibians. why is my pond green? algae. reduce algae by keeping 30 percent of the surface clear, adding oxygenating plants, and avoiding tap water. should i have fish in a wildlife pond? no. fish eat frog spawn, newt larvae, and dragonfly nymphs. a wildlife pond should be fish-free. get help with wildlife pond maintenance if you have a wildlife pond in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire and want help with maintenance, send us a whatsapp with photos and we will aim to respond within one day. whatsapp best native shrubs for birds in uk gardens best native shrubs for birds in uk gardens. berry-producing, shelter-providing and insect-attracting shrubs that support garden birds. best native shrubs for birds in uk gardens wildlife & wildflower gardens garden birds need three things: food, shelter and nesting sites. the right shrubs provide all three. native shrubs are best because they support the insects that birds feed on, as well as producing berries. this guide lists the best native shrubs for birds in uk gardens. quick answer the best native shrubs for birds in uk gardens: hawthorn (flowers, berries, nesting), blackthorn (sloes, nesting), holly (berries, evergreen shelter), hazel (catkins, nuts), dogwood (berries, nesting), guelder rose (berries), and spindle (berries). plant a mix of 3 to 5 species for year-round food and shelter. in the chilterns, these native shrubs thrive on the chalky soils and support a wide range of bird species. why native shrubs matter for birds native shrubs are the best plants for birds. they provide berries, seeds, insects, and nesting sites. they have evolved alongside uk birds, so the birds are adapted to eat their berries and nest in their branches. non-native shrubs may look good, but they support far fewer birds. in the chilterns, where farmland hedges have been removed over decades, garden shrubs are increasingly important for birds. a garden with a mix of native shrubs can support more bird species than a garden with non-native shrubs. this guide lists the best native shrubs for birds in uk gardens, with specific recommendations for chiltern soils. the 7 best native shrubs for birds 1. hawthorn (crataegus monogyna) the best single shrub for birds. flowers in may (hawthorn blossom), berries in autumn (haws), thorny nesting sites. supports over 300 species of insect, which feed the birds. loves chalk. plant 3 to 5 in a hedge or as specimens. 2. blackthorn (prunus spinosa) flowers in march before the leaves, sloes in autumn. thorns provide dense nesting cover. supports many moth caterpillars, which feed the birds. native. loves chalk. 3. holly (ilex aquifolium) evergreen, berries in winter (on female plants), nesting sites. slow-growing but very long-lived. berries are eaten by thrushes, blackbirds, and finches. loves chalk. 4. hazel (corylus avellana) catkins in spring (yellow pollen for early bees), hazelnuts in autumn. supports dormice (in some areas) and many moth caterpillars. nuts are eaten by jays, woodpeckers, and squirrels. native. 5. dogwood (cornus sanguinea) white flowers in spring, black berries in autumn, coloured stems in winter. good for wet soils. berries are eaten by thrushes and blackbirds. native. 6. guelder rose (viburnum opulus) white flowers in spring, red berries in autumn. good for damp soils. berries are eaten by birds in winter. native. 7. spindle (euonymus europaeus) unusual pink and orange berries in autumn. supports many moth caterpillars. berries are eaten by birds. native. other native shrubs for birds elder (sambucus nigra): berries for birds, flowers for insects field maple (acer campestre): seeds for birds, native wayfaring tree (viburnum lantana): berries for birds purging buckthorn (rhamnus cathartica): berries for birds, food for brimstone butterfly caterpillars how to plant for birds plant a mix of species: 3 to 5 species, in a hedge or mixed border plant in autumn or winter: bare-root, november to march plant 3 to 5 per metre for a hedge, or 1 to 2 metres apart for specimens water regularly for the first season mulch annually to suppress weeds prune in late winter (february) to avoid disturbing nesting birds leave berries for the birds. do not cut all the berries off for decoration. the bird-friendly hedge a mixed native hedge is the best single feature for birds. plant: 50 percent hawthorn 25 percent blackthorn 15 percent hazel 10 percent holly (or other species) plant 5 to 7 per metre in a double staggered row. cut once a year in late winter. see our hedge for birds guide . the year-round food calendar season what is available which birds spring catkins (hazel), flowers (hawthorn, blackthorn) blue tits, great tits (insects) summer insects on shrubs blue tits, great tits, warblers autumn berries (hawthorn, holly, guelder rose, spindle) thrushes, blackbirds, finches winter berries (holly, ivy), nuts (hazel) thrushes, blackbirds, finches, jays the cost of planting for birds item cost bare-root shrubs (60-80cm) 2-4 pounds each native hedge (10m, 50 plants) 100-200 pounds specimen shrubs (5) 50-100 pounds mulch 20-50 pounds planting labour 50-200 pounds total 220-550 pounds common mistakes planting non-native shrubs. non-native shrubs support far fewer birds. choose native species. planting a single species. a mix of 3 to 5 species provides year-round food and shelter. cutting in nesting season. it is an offence to damage active nests. cut in late winter. cutting all the berries off. leave berries for the birds. do not cut all the berries off for decoration. not watering new plants. new shrubs need weekly watering for the first growing season. frequently asked questions what is the best single shrub for birds? hawthorn. it flowers in may, berries in autumn, and provides thorny nesting sites. plant 3 to 5 in a hedge. when should i cut my bird-friendly hedge? once a year, in late winter (february), after the berries have been eaten. avoid cutting between march and august when birds are nesting. will native shrubs grow on chalky chiltern soil? yes. containers and overgrown borders. this checklist explains what to do before you go away. quick answer the pre-holiday garden checklist: mow the lawn, water deeply, deadhead, weed, secure the garden, post-holiday garden rescue: what to do when everything has overgrown post-holiday garden rescue. what to do when everything has overgrown while you were away. mowing, watering, weeding and recovery. post-holiday garden rescue: what to do when everything has overgrown seasonal garden jobs coming back from holiday to an overgrown garden is disheartening. but with the right approach, you can get it back under control in a day or two. this guide explains what to do when everything has overgrown while you were away. quick answer post-holiday garden rescue: mow the lawn (high setting), water deeply, deadhead, weed, harvest overripe produce, check for pests, and prune damaged plants. most gardens recover from a 1 to 2 week holiday within 2 to 3 weeks. in the chilterns, where summers can be dry, post-holiday rescue focuses on watering and lawn recovery. the post-holiday garden shock coming home from holiday to an overgrown, dry garden is a common experience. the lawn is long, the containers are dead, the weeds have taken over, and the slugs have eaten the hostas. the good news is that most gardens recover from a 1 to 2 week holiday within 2 to 3 weeks, with a bit of effort. in the chilterns, where summers can be dry and hot, post-holiday rescue focuses on watering and lawn recovery. this guide explains how to rescue a garden after a holiday. the 8-step post-holiday rescue 1. mow the lawn (high setting) if the lawn is long, mow it on a high setting (40 to 50mm) first. do not cut it short in one go, or you will shock the grass. lower the mower over 2 to 3 cuts, a few days apart. 2. water deeply water the garden deeply. soak the lawn, borders, and containers. this revives drought-stressed plants and helps the lawn recover. 3. deadhead deadhead all flowering plants. this encourages new flowers and tidies up the plants. 4. weed weed the borders. weeds will have grown while you were away. dig out perennial weeds, hoe annual weeds. 5. harvest overripe produce harvest any overripe fruit and vegetables. overripe produce attracts pests and diseases. compost anything that is past its best. 6. check for pests check for pests that may have multiplied while you were away: aphids on roses and perennials slugs and snails on hostas and seedlings caterpillars on brassicas red spider mite in greenhouses treat as needed. 7. prune damaged plants prune plants that have been damaged by drought, wind, or pests. remove dead and diseased growth. 8. feed feed the garden with a balanced fertiliser. this helps plants recover from the stress of drought and neglect. the post-holiday rescue timeline day job day 1 mow (high), water deeply, deadhead, harvest day 2 weed, check for pests, prune damaged plants day 3 feed, mulch, further tidying week 2 lower mower, second weed, second deadhead week 3 garden should be back to normal what to do with dead containers if containers have died while you were away: check if they are dead or just dormant: scratch the bark. if green underneath, they may recover. cut back dead growth: and water deeply. some plants will regrow. replace if dead: with new plants from the garden centre. install automatic watering: for next time you go away. the cost of a post-holiday rescue item cost mowing and weeding (diy) 0 mowing and weeding (professional) 100-250 pounds replacement plants 50-200 pounds fertiliser 10-30 pounds automatic watering (for next time) 50-200 pounds total 160-680 pounds common mistakes cutting the lawn too short. a long lawn needs to be cut back gradually, not scalped in one go. not watering enough. drought-stressed plants need deep watering to recover. not checking for pests. pests multiply quickly. check and treat promptly. throwing away plants that could recover. some plants recover from drought. check before you replace. not installing automatic watering for next time. install drip irrigation before your next holiday. frequently asked questions will my lawn recover from going brown? yes, in most cases. grass goes dormant in drought and recovers when watered. water deeply and mow on a high setting. how long does it take to rescue a garden after holiday? 2 to 3 weeks for most gardens. the first week is the heavy work (mowing, weeding, watering). the second and third weeks are recovery. should i replace dead containers? check first if they are dead or just dormant. scratch the bark: if green underneath, they may recover. if brown and brittle, replace. get help rescuing your garden if you have come home from holiday to an overgrown garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, storm damage garden checklist: what to inspect first storm damage garden checklist. what to inspect first after a storm. trees, fences, structures, paths and plants. safety first. storm damage garden checklist: what to inspect first seasonal garden jobs after a storm, the garden can be a dangerous place. fallen branches, damaged fences and loose structures all pose risks. this checklist explains what to inspect first after storm damage. quick answer the storm damage garden checklist: check for fallen trees and branches, check fences and structures, check the roof and gutters, check paths and patios, check plants for damage, and document everything for insurance. in the chilterns, where winter storms are common, this checklist helps you assess damage quickly and prioritise repairs. why storm damage checklists matter storms can cause significant damage to gardens. fallen trees, broken fences, damaged patios, and split plants are all common after a storm. the key is to assess the damage quickly, prioritise the repairs, and document everything for insurance. this guide gives a storm damage garden checklist, so you know what to check and in what order. in the chilterns, where winter storms (november to february) are common and many gardens have mature trees, storm damage is a regular occurrence. this checklist is based on what we see after storms across oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire and bedfordshire. the 8-point storm damage checklist 1. check for fallen trees and branches walk the garden and check for: fallen trees broken branches (hanging or on the ground) leaning trees split trunks if a tree has fallen on a path, building, or power line, call a tree surgeon immediately. do not try to remove it yourself. 2. check fences and structures check for: fallen fence panels broken fence posts leaning gates damaged sheds and outbuildings broken trellis and arches see our fencing service for repairs. 3. check the roof and gutters check for: missing or loose tiles blocked gutters (leaves and debris) loose aerials and chimneys damage to fascias and soffits 4. check paths and patios check for: loose paving slabs cracked patios sunken areas blocked drains 5. check plants for damage check for: broken stems on shrubs and perennials split shrubs leaning plants wind-rocked plants (lifted roots) frost damage (blackened leaves) prune damaged growth to prevent disease. 6. check ponds and water features check for: debris in the pond blocked pumps damaged liners flooded ponds 7. check garden furniture check for: blown-over furniture broken tables and chairs damaged parasols displaced pots and containers 8. document everything for insurance if you plan to claim on insurance: take photos of all damage note the date and time of the storm keep receipts for emergency repairs contact your insurer before starting major repairs the priority order for repairs safety: fallen trees on paths, buildings, or power lines weatherproofing: damaged roofs, gutters, and windows security: broken fences and gates plant care: prune damaged plants, stake leaning plants structural: damaged paths, patios, and outbuildings cosmetic: minor damage that does not affect safety or function the cost of storm damage repairs item cost tree removal (small tree) 200-500 pounds tree removal (large tree) 500-2,000 pounds fence repair (per panel) 50-100 pounds fence replacement (10m) 500-1,500 pounds shed repair 100-500 pounds patio repair 200-1,000 pounds plant replacement 50-300 pounds common mistakes not checking for hanging branches. hanging branches can fall and injure people. remove them immediately. trying to remove large trees yourself. large tree removal is dangerous. hire a qualified tree surgeon. not documenting damage for insurance. take photos before starting repairs. not pruning damaged plants. damaged plants are vulnerable to disease. prune them promptly. not checking ponds and water features. debris in ponds can kill fish and wildlife. clean them out. frequently asked questions will my insurance cover storm damage to the garden? usually yes, but check your policy. most policies cover fences, sheds, and outbuildings, but may not cover plants. should i try to remove a fallen tree myself? no, if it is large or has fallen on a building, path, or power line. hire a qualified tree surgeon. small trees (under 5cm trunk diameter) can be removed yourself. how do i prevent storm damage in the future? prune trees regularly, replace loose fence posts, secure garden furniture, and check gutters before winter. get help with storm damage if your garden in oxfordshire, buckinghamshire, berkshire or bedfordshire has been damaged by a storm, send us a whatsapp with photos and we will aim to respond within one day. end-of-tenancy garden checklist for landlords and tenants end-of-tenancy garden checklist for landlords and tenants. what the garden should look like, deposit deductions and dispute resolution. end-of-tenancy garden checklist for landlords and tenants seasonal garden jobs the garden is one of the most common areas of dispute at the end of a tenancy. both landlords and tenants need to know what is expected. this checklist explains what the garden should look like at the end of a tenancy. quick answer the end-of-tenancy garden checklist for landlords and tenants: assess the garden condition, compare to the inventory, agree on repairs, clear rubbish, restore the lawn, repair structures, and document everything. in the chilterns, where rental properties are common, a clear end-of-tenancy process prevents disputes over deposits. the end-of-tenancy garden problem the end of a tenancy is a common source of disputes between landlords and tenants. the garden is often at the centre of these disputes. the landlord claims the garden has been neglected and deducts the cost of restoration from the deposit. the tenant claims the garden was already in poor condition and refuses to pay. the key to avoiding disputes is a clear process: assess the garden, compare to the inventory, agree on repairs, and document everything. this guide gives an end-of-tenancy garden checklist for landlords and tenants, based on what works in the chilterns rental market. the 8-step end-of-tenancy process 1. assess the garden condition walk the garden with the tenant and note: lawn condition border condition hedge condition fence condition shed and outbuilding condition patios and paths plants and trees rubbish and debris 2. compare to the inventory compare the current condition to the inventory taken at the start of the tenancy. the tenant is responsible for returning the garden in the same condition, allowing for fair wear and tear. 3. agree on repairs agree on what needs to be done: tenant's responsibility (clear rubbish, mow lawn, weed) landlord's responsibility (repair fences, replace dead plants) shared cost (major work like hedge cutting) 4. clear rubbish the tenant should clear all rubbish and debris from the garden. this includes: tenant's rubbish (furniture, toys, building materials) garden waste (leaves, branches) broken items (pots, furniture) 5. restore the lawn the tenant should mow and edge the lawn. if the lawn is beyond repair, the landlord may need to reseed or returf. 6. repair structures the landlord should repair: broken fences broken gates rotting sheds damaged paths and patios see our fencing service . 7. document everything take photos of the garden at the end of the tenancy, with dates. this prevents disputes over the deposit. 8. agree on deposit deductions if the garden is in worse condition than at the start of the tenancy, agree on the cost of restoration and deduct from the deposit. if the tenant disputes, use the deposit protection scheme's dispute resolution service. the end-of-tenancy checklist for tenants mow and edge the lawn weed the borders clear all rubbish and debris sweep paths and patio tidy the shed remove all personal items take photos of the garden when you leave the end-of-tenancy checklist for landlords inspect the garden with the tenant compare to the inventory agree on repairs repair fences, gates, and sheds restore the lawn if beyond repair replant dead plants take photos of the garden after the tenant leaves set up a maintenance schedule between tenancies the cost of end-of-tenancy garden restoration job cost clear rubbish 50-200 pounds mow and tidy 50-150 pounds lawn restoration 150-400 pounds fence repair 100-500 pounds shed repair or removal 100-500 pounds replanting 100-400 pounds total 550-2,150 pounds common mistakes not having an inventory. without an inventory, you cannot prove the condition at the start of the tenancy. not documenting the end condition. take photos with dates to prevent disputes. not agreeing on repairs before the tenant leaves. agree on what needs to be done before the tenant leaves. charging the tenant for fair wear and tear. tenants are not responsible for fair wear and tear, only for damage. not setting up maintenance between tenancies. the garden will become overgrown between tenancies without maintenance. frequently asked questions can i deduct garden restoration from the deposit? yes, if the garden is in worse condition than at the start of the tenancy (allowing for fair wear and tear). document the damage with photos. what is fair wear and tear in a garden? fair wear and tear includes: lawn wear, minor weed growth, plant death (if not deliberate), and ageing of structures. it does not include: rubbish, broken fences, or deliberately damaged plants. should the tenant mow the lawn before leaving? yes. the tenant should return the garden in the same condition as at the start, which includes mowing the lawn. get help
